topics.title: Calibration of Vernier Caliper, Micrometer, LVDT and Dial Gauge; Determination of Class of Fits Between Shaft and Hole; Study and Analysis of Repeatability and Reproducibility

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB

> - **Topic:** Calibration of Vernier Caliper, Micrometer, LVDT and Dial Gauge; Determination of Class of Fits Between Shaft and Hole; Study and Analysis of Repeatability and Reproducibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Calibration?

**Calibration** is the process of comparing the readings of an instrument against a reference standard of known accuracy, and adjusting it (if necessary) to bring its output within an acceptable range of error. In metrology, calibration establishes the **traceability** of a measuring instrument to a national or international standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Calibration is *not* the same as "zeroing" an instrument. Zeroing removes offset error only. Calibration verifies *accuracy*, *linearity*, *repeatability*, and *hysteresis* across the full measuring range.

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy
Think of calibration like checking your **weighing scale at home** against the certified scale at a doctor's clinic. You place a known 1 kg weight (standard) and check if the scale reads exactly 1.000 kg. The difference is the **error**, and adjusting for it is **calibration**.

---

## 1.2 Vernier Caliper — Definition

A **Vernier Caliper** is a precision linear measuring instrument used to measure **outside dimensions (OD)**, **inside dimensions (ID)**, **depth**, and **steps** of an object with high accuracy. It consists of a **main scale (MSR)** and a **sliding vernier scale (VSR)**.

**Least Count (LC)** of a standard Vernier Caliper = **0.02 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Least count is the smallest measurable increment that an instrument can resolve. For Vernier Caliper with 20 VSD (Vernier Scale Divisions) per 19 MSD (Main Scale Divisions):
> $$LC = \frac{1 \text{ MSD}}{20} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{20} = 0.02 \text{ mm}$$

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **ruler with a secondary sliding scale** that lets you measure fractions without guessing. The Vernier scale slides along the main scale, and where its marks align perfectly with the main scale's marks tells you the fractional part (the part smaller than 1 mm).

---

## 1.3 Micrometer (Screw Gauge) — Definition

A **Micrometer** is a precision instrument used to measure small linear dimensions accurately (typically up to **0.01 mm** resolution). It works on the principle of a **precisely machined screw thread** that converts rotary motion into linear motion.

**Least Count** of a standard metric micrometer with 0.5 mm pitch and 50 thimble divisions:
$$LC = \frac{0.5 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$

### 1.3.1 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a **C-clamp with a very precise screw**. Every full turn moves the spindle exactly **0.5 mm**. The thimble tells you the fractional part of that 0.5 mm. Just like reading a car's odometer where one full turn gives a major unit and the dial gives the fraction.

---

## 1.4 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) — Definition

An **LVDT** is an **electromechanical transducer** that converts linear displacement into a proportional electrical (AC) voltage. It consists of one **primary coil** and two identical **secondary coils** wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic **core** inside.

### 1.4.1 Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **magnetic seesaw** — when a soft iron core slides through the middle, it shifts the magnetic coupling between the primary (input) and the two secondaries (output). If the core is in the center, both secondaries receive equal voltage (zero net output). If the core moves toward one secondary, that secondary's voltage goes up and the other goes down.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** LVDT is a **passive inductive transducer** requiring an external AC excitation (typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz** at **1 V to 24 V RMS**).

---

## 1.5 Dial Gauge (Dial Indicator) — Definition

A **Dial Gauge** (or **Dial Indicator**) is a precision instrument used for measuring small linear displacements with high magnification, typically via a **rack and pinion** or **lever mechanism** that drives a pointer on a graduated dial.

**Least Count** of a standard metric dial gauge = **0.01 mm** (one pointer revolution = **1 mm** typically).

### 1.5.1 Intuitive Analogy
Like the **speedometer of a car** — a small movement at the input (gear) is multiplied and shown as a large, easy-to-read movement on the dial face.

---

## 1.6 Class of Fits Between Shaft and Hole — Definition

A **Fit** is the degree of **tightness** or **looseness** between two mating parts — a shaft and a hole. According to **ISO 286 (Indian Standard IS 919)**, fits are classified into three broad categories based on the relative sizes of shaft and hole.

### 1.6.1 Three Fundamental Classes of Fit (KTU Syllabus)

| Fit Type | Condition | Typical Application |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Clearance Fit** | Hole > Shaft (always) | Sliding parts, bearings, bushings |
| **Transition Fit** | Sometimes clearance, sometimes interference | Gears, pulleys, couplings on shafts |
| **Interference Fit** | Shaft > Hole (always) | Permanent joints, rivets, shrink-fit assemblies |

### 1.6.2 Intuitive Analogy
Think of fitting a **ring on your finger**:
- **Clearance fit** = a loose ring that spins freely.
- **Transition fit** = a snug ring that requires a slight push but goes on.
- **Interference fit** = a tight ring that needs force (or hammer) and stays put.

---

## 1.7 Repeatability and Reproducibility (Gage R&R)

- **Repeatability (Equipment Variation, EV):** The variation observed when the **same operator** measures the **same part** with the **same instrument** multiple times.
- **Reproducibility (Operator Variation, AV):** The variation observed when **different operators** measure the **same part** with the **same instrument**.

### 1.7.1 Intuitive Analogy
Think of three judges scoring a **diving competition**:
- **Repeatability** = Judge A scores the same diver consistently (small variation across multiple rounds of the same dive).
- **Reproducibility** = The agreement among all three judges. If judges widely disagree, reproducibility is poor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gage R&R** is the standard metric defined by **AIAG MSA (Measurement System Analysis)** to quantify total measurement system variation. It is the **sum of Repeatability + Reproducibility**.

---

## 1.8 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Summary

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|----------------|
| Vernier Caliper LC | **0.02 mm** |
| Micrometer pitch (standard metric) | **0.5 mm** |
| Micrometer LC | **0.01 mm** |
| LVDT excitation frequency | **1 kHz – 10 kHz** |
| LVDT excitation voltage | **1 V – 24 V RMS** |
| Dial Gauge LC | **0.01 mm** |
| Slip Gauge accuracy class | **Grade 0, Grade 1, Grade 2** |
| ISO Tolerance grade symbol | **IT01 to IT18** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Vernier Caliper Calibration

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
The Vernier caliper works on the principle of **vernier interpolation** — a method for precisely measuring fractions of the smallest main-scale division by using a secondary scale where divisions are slightly offset.

### 2.1.2 Calibration Procedure (Step-by-Step)

1. **Clean the instrument** — Wipe main jaws, depth rod, and vernier scale with a lint-free cloth.
2. **Zero check** — Close the jaws gently. Check if zero of main scale aligns with zero of vernier scale.
3. **Record zero error** — If zero of vernier is to the *right* of main scale zero → **positive zero error**; if to the *left* → **negative zero error**.
4. **Calibrate with slip gauges** — Measure standard slip gauges of known length (e.g., 10 mm, 25 mm, 50 mm).
5. **Calculate error** — Compare indicated value with actual (slip gauge) value.
6. **Repeat** at multiple points across the range (typically **5 to 10 points**).
7. **Plot calibration curve** — Error vs. Indicated Value.

### 2.1.3 Reading Calculation

$$\text{Measured Value} = \text{MSR} + (\text{VSR} \times \text{LC}) - \text{Zero Error}$$

### 2.1.4 Sources of Error
- **Zero error** (instrumental)
- **Parallax error** (operator)
- **Worn or damaged jaws** (instrumental)
- **Excess measuring force** (operator)
- **Dirty contact surfaces** (environmental)

---

## 2.2 Theory of Micrometer Calibration

### 2.2.1 Working Principle
A micrometer uses a **precision screw thread** to convert angular rotation of the thimble into axial linear motion of the spindle. One full rotation of the thimble advances the spindle by exactly the **pitch** of the screw (typically 0.5 mm).

### 2.2.2 Calibration Procedure

1. **Clean anvil and spindle faces.**
2. **Zero check** — Bring anvil and spindle together using the **ratchet stop** (NOT directly turning the thimble, to avoid excess force).
3. **Record zero error.**
4. **Measure known standards** — Slip gauges or precision cylinder pins.
5. **Check parallelism** using an optical flat and **monochromatic light** (interference bands indicate parallelism).
6. **Plot error curve.**

### 2.2.3 Reading Calculation

$$\text{Measured Value} = \text{MSR} + (\text{CSR} \times \text{LC}) \pm \text{Zero Error}$$

Where **CSR = Coinciding Scale Reading** on the thimble.

---

## 2.3 Theory of LVDT Calibration

### 2.3.1 Construction
- **Primary coil (P):** Energized by AC excitation source.
- **Secondary coils (S₁ and S₂):** Identical, wound in **opposite polarity (anti-series)**.
- **Core:** Ferromagnetic, free to slide inside the bore.
- **Former:** Hollow non-magnetic cylinder housing the coils.

### 2.3.2 Working Principle
When the core is in the **null (central) position**, voltages induced in S₁ and S₂ are **equal and opposite**, so the **net output is zero**.

$$\text{Output Voltage } V_{\text{out}} = V_{S_1} - V_{S_2}$$

- Core moves toward **S₁** → V_S₁ > V_S₂ → Output is **positive**.
- Core moves toward **S₂** → V_S₂ > V_S₁ → Output is **negative**.

The magnitude of V_out is **proportional to displacement** within the linear range.

### 2.3.3 Calibration Procedure

1. **Mount LVDT** in a calibration jig with a precision micrometer head.
2. **Connect AC excitation** (e.g., 5 V, 5 kHz).
3. **Connect DMM or CRO** to measure differential output.
4. **Set micrometer to zero** (core at null position) — record output.
5. **Incrementally move micrometer** by 0.5 mm steps across the range.
6. **Record output voltage** at each step (both directions for hysteresis).
7. **Plot:** Output Voltage vs. Displacement → Calibration curve.
8. **Calculate sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis.**

### 2.3.4 LVDT Performance Parameters

- **Sensitivity** = dV/dx (V/mm)
- **Linearity** = (Max deviation from best-fit line / Full-scale output) × 100%
- **Hysteresis** = (Max difference in output for same displacement on forward vs. reverse stroke) / Full-scale output × 100%
- **Range** = Maximum linear displacement (±x mm)

---

## 2.4 Theory of Dial Gauge Calibration

### 2.4.1 Working Principle
Linear displacement of the plunger is converted into rotation via a **rack-and-pinion** mechanism. The pointer rotates over a calibrated dial face. A **bezel** allows zero setting.

### 2.4.2 Calibration Procedure

1. **Mount dial gauge** on a **calibration stand** or **height master**.
2. **Zero the dial** at a reference position.
3. **Move plunger** in known increments using slip gauges on a surface plate.
4. **Record readings** at each increment.
5. **Repeat for full range** in both directions (to assess hysteresis).
6. **Calculate error, repeatability, and hysteresis.**

---

## 2.5 Theory of Limits and Fits (Class of Fits)

### 2.5.1 Key Definitions

| Term | Shaft | Hole |
|------|-------|------|
| **Upper Deviation** | es (lowercase) | ES (uppercase) |
| **Lower Deviation** | ei | EI |
| **Maximum Size** | Shaft max = es | Hole max = ES |
| **Minimum Size** | Shaft min = ei | Hole min = EI |
| **Tolerance** | T_s = es - ei | T_h = ES - EI |
| **Basic Size** | Same for both | Same for both |

### 2.5.2 Hole-Based System (KTU Standard)
In the **hole-based system**, the **hole is fixed** and the shaft is varied to obtain different fits. The **fundamental deviation of hole H** has its **lower deviation EI = 0** (i.e., minimum size = basic size).

### 2.5.3 Fit Identification Symbol
A fit is denoted as: **Hole Code / Shaft Code**

**Example:** `H7/g6` — Hole H7 (clearance), shaft g6 (clearance) → **Clearance Fit**

### 2.5.4 Fit Categories Based on Hole-Shaft Tolerance Relation

| Fit Type | Relation between Hole and Shaft |
|----------|----------------------------------|
| **Clearance Fit** | Minimum hole > Maximum shaft (always clearance) |
| **Transition Fit** | Hole range overlaps shaft range |
| **Interference Fit** | Maximum hole < Minimum shaft (always interference) |

---

## 2.6 Theory of Gage R&R (Repeatability and Reproducibility)

### 2.6.1 AIAG MSA Method — Average and Range Method

**Procedure:**
1. Select **n = 10 parts** randomly from production.
2. Use **o = 3 operators** (or 2 to 3).
3. Each operator measures each part **r = 2 or 3 times** in random order.
4. Calculate ranges and averages.

### 2.6.2 Calculation Steps (Detailed)

Let:
- n = number of parts
- r = number of trials per operator
- o = number of operators

For each operator i and part j:
- Range of trials = $R_{ij}$ (max – min)
- Average of trials = $\bar{X}_{ij}$

Then compute:
- Average range per operator: $\bar{R}_i$
- Overall average range: $\bar{\bar{R}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i \sum_j R_{ij} / (\text{operator})$ — simplified: $\bar{\bar{R}}$ averaged across operators.
- Overall average per part: $\bar{X}_j$ (averaged over all operators and trials).

### 2.6.3 Standard Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula |
|----------|---------|
| Repeatability (Equipment Variation, EV) | $EV = \bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1$ |
| Reproducibility (Operator Variation, AV) | $AV = \sqrt{(X_{\text{diff}} \times K_2)^2 - \frac{EV^2}{n r}}$ |
| Gage R&R | $GRR = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2}$ |
| Part Variation (PV) | $PV = R_p \times K_3$ |
| Total Variation (TV) | $TV = \sqrt{GRR^2 + PV^2}$ |
| %GRR (Tolerance) | $\%GRR_T = \frac{GRR}{\text{Tolerance}} \times 100$ |
| %GRR (Process) | $\%GRR_P = \frac{GRR}{TV} \times 100$ |

**Constants (AIAG MSA):**

| Trials (r) | K₁ | K₂ | K₃ (n=10) |
|------------|-----|-----|-----------|
| 2 | 0.8862 | 0.7071 | 0.4030 |
| 3 | 0.5908 | 0.5231 | 0.4468 |

Where:
- $X_{\text{diff}} = \max(\bar{X}_i) - \min(\bar{X}_i)$ (max difference between operator averages).
- $R_p = \max(\bar{X}_j) - \min(\bar{X}_j)$ (range of part averages).

### 2.6.4 Acceptance Criteria (AIAG)

| %GRR | Decision |
|------|----------|
| **< 10%** | **Acceptable** — measurement system is capable. |
| **10% – 30%** | **Conditionally acceptable** — depends on cost, importance, application. |
| **> 30%** | **Unacceptable** — measurement system needs improvement. |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Vernier LC | $LC = \frac{1 \text{ MSD}}{\text{No. of VSD}}$ | mm |
| 2 | Micrometer LC | $LC = \frac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{No. of thimble divisions}}$ | mm |
| 3 | Vernier Reading | $L = MSR + (VSR \times LC) \pm ZE$ | mm |
| 4 | Micrometer Reading | $L = MSR + (CSR \times LC) \pm ZE$ | mm |
| 5 | LVDT Sensitivity | $S = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta x}$ | V/mm |
| 6 | LVDT Linearity Error | $\%L = \frac{\Delta V_{\max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| 7 | LVDT Hysteresis | $\%H = \frac{V_{\text{up}} - V_{\text{down}}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Shaft Tolerance | $T_s = es - ei$ | mm |
| 9 | Hole Tolerance | $T_h = ES - EI$ | mm |
| 10 | Max Clearance | $C_{\max} = H_{\max} - S_{\min}$ | mm |
| 11 | Max Interference | $I_{\max} = S_{\max} - H_{\min}$ | mm |
| 12 | Repeatability (EV) | $EV = \bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1$ | mm |
| 13 | Reproducibility (AV) | $AV = \sqrt{(X_{\text{diff}} \cdot K_2)^2 - \frac{EV^2}{n r}}$ | mm |
| 14 | Gage R&R | $GRR = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2}$ | mm |
| 15 | %GRR (Tolerance) | $\%GRR_T = \frac{GRR}{T} \times 100$ | % |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vernier Caliper & Micrometer:** Daily inspection in workshops, QC departments, machining cells.
- **LVDT:** Used in **strain measurement**, **position feedback** in CNC machines, **profilometers**, **load cells** (as core component), **pressure transducers**, **automotive suspension testing**, **bridge deflection monitoring**.
- **Dial Gauge:** Used in **lathe tailstock alignment**, **surface grinder setup**, **runout measurement**, **flatness testing**, **comparator stands**.
- **Class of Fits:** Direct application in **bearing selection (e.g., H7/g6 for loose bearings, H7/r6 for tight bearings)**, **gearing assemblies**, **engine pistons and cylinders**, **tooling**.
- **Gage R&R:** Mandatory in **automotive industry** (IATF 16949), **aerospace**, **medical devices** for **PPAP (Production Part Approval Process)** submissions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Vernier Caliper Calibration

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A vernier caliper has a main scale reading of **24 mm** and the **12th division** of the vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. The least count is **0.02 mm**. The instrument has a **+0.04 mm** zero error.

**Find:** (a) The actual reading, (b) Whether the instrument reads high or low.

### 3.1.2 Solution

**Given Data:**
- Main Scale Reading (MSR) = 24 mm
- Vernier Scale Reading (VSR) = 12 divisions
- Least Count (LC) = 0.02 mm
- Zero Error (ZE) = +0.04 mm (positive)

**Step 1:** Identify zero error sign convention.
Positive zero error means the vernier zero is to the **right** of main scale zero when jaws are closed. This must be **subtracted**.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$\text{Measured Value} = MSR + (VSR \times LC) - ZE$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$\text{Measured Value} = 24 + (12 \times 0.02) - 0.04$$

**Step 4:** Compute inside parentheses:

$$\text{Measured Value} = 24 + 0.24 - 0.04$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$\boxed{\text{Measured Value} = 24.20 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6:** Check whether high or low.
A positive zero error of +0.04 mm means that with no actual dimension, the caliper reads +0.04 mm. Therefore, all measured values are **higher than true value by 0.04 mm** → the instrument reads **high**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Micrometer Calibration

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement
A micrometer (0–25 mm range) has the following scales:
- Main scale shows **14 mm** (above thimble).
- Thimble shows **25 divisions** coinciding with the datum line.
- Zero error = **−0.03 mm**.

**Find:** Actual measured dimension.

### 3.2.2 Solution

**Given Data:**
- Main Scale Reading (MSR) = 14 mm
- Coinciding Scale Reading (CSR) = 25 divisions
- Least Count (LC) = 0.01 mm (standard)
- Zero Error (ZE) = −0.03 mm

**Step 1:** Identify zero error convention.
A **negative zero error** means the thimble zero is **below** the datum line when faces touch. This is **added** algebraically (since subtracting a negative is adding).

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$\text{Measured Value} = MSR + (CSR \times LC) \pm ZE$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values. For negative ZE, we subtract:

$$\text{Measured Value} = 14 + (25 \times 0.01) - (-0.03)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the multiplication:

$$\text{Measured Value} = 14 + 0.25 + 0.03$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:

$$\boxed{\text{Measured Value} = 14.28 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A negative zero error must be **subtracted** (which mathematically is the same as adding its magnitude). Many students make the mistake of adding it directly, causing double-correction.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — LVDT Calibration Curve

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
An LVDT is calibrated using a micrometer head. The following readings are recorded (excitation: 5 V, 5 kHz):

| Displacement x (mm) | Output V_out (V, forward) | Output V_out (V, reverse) |
|----------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| −5.0 | −4.95 | −4.90 |
| −2.5 | −2.50 | −2.48 |
| 0.0 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| +2.5 | +2.52 | +2.50 |
| +5.0 | +5.05 | +5.00 |

**Find:** (a) Sensitivity, (b) % Linearity, (c) % Hysteresis.

### 3.3.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Compute average V_out at each point:**

$$V_{\text{avg}}(0) = 0.00 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(+2.5) = \frac{2.52 + 2.50}{2} = 2.51 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(+5.0) = \frac{5.05 + 5.00}{2} = 5.025 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(-2.5) = -2.49 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(-5.0) = -4.925 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity (in linear region, +2.5 to +5.0):**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta x} = \frac{5.025 - 2.51}{5.0 - 2.5} = \frac{2.515}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitivity} = 1.006 \text{ V/mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Best-fit linear line:**
Ideal sensitivity ≈ 1.000 V/mm. Best-fit line: $V = 1.000 \cdot x$.

**Step 4 — Compute deviation from ideal:**

| x (mm) | V_avg (V) | V_ideal (V) | Deviation (V) |
|---------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| −5.0 | −4.925 | −5.000 | +0.075 |
| −2.5 | −2.49 | −2.500 | +0.010 |
| 0.0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| +2.5 | +2.51 | +2.500 | +0.010 |
| +5.0 | +5.025 | +5.000 | +0.025 |

**Maximum deviation** = **0.075 V**
**Full-scale output (FS)** = **5.000 V**

**Step 5 — % Linearity:**

$$\%L = \frac{\Delta V_{\max}}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{0.075}{5.000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%L = 1.5\%}$$

**Step 6 — Hysteresis calculation (at +5.0 mm):**

$$V_{\text{forward}} - V_{\text{reverse}} = 5.05 - 5.00 = 0.05 \text{ V}$$

$$\%H = \frac{0.05}{5.000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%H = 1.0\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students compute hysteresis as a difference at **every** point and then take the maximum. The correct approach is to compute the **forward–reverse** difference at **each** displacement, identify the **maximum**, and then express it as a percentage of FS output.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Class of Fit (Hole-Shaft Limits)

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement
Given a **H7/h6 fit** at a basic size of **40 mm**.

From standard tables:
- Hole H7 at 40 mm: $ES = +0.025$ mm, $EI = 0$ mm.
- Shaft h6 at 40 mm: $es = 0$ mm, $ei = -0.016$ mm.

**Find:**
(a) Maximum and minimum hole sizes.
(b) Maximum and minimum shaft sizes.
(c) Maximum clearance and minimum clearance.
(d) Type of fit.

### 3.4.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Hole dimensions:**

$$H_{\max} = \text{Basic} + ES = 40 + 0.025 = 40.025 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{\min} = \text{Basic} + EI = 40 + 0 = 40.000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft dimensions:**

$$S_{\max} = \text{Basic} + es = 40 + 0 = 40.000 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_{\min} = \text{Basic} + ei = 40 - 0.016 = 39.984 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Clearance:**

$$C_{\max} = H_{\max} - S_{\min} = 40.025 - 39.984$$

$$\boxed{C_{\max} = 0.041 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Clearance:**

$$C_{\min} = H_{\min} - S_{\max} = 40.000 - 40.000$$

$$\boxed{C_{\min} = 0.000 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Fit Type:**
Since $C_{\min} = 0$ and $C_{\max} > 0$, the hole is always equal to or larger than the shaft. This is a **Clearance Fit** (specifically, a **snug clearance fit** — sometimes called "sliding fit with zero minimum clearance").

### 3.4.3 Visual Representation

```
Hole range:   [40.000 ..................... 40.025]
Shaft range:  [39.984 ............ 40.000]
                                ↑ Touching point
```

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Gage R&R (Average & Range Method)

### 3.5.1 Problem Statement
**10 parts** are measured by **3 operators**, each measuring **2 trials**. The following average and range data are obtained:

| Part j | Op A Avg $\bar{X}_{Aj}$ | Op A Range $R_{Aj}$ | Op B Avg $\bar{X}_{Bj}$ | Op B Range $R_{Bj}$ | Op C Avg $\bar{X}_{Cj}$ | Op C Range $R_{Cj}$ |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 20.10 | 0.05 | 20.25 | 0.10 | 20.30 | 0.12 |
| 2 | 25.20 | 0.08 | 25.35 | 0.06 | 25.50 | 0.09 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

(Summarized — assume $\bar{\bar{R}} = 0.08$, $X_{\text{diff}} = 0.30$, $R_p = 9.50$, Tolerance T = 5.00 mm.)

**Constants:** $K_1 = 0.8862$ (for r=2), $K_2 = 0.7071$ (for r=2), $K_3 = 0.4030$ (for n=10).

**Find:** EV, AV, GRR, %GRR.

### 3.5.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Equipment Variation (EV = Repeatability):**

$$EV = \bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1 = 0.08 \times 0.8862$$

$$\boxed{EV = 0.0709 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Reproducibility (AV):**

$$AV = \sqrt{\left( X_{\text{diff}} \times K_2 \right)^2 - \frac{EV^2}{n r}}$$

$$AV = \sqrt{(0.30 \times 0.7071)^2 - \frac{(0.0709)^2}{10 \times 2}}$$

$$AV = \sqrt{(0.2121)^2 - \frac{0.00503}{20}}$$

$$AV = \sqrt{0.04499 - 0.000251}$$

$$AV = \sqrt{0.04474}$$

$$\boxed{AV = 0.2115 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Gage R&R:**

$$GRR = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2} = \sqrt{(0.0709)^2 + (0.2115)^2}$$

$$GRR = \sqrt{0.00503 + 0.04473}$$

$$GRR = \sqrt{0.04976}$$

$$\boxed{GRR = 0.2231 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Part Variation (PV):**

$$PV = R_p \times K_3 = 9.50 \times 0.4030$$

$$\boxed{PV = 3.8285 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Total Variation (TV):**

$$TV = \sqrt{GRR^2 + PV^2} = \sqrt{(0.2231)^2 + (3.8285)^2}$$

$$TV = \sqrt{0.04977 + 14.6574}$$

$$TV = \sqrt{14.7072}$$

$$\boxed{TV = 3.8350 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6 — %GRR (Tolerance):**

$$\%GRR_T = \frac{GRR}{T} \times 100 = \frac{0.2231}{5.00} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%GRR_T = 4.46\%}$$

**Step 7 — %GRR (Process):**

$$\%GRR_P = \frac{GRR}{TV} \times 100 = \frac{0.2231}{3.8350} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%GRR_P = 5.82\%}$$

### 3.5.3 Conclusion
Since $\%GRR < 10\%$, the measurement system is **ACCEPTABLE** per AIAG criteria.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Measuring Instruments

| Parameter | Vernier Caliper | Micrometer | Dial Gauge | LVDT |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|------|
| **Principle** | Vernier interpolation | Screw thread | Rack-pinion | Mutual inductance |
| **Range** | 0–150, 0–300 mm | 0–25, 25–50 mm | 0–10 mm | ±1 to ±500 mm |
| **Least Count** | 0.02 mm | 0.01 mm | 0.01 mm | Infinite (analog output) |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.02 mm | ±0.01 mm | ±0.01 mm | ±0.5% FS |
| **Output Type** | Manual/visual | Manual/visual | Manual/visual | Electrical (analog AC) |
| **Application** | OD, ID, depth | Small OD only | Small displacements | Dynamic/transient measurements |
| **Calibration Standard** | Slip gauge | Slip gauge / Optical flat | Slip gauge + Surface plate | Micrometer head + DMM |
| **Best for** | Workshop | Lab/inspection | Comparator | Electronic instrumentation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Calibration] --> B[Clean Instrument and Reference Standard]
    B --> C[Check and Record Zero Error]
    C --> D{Is Zero Error within Tolerance?}
    D -- No --> E[Adjust or Note for Correction]
    D -- Yes --> F[Measure Reference Standards]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Record Readings at 5-10 Points]
    G --> H[Calculate Error at Each Point]
    H --> I[Plot Calibration Curve: Error vs Indicated Value]
    I --> J[Compute Repeatability and Hysteresis]
    J --> K{Is Instrument within Spec?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Pass - Issue Calibration Certificate]
    K -- No --> M[Reject - Service or Replace]
    M --> N[End]
    L --> N
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vernier Caliper Reading Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hold Part Firmly] --> B[Close Jaws Gently on Part]
    B --> C[Read MSR from Main Scale]
    C --> D[Find Coinciding VSD on Vernier]
    D --> E[Apply Formula MSR plus VSR times LC]
    E --> F[Apply Zero Error Correction]
    F --> G[Final Measured Value in mm]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Micrometer Reading Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Position Part Between Anvil and Spindle] --> B[Rotate Thimble via Ratchet Stop]
    B --> C[Read MSR Above Datum Line]
    C --> D[Read Coinciding CSR on Thimble]
    D --> E[Apply Formula MSR plus CSR times LC]
    E --> F[Apply Zero Error Sign Convention]
    F --> G[Final Measured Value in mm]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Internal Architecture and Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Excitation Source 5V 5kHz] --> B[Primary Coil P]
    B --> C{Position of Ferromagnetic Core}
    C -- Core at Null --> D[Both Secondaries Induce Equal Voltage]
    D --> E[Net Output is Zero]
    C -- Core Displaces Right --> F[Secondary S1 Receives More Flux]
    F --> G[V_S1 greater than V_S2]
    G --> H[Output is Positive Proportional to Displacement]
    C -- Core Displaces Left --> I[Secondary S2 Receives More Flux]
    I --> J[V_S2 greater than V_S1]
    J --> K[Output is Negative Proportional to Displacement]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gage R and R Study Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select n=10 Parts Randomly from Process] --> B[Select o=3 Operators]
    B --> C[Each Operator Measures Each Part r=2 or 3 Times]
    C --> D[Record Data in Random Order to Avoid Bias]
    D --> E[Compute Range R for each Operator-Part Combo]
    E --> F[Compute Average X-bar for each Operator-Part Combo]
    F --> G[Compute Average of Ranges R-bar-bar]
    G --> H[Compute EV equals R-bar-bar times K1]
    H --> I[Compute X-diff equals Max minus Min Operator Averages]
    I --> J[Compute AV equals sqrt of X-diff times K2 squared minus EV squared over nr]
    J --> K[Compute GRR equals sqrt of EV squared plus AV squared]
    K --> L[Compute PV equals R-part times K3]
    L --> M[Compute TV equals sqrt of GRR squared plus PV squared]
    M --> N[Compute Percent GRR for Tolerance and Process]
    N --> O{Percent GRR less than 10 percent}
    O -- Yes --> P[Measurement System Acceptable]
    O -- No --> Q{Percent GRR between 10 and 30}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Conditionally Acceptable]
    Q -- No --> S[Unacceptable - Improve System]
```

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Decision Diagram — Class of Fit Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Hole and Shaft Tolerance Zones] --> B{Is Minimum Hole greater than Maximum Shaft?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Clearance Fit]
    B -- No --> D{Is Maximum Hole less than Minimum Shaft?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Interference Fit]
    D -- No --> F[Transition Fit]
    C --> G[Examples H7_g6, H8_f7]
    F --> H[Examples H7_k6, H7_n6]
    E --> I[Examples H7_p6, H7_s6]
```

---

## 4.7 Mermaid Block Diagram — Dial Gauge Calibration Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dial Gauge Mounted on Calibration Stand] --> B[Plunger Resting on Surface Plate]
    B --> C[Slip Gauge Stack Inserted Under Plunger]
    C --> D[Dial Gauge Reading Recorded]
    D --> E[Slip Gauge Value Compared with Reading]
    E --> F[Error Computed]
    F --> G[Repeat for 5-10 Different Heights]
    G --> H[Plot Calibration Curve]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Least Count. Calculate the least count of a Vernier Caliper having 25 Vernier Scale Divisions (VSD) coinciding with 24 Main Scale Divisions (MSD), where 1 MSD = 1 mm.**

#### Model Answer

**Least Count:** The smallest measurable increment that an instrument can resolve, or the difference between the smallest main-scale division and one vernier-scale division.

**Calculation:**
$$\text{LC} = 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD} = \frac{1 \text{ MSD}}{\text{Number of VSD}}$$

$$\text{LC} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{25} = 0.04 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{LC} = 0.04 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answering Tip:** Always state the **definition** first (1 mark) and then **show the calculation** clearly (2 marks).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the difference between Repeatability and Reproducibility in measurement systems?**

#### Model Answer

| Aspect | Repeatability | Reproducibility |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Definition | Variation when the **same operator** measures the **same part** with the **same instrument** repeatedly under **same conditions**. | Variation when **different operators** measure the **same part** with the **same instrument**. |
| Cause | **Equipment Variation (EV)** — instrument intrinsic variation. | **Operator Variation (AV)** — due to differences in technique, training, or judgement. |
| Metric | $\bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1$ | $\sqrt{(X_{\text{diff}} \times K_2)^2 - \frac{EV^2}{nr}}$ |
| AIAG Term | Equipment Variation (EV) | Operator Variation (AV) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Solve **ONE** out of the two questions. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an LVDT. List any **four** advantages and **two** limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An LVDT is calibrated and the following data are obtained for forward and reverse strokes:

| x (mm) | +5.0 | +2.5 | 0.0 | −2.5 | −5.0 |
|--------|------|------|-----|-------|------|
| V_forward (V) | +4.95 | +2.50 | 0.00 | −2.48 | −4.92 |
| V_reverse (V) | +4.85 | +2.45 | 0.00 | −2.50 | −4.90 |

Compute the **sensitivity**, **% linearity**, and **% hysteresis** of the LVDT.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Working Principle of LVDT:**

The LVDT is an electromechanical transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional AC voltage. It consists of:
- **One primary coil (P)** — wound centrally on a hollow cylindrical former, energized by an AC source.
- **Two secondary coils (S₁ and S₂)** — identical in size and turns, wound on either side of the primary, connected in **series opposition (anti-polarity)**.
- **A movable soft-iron core** — slides inside the bore, attached to the object whose displacement is to be measured.

**Operating Conditions:**
- When the core is at the **null (central) position**, equal voltages are induced in S₁ and S₂. Since they are connected in opposition, the **net differential output is zero**.
- When the core moves **toward S₁**, the flux linking S₁ increases and S₂ decreases → **V_out = V_S₁ − V_S₂ is positive**.
- When the core moves **toward S₂**, the reverse occurs → **V_out is negative**.
- Within the linear range, **V_out is directly proportional to displacement**.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Infinite resolution** — output is a smooth continuous voltage; no quantisation steps.
2. **High sensitivity** — typically 1 V/mm or higher.
3. **Frictionless operation** — no mechanical contact between core and coils.
4. **Low hysteresis** and excellent linearity.

**Two Limitations:**
1. Requires **AC excitation** and **phase-sensitive demodulation** circuitry.
2. Sensitive to **external magnetic fields** and **temperature variations**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute average V at each displacement:**

$$V_{\text{avg}}(+5.0) = \frac{4.95 + 4.85}{2} = 4.900 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(+2.5) = \frac{2.50 + 2.45}{2} = 2.475 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(0) = 0.00 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(-2.5) = \frac{-2.48 + (-2.50)}{2} = -2.490 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{\text{avg}}(-5.0) = \frac{-4.92 + (-4.90)}{2} = -4.910 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity (using +2.5 to +5.0 range):**

$$S = \frac{4.900 - 2.475}{5.0 - 2.5} = \frac{2.425}{2.5}$$

$$\boxed{S = 0.970 \text{ V/mm}}$$

**Step 3 — % Linearity:**
Ideal best-fit line: $V_{\text{ideal}} = 0.970 \cdot x$

| x (mm) | V_avg | V_ideal | Deviation |
|--------|-------|---------|-----------|
| +5.0 | 4.900 | 4.850 | 0.050 |
| +2.5 | 2.475 | 2.425 | 0.050 |
| 0.0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| −2.5 | −2.490 | −2.425 | 0.065 |
| −5.0 | −4.910 | −4.850 | 0.060 |

Max deviation = 0.065 V; FS = 4.900 V

$$\%L = \frac{0.065}{4.900} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%L = 1.33\%}$$

**Step 4 — % Hysteresis:**
Max forward-reverse difference = $4.95 - 4.85 = 0.10$ V (at +5.0 mm)

$$\%H = \frac{0.10}{4.900} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%H = 2.04\%}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch, the **Hole-Based System of Limits and Fits**. State the **three types of fits** with one example each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shaft of size **$45_{-0.025}^{-0.010}$ mm** and a hole of size **$45_{0}^{+0.025}$ mm** are assembled. Determine:
- (i) Maximum clearance / interference.
- (ii) Minimum clearance / interference.
- (iii) Type of fit.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Hole-Based System:**
In the hole-based system (preferred by ISO and IS), the **hole is taken as the constant member** with its **lower deviation (EI) = 0** (for fundamental deviation H). All fits are obtained by varying the **shaft** dimensions.

**Shaft fundamental deviations:**
- Shafts **a to h** have the upper deviation **es** as fundamental (clearance fit shafts).
- Shafts **j to zc** have the lower deviation **ei** as fundamental (interference/transition fit shafts).

**Three Types of Fits:**

| Fit Type | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Clearance Fit** | Hole is always larger than the shaft; minimum hole > maximum shaft | H7/g6 — sliding bearings |
| **Transition Fit** | Either clearance or interference is possible | H7/k6 — gear on shaft |
| **Interference Fit** | Shaft is always larger than the hole; maximum hole < minimum shaft | H7/p6 — pin in bushing |

**Sketch (described):**
- A horizontal line at the basic size with shaded tolerance zones above and below.
- Hole H zone is drawn above the basic line (since EI = 0).
- Shaft zones are drawn at different positions: **g** zone is below basic line (clearance), **k** straddles basic line (transition), **p** is above basic line (interference).

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- Hole: 45 mm with $EI = 0$, $ES = +0.025$
- Shaft: 45 mm with $es = -0.010$, $ei = -0.025$

**Step 1 — Hole limits:**

$$H_{\max} = 45 + 0.025 = 45.025 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_{\min} = 45 + 0 = 45.000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft limits:**

$$S_{\max} = 45 - 0.010 = 44.990 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_{\min} = 45 - 0.025 = 44.975 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum value (clearance or interference):**

$$H_{\max} - S_{\min} = 45.025 - 44.975 = +0.050 \text{ mm}$$

Since this is **positive**, it is the **Maximum Clearance**.

$$\boxed{C_{\max} = 0.050 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum value:**

$$H_{\min} - S_{\max} = 45.000 - 44.990 = +0.010 \text{ mm}$$

This is positive, so **Minimum Clearance**.

$$\boxed{C_{\min} = 0.010 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Type of Fit:**
Since both $C_{\max}$ and $C_{\min}$ are **positive** (hole always larger than shaft), this is a **CLEARANCE FIT** (specifically a **running fit** — `H8/e8` family equivalent).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "maximum clearance" with "minimum interference." Always compute **both** $(H_{\max} - S_{\min})$ and $(H_{\min} - S_{\max})$. If both are positive → clearance fit; both negative → interference fit; opposite signs → transition fit. **Two marks** are lost if the type is misidentified.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Vernier Zero Error Sign:**
> Always identify whether zero error is **positive or negative** before substituting. Subtract positive ZE; subtract (i.e., add magnitude of) negative ZE. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Micrometer Pitch Confusion:**
> Some micrometers have **1 mm pitch** (thread pitch = 1 mm) instead of 0.5 mm. Always verify the pitch from the instrument. With pitch = 1 mm and 100 divisions → LC = 0.01 mm; with pitch = 0.5 mm and 50 divisions → LC = 0.01 mm. Same LC, different reasoning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — LVDT Hysteresis Direction:**
> Hysteresis = (V_forward − V_reverse) at the **same** displacement. The maximum of this **across the range** is divided by **Full-Scale Output**, not by the value at the point of measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Class of Fit Sign Confusion:**
> Always remember: **Upper deviation − Lower deviation = Tolerance (always positive).** Lower deviation can be negative; upper deviation can be positive. Don't confuse "upper" with "positive" or "lower" with "negative."

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 5 — GRR Constants:**
> AIAG constants depend on the **number of trials (r)** and **number of parts (n)**. Use the correct table. Mixing constants leads to wrong GRR value — losing 3+ marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 6 — Units:**
> Keep units consistent throughout. Slip gauges are typically in **mm**. LVDT voltages in **V**. Clearance/Interference in **mm**. Mismatch = -1 mark minimum.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🔹 Calibration Fundamentals
- Calibration = comparison with known standard + adjustment/documentation.
- Always check and record **zero error first**.
- Use **slip gauges** as reference standards (Grade 0 or Grade 1).
- Plot **error vs. indicated value** calibration curve.

### 🔹 Vernier Caliper
- LC = 1 MSD / Number of VSD (typically 1/20 = 0.02 mm).
- Formula: $L = MSR + (VSR \times LC) \pm ZE$.
- Used for OD, ID, depth, step.

### 🔹 Micrometer
- LC = Pitch / Number of thimble divisions (typically 0.5/50 = 0.01 mm).
- Always use **ratchet stop** to avoid excess pressure.
- Formula: $L = MSR + (CSR \times LC) \pm ZE$.
- Check **parallelism** using optical flat and monochromatic light.

### 🔹 LVDT
- Passive inductive transducer, needs AC excitation.
- Output is **differentially proportional** to core displacement.
- **Sensitivity** = V/mm; **Linearity** < 1–2% ideally; **Hysteresis** < 1% ideally.
- Calibrate using **micrometer head + DMM/CRO**.

### 🔹 Dial Gauge
- LC typically 0.01 mm.
- Calibration on a **surface plate with slip gauge stacks**.
- Check **repeatability** and **hysteresis** across full range.

### 🔹 Class of Fits (ISO 286 / IS 919)
- **Hole-based system** is standard.
- Hole **H**: $EI = 0$; varying shaft for different fits.
- Three fits: **Clearance, Transition, Interference**.
- Key formulae:
  - $C_{\max} = H_{\max} - S_{\min}$
  - $C_{\min} = H_{\min} - S_{\max}$
  - $I_{\max} = S_{\max} - H_{\min}$
  - $I_{\min} = S_{\min} - H_{\max}$

### 🔹 Gage R&R
- AIAG MSA method: 10 parts × 3 operators × 2–3 trials.
- $EV = \bar{\bar{R}} \times K_1$.
- $AV = \sqrt{(X_{\text{diff}} \cdot K_2)^2 - \frac{EV^2}{n r}}$.
- $GRR = \sqrt{EV^2 + AV^2}$.
- $\%GRR_T = \frac{GRR}{T} \times 100$.
- **<10%** → Acceptable; **10–30%** → Conditionally acceptable; **>30%** → Unacceptable.

### 🔹 Examiner Pitfall Summary
- Watch the **sign of zero error**.
- Watch the **sign of deviations** (es, ei, ES, EI) — they can be negative.
- Watch the **hysteresis formula** — denominator is **full-scale output**, not the value at the point.
- Watch the **constants** K₁, K₂, K₃ — they depend on r and n.

### 🔹 KTU Frequently Asked Topics
1. Least count calculation (Vernier and Micrometer).
2. Reading calculation with zero error.
3. LVDT sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis.
4. Fit determination given deviation values.
5. Gage R&R full calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angular Measurements Using Sine Bar, Bevel Protractor, Combination Sets, Clinometers, and Angle Dekkor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Angular Measurements Using Sine Bar, Bevel Protractor, Combination Sets, Clinometers, and Angle Dekkor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Angular Measurement — Formal KTU Definition

**Angular measurement** in metrology refers to the determination of the inclination of a surface, edge, or feature with respect to a reference plane or axis. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMEL508 — Mechanical Engineering Lab), this is experimentally achieved through five principal precision instruments: the **Sine Bar**, **Universal Bevel Protractor**, **Combination Set**, **Clinometer**, and **Angle Dekkor**. Each instrument is engineered to operate on a specific physical principle — trigonometric, optical, mechanical, or gravitational — and is selected based on the **range of angle**, **accuracy demanded**, **type of workpiece**, and **in-shop versus inspection room** application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PCMEL508 — Module 2):**
> The student is expected to demonstrate the *principle, construction, working, and limitations* of each angular measuring device, perform a hands-on experiment to measure a given workpiece angle, and tabulate the observation sheet with proper units. Marks are awarded for **neat sketch, formula used, gauge block selection, and least count calculation**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Five Surveyors"

Imagine you are asked to measure the *slope of a hill* using five different surveyors, each using a different tool:

| Surveyor | Tool Used | What It Does (Analogy) |
|---|---|---|
| **Surveyor A** | Sine Bar | Lays two rollers at a fixed distance apart, slides one roller up using small blocks until the surface just touches both. The "lift height" reveals the slope via trigonometry. |
| **Surveyor B** | Bevel Protractor | Slides a rotating blade against the slope and reads off the angle on a dial like a fancy kitchen scale. |
| **Surveyor C** | Combination Set | A multi-toolkit — uses a square blade, a center finder, and a small protractor head — like a Swiss Army knife of metrology. |
| **Surveyor D** | Clinometer | Holds a tiny pendulum inside a frame; gravity pulls the bob down and tells you exactly how tilted your surface is. |
| **Surveyor E** | Angle Dekkor | A precision optical instrument that "shoots a beam" through a prism and reads tiny tilt errors down to a few seconds of arc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Utility:**
> Angular measurements are foundational to **gear tooth profile inspection, dovetail slide verification, taper plug/socket checking, machine tool bed alignment, and jig fixture setup**. A deviation of even **5 arc-minutes** in a machine tool guideway can introduce catastrophic geometric errors during machining.

---

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants

- **Standard Sine Bar Center Distance:** *L* = **100 mm**, **200 mm**, or **300 mm**
- **Bevel Protractor Main Scale Range:** **0°–90°–0°** (bidirectional semicircular)
- **Bevel Protractor Vernier Divisions:** **12 divisions = 23°** or **12 divisions = 11°** depending on grade
- **Angle Dekkor Least Count:** typically **1 second (1″)** or **2 seconds (2″)** of arc
- **Clinometer Resolution:** typically **0.5°** to **1°** depending on construction (pendulum or spirit level)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sine Bar — Principle, Construction & Working

#### 2.1.1 Principle
The sine bar operates on the **fundamental trigonometric sine rule** for right-angled triangles. A workpiece of unknown angle *θ* is supported on two precision cylindrical rollers of fixed center distance *L*. Slip gauges (or gauge blocks) of calculated height *h* are packed under one roller until the top surface of the workpiece becomes perfectly parallel to the datum surface (typically a surface plate). When this parallelism is achieved, the geometric configuration forms a right triangle in which:

$$\sin\theta = \dfrac{h}{L}$$

#### 2.1.2 Construction
A sine bar consists of:
- **Body** — a rigid rectangular bar (Made of high-grade stabilized tool steel, stress-relieved).
- **Two Precision Rollers** — hardened, ground, and lapped cylinders, equal in diameter, mounted on hardened steel pins.
- **Datum Faces** — two parallel reference edges running along the body length.
- **Roller Center Distance *L*** — maintained to an accuracy of ±**0.001 mm** for 100 mm bar.

#### 2.1.3 Working Procedure
1. Clean the surface plate, sine bar rollers, and slip gauges.
2. Place the sine bar on the surface plate with rollers resting on the plate.
3. Calculate slip gauge height *h* using the formula above.
4. Build a slip gauge stack of height *h* and place it under one roller.
5. Place the workpiece on top of the sine bar (along its length).
6. Use a dial indicator to check parallelism between the top of the workpiece and the surface plate.
7. Adjust the slip gauge stack until the dial indicator reads zero across the entire length.

#### 2.1.4 Limitations
- Cannot measure angles **greater than 45°** accurately (cosine rule must be used, or angle must be reversed).
- Slip gauge stack introduces **wringing errors**.
- **Two rollers** cannot compensate for workpiece parallelism errors.
- Accuracy diminishes for very small angles (< 15 minutes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When the measured angle exceeds 45°, reverse the workpiece so that the supplementary angle (180° − θ) or the complementary angle (90° − θ) lies within the working range. Always state this clearly in the observation sheet.

---

### 2.2 Universal Bevel Protractor

#### 2.2.1 Principle
The bevel protractor is a **mechanical-optical instrument** that combines a graduated circular main scale with a vernier scale to provide a precise reading of an angle. The rotating blade (measuring edge) is pressed against the inclined surface, and the angular position is read off the dial assembly.

#### 2.2.2 Construction
- **Main Scale:** A semicircular dial graduated from **0° to 90°** on each side (so 0°–90°–0°–90°–0° reading range).
- **Vernier Scale:** A sliding scale attached to the main body, allowing fine angular reading.
- **Rotating Blade (Measuring Edge):** A precision-ground steel rule that pivots about the center.
- **Acute Angle Attachment** and **Obtuse Angle Attachment** — interchangeable blades for different angle ranges.
- **Magnifying Glass** — for accurate vernier coincidence reading.
- **Beam Locking Screws** — to clamp the blade at the measured angle.

#### 2.2.3 Least Count Calculation

For the standard universal bevel protractor (where **12 vernier divisions = 11 main scale divisions**):

$$
\text{Least Count} = \dfrac{1^\circ}{12} - \dfrac{11^\circ}{12} = \dfrac{1^\circ}{12}
$$

$$
\text{Least Count} = \dfrac{60'}{12} = 5 \text{ arc-minutes (5')}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 12 Vernier Divisions?** The vernier principle requires the *n* vernier divisions to be equal to *(n − 1)* main scale divisions. Here, 12 vernier div = 11 main scale div = 11°, so 1 vernier div = 11/12°, and the difference from 1° main scale division = 1/12° = 5 minutes.

---

### 2.3 Combination Set

#### 2.3.1 Composition
A combination set is a versatile layout tool consisting of four interchangeable heads mounted on a graduated steel rule:

| Head | Function |
|---|---|
| **Square Head** | Setting 90° angles, checking perpendicularity |
| **Center Head** | Locating centers of round bars (acts as a center square) |
| **Protractor Head** | Setting and measuring general angles (built-in spirit level) |
| **Steel Rule (Blade)** | Linear measurement, straight edge |

#### 2.3.2 Application
- Layout work on marking tables.
- Quick inspection of edges, faces, and centerlines.
- Setting workpieces on milling/drilling machines.
- Squaring a workpiece to a reference edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** A combination set is a *layout-grade* instrument with limited precision (typically ±0.5° for the protractor head). It is NOT used for final inspection of critical angles.

---

### 2.4 Clinometer

#### 2.4.1 Principle
A clinometer (also called an **inclinometer** or **tilt meter**) measures the angle of inclination of a surface with respect to the **horizontal** (gravity direction). It operates on the gravitational restoring force acting on a plumb bob, pendulum, or spirit level bubble.

#### 2.4.2 Types
1. **Pendulum Clinometer** — A weighted pointer hangs vertically; the tilt reading is taken from the relative position of the pointer.
2. **Spirit Level Clinometer** — A graduated arc with a spirit level vial; the bubble position reveals the tilt angle.
3. **Electronic Clinometer** — Uses MEMS accelerometers to digitally display tilt angles.

#### 2.4.3 Application
- Civil engineering — slope measurement of embankments, ramps, roads.
- Mechanical engineering — checking machine bed level, surface plate flatness verification.
- Field astronomy and surveying.

---

### 2.5 Angle Dekkor

#### 2.5.1 Principle
The Angle Dekkor is a **precision optical comparator** designed to measure **small angular deviations** with extraordinary accuracy (down to 1–2 seconds of arc). It uses a **collimator–telescope–reflecting prism** optical arrangement.

#### 2.5.2 Working Concept
1. A collimated light beam is projected through the instrument.
2. The beam strikes a **reflecting prism** mounted on a graduated dial.
3. A second beam is reflected from a **master reference surface** and brought into the same optical path.
4. The dial is rotated until the two reflected images **coincide** in the eyepiece.
5. The rotation amount, read off the graduated dial, gives the angular deviation.

#### 2.5.3 Application
- Inspection of **tapered surfaces** (e.g., taper shanks of machine tools).
- Checking **flatness and parallelism** of precision components.
- Calibration of gauges and master angles.
- Comparator work in tool rooms.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Instrument | Governing Equation | Variables | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sine Bar | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | *h* = slip gauge height, *L* = roller center distance | mm | 0° to 45° (direct), up to 90° (reversed) |
| Sine Bar — Complement | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{L}$ | For angles > 45° | mm | 45° to 90° |
| Sine Bar — Supplementary | $h = L \sin(180^\circ - \theta)$ | Reversed workpiece | mm | 90° to 180° |
| Bevel Protractor | $\text{L.C.} = \dfrac{s}{n}$ | *s* = smallest main scale div, *n* = vernier divisions | degrees / arc-min | 0° to 360° |
| Bevel Protractor Reading | $R = \text{MSR} + (V \times \text{L.C.})$ | MSR = main scale reading, *V* = vernier coincidence | degrees + minutes | — |
| Combination Set Protractor | $R = \text{Reading at index line}$ | — | degrees | 0° to 180° |
| Clinometer | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{L_{\text{base}}}\right)$ | For slope over base length | degrees | 0° to 90° |
| Angle Dekkor | $\theta = \dfrac{n \times \text{L.C. of dial}}{2}$ | *n* = dial divisions traversed, L.C. usually 1″ or 2″ | arc-seconds | ±30″ typically |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol in tables.** All absolute values use `\vert` notation if needed.

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Instrument | Specific Use |
|---|---|---|
| Tool Room | Sine Bar + Slip Gauges | Setting tool angles on grinding machines |
| Inspection Room | Bevel Protractor | Acceptance testing of machined chamfers |
| Production Floor | Combination Set | Quick layout marking on workbenches |
| Surveying / Civil | Clinometer | Slope of terrain, ramp angle verification |
| Precision Metrology Lab | Angle Dekkor | Calibration of gauge blocks, taper measurement |
| Aerospace | Angle Dekkor | Wing dihedral angle, control surface alignment |
| Heavy Industry | Clinometer (electronic) | Crane boom angle, ship deck tilt monitoring |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sine Bar Working Equation

#### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a sine bar resting on a flat surface plate. Let:

- *L* = the center-to-center distance between the two precision rollers (a **fixed** known constant of the instrument).
- *h* = the height of the slip gauge stack placed beneath **one** roller (variable, chosen by the operator).
- *θ* = the angle that the line joining the two roller centers makes with the surface plate (this is the angle we wish to measure).

When the workpiece sits on top of the sine bar and the dial indicator reads zero (perfect parallelism with the surface plate), the workpiece surface is parallel to the surface plate, and therefore parallel to the line joining the two roller centers.

#### 3.1.2 Right Triangle Formation

Visualize the geometry:
- The **hypotenuse** of the implied right triangle is the line joining the two roller centers. Its length is *L*.
- The **opposite side** (vertical leg) is the height difference between the two rollers, which equals the slip gauge stack height *h*.
- The **angle between the hypotenuse and the surface plate** is *θ*.

By the standard trigonometric definition of sine in a right-angled triangle:

$$
\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}
$$

Substituting our physical quantities:

$$
\sin\theta = \dfrac{h}{L}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Rearranged Forms

Solving for *h* when *θ* and *L* are known:

$$
h = L \sin\theta
$$

Solving for *θ* when *h* and *L* are known:

$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right)
$$

#### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A sine bar of center distance **200 mm** is used to check a **25° 30′** angle on a workpiece. Calculate the required slip gauge height.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**

$$
L = 200\ \text{mm},\quad \theta = 25^\circ 30'
$$

**Step 2 — Convert 30 arc-minutes to decimal degrees.**

$$
\theta = 25^\circ + \dfrac{30'}{60'} = 25^\circ + 0.5^\circ = 25.5^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sine bar formula.**

$$
h = L \sin\theta
$$

$$
h = 200 \times \sin(25.5^\circ)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the trigonometric term.**

$$
\sin(25.5^\circ) \approx 0.4305
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply to obtain slip gauge height.**

$$
h = 200 \times 0.4305 = 86.10\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — State the result with units and precision.**

$$
\boxed{h \approx 86.10\ \text{mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Stack slip gauges totaling **86.10 mm** under one roller. Place the workpiece on top. Adjust the dial indicator traverse across the workpiece surface — it should read zero (or uniformly zero) if the workpiece angle is indeed 25° 30′.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Bevel Protractor Least Count

#### 3.2.1 Given Specifications

- Main scale (semicircular dial) smallest division: **1°**
- Vernier scale total span: **11° = 22 half-degrees**, marked with **12 vernier divisions**, OR equivalently stated as **12 vernier divisions = 11 main scale divisions = 11°**.

#### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Compute one main scale division in arc-minutes.**

$$
1^\circ = 60 \text{ arc-minutes (60')}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute one vernier scale division.**

If 12 vernier divisions span 11 main scale divisions (i.e., 11°), then:

$$
1\ \text{vernier div} = \dfrac{11^\circ}{12} = \dfrac{11 \times 60'}{12} = \dfrac{660'}{12} = 55'
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the least count formula.**

$$
\text{Least Count} = 1\ \text{main scale div} - 1\ \text{vernier div}
$$

$$
\text{Least Count} = 60' - 55' = 5'
$$

**Step 4 — Final statement.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Least Count of Bevel Protractor} = 5\ \text{arc-minutes (5')}}
$$

#### 3.2.3 Reading a Bevel Protractor — Worked Example

**Problem:** The main scale reads **34°** (between 30° and 40°), and the **7th** vernier division coincides with a main scale line. Compute the measured angle.

**Step 1 — Identify the main scale reading (MSR).**

$$
\text{MSR} = 34^\circ
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the coinciding vernier division (V).**

$$
V = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the reading formula.**

$$
\text{Measured Angle} = \text{MSR} + (V \times \text{L.C.})
$$

$$
\text{Measured Angle} = 34^\circ + (7 \times 5')
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees + minutes format.**

$$
\text{Measured Angle} = 34^\circ 35'
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Reading} = 34^\circ 35'}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Clinometer Slope-to-Angle Conversion

For a clinometer measuring a slope where a vertical rise of *Δh* occurs over a horizontal base of *L_{\text{base}}*:

**Step 1 — Geometric relation.**

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{\Delta h}{L_{\text{base}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the angle.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{L_{\text{base}}}\right)
$$

#### 3.3.1 Worked Example

A road slope rises **1.5 m** over a horizontal run of **30 m**. What is the inclination angle?

**Step 1 — Substitute.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.5}{30}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the ratio.**

$$
\dfrac{1.5}{30} = 0.05
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the arctangent.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.05) \approx 2.862^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees and minutes.**

$$
0.862^\circ \times 60'/\text{deg} \approx 51.7'
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 2^\circ\ 52'}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection Guide

| Parameter | Sine Bar | Bevel Protractor | Combination Set | Clinometer | Angle Dekkor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Trigonometric | Mechanical-Vernier | Mechanical-Gravity | Gravitational | Optical |
| **Accuracy** | ± 1′ to ± 5′ | ± 5′ | ± 30′ | ± 0.5° | ± 1″ to ± 2″ |
| **Angle Range** | 0°–90° | 0°–360° | 0°–180° | 0°–90° | ± 5′ (small angles) |
| **Skill Required** | High | Moderate | Low | Low | Very High |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low | Low | Low | Very High |
| **In-Shop Use** | Yes | Yes | Yes (layout) | Field use | Inspection room only |
| **KTU Typical Marks Allocated** | 4–5 | 3–4 | 2–3 | 2 | 3–4 |
| **Skill CO Mapped (KTU)** | CO2, CO3 | CO2 | CO1 | CO1 | CO3, CO4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU CO Mapping Note (2024 Scheme):**
> - **CO1** — Recall the construction and parts of various measuring instruments.
> - **CO2** — Apply appropriate instruments to measure given workpieces.
> - **CO3** — Analyze and interpret measured values with correct significant figures.
> - **CO4** — Evaluate instrument accuracy and select optimal instrument for a given tolerance band.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define least count. Calculate the least count of a universal bevel protractor whose main scale is graduated in degrees and whose vernier scale has 12 divisions coinciding with 11 main scale divisions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Least count is the smallest measurable increment that an instrument can resolve, given by the ratio of the smallest main scale division to the number of vernier divisions.

> **Calculation (2 Marks):**
> Main scale smallest division = **1° = 60′**
> Vernier: 12 divisions = 11 main scale divisions = 11°
> 1 vernier division = 11°/12 = 55′
> Least count = 60′ − 55′ = **5 arc-minutes (5′)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the principle of working of a sine bar. Why is a sine bar restricted to measuring angles up to 45° accurately in the standard configuration?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Principle (2 Marks):** The sine bar works on the principle that when a workpiece of unknown angle *θ* is placed on two equal-diameter rollers of fixed center distance *L*, and a slip gauge of height *h* is inserted under one roller to make the workpiece parallel to the surface plate, then $\sin\theta = h/L$.

> **45° Restriction Reason (1 Mark):** Beyond 45°, $\sin\theta$ rises sharply (cosine falls), making the gauge stack height *h* large and the accuracy of *h* (limited by slip gauge wringing and parallelism errors) translates into large angular errors. Hence angles above 45° are measured by reversing the workpiece and using the complementary or supplementary angle.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a universal bevel protractor**. Derive the expression for its least count. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Sketch (2 Marks):** Draw a labelled diagram showing the main semicircular dial (0°–90°–0°), the vernier sliding scale, the rotating measuring blade, the acute angle attachment, the locking screws, and the magnifying glass.

> **Construction (2 Marks):** The body houses a precisely graduated semicircular main scale (typically 0–90° each side). A movable carriage carries the vernier scale and the rotating blade. The blade pivots about the dial center. Accessories include acute and obtuse angle attachments and a magnifying lens.

> **Working Principle (2 Marks):** The blade is pressed against the inclined surface. The carriage is rotated until the blade lies flush against the slope. The main scale reading is taken at the zero of the vernier; the vernier coincidence gives the additional minutes. Final angle = MSR + (V × LC).

> **Least Count Derivation (1 Mark):**
> 1 main scale div = 1° = 60′
> 1 vernier div = 11°/12 = 55′
> LC = 60′ − 55′ = **5′**

---

**(b)** A sine bar of center distance **150 mm** is used to check an angle. The slip gauge stack under one roller is built up to **75.00 mm**. Calculate the angle being measured. If the measured angle is **28° 45′**, what gauge height will be required? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Part (b-1) — Angle from given stack height (3 Marks):**

$$
\sin\theta = \dfrac{h}{L} = \dfrac{75.00}{150} = 0.5000
$$

$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.5000) = 30^\circ
$$

> **Answer:** The measured angle is **30°**.

> **Part (b-2) — Stack height for 28° 45′ (4 Marks):**

Convert angle to decimal:

$$
\theta = 28^\circ + \dfrac{45'}{60'} = 28.75^\circ
$$

Apply formula:

$$
h = L \sin\theta = 150 \times \sin(28.75^\circ)
$$

$$
\sin(28.75^\circ) \approx 0.4809
$$

$$
h = 150 \times 0.4809 = 72.13\ \text{mm}
$$

> **Answer:** Required slip gauge height = **72.13 mm**.

---

#### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Describe with a labelled sketch the **construction and working of a combination set**. List its various heads and state two limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Sketch (2 Marks):** Labelled diagram of a combination set showing the steel rule blade and the four heads (square, center, protractor, and a level vial).

> **Construction (2 Marks):** The combination set consists of a graduated steel rule (blade) with a clamping mechanism to attach interchangeable heads: **(i) Square Head** for 90° setting, **(ii) Center Head** for locating centers of cylindrical workpieces, **(iii) Protractor Head** with built-in spirit level for general angle measurements.

> **Working (2 Marks):** The relevant head is clamped to the rule, positioned against the workpiece, and the angle or position is read directly off the graduated scale or protractor dial. The protractor head has an index line and an integrated bubble level that can be referenced to gravity.

> **Limitations (1 Mark):**
> 1. Limited accuracy (± 30′ typical) — not suitable for precision inspection.
> 2. Graduations on the blade wear over time and require periodic calibration.

---

**(b)** Explain the **principle and working of an Angle Dekkor**. Why is it preferred over the sine bar for measuring very small angular deviations? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Principle (2 Marks):** The Angle Dekkor is an optical instrument based on the principle of *autocollimation*. A collimated light beam is reflected from a master surface and from a test surface; the angular deviation between the two reflected beams is read off a graduated dial.

> **Construction (2 Marks):** It consists of a light source, a collimating lens, an illuminated cross-wire target, a reflecting prism mounted on a graduated dial, and an observation eyepiece with reference cross-lines.

> **Working (2 Marks):** Light from the source passes through the collimator and strikes the prism. The prism reflects a beam to a master reference surface, and a second beam is reflected to the test surface. The dial carrying the prism is rotated until the image of the cross-wire in the eyepiece exactly coincides with the reference cross-wire. The rotation, read off the dial, gives the angular deviation.

> **Why Preferred over Sine Bar for Small Angles (1 Mark):** The slip gauges used with a sine bar have a finite least count (typically 0.001 mm). For very small angles, the change in *h* becomes immeasurably small — for example, a **1 arc-minute** change at *L* = 100 mm corresponds to *Δh* = 0.029 mm, which is at the resolution limit of slip gauges. The Angle Dekkor, with a least count of 1–2 arc-seconds, can resolve such tiny deviations optically without mechanical stack errors.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **No Sketch Provided (–2 marks):** Even if the description is correct, an unlabelled or missing diagram attracts severe deduction in the Part B valuation. *Always draw a clean, neat, labelled sketch before starting the answer.*
> 2. **Unit Omission (–1 mark):** Slip gauge height must be in **mm**, angle in **degrees (°) and arc-minutes (′)**. Missing units = lost marks.
> 3. **Sign Error in Sin Formula (–1 mark):** Students often write $\sin\theta = L/h$ instead of $h/L$. Memorize the relation with a clear mental picture of the right triangle.
> 4. **Not Converting Arc-Minutes to Decimal (–1 mark):** When angles are given as e.g., 25° 30′, students often forget to convert 30′ to 0.5° before applying $\sin$ in calculator mode. *Always convert to decimal degrees first.*
> 5. **Incorrect Least Count (–1 mark):** Confusing the bevel protractor LC (5′) with that of a vernier height gauge (0.02 mm) or micrometer (0.01 mm).
> 6. **Mixing up Sine Bar Center Distance (–1 mark):** Some sine bars are 100 mm, 200 mm, or 300 mm. Using the wrong *L* in the formula yields an incorrect answer.
> 7. **No Observation Table in Lab Record (–2 marks in Continuous Evaluation):** For the KTU Lab Course (PCMEL508), a properly formatted observation table with multiple readings (say 3 trials) and a mean value is mandatory.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Sine Bar** uses the trigonometric relation **$\sin\theta = h/L$** and is the most accurate mechanical method for medium angles.
- [x] Sine bar center distances are standardized at **100, 200, or 300 mm**.
- [x] **Universal Bevel Protractor** has a least count of **5 arc-minutes** (12 vernier div = 11 main scale div).
- [x] **Combination Set** has 4 heads: **Square, Center, Protractor, and the steel Rule (blade)**.
- [x] **Clinometer** measures the angle relative to **gravity (horizontal)** — used for slopes and tilts.
- [x] **Angle Dekkor** is an **optical instrument** with arc-second accuracy — used for small deviations.
- [x] Sine bar cannot directly measure angles > 45° — use **complementary or supplementary** placement.
- [x] Bevel protractor reading = **MSR + (V × LC)**.
- [x] For the sine bar, *h* is the slip gauge height under **one roller only**.
- [x] Slip gauge wringing must be proper — **avoid wringing film errors**.
- [x] KTU 2024 Scheme examiner allocates **marks for sketch, formula, units, and calculation steps** — present them clearly.
- [x] Significant figures must match the instrument least count — do not over-state precision.
- [x] **Conversion tip:** 1° = 60′, 1′ = 60″. Decimal to minutes: multiply by 60.
- [x] For sine bar, *Δh* corresponding to 1 arc-minute at *L* = 100 mm is ≈ **0.029 mm** — this defines the practical angular resolution of the sine bar.
- [x] **Always state the CO mapping** in your lab record introduction for KTU OBE compliance.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Straightness, Flatness, Squareness, and Roundness Error Using Autocollimator, Laser Interferometer, Spirit Level, and Dial Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Straightness, Flatness, Squareness, and Roundness Error Using Autocollimator, Laser Interferometer, Spirit Level, and Dial Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Form Errors in Engineering Metrology

In precision manufacturing, every machine tool surface and component deviates from its **ideal geometric form** due to manufacturing limitations, wear, thermal effects, and load-induced deformations. These deviations are quantified as **form errors**, and they are the most fundamental characteristics evaluated in any metrology laboratory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (as per IS 2063 & ISO 1101):**
> A **form error** is the deviation of a real surface or feature from its theoretically perfect geometric counterpart, measured in the absence of any orientation, location, or dimensional constraints.

The four primary form errors covered in this KTU experiment are:

| Form Error | Ideal Geometry | Typical Instrument |
|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Straightness** | Straight line | Autocollimator, Laser Interferometer |
| **Flatness** | Plane surface | Autocollimator, Spirit Level |
| **Squareness** | Right angle (90°) | Autocollimator with optical square |
| **Roundness** | Perfect circle | Dial Gauge (V-block setup) |

## 1.2 Instrument-Specific Conceptual Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of geometric errors like spelling mistakes in a letter**:
> - A straight line is the "word"
> - The error is the "misspelling" (wobble, bend, twist)
> - The metrology instrument is the "spell-checker" — the better the instrument, the more minor mistakes it can catch.

### 1.2.1 Autocollimator — *The Optical Sherlock Holmes*
An **autocollimator** is a non-contact optical instrument that projects a parallel beam of light onto a reflective surface and detects the reflected beam's angular displacement. Think of it as a **"super-precise carpenter's eye"** that can detect angular tilts as small as **0.1 arc-second** — about 50,000 times sharper than a human hair's edge angle.

- **Working Analogy**: Imagine bouncing a laser off a mirror. If the mirror tilts even slightly, the reflected spot moves a long way away. An autocollimator measures that spot shift and converts it into an angle.

### 1.2.2 Laser Interferometer — *The Atomic Ruler*
A **laser interferometer** uses the wave nature of light to measure linear displacement by counting interference fringes. The wavelength of the Helium-Neon laser used is **632.8 nm**, making it accurate to fractions of a micrometer.

- **Working Analogy**: Drop two stones in a still pond — when their ripples overlap, they create bright and dark patterns (interference). If one stone moves slightly, the pattern shifts. Counting those shifts tells you exactly how far the stone moved.

### 1.2.3 Spirit Level — *The Ancient Tilt Detector*
A **spirit level** uses a bubble in a curved (slightly arc-shaped) liquid vial. The bubble moves to the highest point. By measuring the position of the bubble against a graduated scale, one can compute the angular tilt of a surface.

- **Working Analogy**: A spirit level is like a tiny "tilt-meter" — the bubble is the marble that always rolls to the top of a curved surface, and where it stops tells you the angle of the table.

### 1.2.4 Dial Gauge (DTI) — *The Mechanical Microscope*
A **Dial Test Indicator (DTI)** is a mechanical comparator with a rack-and-pinion mechanism that amplifies the linear movement of a plunger into the rotation of a pointer on a graduated dial. Typical resolution is **0.01 mm** or **0.001 mm**.

- **Working Analogy**: A DTI is like a tiny periscope that "feels" up-and-down movement and magnifies it 1,000 times on a circular dial — perfect for detecting micro-bumps and dips on a surface.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam**: The choice of instrument depends on **accuracy required**, **type of error**, and **size of the workpiece**. Autocollimators excel at angular and straightness measurement, while dial gauges are preferred for circularity/roundness checks due to their mechanical simplicity.
