# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define bore diameter. List any four instruments used for measuring bore diameter.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bore** is an internal cylindrical surface of a hole produced in a workpiece by drilling, boring, or reaming. **Bore diameter** is the diametrical distance between two diametrically opposite points on this internal cylindrical surface.

Four instruments used for bore diameter measurement:

1. **Dial Bore Gauge (Bore Gauge)** — comparison type, high accuracy.
2. **Inside Micrometer (Tubular Micrometer)** — direct reading type.
3. **Telescoping Gauge** — used with outside micrometer.
4. **Small Hole Gauge** — for small bores below 10 mm.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for 4 instruments = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**What is ovality? How is it calculated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Ovality** (also called **out-of-roundness**) is the geometric error in a cylindrical bore where a cross-section deviates from a perfect circle and assumes an elliptical shape.

It is calculated as the difference between the maximum and minimum diameters measured in the same cross-sectional plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovality} &= D_{max} - D_{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Unit: mm or µm.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for unit = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a dial bore gauge**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During bore diameter measurement using a dial bore gauge set against a **50.000 mm** ring gauge, the following readings were taken at three axial depths and four angular positions. Calculate the **ovality at each section**, **mean diameter**, and **overall taper** of the bore. State whether the bore conforms to a Ø50 H7 specification. **(7 Marks)**

| Axial Depth | 0° | 90° | 180° | 270° |
|-------------|-----|-----|------|------|
| Front (D₁) | +0.020 | +0.015 | +0.022 | +0.018 |
| Middle (D₂) | +0.025 | +0.020 | +0.028 | +0.023 |
| Back (D₃) | +0.030 | +0.025 | +0.033 | +0.028 |

*(All readings in mm)*

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Construction and Working of Dial Bore Gauge (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**

A dial bore gauge consists of the following parts:

1. **Dial Indicator Head:** A precision dial gauge with a typical least count of 0.01 mm.
2. **Bevel Gear Transfer Mechanism:** Converts linear axial motion of the spindle into rotational motion of the dial needle.
3. **Spindle Assembly:** Moves axially when the anvils are displaced.
4. **Three Contact Anvils:** Two lower anvils at 180° and one upper centring anvil at 90°, providing self-centring.
5. **Centring Bridge with Spring:** Maintains uniform contact pressure.
6. **Heat-Insulated Handle:** Prevents body heat from causing thermal expansion errors.
7. **Extension Rods (optional):** For bores beyond standard length.

**Working Principle:**

1. The gauge is calibrated against a **ring gauge** of known diameter (e.g., 50.000 mm).
2. The dial is zeroed.
3. The gauge is inserted into the bore, and the anvils contact the bore wall.
4. The operator gently **rocks** the gauge in the plane perpendicular to the anvil line.
5. **Minimum reading** is noted (true diameter position).
6. The deviation from the reference is read on the dial.

*Mark Distribution:* 1 mark for list of parts, 2 marks for description, 2 marks for diagram explanation, 2 marks for working steps.

---

**(b) Calculation of Ovality, Mean Diameter, and Taper (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate actual diameters at each position**

Reference diameter $D_{ref} = 50.000 \, \text{mm}$

**Section 1 (Front):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{1,0} &= 50.000 + 0.020 = 50.020 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,90} &= 50.000 + 0.015 = 50.015 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,180} &= 50.000 + 0.022 = 50.022 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,270} &= 50.000 + 0.018 = 50.018 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Section 2 (Middle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{2,0} &= 50.000 + 0.025 = 50.025 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,90} &= 50.000 + 0.020 = 50.020 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,180} &= 50.000 + 0.028 = 50.028 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,270} &= 50.000 + 0.023 = 50.023 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Section 3 (Back):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{3,0} &= 50.000 + 0.030 = 50.030 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{3,90} &= 50.000 + 0.025 = 50.025 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{3,180} &= 50.000 + 0.033 = 50.033 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{3,270} &= 50.000 + 0.028 = 50.028 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Ovality at each section**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovality}_{Front} &= 50.022 - 50.015 = 0.007 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{Ovality}_{Middle} &= 50.028 - 50.020 = 0.008 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{Ovality}_{Back} &= 50.033 - 50.025 = 0.008 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Mean Diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{mean,Front} &= \frac{50.020 + 50.015 + 50.022 + 50.018}{4} = 50.01875 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{mean,Middle} &= \frac{50.025 + 50.020 + 50.028 + 50.023}{4} = 50.02400 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{mean,Back} &= \frac{50.030 + 50.025 + 50.033 + 50.028}{4} = 50.02900 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Taper (using mean diameters)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Taper} &= D_{mean,Back} - D_{mean,Front} \\
&= 50.02900 - 50.01875 \\
&= 0.01025 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Check against Ø50 H7 Specification**

From earlier derivation: $D_{max} = 50.027 \, \text{mm}$ and $D_{min} = 50.000 \, \text{mm}$.

- **Maximum diameter observed:** $50.033 \, \text{mm}$ — **EXCEEDS upper limit by 0.006 mm** ❌
- **Minimum diameter observed:** $50.015 \, \text{mm}$ — within lower limit ✓

**Conclusion:** The bore **DOES NOT conform** to Ø50 H7 specification because the **maximum measured diameter (50.033 mm) exceeds the upper limit of 50.027 mm**.

*Mark Distribution:* 1 mark for reference, 2 marks for diameter calculation, 1 mark for ovality, 1 mark for mean, 1 mark for taper, 1 mark for conclusion.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **principle of minimum reading** in bore diameter measurement. Why is rocking motion essential during measurement? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **Ø40 H7** bore is to be measured. The following data were obtained at two depths using a dial bore gauge set against a **40.000 mm** ring gauge:

- At front: readings = **+0.018, +0.022, +0.020, +0.025** mm at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°.
- At back: readings = **+0.012, +0.015, +0.018, +0.014** mm at 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°.

Determine: **(i)** ovality at each section, **(ii)** the overall taper, and **(iii)** whether the bore lies within **Ø40 H7** tolerance limits. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Principle of Minimum Reading (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** The principle of minimum reading states that when a bore gauge is rocked inside a bore, the **smallest reading observed on the dial** corresponds to the **true bore diameter**.

**Geometric Reasoning:**

When the gauge anvils are NOT perpendicular to the bore axis, they span a chord larger than the diameter. As the gauge is tilted toward perpendicularity, the chord length decreases until it equals the true diameter at perpendicular position.

**Mathematical Justification:**

Let $D_{true}$ be the true bore diameter and $\theta$ be the tilt angle of the gauge. The measured chord length $C$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{D_{true}}{\cos \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

- When $\theta = 0$ (perpendicular): $C = D_{true}$ (minimum).
- When $\theta > 0$ (tilted): $C > D_{true}$ (over-estimation).

**Why Rocking is Essential:**

1. Eliminates operator-induced angular misalignment.
2. Ensures anvils are perpendicular to the bore axis.
3. Compensates for any initial tilt while inserting the gauge.
4. Locates the true diameter position reliably.

**Practical Steps:**

- Hold the gauge lightly and tilt it ±5° to ±10°.
- Observe the dial needle swing.
- Record the **lowest point** of the needle swing.

*Mark Distribution:* 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for geometric reasoning, 2 marks for practical explanation, 1 mark for diagram/illustration reference.

---

**(b) Calculations for Ø40 H7 Bore (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate actual diameters**

$D_{ref} = 40.000 \, \text{mm}$

**Front Section:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{1,0} &= 40.018 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,90} &= 40.022 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,180} &= 40.020 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,270} &= 40.025 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{1,mean} &= \frac{40.018 + 40.022 + 40.020 + 40.025}{4} = 40.02125 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Back Section:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{2,0} &= 40.012 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,90} &= 40.015 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,180} &= 40.018 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,270} &= 40.014 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{2,mean} &= \frac{40.012 + 40.015 + 40.018 + 40.014}{4} = 40.01475 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Ovality at each section**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovality}_{Front} &= D_{1,max} - D_{1,min} = 40.025 - 40.018 = 0.007 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{Ovality}_{Back} &= D_{2,max} - D_{2,min} = 40.018 - 40.012 = 0.006 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Overall Taper**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Taper} &= D_{front,mean} - D_{back,mean} \\
&= 40.02125 - 40.01475 \\
&= 0.0065 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Determine Ø40 H7 Tolerance Limits**

For Ø40 (in range 30–50 mm), fundamental deviation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{40} + 0.001 \cdot 40 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.420 + 0.040 \\
i &= 1.539 + 0.040 \\
i &= 1.579 \, \mu m \\
\text{IT7} &= 16i = 16 \times 1.579 = 25.26 \, \mu m \approx 0.025 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{max} &= 40.000 + 0.025 = 40.025 \, \text{mm} \\
D_{min} &= 40.000 + 0.000 = 40.000 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Conformance Check**

| Parameter | Measured | Limit | Status |
|-----------|----------|-------|--------|
| Maximum Diameter | 40.025 mm | ≤ 40.025 mm | ✓ At upper limit |
| Minimum Diameter | 40.012 mm | ≥ 40.000 mm | ✓ Within limit |
| Ovality (Front) | 0.007 mm | — | ✓ Acceptable |
| Ovality (Back) | 0.006 mm | — | ✓ Acceptable |

**Conclusion:** The bore **CONFORMS** to Ø40 H7 specification, though it is **exactly at the upper limit**. This indicates the bore is on the verge of rejection; closer tolerance control is recommended in subsequent production.

*Mark Distribution:* 1 mark for diameter conversion, 1 mark for ovality, 1 mark for taper, 2 marks for H7 limits, 2 marks for conclusion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to ADD the reference diameter:** Many students only write down the dial reading without computing $D = D_{ref} + R$. This leads to a **2–3 mark loss** in calculation problems.
> 2. **Mixing up ovality and taper:** Ovality is in the SAME plane (different angles); taper is along the LENGTH (different depths). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 3. **Ignoring the H7 limits check:** Even if your calculations are correct, failing to compare with tolerance limits means you lose **1–2 marks** on the conclusion.
> 4. **Not mentioning "minimum reading principle":** Always state that the minimum reading is taken during rocking. Worth **1 mark**.
> 5. **Using wrong units:** Tolerance for IT7 of Ø50 is in **micrometres (µm)** initially — convert to mm for final diameter limits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bore Diameter Measurement**

- ✅ **Bore** = Internal cylindrical surface; **Bore Diameter** = Distance between diametrically opposite internal points.
- ✅ Most versatile instrument = **Dial Bore Gauge** (comparison type, least count 0.01 mm).
- ✅ **Core equation:** $D_{actual} = D_{reference} + \text{Dial Reading}$
- ✅ **Ovality** = $D_{max} - D_{min}$ (same plane, different angles).
- ✅ **Taper** = $D_{front} - D_{back}$ (different axial depths).
- ✅ **Mean Diameter** = $(D_{max} + D_{min})/2$.
- ✅ **Minimum Reading Principle** → Always rock gauge; record lowest dial reading.
- ✅ **Reference Standard** → Ring gauge (for bore gauge) or micrometer (for telescoping/small hole gauge).
- ✅ **H7 Hole Basis:** $D_{max} = D_{nominal} + \text{IT7}$; $D_{min} = D_{nominal}$.
- ✅ **IT7 tolerance (for D = 30–50 mm):** $16i$ where $i = 0.45 \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001D$ µm.
- ✅ **Self-centring design** with three anvils ensures alignment and reduces operator error.
- ✅ **Precautions:** Clean bore and gauge, zero against reference, avoid parallax, rock gently, take multiple readings.
- ✅ **Common applications:** Engine cylinders (IT7), hydraulic cylinders (IT6), bearing housings (IT7).
- ✅ **Source of errors:** Instrument calibration, parallax, bore taper/ovality, thermal expansion, surface roughness.
- ✅ **Standard temperature:** 20 °C (per ISO 1) for all precision measurements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Tool Maker's Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Tool Maker's Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — USE OF TOOL MAKER'S MICROSCOPE

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tool Maker's Microscope (TMM)** — A non-contact, optical-precision metrology instrument used to inspect and measure the geometry of precision tools, gauges, threaded components, and small engineering parts with an accuracy typically in the range of **±0.005 mm to ±0.01 mm** and angular resolution up to **1 minute (1′)** of arc.

In strict KTU/Metrology parlance, the Tool Maker's Microscope is classified under **Optical Comparators / Measuring Microscopes** in the family of **Non-Contact Length Measuring Instruments**. It combines the principles of a travelling **microscope** (with two graduated orthogonal axes X and Y) and an **angle-measuring protractor eyepiece**, enabling two-dimensional geometric evaluation of a workpiece on its reference surface without physical probing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For the PCMEL508 lab, the experiment typically requires the student to:
> 1. Set up a given cutting tool (e.g., single-point HSS tool) or a threaded specimen on the work-holding fixture.
> 2. Measure the **tool angles** (rake angle, clearance/relief angle, cutting edge angle, nose radius) **OR** the **thread parameters** (major diameter, minor diameter, effective diameter, pitch, thread angle) of a specimen.
> 3. Compare measured values with standard values and compute the percentage error.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "Plain English + Real-World Analogy"

Imagine you have a tiny watch gear lying on a table and you want to measure the exact angle of one of its teeth. A regular vernier cannot do it because it physically touches the part and may damage it, and a normal magnifying glass gives a blurry, depthless view. The Tool Maker's Microscope is essentially a **supercharged magnifying system mounted on a precision XY cross-slide table**, with a **transparent protractor scale inside the eyepiece** for angle measurement.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Optical Ruler on Rails":**
> Think of the TMM as an optical ruler placed on a smooth sliding track. The ruler itself is a crosshair etched in glass. You slide the part (or the ruler) on two perpendicular rails (X and Y), reading the position off vernier scales that look like those on a height gauge. The "ruler" can be rotated about the optical axis, and a built-in protractor tells you the exact angle the crosshair is making with the horizontal. So, in one instrument, you get the functionality of:
> * A **Travelling Microscope** (for length),
> * A **Protractor** (for angles),
> * A **Comparator** (for contour/profile tracing via projected light).

For a B.Tech student, the core takeaway is: **TMM lets you "see" and "measure" small engineering features (often smaller than 1 mm) without ever touching them with a probe — purely through magnified optics and precision linear/angular scales.**

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Total Optical Magnification | **10×, 20×, 30×, 50×** (switchable) |
| Measuring Range (X-axis) | **0 – 150 mm** (typical) |
| Measuring Range (Y-axis) | **0 – 50 mm** (typical) |
| Least Count — Longitudinal (X) | **0.005 mm** (with vernier) |
| Least Count — Transverse (Y) | **0.005 mm** (with vernier) |
| Angular Least Count (Protractor) | **1 minute (1′)** or **0.0167°** |
| Field of View | **5 mm – 20 mm** (depends on objective) |
| Resolution | **±0.005 mm** |
| Working Distance | **35 mm – 75 mm** (typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Least Count of the angular protractor eyepiece** is typically $\text{LC} = \dfrac{0.5°}{30} = 1' = \dfrac{1}{60}° \approx 0.0167°$, because the main scale is divided in degrees and the vernier scale has 30 divisions covering 29° of main scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 Operating Principle — "The Why and the How"

The TMM operates on three simultaneous optical–mechanical principles:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle 1 — Optical Magnification:** A compound microscope (objective + eyepiece) produces an enlarged virtual image of the workpiece surface, allowing the operator to align fine features (e.g., cutting edge, thread flank) precisely with the reticle crosshair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle 2 — Coordinate Measurement via Two Linear Axes:** The workpiece is mounted on a **precision cross-slide table** with two perpendicular micrometer screws (X for longitudinal, Y for transverse motion). Each screw is fitted with a vernier/micrometer reading head. Reading the X and Y coordinates of two reference points allows distance and angle computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle 3 — Angular Measurement via Protractor Eyepiece:** The entire optical tube (microscope body) can be rotated about a vertical axis above the workpiece. A main circular scale (0°–360°) and a vernier scale attached to the rotating body allow direct reading of angles to within 1 minute. The angle between any two features of the workpiece is read directly by aligning the crosshair on each feature and recording the angular displacement.

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Measurement

1. **Mounting the Specimen:** Place the cutting tool (or threaded plug) on the centre-stage between centres, V-block, or chuck, depending on geometry. Ensure the axis of the feature being measured lies on the optical axis of the microscope.
2. **Illumination Selection:** For **surface features** (tool tip, cutting edge, nose radius), use the **surface illumination system** (oblique lamp shining down). For **profile/edge features** (thread flanks, contour edges), use the **profile illumination system** (collimated light from behind the workpiece forming a sharp silhouette).
3. **Focusing and Alignment:** Rotate the coarse/fine focusing knob to bring the image sharp. Use the cross-hair reticle as the reference line.
4. **Coordinate Recording:** Bring point A into coincidence with the crosshair. Note the X and Y micrometer readings. Slide the table (or rotate the tube) to bring point B into coincidence. Record X, Y again.
5. **Computation:** Compute length, angle, or pitch using geometric formulas (see Section 3).
6. **Repeat and Average:** Take a minimum of **three readings** and report the mean to improve accuracy and reduce the effect of backlash/observation error.

## 2.2 Major Sub-Systems and Their Functions

| Sub-System | Function in Measurement |
|---|---|
| **Heavy Cast Iron Base** | Provides rigidity, vibration isolation, and a flat datum reference plane. |
| **Cross-Slide Table (X & Y Stages)** | Provides two perpendicular precision linear motions; carries the workpiece. Each axis has a micrometer with least count **0.005 mm**. |
| **Work-Holding Devices** | Between-centres with adjustable top rests, V-block fixture, and magnetic chuck — chosen per workpiece geometry. |
| **Illumination: Surface Lamp** | Oblique light source from above for inspecting surface features like cutting edges. |
| **Illumination: Profile Lamp** | Collimated light from the rear creating a contour silhouette of the workpiece — used for thread flanks and contour tracing. |
| **Microscope Body (Optical Tube)** | Houses the **objective**, **intermediate lens system**, and the **angle-measuring protractor eyepiece**. |
| **Protractor Eyepiece** | Graduated 0°–360° main scale + vernier for direct angular readout. Contains a crosshair / graticule. |
| **Magnification Changer** | Allows switching between 10×, 20×, 30×, 50× to suit feature size. |
| **Fine Focus Knob** | Allows sharp focusing without disturbing lateral position. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> All formulas required for the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) lab viva, record, and viva-voce** on Tool Maker's Microscope are consolidated below.

| # | Quantity Measured | Governing Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear distance between two points** | $L = \sqrt{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2}$ | $L$ in mm; $\Delta X = X_2 - X_1$; $\Delta Y = Y_2 - Y_1$ |
| 2 | **Angle of a feature line (slope)** | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}\right)$ | $\theta$ in degrees |
| 3 | **Tool Rake Angle (γ)** | $\gamma = \phi - \theta$ or by direct alignment on tool face | $\gamma, \phi, \theta$ in degrees |
| 4 | **Tool Clearance Angle (α)** | $\alpha = \theta' - \theta$ (depending on reference) | $\alpha$ in degrees |
| 5 | **Thread Pitch** | $P = \dfrac{L}{n}$ where $n$ = no. of threads over length $L$ | $P$ in mm |
| 6 | **Major Diameter (D)** | Read directly on X-axis at thread crests | $D$ in mm |
| 7 | **Minor Diameter ($D_c$)** | Read directly on X-axis at thread roots | $D_c$ in mm |
| 8 | **Effective / Pitch Diameter ($D_p$)** | $D_p = D - \dfrac{P}{2}$ (for V-threads) | $D_p$ in mm |
| 9 | **Thread Half-Angle (α/2)** | $\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{P/2}{(D - D_c)/2}\right)$ | $\alpha$ in degrees |
| 10 | **Percentage Error** | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Measured} - \text{Standard}\vert}{\text{Standard}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | **Angular Least Count of Protractor** | $\text{LC} = \dfrac{\text{Main scale division}}{\text{No. of vernier divisions}}$ | In degrees/minutes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Check:** The above formulas are universal geometric relationships; their physical correctness can be verified by dimensional analysis. Avoid inventing "textbook-specific" equations for TMM — standard coordinate-geometry is the KTU-recommended basis.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Cutting Tool Inspection:** Manufacturers of HSS and carbide inserts (e.g., Sandvik, Kennametal) use TMM-equivalent optical comparators in quality control to verify **rake, clearance, and nose radius** before dispatch.
* **Thread Plug & Ring Gauge Calibration:** TMM is used by metrology labs (NABL-accredited) for traceable measurement of thread parameters on precision gauges.
* **Press Tools and Punches:** Checking the **profile angle and corner radius** of blanking and piercing punches, where any deviation causes burr or cracking.
* **Watch and Instrument Industry:** Inspection of tiny gear teeth, escapement pallets, and balance staffs.
* **Aerospace Manufacturing:** Verification of **tapered thread profiles** (e.g., NPT, BSPT) and small-diameter aerospace fasteners.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Distance Between Two Points from Cross-Slide Readings

When the crosshair is brought to coincide with point $A$ on the workpiece, the micrometers on the X-stage and Y-stage register readings $X_1$ and $Y_1$. Similarly, when aligned with point $B$, the readings are $X_2$ and $Y_2$.

The displacement in X direction is:

$$\Delta X = X_2 - X_1$$

The displacement in Y direction is:

$$\Delta Y = Y_2 - Y_1$$

These two displacements form the legs of a right-angled triangle whose hypotenuse is the true straight-line distance $L$ between points $A$ and $B$. By the **Pythagorean Theorem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{(X_2 - X_1)^2 + (Y_2 - Y_1)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** The X and Y slides are precision orthogonal prismatic guides (typically dovetail slides), so their motions are mathematically perpendicular. This justifies the use of the Pythagorean theorem for true distance.

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $X_1 = 10.000 \text{ mm}$, $X_2 = 22.500 \text{ mm}$, $Y_1 = 5.000 \text{ mm}$, $Y_2 = 11.300 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta X &= 22.500 - 10.000 = 12.500 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta Y &= 11.300 - 5.000 = 6.300 \text{ mm} \\
L &= \sqrt{(12.500)^2 + (6.300)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{156.250 + 39.690} \\
  &= \sqrt{195.940} \\
  &\approx 13.998 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inclination Angle (Slope Angle) of a Feature Line

Once the two endpoints of a line (e.g., the cutting edge or a thread flank) are located, the inclination $\theta$ that the line makes with the horizontal X-axis is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Opposite side}}{\text{Adjacent side}}\right) \\
       &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}\right) \\
       &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{Y_2 - Y_1}{X_2 - X_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Explanation:** The X-axis of the cross-slide is taken as the horizontal datum. The line from $A$ to $B$ has a vertical rise $\Delta Y$ over a horizontal run $\Delta X$, hence the slope is the ratio $\Delta Y / \Delta X$. The inverse tangent converts slope to angle in degrees or radians.

**Numerical Example (continuing from above):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{6.300}{12.500}\right) \\
       &= \tan^{-1}(0.5040) \\
       &\approx 26.74°
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tool Geometry: Measuring Rake Angle (γ) and Clearance Angle (α)

Consider a single-point cutting tool with the standard nomenclature:
* $\phi$ = Cutting edge angle (plan view) measured from the longitudinal feed direction.
* $\theta$ = Inclination angle of the cutting edge (elevation view, measured from horizontal).
* $\gamma$ = Rake angle (angle of the tool face from the horizontal reference plane of the workpiece).
* $\alpha$ = Clearance / Relief angle (angle of the flank from the workpiece surface).

**Procedure on the TMM:**

1. Mount the tool in the work-holding fixture such that the cutting edge lies in a vertical plane, ideally parallel to one of the cross-slide axes.
2. Use **surface illumination** to focus on the tool tip. The horizontal crosshair is aligned parallel to the workpiece top surface (datum).
3. Align the crosshair along the **cutting edge**. Rotate the protractor eyepiece to bring its reference line onto the cutting edge. Read the protractor angle — this is $\phi$.
4. Now align the crosshair along the **tool face (rake face)**. Read the new protractor angle — the difference gives $\gamma$.

Mathematically, the **rake angle** is the difference between the measured protractor angle of the rake face ($\phi_f$) and the angle of the cutting edge ($\phi_e$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma = \phi_f - \phi_e
\end{aligned}
$$

The **clearance angle** is similarly the difference between the angle of the flank ($\phi_{\text{flank}}$) and the cutting edge angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = \phi_{\text{flank}} - \phi_e
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose
* Protractor reading when crosshair aligns with **cutting edge** = $0°\ 12'$
* Protractor reading when crosshair aligns with **rake face** = $-7°\ 48'$
* Protractor reading when crosshair aligns with **flank** = $8°\ 36'$

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \phi_f - \phi_e = (-7°48') - (0°12') = -8°00' \\
\alpha &= \phi_{\text{flank}} - \phi_e = (8°36') - (0°12') = 8°24'
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative sign on $\gamma$ indicates a **negative rake angle** (typical for hardened-steel cutting tools).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thread Parameter Measurement

For an external or internal thread, the standard geometric parameters that the TMM can resolve are:

* **Pitch (P):** Slide the X-stage so that the crosshair coincides with $n$ successive thread crests. The total displacement divided by $n$ gives $P$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \dfrac{X_{\text{final}} - X_{\text{initial}}}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

* **Major Diameter (D):** Set the crosshair tangent to the outermost crests of the thread. Read the X-coordinate. Repeat on the opposite flank (for external threads, the difference across the thread axis gives $D$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D = X_{\text{right crest}} - X_{\text{left crest}}
\end{aligned}
$$

* **Minor Diameter ($D_c$):** Same procedure, but aligned with the thread roots.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_c = X_{\text{right root}} - X_{\text{left root}}
\end{aligned}
$$

* **Effective / Pitch Diameter ($D_p$):** For ISO metric V-threads, $D_p$ is the diameter of an imaginary cylinder passing through the thread profile such that the thread width on the crest equals the thread space on the root. Theoretically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_p = D - \dfrac{P}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This comes from the geometry of an isoceles V-thread of half-angle $30°$ (for ISO metric) and depth $(D - D_c)/2 = 0.5413 P$. Hence the radial offset of the pitch line from the crest is exactly $P/2$.

**Numerical Example (ISO M10 × 1.5 thread):** Standard values: $D = 10.000 \text{ mm}$, $P = 1.5 \text{ mm}$, $D_c = 8.376 \text{ mm}$.

Measured on TMM: $D_{\text{meas}} = 9.985 \text{ mm}$, $D_{c,\text{meas}} = 8.390 \text{ mm}$, $P_{\text{meas}} = 1.495 \text{ mm}$.

Compute theoretical $D_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_p = 10.000 - \dfrac{1.5}{2} = 10.000 - 0.750 = 9.250 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage error on major diameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Error}_D = \dfrac{\vert 9.985 - 10.000 \vert}{10.000} \times 100 = 0.15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage error on pitch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Error}_P = \dfrac{\vert 1.495 - 1.500 \vert}{1.500} \times 100 \approx 0.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Thread Half-Angle from Profile Readings

Align the crosshair with one flank of the thread profile. Rotate the protractor eyepiece to bring the reference line parallel to the flank. Record angle $\theta_1$. Repeat on the opposite flank to get $\theta_2$. The thread included angle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = \theta_2 - \theta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

The thread half-angle is then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = \dfrac{\theta_2 - \theta_1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For ISO metric threads, the standard thread angle is $60°$, so the half-angle should be $30°$. Any measured deviation indicates thread manufacturing error or tool wear in the die/tap.

## 3.6 Least Count Derivation of the Protractor Eyepiece

The main circular scale of the protractor is divided in degrees (i.e., $0.5°$ per main division in some models, $1°$ in others). The vernier scale typically has 30 divisions spanning 29° of the main scale (or 60 divisions spanning 29.5°).

For the common case of 30 vernier divisions over 29 main-scale divisions of $1°$ each:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LC} &= \dfrac{1°}{30} = 0.0333° \\
         &= 0.0333° \times 60' \text{ per degree} \\
         &= 2' \text{ of arc}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In many KTU-lab TMMs, the configuration is 30 divisions on the vernier coinciding with 29 divisions of the main scale, giving $\text{LC} = 1'$. Always **verify the LC from the actual instrument dial** before recording values.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS AND SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Tool Maker's Microscope

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **sub-system interaction flow** inside a typical Tool Maker's Microscope. It uses alphanumeric node identifiers (per protocol) and clean uppercase labels without markdown formatting inside the labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[BASE_AND_DATUM] --> nodeB[CROSS_SLIDE_TABLE_X_AXIS]
    nodeA --> nodeC[CROSS_SLIDE_TABLE_Y_AXIS]
    nodeB --> nodeD[WORK_HOLDING_FIXTURE]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[WORKPIECE_UNDER_TEST]
    nodeF[SURFACE_LAMP] --> nodeE
    nodeG[PROFILE_LAMP] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH[OBJECTIVE_LENS]
    nodeH --> nodeI[INTERMEDIATE_LENS_BODY]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[PROTRACTOR_EYEPIECE_WITH_RETICLE]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[OBSERVER_EYE]
    nodeB --> nodeL[MAIN_SCALE_X]
    nodeC --> nodeM[MAIN_SCALE_Y]
    nodeJ --> nodeN[ANGULAR_MAIN_SCALE_0_TO_360]
    nodeJ --> nodeO[ANGULAR_VERNIER_30_DIV]
    nodeL --> nodeP[LC_0_005_MM]
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeQ[ANGULAR_LC_1_MINUTE]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Measurement Procedure Flowchart

This flowchart depicts the **logical step-by-step procedure** a KTU student must follow during the lab experiment, from instrument switch-on to final report submission.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[START_INSTRUMENT_WARMUP_5_MIN] --> stepB[SELECT_ILLUMINATION_MODE]
    stepB -->|SURFACE_FEATURES| stepC1[SWITCH_ON_SURFACE_LAMP]
    stepB -->|PROFILE_FEATURES| stepC2[SWITCH_ON_PROFILE_LAMP]
    stepC1 --> stepD[MOUNT_WORKPIECE_ON_FIXTURE]
    stepC2 --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE[COARSE_FOCUS_USING_FOCUS_KNOB]
    stepE --> stepF[FINE_FOCUS_FOR_SHARP_IMAGE]
    stepF --> stepG[ALIGN_CROSSHAIR_TO_POINT_A]
    stepG --> stepH[RECORD_X1_Y1_AND_ANGLE_1]
    stepH --> stepI[ALIGN_CROSSHAIR_TO_POINT_B]
    stepI --> stepJ[RECORD_X2_Y2_AND_ANGLE_2]
    stepJ --> stepK[COMPUTE_DELTA_X_DELTA_Y]
    stepK --> stepL[COMPUTE_LENGTH_AND_ANGLE]
    stepL --> stepM[REPEAT_FOR_N_3_READINGS]
    stepM --> stepN[CALCULATE_MEAN_AND_PERCENT_ERROR]
    stepN --> stepO[RECORD_IN_OBSERVATION_TABLE]
    stepO --> stepP[PLOT_GRAPH_IF_REQUIRED]
    stepP --> stepQ[END_OF_EXPERIMENT]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Optical Light Path (Schematic Equivalent)

Because the precise lens arrangement of a TMM is a physical drawing (compound microscope + protractor eyepiece), the protocol allows adaptation to a functional block-level architecture flow showing the optical chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    opt1[WORKPIECE_FEATURE] --> opt2[OBJECTIVE_LENS_F1]
    opt2 --> opt3[INTERMEDIATE_TUBE_LENS_F2]
    opt3 --> opt4[PROTRACTOR_ANGULAR_DISK]
    opt4 --> opt5[RETICLE_WITH_CROSSHAIR]
    opt5 --> opt6[EYEPIECE_LENS_F3]
    opt6 --> opt7[OBSERVER_EYE_RETINA]
    profLamp[PROFILE_LAMP_COLLIMATOR] -.-> opt1
    surfLamp[SURFACE_LAMP_OBLIQUE] -.-> opt1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Reduction Chain for Tool Angle Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    d1[X1_X2_X3_READINGS] --> d2[MEAN_X]
    d1 --> d3[STANDARD_DEVIATION]
    d2 --> d4[FINAL_RAKE_ANGLE_GAMMA]
    e1[Y1_Y2_Y3_READINGS] --> e2[MEAN_Y]
    e2 --> d4
    f1[PROTRACTOR_ANGLE_THETA_E] --> f2[ANGLE_OF_CUTTING_EDGE]
    f2 --> d4
    g1[PROTRACTOR_ANGLE_THETA_F] --> g2[ANGLE_OF_RAKE_FACE]
    g2 --> d4
    d4 --> h1[REPORT_GAMMA_WITH_UNCERTAINTY]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define Tool Maker's Microscope. List any two applications.**
*Model Answer:*
The Tool Maker's Microscope (TMM) is an **optical, non-contact precision measuring instrument** used for inspecting and measuring the linear dimensions and angular geometry of cutting tools, threaded components, and small engineering parts with sub-millimetre accuracy.
Two applications:
1. Measurement of tool angles — **rake angle, clearance angle, nose radius** of single-point cutting tools.
2. Inspection of thread parameters — **major diameter, minor diameter, pitch, and thread angle** of screw-thread plugs and ring gauges.
*(Additional: contour tracing of press-tool punches and dies.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the principle of measurement in a Tool Maker's Microscope?**
*Model Answer:*
The TMM works on three principles combined into one instrument:
1. **Optical magnification** of the workpiece image through a compound microscope (objective + eyepiece).
2. **Two-axis linear coordinate measurement** using an XY cross-slide table fitted with micrometers and verniers (least count ~ **0.005 mm**).
3. **Direct angular measurement** using a **protractor eyepiece** with main and vernier scales giving an angular least count of typically **1 minute**.
These three together allow length, distance, and angle to be read simultaneously without contact with the workpiece.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Experiment Pattern]**

### **Question A — Tool Angle Measurement (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Tool Maker's Microscope. State clearly the function of the protractor eyepiece and the dual illumination system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Construction:* A Tool Maker's Microscope consists of the following main parts:

1. **Heavy cast iron base** — provides a flat reference datum and vibration resistance.
2. **Cross-slide table (X and Y stages)** — provides two perpendicular precision linear motions, each fitted with a micrometer having least count **0.005 mm**.
3. **Work-holding fixture** — typically a set of centres with adjustable top rests, or a V-block, or a magnetic chuck for flat specimens.
4. **Dual illumination system** —
   * **Surface lamp:** an oblique light source from above, used for inspecting surface features like cutting edges and nose radius.
   * **Profile lamp:** a collimated light source from behind the workpiece, producing a sharp contour silhouette of the edge profile — essential for thread flanks and contour tracing.
5. **Microscope body (optical tube)** — houses the objective lens, magnification changer, intermediate tube lens, and the protractor eyepiece. It can be rotated about a vertical axis.
6. **Protractor eyepiece** — graduated circular main scale (0°–360°) and a vernier scale (typically 30 divisions over 29° of main scale), giving angular least count of **1 minute**. Contains a crosshair graticule.
7. **Magnification changer** — rotating turret to switch between 10×, 20×, 30×, and 50× magnification.

*Working:* The workpiece is mounted on the fixture. The operator selects surface or profile illumination based on the feature. The image of the feature is brought into sharp focus. The crosshair is aligned on point A; readings $X_1$, $Y_1$ and protractor angle $\theta_1$ are noted. The crosshair is aligned on point B; readings $X_2$, $Y_2$ and protractor angle $\theta_2$ are noted.

*Function of Protractor Eyepiece:* Allows **direct angular measurement** between two lines/edges of the workpiece by rotating the eyepiece and reading the circular vernier. No trigonometric calculation is needed if the feature is aligned directly with the reticle.

*Function of Dual Illumination:*
* **Surface illumination** is used when the feature is on the **face of the workpiece** (e.g., the cutting edge surface, nose radius). It illuminates the surface obliquely so that edges and surface markings become visible.
* **Profile illumination** is used when the **silhouette (outline)** of the feature is to be inspected (e.g., thread flanks, punch profile). It gives a sharp edge contour against a bright background.

*Block Diagram:* Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram of the functional architecture.

**(b)** A single-point cutting tool is mounted on the TMM. The X-axis readings at the cutting edge and the end of the tool face are **10.000 mm** and **16.500 mm** respectively. The corresponding Y-axis readings are **5.000 mm** and **9.250 mm**. The protractor eyepiece reads **$12°\,10'$** on the cutting edge and **$38°\,40'$** on the rake face. Calculate the **rake angle** of the tool. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:*
$X_1 = 10.000 \text{ mm}$, $Y_1 = 5.000 \text{ mm}$ (cutting edge point)
$X_2 = 16.500 \text{ mm}$, $Y_2 = 9.250 \text{ mm}$ (rake face reference point)
Protractor at cutting edge: $\theta_e = 12°10'$
Protractor at rake face: $\theta_f = 38°40'$

*Step 1: Slope of cutting edge from X-Y readings:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta X_e &= X_2 - X_1 = 16.500 - 10.000 = 6.500 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta Y_e &= Y_2 - Y_1 = 9.250 - 5.000 = 4.250 \text{ mm} \\
\theta_{e,\text{slope}} &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{4.250}{6.500}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.6538) \approx 33.18°
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2: Rake angle by direct protractor method (preferred for accuracy):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \theta_f - \theta_e \\
       &= 38°40' - 12°10' \\
       &= 26°30'
\end{aligned}
$$

*Result:* $\gamma = 26°30'$ (positive rake — typical for finishing cuts on mild steel with HSS tool).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert minutes** while subtracting angles: $40' - 10' = 30'$, $38° - 12° = 26°$ → correct.
> 2. **Sign confusion:** A negative rake angle does not mean a wrong measurement — it simply indicates the tool face slopes downward from the cutting edge. Always report with the sign.
> 3. **Mixing up surface and profile illumination:** Using profile light on a cutting edge will give a faint, invisible image. Always identify the feature type first.
> 4. **Not averaging multiple readings:** KTU record evaluation gives 1–2 marks for "reliability of observation." Take at least **three** independent readings.

---

### **Question B — Thread Parameter Measurement (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to measure the **pitch, major diameter, and minor diameter** of a screw thread using a Tool Maker's Microscope. Why is **profile illumination** preferred for thread inspection? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Procedure for Pitch (P):*

1. Mount the threaded plug (or ring) on the work-holding fixture between centres such that the thread axis is parallel to the X-slide direction.
2. Switch on **profile illumination** to obtain a sharp silhouette of the thread flanks.
3. Focus the microscope on the thread crests.
4. Align the crosshair tangentially with the **first thread crest**. Record the X-coordinate reading $X_a$.
5. Slide the table along X until the crosshair coincides with the **$n^{\text{th}}$ successive crest** (typically $n = 5$ or $10$). Record $X_b$.
6. Calculate pitch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \dfrac{X_b - X_a}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Procedure for Major Diameter (D):*

1. With profile illumination focused, align the crosshair on the outermost crest on the **left flank** of the thread. Record $X_L$.
2. Slide the table in X until the crosshair aligns on the outermost crest of the **right flank**. Record $X_R$.
3. Calculate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D = X_R - X_L
\end{aligned}
$$

*Procedure for Minor Diameter ($D_c$):*

1. Repeat the above procedure, but align the crosshair on the **thread roots** on both flanks.
2. Calculate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_c = X_R' - X_L'
\end{aligned}
$$

where $X_R'$ and $X_L'$ are the X-readings at the right and left thread roots.

*Why Profile Illumination is Preferred for Threads:*

The thread profile consists of **flanks and crests forming a sharp geometric edge**. Surface illumination produces scattered reflections from the curved crest and makes the silhouette diffuse. Profile (silhouette) illumination passes collimated light **behind the workpiece**, casting a sharp dark outline of the thread flanks against a bright background. This makes the **thread half-angle and pitch line unambiguous** and allows the crosshair to be aligned precisely on the flanks and roots. Therefore, profile illumination is mandatory for accurate thread parameter measurement.

**(b)** A thread plug gauge marked **M20 × 2.5** is measured on a TMM. The following readings are obtained (averaged over 5 observations):

| Quantity | Measured Value | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Major Diameter (D) | 19.978 mm | 20.000 mm |
| Minor Diameter ($D_c$) | 17.294 mm | 17.294 mm |
| Pitch (P) | 2.510 mm | 2.500 mm |

Calculate the **effective (pitch) diameter**, the **percentage errors**, and comment on whether the gauge lies within the ISO tolerance zone for M20 × 2.5 (6H tolerance, fundamental deviation = 0 µm). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1: Theoretical effective diameter:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_p &= D - \dfrac{P}{2} \\
    &= 20.000 - \dfrac{2.500}{2} \\
    &= 20.000 - 1.250 \\
    &= 18.750 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2: Measured effective diameter:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{p,\text{meas}} &= D_{\text{meas}} - \dfrac{P_{\text{meas}}}{2} \\
                  &= 19.978 - \dfrac{2.510}{2} \\
                  &= 19.978 - 1.255 \\
                  &= 18.723 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3: Percentage errors:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Error}_D &= \dfrac{\vert 19.978 - 20.000 \vert}{20.000} \times 100 = 0.110\% \\
\%\text{Error}_{D_c} &= \dfrac{\vert 17.294 - 17.294 \vert}{17.294} \times 100 = 0.000\% \\
\%\text{Error}_P &= \dfrac{\vert 2.510 - 2.500 \vert}{2.500} \times 100 = 0.400\% \\
\%\text{Error}_{D_p} &= \dfrac{\vert 18.723 - 18.750 \vert}{18.750} \times 100 = 0.144\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4: Comparison with ISO 6H tolerance:*

For M20 × 2.5 with **6H internal tolerance**, the pitch diameter tolerance is approximately **+0.250 / 0 mm** (per ISO 965-1). The measured pitch diameter $18.723 \text{ mm}$ is **0.027 mm below the lower limit** ($18.750 \text{ mm}$). Hence the gauge falls **outside the tolerance zone** and must be rejected.

*Comment:* While the major diameter and minor diameter are within acceptable deviation (sub-0.2%), the **pitch is over-sized by 0.4%**, which has pulled the effective diameter below the lower tolerance limit. The gauge is **non-conforming** and should not be used for GO inspection.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Thread Inspection:**
> 1. **Do not confuse effective diameter with mean diameter.** For ISO V-threads, $D_p = D - P/2$, NOT $D - 0.5413P$ (that is the **thread depth formula**, often confused).
> 2. **Always state the tolerance grade and zone** when commenting on acceptance.
> 3. **Percentage error must use absolute value** of deviation divided by standard value, multiplied by 100. Forgetting absolute value can give negative percentage error, which is meaningless.
> 4. **Do not skip units** in the final answer — KTU record sheets require **mm** or **µm** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP AND IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use of Tool Maker's Microscope**

* **Principle:** Optical magnification + XY coordinate measurement + angular protractor readout — three-in-one.
* **Typical least counts:** Linear = **0.005 mm**, Angular = **1 minute of arc (1′)**.
* **Two illumination systems:** Surface (oblique, for face features) and Profile (collimated, for edges/silhouettes). **Profile illumination is mandatory for thread inspection.**
* **Linear distance formula:** $L = \sqrt{(\Delta X)^2 + (\Delta Y)^2}$.
* **Inclination formula:** $\theta = \tan^{-1}(\Delta Y / \Delta X)$.
* **Tool angles:** Rake angle $\gamma = \phi_f - \phi_e$; Clearance angle $\alpha = \phi_{\text{flank}} - \phi_e$.
* **Thread formulas:** $P = (\text{Total X-displacement}) / n$; $D_p = D - P/2$ (for ISO V-threads).
* **Standard thread angle (ISO metric):** $60°$ included, half-angle $30°$.
* **Always take minimum 3 readings** and report the mean to satisfy KTU record-evaluation reliability criteria.
* **Non-contact method:** TMM is a non-contact instrument, ideal for delicate and small parts — never force the workpiece against the lens.
* **Common applications:** Cutting tool inspection, thread gauge calibration, punch/die profile verification, instrument-industry parts.
* **Typical accuracy:** $\pm 0.005 \text{ mm}$ on length, $\pm 1'$ on angle.
* **Always clean the workpiece** before mounting — dust on the part appears magnified and disturbs alignment.
* **Verify LC from instrument dial** before recording — every lab TMM may differ slightly in vernier configuration.
* **Compute and report percentage error** for every measured quantity — KTU awards marks for error analysis, not just raw readings.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Automobile Exhaust Gas and Flue Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Automobile Exhaust Gas and Flue Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Flue Gas Analysis** is the experimental procedure of determining the volumetric (or molar) composition of combustion products leaving a furnace, boiler, or internal combustion engine. The analysis quantifies the relative proportions of **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Oxygen (O₂)**, and **Nitrogen (N₂)** — the four principal constituents formed during the combustion of hydrocarbon fuels with air. The standard laboratory instrument used in KTU 2024 curriculum is the **Orsat Apparatus** (also called the Orsat Gas Analyzer), which operates on the principle of **selective absorption** of specific gas components in chemical reagents.

**Automobile Exhaust Gas Analysis** is the application of the same Orsat principle to determine the combustion efficiency, air-fuel ratio, and pollution level of an automotive engine (SI or CI) by analyzing the tail-pipe emissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module targets the *volumetric Orsat method*, the *theoretical determination of air-fuel ratio*, the *percentage of excess air*, and the *combustion efficiency calculation* based on dry flue gas composition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a **forensic scientist examining a fire's aftermath**. The smoke, soot, and gases are the "witnesses" of how the fire (combustion) actually behaved. If the fire had **plenty of oxygen** (excess air), the smoke would be rich in **CO₂ and O₂**, with little CO — a clean burn. If the fire was **starved of oxygen** (deficient air), you would find **leftover CO and unburnt fuel** — a smoky, inefficient burn.

The Orsat apparatus acts like a **chemical detective**, passing the flue gas through a sequence of "interrogation chambers" (absorption pipettes), where each specific gas surrenders itself to a particular chemical reagent. What remains in the gas volume tells you the precise composition.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Constants
- **Atmospheric Pressure (Pₐₜₘ) = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R̄) = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
- **Standard Temperature (Tₛₜ) = 25°C = 298.15 K** for reference
- **Molecular weight of dry air = 28.97 kg/kmol**
- **Molecular weight of N₂ = 28.0 kg/kmol**

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU flue gas problems assume **complete combustion of carbon to CO₂ and hydrogen to H₂O**, and that the difference between original air supplied and that required for stoichiometric combustion is reported as **% Excess Air**.

### 1.4 The Four Gases Detected by Orsat

| Order | Gas | Absorbing Reagent | Chemical Reaction |
|-------|-----|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1st | **CO₂** | Potassium Hydroxide (KOH) | CO₂ + 2KOH → K₂CO₃ + H₂O |
| 2nd | **O₂** | Pyrogallol (C₆H₃(OH)₃) | O₂ absorbed in alkaline solution |
| 3rd | **CO** | Ammoniacal Cuprous Chloride (Cu₂Cl₂) | Cu₂Cl₂ + 2CO → Cu₂Cl₂·2CO |
| Residual | **N₂** | None (by difference) | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Flue Gas Analysis

The combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel with air may be represented as:

$$C_xH_y + a(O_2 + 3.76N_2) \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + bO_2 + cCO + dN_2$$

Where:
- `a` = moles of O₂ actually supplied
- `b` = moles of O₂ in flue gas (excess)
- `c` = moles of CO (incomplete combustion)
- The N₂ balance gives `d = 3.76a`

### 2.2 Key Engineering Principles (Bulleted Logic Steps)

1. **Combustion Products are Sampled at Source** — Gas is drawn from the chimney/exhaust through a water-cooled probe to stop further reactions.
2. **Water Vapor is Condensed Out** — The gas is passed through a U-tube cooled by ice/water; H₂O is removed so the analysis is on a **dry basis**.
3. **Selective Absorption Sequence** — The dry gas is passed through reagents in a fixed order: KOH (CO₂) → Pyrogallol (O₂) → Cu₂Cl₂ (CO). The volume reduction at each stage = volume of absorbed gas.
4. **Nitrogen is Determined by Difference** — The remaining gas, after absorbing CO₂, O₂, and CO, is assumed to be N₂.
5. **Air-Fuel Ratio is Inferred from N₂** — Since N₂ in flue gas equals N₂ in supplied air (N₂ is inert), and air contains 79% N₂, the total moles of air supplied can be back-calculated.
6. **Excess Air Indicates Combustion Quality** — High excess air = heat lost to N₂; Low excess air = risk of CO and smoke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Power plant operators use flue gas analysis to tune burner settings, achieving 1–3% excess air for gas-fired boilers and 5–10% for coal/oil-fired boilers — directly impacting thermal efficiency and emissions compliance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical O₂ required | $O_2^{st} = x + \dfrac{y}{4}$ | kmol/kmol fuel | For fuel CₓHᵧ |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_{st} = 4.76 \cdot O_2^{st}$ | kmol air/kmol fuel | 1 kmol O₂ needs 4.76 kmol air |
| 3 | Mass of air supplied (from N₂) | $m_a = \dfrac{m_{N_2}}{0.77}$ | kg | 23% O₂ + 77% N₂ by mass in air |
| 4 | Actual Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR_{act} = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Direct from N₂ |
| 5 | Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio | $AFR_{st} = \dfrac{4.76 \cdot O_2^{st} \cdot 28.97}{M_f}$ | kg/kg | Mₓ = molecular wt of fuel |
| 6 | % Excess Air | $\%EA = \dfrac{AFR_{act} - AFR_{st}}{AFR_{st}} \times 100$ | % | Or $= \dfrac{O_2^{excess}}{O_2^{st}} \times 100$ |
| 7 | Stoichiometric air (vol) | $A_{st}^{vol} = 4.76 \cdot O_2^{st}$ | m³/m³ | At same T, P |
| 8 | Actual air from flue gas | $A_{act} = \dfrac{N_2^{fg}}{3.76 \cdot O_2^{st}}$ | kmol | From Orsat reading |
| 9 | Dry flue gas per kg fuel | $G_{dry} = \dfrac{N_2^{fg}}{0.79 \cdot m_f}$ | kmol/kg | N₂ balance |
| 10 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb} = 100 - \dfrac{\text{Heat losses}}{\text{Heat input}}$ | % | Includes dry gas + moisture + CO + soot losses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in formulas, never the pipe symbol `\|`**, to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.4 Composition Benchmarks (Quick Reference)

| Combustion State | CO₂ | O₂ | CO |
|------------------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Perfect (stoichiometric)** | Maximum | 0% | 0% |
| **With excess air (normal)** | Reduced | Present | Trace |
| **Deficient air** | Reduced | 0% | High |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Air-Fuel Ratio from Dry Flue Gas Composition

**Given:** A fuel of formula CₓHᵧ is burned and the dry flue gas analysis by volume is:
- CO₂ = a%, O₂ = b%, CO = c%, N₂ = d%

**Step 1 — Normalize the flue gas to 1 kmol of dry flue gas:**

$$1 \text{ kmol dry FG} = \dfrac{a}{100} CO_2 + \dfrac{b}{100} O_2 + \dfrac{c}{100} CO + \dfrac{d}{100} N_2$$

**Step 2 — Carbon balance (every C atom in fuel appears as CO₂ or CO):**

$$x = \dfrac{a}{100} + \dfrac{c}{100} = \dfrac{a+c}{100}$$

**Step 3 — Nitrogen balance (all N₂ in flue gas comes from supplied air):**

$$\dfrac{d}{100} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2}^{supplied}$$

$$n_{O_2}^{supplied} = \dfrac{d}{376}$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen balance (oxygen supplied = oxygen in products + oxygen in excess):**

$$n_{O_2}^{supplied} = n_{O_2}^{CO_2} + n_{O_2}^{CO} + n_{O_2}^{H_2O} + n_{O_2}^{excess}$$

$$n_{O_2}^{supplied} = \dfrac{a}{100} + \dfrac{c}{200} + \dfrac{y}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{n_{fuel}} + \dfrac{b}{100}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for y (hydrogen in fuel):**

$$y = 4 \left[ n_{O_2}^{supplied} - \dfrac{a}{100} - \dfrac{c}{200} - \dfrac{b}{100} \right] \cdot n_{fuel}$$

Where $n_{fuel} = 1$ kmol assumed.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Theoretical Stoichiometric Air for CH₄

For **methane (CH₄)**, the combustion reaction is:

$$CH_4 + 2(O_2 + 3.76N_2) \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O + 7.52 N_2$$

So $O_2^{st} = 2$ kmol/kmol fuel.

Stoichiometric air = $4.76 \times 2 = 9.52$ kmol air/kmol CH₄.

$$AFR_{st} = \dfrac{9.52 \times 28.97}{16.04} = 17.19 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical)

> **Problem:** A fuel of composition **C = 84%, H₂ = 12%, O₂ = 2%, N₂ = 2% (by mass)** is burned in a boiler. The Orsat analysis of the flue gas gives: **CO₂ = 12.5%, CO = 0.5%, O₂ = 4.5%, N₂ = 82.5%**. Calculate: (i) Weight of air actually supplied per kg of fuel, (ii) % Excess Air, (iii) Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel.

**Step A — Molecular weight of fuel (basis 1 kg fuel):**

$$C : \dfrac{0.84}{12} = 0.0700 \text{ kg-atom C}$$
$$H : \dfrac{0.12}{2} = 0.0600 \text{ kg-atom H}$$
$$O : \dfrac{0.02}{32} = 0.000625 \text{ kg-atom O (already combined as fuel-O}_2\text{)}$$
$$N : \dfrac{0.02}{28} = 0.000714 \text{ kg-atom N}$$

**Step B — Stoichiometric O₂ requirement:**

Each C needs 1 O₂; each 2H needs 0.5 O₂; subtract the fuel's own O₂:

$$O_2^{st} = 0.0700 + 0.0300 - 0.000625 = 0.09938 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step C — Stoichiometric Air:**

$$A_{st} = 4.76 \times 0.09938 = 0.4730 \text{ kmol air/kg fuel}$$

**Step D — Theoretical dry flue gas composition (basis 0.0700 kmol C):**
- CO₂ = 0.0700 kmol
- H₂O = 0.0600 kmol (leaves as vapor, not in dry FG)
- N₂ (from air) = $3.76 \times 0.09938 = 0.3737$ kmol

Theoretical dry FG = 0.0700 + 0.3737 = 0.4437 kmol (per kg fuel).

**Step E — Use N₂ to find actual air supplied (N₂ balance):**

From Orsat, dry flue gas has **82.5% N₂**. Let actual dry FG = G kmol/kg fuel.

$$0.825 G = \text{kmol N}_2 \text{ in actual flue gas} = 3.76 \cdot n_{O_2}^{act}$$

Also from CO₂ + CO = 12.5% + 0.5% = 13% of dry FG = kmol C atoms per kg fuel = 0.0700

$$0.13 G = 0.0700 \implies G = 0.5385 \text{ kmol dry FG/kg fuel}$$

**Step F — Actual moles of air:**

$$\text{N}_2 \text{ in flue gas} = 0.825 \times 0.5385 = 0.4442 \text{ kmol}$$

$$A_{act} = \dfrac{0.4442}{3.76} = 0.1181 \text{ kmol O}_2 \text{ supplied/kg fuel}$$

$$A_{act} \text{ (in air)} = \dfrac{0.4442}{0.79} = 0.5623 \text{ kmol air/kg fuel}$$

**Step G — Mass of air actually supplied per kg fuel:**

$$m_a = 0.5623 \times 28.97 = 16.29 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step H — Stoichiometric air (mass):**

$$m_{a,st} = 0.4730 \times 28.97 = 13.70 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step I — % Excess Air:**

$$\%EA = \dfrac{16.29 - 13.70}{13.70} \times 100 = 18.9\%$$

**Step J — Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel:**

$$m_{fg,dry} = 0.5385 \times 29 \approx 15.6 \text{ kg dry FG/kg fuel}$$

(Using mean MW of dry FG ≈ 29, since N₂ dominates)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combustion Parameter Inference

| Orsat Reading | Engineering Inference |
|---------------|----------------------|
| **High CO₂ + 0% O₂ + 0% CO** | Near-stoichiometric — ideal but hard to sustain |
| **High CO₂ + moderate O₂ + 0% CO** | Excess air present, complete combustion — typical boiler operation |
| **High CO + 0% O₂** | Insufficient air — incomplete combustion, energy loss |
| **High O₂ + low CO₂** | Too much excess air — efficiency loss from hot N₂ stack gas |
| **Moderate CO + moderate O₂** | Local air deficiency (poor mixing) — burner tuning needed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Orsat Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Sampling Probe] --> B[Water Cooled Tube]
    B --> C[U-Tube Moisture Trap]
    C --> D[Measuring Burette 100 mL]
    D --> E[CO2 Absorption Pipette with KOH]
    E --> F[O2 Absorption Pipette with Pyrogallol]
    F --> G[CO Absorption Pipette with Cu2Cl2]
    G --> H[Residual Gas N2 by Difference]
    H --> I[Volume Reduction Reading]
    I --> J[Orsat Composition Table]
    J --> K[Air Fuel Ratio Calculation]
    K --> L[Excess Air Percentage]
    L --> M[Combustion Efficiency Report]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Flue Gas Analysis Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air In] --> B[Fuel + Air Mixture]
    B --> C[Combustion Chamber or Furnace]
    C --> D[Hot Flue Gas Stream]
    D --> E[Sample Probe]
    E --> F[Drying Train Removes H2O]
    F --> G[Orsat Analysis Sequence]
    G --> G1[CO2 Measured First]
    G1 --> G2[O2 Measured Second]
    G2 --> G3[CO Measured Third]
    G3 --> G4[N2 by Difference]
    G4 --> H[Data Reduction]
    H --> I[AFR and Excess Air]
    I --> J[Combustion Efficiency]
    style C fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#3498db,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#27ae60,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Automobile Exhaust Analysis Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Automobile Engine SI or CI] --> B[Exhaust Manifold]
    B --> C[Tail Pipe]
    C --> D[Exhaust Gas Sampling Port]
    D --> E[Condenser Trap Removes Moisture]
    E --> F[Filter Removes Soot Particulates]
    F --> G[Orsat Burette 100 mL]
    G --> G1[KOH for CO2]
    G1 --> G2[Pyrogallol for O2]
    G2 --> G3[Cu2Cl2 for CO]
    G3 --> G4[N2 Remaining]
    G4 --> H[Calculate Actual AFR]
    H --> H1[Compare with Stoichiometric AFR]
    H1 --> I{Emission Compliance}
    I -->|Yes| J[Engine Tuned Correctly]
    I -->|No| K[Carburetor or Injector Adjustment]

    style G fill:#16a085,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

```
