## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the sequence of gases absorbed in an Orsat apparatus with the corresponding reagents used.*

**Model Answer:**
The Orsat apparatus absorbs gases in the following fixed sequence:
1. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** is absorbed first by **Potassium Hydroxide (KOH)**.
2. **Oxygen (O₂)** is absorbed next by **alkaline Pyrogallol solution (C₆H₃(OH)₃)**.
3. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** is absorbed last by **ammoniacal Cuprous Chloride (Cu₂Cl₂)**.
4. The remaining gas, after these three absorptions, is reported as **Nitrogen (N₂) by difference**.

**[3 Marks — 1 mark for correct order, 1 mark for reagents, 1 mark for residual N₂]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is nitrogen determined "by difference" in Orsat analysis and not by direct absorption?*

**Model Answer:**
Nitrogen is an **inert, chemically non-reactive gas** under the conditions of the Orsat analysis (room temperature, aqueous reagents). It does not react with any of the standard absorption chemicals (KOH, pyrogallol, Cu₂Cl₂). Hence, no specific reagent exists to absorb N₂ selectively at ambient conditions. Therefore, the volume of gas remaining after absorbing CO₂, O₂, and CO is **measured and reported as N₂**. This is an indirect "by difference" method based on the assumption that the dry flue gas contains only these four constituents.

**[3 Marks — 1 mark inertness, 1 mark no reagent, 1 mark by-difference logic]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of the Orsat apparatus** used for flue gas analysis. Describe the function of each component. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The Orsat analysis of flue gas from a boiler furnace using furnace oil (assume C = 86%, H₂ = 12%, S = 2%) gives: **CO₂ = 11.8%, O₂ = 5.6%, CO = 0.6%, N₂ = 82.0%**. Calculate: (i) Mass of air supplied per kg of fuel, (ii) % Excess Air, (iii) Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The Orsat apparatus consists of:

1. **Water-Jacketed Measuring Burette (100 mL)** — Calibrated glass tube enclosed in a water jacket to maintain constant temperature. It has a level bottle (water reservoir) connected via a flexible tube to bring the gas to atmospheric pressure before reading.

2. **Three Absorption Pipettes** — Each is a sealed glass vessel containing the specific reagent. They are connected in series with stopcocks:
   - **Pipette 1:** KOH (specific gravity 1.27) — absorbs CO₂.
   - **Pipette 2:** Alkaline pyrogallol — absorbs O₂.
   - **Pipette 3:** Ammoniacal cuprous chloride — absorbs CO.

3. **Distribution Manifold** — A multi-way stopcock system to direct the gas sample into any one of the three pipettes or to the atmosphere.

4. **Gas Inlet Tube** — Connected to the gas sampling line (from the flue/automobile exhaust).

5. **Leveling Bottle** — Contains water (slightly acidified to prevent CO₂ absorption). Used to push the gas from the burette into the absorption pipettes.

**Working Principle:** A 100 mL gas sample is drawn into the burette. It is then forced into Pipette 1 (KOH); the CO₂ is absorbed, and the reduced volume is read. The process is repeated for O₂ and CO. The final volume equals N₂.

**[7 Marks: 3 for construction, 2 for components, 2 for working]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** C = 86%, H₂ = 12%, S = 2% (mass basis), 1 kg fuel.

**Step 1 — Moles per kg of fuel:**

$$n_C = \dfrac{0.86}{12} = 0.07167 \text{ kmol C/kg fuel}$$
$$n_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.12}{2} = 0.06000 \text{ kmol H}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$
$$n_S = \dfrac{0.02}{32} = 0.000625 \text{ kmol S/kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ required:**

$$O_2^{st} = n_C + 0.5 n_{H_2} + n_S = 0.07167 + 0.03000 + 0.000625 = 0.1023 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air:**

$$A_{st} = 4.76 \times 0.1023 = 0.4869 \text{ kmol air/kg fuel}$$
$$m_{a,st} = 0.4869 \times 28.97 = 14.11 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Carbon and Nitrogen balance using Orsat:**

In dry flue gas, CO₂ + CO = 11.8% + 0.6% = 12.4% of total dry FG = kmol C atoms per kg fuel.

$$0.124 \cdot G = 0.07167 + 0.000625 = 0.07230 \implies G = 0.5831 \text{ kmol dry FG/kg fuel}$$

N₂ in flue gas = 0.820 × 0.5831 = 0.4782 kmol

**Step 5 — Actual O₂ supplied (from N₂ balance):**

$$A_{act,O_2} = \dfrac{0.4782}{3.76} = 0.1272 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of air actually supplied:**

$$A_{act} = \dfrac{0.4782}{0.79} = 0.6053 \text{ kmol air/kg fuel}$$
$$m_a = 0.6053 \times 28.97 = 17.54 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 7 — % Excess Air:**

$$\%EA = \dfrac{17.54 - 14.11}{14.11} \times 100 = 24.3\%$$

**Step 8 — Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel:**

$$m_{fg,dry} = 0.5831 \times 29 = 16.91 \text{ kg dry FG/kg fuel}$$

**[7 Marks: 1 each for steps 1, 2/3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]**

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **composition of automobile exhaust gases** for both SI and CI engines, and explain how the Orsat apparatus is used to assess engine performance. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A coal sample with composition **C = 75%, H₂ = 5%, O₂ = 10%, N₂ = 3%, S = 2%, Ash = 5%** is burned with 30% excess air. The Orsat analysis is reported as: **CO₂ = 13.2%, CO = 0.4%, O₂ = 5.1%, N₂ = 81.3%**. Determine: (i) Theoretical air required per kg of coal, (ii) Actual air supplied per kg of coal, (iii) Mass of dry products per kg of coal. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**SI Engine Exhaust Composition:**
- CO₂: 8–15% (lower with rich mixture)
- CO: 0.5–5% (rises sharply with rich mixture)
- O₂: 0.5–2% (stoichiometric) to 5% (lean)
- HC (unburnt): 100–1000 ppm
- NOₓ: 500–3000 ppm
- N₂: balance (~70–80%)

**CI Engine Exhaust Composition:**
- CO₂: 2–8% (diesel is always lean)
- CO: 0.05–0.5% (very low due to excess air)
- O₂: 5–15% (large excess air)
- HC: 50–500 ppm
- NOₓ: 500–2000 ppm
- Soot/PM: 0.05–0.5 g/m³
- N₂: balance

**Orsat for Engine Analysis:** Tail-pipe sample is drawn through a probe → passed through a soot filter → dried in a cold trap → analyzed in the Orsat burette. From the CO₂, CO, O₂, and N₂ values, the actual air-fuel ratio is computed and compared with the stoichiometric AFR. Any deviation indicates fuel metering or carburetion issues.

**[7 Marks: 2 for SI, 2 for CI, 3 for Orsat application]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Moles per kg coal (neglecting ash and N₂ in coal):**

$$n_C = \dfrac{0.75}{12} = 0.0625 \text{ kg-atom C/kg}$$
$$n_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.05}{2} = 0.0250 \text{ kg-atom H/kg}$$
$$n_S = \dfrac{0.02}{32} = 0.000625 \text{ kg-atom S/kg}$$
$$n_{O_2, fuel} = \dfrac{0.10}{32} = 0.003125 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric O₂ required:**

$$O_2^{st} = 0.0625 + 0.0125 + 0.000625 - 0.003125 = 0.0725 \text{ kmol O}_2/\text{kg coal}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Air:**

$$A_{st} = 4.76 \times 0.0725 = 0.3451 \text{ kmol air/kg coal}$$
$$m_{a,st} = 0.3451 \times 28.97 = 9.998 \approx 10.0 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Air Supplied (30% excess):**

$$A_{act} = 1.30 \times 0.3451 = 0.4486 \text{ kmol air/kg coal}$$
$$m_{a,act} = 1.30 \times 10.0 = 13.0 \text{ kg air/kg coal}$$

**Step 5 — Carbon + Sulfur balance from Orsat:**

In dry flue gas, CO₂ + CO = 13.2% + 0.4% = 13.6% = kmol C + S per kg coal.

$$0.136 \cdot G = 0.0625 + 0.000625 = 0.063125$$
$$G = 0.4642 \text{ kmol dry FG/kg coal}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of dry products:**

$$m_{fg,dry} = 0.4642 \times 29 = 13.46 \text{ kg dry FG/kg coal}$$

**Verification of N₂ balance:**

N₂ in flue gas = 0.813 × 0.4642 = 0.3774 kmol
N₂ supplied = 0.4486 × 0.79 = 0.3544 kmol

The slight discrepancy (≈6%) is due to the assumed 30% excess air. To match exactly, excess air should be recomputed as:

$$A_{act,O_2} = \dfrac{0.3774}{3.76} = 0.1004 \text{ kmol O}_2 \implies A_{act} = \dfrac{0.1004}{0.21} = 0.4781 \text{ kmol air/kg}$$

$$\%EA = \left(\dfrac{0.4781}{0.3451} - 1\right) \times 100 = 38.5\%$$

**[7 Marks: 1 each for steps 1–6, plus 1 for verification]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the fuel's own O₂** in Step 2 (Part b) — students often use raw C and H only and get a higher O₂ demand.
> 2. **Using mass% N₂ = 79%** as 0.79 (correct), but some students confuse it with the 3.76 factor (molar ratio) — both are correct but in different formulas.
> 3. **Mixing up stoichiometric and actual air** — always label the subscripts clearly (st vs. act).
> 4. **Ignoring CO in the carbon balance** — CO also carries carbon atoms to the dry FG, so include `c` (CO%) when computing G.
> 5. **Forgetting dry vs. wet basis** — Orsat gives dry composition; H₂O is excluded.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flue Gas & Automobile Exhaust Analysis**

- ✅ **Orsat Apparatus** is the standard KTU instrument; it uses **selective absorption** in three pipettes plus a residual.
- ✅ **Reagent Order:** KOH (CO₂) → Pyrogallol (O₂) → Cu₂Cl₂ (CO) → N₂ (residual).
- ✅ **Sample must be dry** (water removed) and at **room conditions** — Orsat reports **dry gas composition** by volume.
- ✅ **N₂ is the "key" gas** — it traces back the air supplied (since N₂ in FG = N₂ in air, due to inertness).
- ✅ **AFR (mass) = (N₂ kg in FG) / 0.77 / (kg fuel)** — uses 77% N₂ by mass in air.
- ✅ **% Excess Air** = (Actual AFR − Stoichiometric AFR) / Stoichiometric AFR × 100.
- ✅ **SI engines** have higher CO and HC; **CI engines** have higher O₂ and particulates.
- ✅ **Combustion efficiency** improves with proper air-fuel ratio; ideal %EA = 5–15% for most fuels.
- ✅ **Mean molecular weight of dry flue gas** ≈ **29 kg/kmol** (used as a quick estimate).
- ✅ **Always check the carbon balance:** `CO₂% + CO% × 0.5 = kmol C per kmol dry FG` portion; ensure consistency.
- ✅ **For coal/oil** with S, N, and ash: include S in the O₂ demand and N₂ in the N₂ balance.
- ✅ **Practical limitations** of Orsat: slow, manual, no NOₓ/HC detection — for modern emission testing, **NDIR and FID analyzers** are used; however, Orsat remains the **academic standard** for KTU lab examinations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstration of Coordinate Measuring Machine for Evaluation of Form Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstration of Coordinate Measuring Machine for Evaluation of Form Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** is a precision metrological instrument used to measure the geometric characteristics of a physical object. It accomplishes this by sensing discrete spatial coordinates on the workpiece surface using a contact (touch-trigger) or non-contact (laser/optical) probe, and subsequently analyzing the acquired point cloud against mathematical reference geometries to determine dimensional and form deviations.

**Form Error** is defined by **ISO 1101 / ASME Y14.5** as the deviation of a real (extracted/associated) feature from its perfect (nominal) geometric counterpart, evaluated independently of any datums. Form errors are intrinsic to the feature itself and represent four classical categories: **Straightness, Flatness, Roundness, and Cylindricity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Highlight:** Form errors are ALWAYS evaluated in the Least Material Condition (LMC) – Minimum Zone (MZ) framework per ISO 1101:2017. The Tolerance Zone (TZ) defines the upper bound of allowable deviation, while the Form Error (FE) is the actual measured value.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are a blindfolded sculptor running your fingertips along a wooden plank declared to be "perfectly straight." As your fingers move, you feel small humps, dips, and tiny twists. The difference between what your fingers actually trace and a hypothetical **laser-straight reference line** is the **form error**.

A **CMM** is essentially a mechanical "blindfolded finger" — but with three super-precise fingers (X, Y, Z axes) that record their exact position the moment they touch the part. The computer then mathematically "draws" a perfect reference (like a virtual ruler) and compares it with the touched points to compute how far the real part deviates from perfection.

- **The Probe** → Your fingertip (the sensor).
- **The X-Y-Z Slides** → Your shoulder, elbow, and wrist (the positioning system).
- **The Software** → Your brain (does the math and comparison).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Context:** A CMM with 1.5 μm repeatability is used to qualify engine cylinder bores, turbine blades, and orthopedic implants — anywhere a deviation of 0.01 mm can cause a $50,000 part to fail in service.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used in CMM Evaluation

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Reference | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Probe Tip Diameter | **D_p** | Renishaw / Zeiss catalogs | **2 mm – 5 mm** |
| CMM Accuracy (MPE_E) | **MPE_E** | ISO 10360-2 | **1.5 + L/333 μm** (M-class) |
| Form Error (Min Zone) | **FE_MZ** | ISO 1101 | **0.001 mm – 0.05 mm** |
| Least Squares Residual | **R_LSQ** | ISO 12781-1 | **Sum of squared offsets** |

The **MPE_E (Maximum Permissible Error of the CMM)** is the bidirectional length-measurement error declared by the manufacturer under ISO 10360-2. The total measurement uncertainty **U = MPE_E + Probe + Thermal + Part-Fixture contributions** must be ≤ **15% of the form tolerance** per ISO 14253-1 decision rules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a CMM (Logical Breakdown)

A CMM does not "measure form" directly. It executes a strict three-stage process:

**Stage 1 – Point Acquisition (Sampling)**
- The probe (typically a **touch-trigger probe with ruby tip**) contacts the workpiece at N discrete points.
- The 3-axis linear encoders (Heidenhain / Renishaw glass scales with 0.01 μm or 0.05 μm resolution) record (X, Y, Z) coordinates simultaneously.
- Sampling strategy follows **Chebyshev spacing** or **equal-angle** distribution to minimize aliasing of higher-order form components.

**Stage 2 – Feature Extraction & Fitting**
- The N points are mathematically fitted to a nominal geometry (line, plane, circle, cylinder, sphere).
- Two fitting philosophies are permitted by ISO 5459 / ISO 1101:
  - **Least Squares (LSQ / Gauss) method** — minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
  - **Minimum Zone (MZ / Chebyshev) method** — minimizes the maximum deviation. This is the KTU-preferred approach for form error.

**Stage 3 – Form Error Quantification**
- The fitted reference is subtracted from the captured point cloud.
- The peak-to-valley deviation is reported as the form error.
- The result is compared to the **Tolerance Zone (TZ)** defined on the engineering drawing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Chebyshev / Minimum Zone Method Wins:** It is independent of the data distribution, gives a single, unique, reproducible value, and is the ONLY method accepted by ISO 1101:2017 for dispute resolution. The LSQ method is faster computationally but is sensitive to outlier points.

### 2.2 The Four Classical Form Errors (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Form Error | Nominal Feature | Reference Geometry | Deviation Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Straightness** | Line / Axis | Straight line | 2 perpendicular directions |
| 2 | **Flatness** | Surface | Plane | 1 direction (normal to plane) |
| 3 | **Roundness / Circularity** | Cylinder cross-section | Circle | 1 radial direction |
| 4 | **Cylindricity** | Cylinder | Perfect cylinder | 2 directions (radial + axial) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **#1 reference** for the KTU 2024 Board Exam. Memorize the **three evaluation methods** for circularity, as questions are frequently asked on this distinction.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Straightness (Min Zone, 2-D) | $FE_{str} = \max_i d_i - \min_i d_i$ | mm | 2 parallel lines bounding all points |
| 2 | Flatness (Min Zone, 3 parallel planes) | $FE_{flat} = \max_i z_i - \min_i z_i$ | mm | 3 parallel planes enclosing all points |
| 3 | Roundness (LSQ Circle) | $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[(x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2\right]}$ | mm | LSQ radius |
| 4 | Roundness (Min Zone, MMR) | $FE_{round} = R_{outer} - R_{inner}$ | mm | Minimum radial separation (MMR method) |
| 5 | Cylindricity (Min Zone) | $FE_{cyl} = \max(R_{outer}(z)) - \min(R_{inner}(z))$ | mm | Concentric inner + outer cylinders |
| 6 | Radial Deviation | $r_i = \sqrt{(x_i-x_c)^2+(y_i-y_c)^2}$ | mm | Single-point distance to center |
| 7 | Form Tolerance Zone | $TZ = T_{form}$ (from drawing) | mm | Drawing specification |
| 8 | Acceptance Rule | $FE \le T_{form}$ | mm | Pass/Fail per ISO 8015 |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar for absolute value has been written as `\vert` to keep the table syntactically valid. Example: $FE_{str} = \max \vert d_i \vert$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Form error of turbine blade airfoils is verified using CMM to ensure aerodynamic efficiency (deviation of 25 μm causes 1% loss in stall margin).
- **Automotive**: Cylinder bores in engine blocks are checked for roundness and cylindricity to control oil consumption and piston slap.
- **Medical Implants**: Hip joint femoral heads require sphericity (3-D roundness) below 5 μm to prevent premature wear.
- **Precision Tooling**: Machine tool guideways are qualified for straightness within 2 μm/m to guarantee machining accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Least-Squares (LSQ) Circle for Roundness Evaluation

A CMM records **N points** $(x_i, y_i)$ around a circular feature. The goal is to find the LSQ center $(x_c, y_c)$ and radius $R$ that minimize:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( r_i - R \right)^2
$$

where the radial distance of point $i$ from the candidate center is:

$$
r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared residual:**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( r_i^2 - 2R r_i + R^2 \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Take partial derivative w.r.t. $R$ and equate to zero** (necessary condition for minimum):

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial R} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i + 2 N R = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the LSQ radius:**

$$
R_{LSQ} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_{LSQ} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sqrt{(x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Take partial derivatives w.r.t. $x_c$ and $y_c$** to locate the LSQ center. To avoid the non-linear square root, we use the **algebraic distance** approximation $F(x_c, y_c) = (x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2 - R^2$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x_c} \sum (x_i - x_c)^2 + (y_i - y_c)^2 = 0
$$

$$
-2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - x_c) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x_c = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
$$

$$
-2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - y_c) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; y_c = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the roundness form error (LSQ definition):**

$$
FE_{round,LSQ} = \max_{i} r_i - \min_{i} r_i
$$

where $\max_i r_i$ is the maximum radius and $\min_i r_i$ is the minimum radius from the LSQ center.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Favorite Format)

**Given Problem:** A CMM is used to measure the roundness of a shaft cross-section. Six equally-spaced points are recorded (angles in degrees, $R_i$ in mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_i \;&:\; 0°, 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300° \\
R_i \;&:\; 25.012,\; 24.998,\; 25.020,\; 25.005,\; 24.990,\; 25.015
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step A — Compute the LSQ radius** (assume $x_c, y_c$ are already the centroid since points are equally spaced):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{LSQ} &= \frac{25.012 + 24.998 + 25.020 + 25.005 + 24.990 + 25.015}{6} \\
        &= \frac{150.040}{6} \\
        &= 25.0067 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute radial deviation for each point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta r_1 &= 25.012 - 25.0067 = +0.0053 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta r_2 &= 24.998 - 25.0067 = -0.0087 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta r_3 &= 25.020 - 25.0067 = +0.0133 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta r_4 &= 25.005 - 25.0067 = -0.0017 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta r_5 &= 24.990 - 25.0067 = -0.0167 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta r_6 &= 25.015 - 25.0067 = +0.0083 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Identify the highest peak and lowest valley:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max(\Delta r_i) &= +0.0133 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(at } \theta = 120°) \\
\min(\Delta r_i) &= -0.0167 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(at } \theta = 240°)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Roundness form error (LSQ method):**

$$
\boxed{\,FE_{round,LSQ} = 0.0133 - (-0.0167) = 0.0300 \text{ mm} = 30 \; \mu\text{m}\,}
$$

**Step E — Acceptance check against drawing tolerance** (assume drawing says $T_{round} = 0.040$ mm):

$$
FE_{round,LSQ} = 0.0300 \text{ mm} \le 0.040 \text{ mm} = T_{round} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{ACCEPTED ✓}
$$

---

### 3.3 Minimum Zone (MMR) Refinement for the Same Example

The Minimum Material Reference (MMR / Min Zone / Chebyshev) method fits **two concentric circles** — an outer circle of radius $R_{out}$ and an inner circle of radius $R_{in}$ — such that all measured points lie **between** them, and their radial gap is **minimized**.

**Step 1 — Define the Min Zone criterion:**

$$
\min_{(R_{out}, R_{in})} \left( R_{out} - R_{in} \right) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad R_{in} \le r_i \le R_{out} \;\; \forall i
$$

**Step 2 — From the measured radii** (interpreted as $r_i$ values, using LSQ center as an initial guess):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{in}  &= \min(r_i) = 24.990 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(at } 240°) \\
R_{out} &= \max(r_i) = 25.020 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(at } 120°)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Min Zone roundness error:**

$$
\boxed{\,FE_{round,MZ} = R_{out} - R_{in} = 25.020 - 24.990 = 0.0300 \text{ mm} = 30 \; \mu\text{m}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this symmetric example, LSQ and MZ give identical results because the high and low points are diametrically opposed. **In real measurements, the MZ value is always ≤ LSQ value** — this is a key KTU concept tested in viva.

---

### 3.4 Straightness Error Evaluation (2-D Minimum Zone)

For a line measured with N points $(x_i, y_i)$, the **2 parallel lines method** for straightness proceeds as:

**Step 1 — Rotate the coordinate system** so that the line is approximately parallel to the X-axis (i.e., find the principal direction using PCA — Principal Component Analysis).

**Step 2 — Project all points onto the Y-axis** to obtain residual offsets $y_i'$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Min-Zone straightness:**

$$
FE_{str,MZ} = \max_i (y_i') - \min_i (y_i')
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Min-Zone condition:** There must exist at least **one point on the upper line, one point on the lower line, AND the line orientation must alternate** (alternating criterion) for true MZ.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Evaluation Methods

| Method | Mathematical Basis | Reproducibility | Comput. Cost | ISO Compliance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Least Squares (LSQ)** | Minimize $\sum r_i^2$ | Moderate | Low | Permitted (workshop) |
| **Minimum Zone (MZR/MMR)** | Chebyshev alternation | High (unique) | High | **Preferred (ISO 1101)** |
| **Circumscribed / Inscribed** | Tangent circles | Unique (shaft/hole) | Low | Used for size features only |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define **form error** as per ISO 1101. List the four classical form errors with their reference geometries.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Form error is the deviation of a real (extracted) feature from its nominal geometric shape, evaluated independent of any datum, as defined in **ISO 1101:2017**.

The four classical form errors are:
1. **Straightness** — Reference: Straight line (or 2 parallel lines tolerance zone).
2. **Flatness** — Reference: Plane (or 2 parallel planes tolerance zone).
3. **Roundness (Circularity)** — Reference: Circle (or 2 concentric circles tolerance zone).
4. **Cylindricity** — Reference: Cylinder (or 2 coaxial cylinders tolerance zone).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four-point table.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between the **Least Squares (LSQ)** and **Minimum Zone (MZ)** methods of form error evaluation. Which one is preferred by ISO 1101 and why?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | LSQ Method | Min Zone (MZ) Method |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Basis | Minimizes sum of squared radial deviations | Minimizes maximum radial deviation (Chebyshev) |
| Reference Geometry | One single best-fit circle/line | Two concentric/tangent bounds (outer + inner) |
| Uniqueness | Unique for given data | **Always unique** and reproducible |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | High | Low |
| Computational Cost | Low (closed-form) | High (iterative optimization) |

**ISO 1101 prefers the Min Zone method** because it is datum-independent, gives a unique reproducible result, and represents the **worst-case (largest) deviation** — providing the most conservative (and therefore safer) judgment of conformance. *(3 marks: 2 for table + 1 for ISO reasoning.)*

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Theory + Numerical

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Suggested Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a Bridge-type Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)**. Describe the role of **(i)** the probe, **(ii)** the linear encoders, and **(iii)** the software in form error evaluation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circular hole is probed at **8 equally-spaced points** by a CMM. The radial distances (in mm) from a reference origin are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&24.998, \; 25.005, \; 25.012, \; 25.008, \\
&24.995, \; 25.000, \; 25.010, \; 25.004
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the **roundness form error** using:
- (i) the **Least Squares (LSQ) method**,
- (ii) the **Minimum Zone (MZ) method**.

Given drawing tolerance $T_{round} = 0.025$ mm, state whether the part is **accepted or rejected** as per ISO 8015. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (a): 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to **Section 4.1** Mermaid architecture above. Examiner must see: Probe → Encoders (X/Y/Z) → DAQ → Fitting Engine → Form Error Calculator → Comparator → Report.

**Role of Components (6 Marks — 2 each):**

**(i) Probe (2 Marks):** The probe is the **touch sensor** at the tip of the Z-axis ram. In a touch-trigger probe (e.g., Renishaw TP200), a ruby ball of 2 mm diameter contacts the part; the resulting deflection breaks an electronic circuit, latching the X-Y-Z coordinates. For form error evaluation, the probe radius and overtravel must be **mathematically compensated** in software to obtain the true surface coordinate (form correction / tip-radius compensation).

**(ii) Linear Encoders (2 Marks):** The three axes carry **glass or steel scales** (Heidenhain, Renishaw) with typical resolution of **0.01 μm to 0.05 μm**. They provide the absolute position of the probe center. The CMM's overall accuracy is declared as **MPE_E** (Maximum Permissible Error per ISO 10360-2) — e.g., for a M-class machine: $MPE_E = (1.5 + L/333)\,\mu m$, where L is the measured length in mm. This is the **uncertainty budget** for the entire form error measurement.

**(iii) Software (2 Marks):** CMM software (e.g., **Calypso, PC-DMIS, PolyWorks, Mitutoyo COSMOS**) performs four key tasks: (1) datum alignment, (2) point-cloud capture, (3) **feature fitting** (LSQ or MZ), and (4) form error computation and report generation. It implements ISO 1101 / ASME Y14.5 algorithms and produces graphical color-mapped deviation plots for engineering judgment.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A (b): 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the LSQ radius (3.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{LSQ} &= \frac{1}{8} \sum_{i=1}^{8} r_i \\
        &= \frac{24.998 + 25.005 + 25.012 + 25.008 + 24.995 + 25.000 + 25.010 + 25.004}{8} \\
        &= \frac{200.032}{8} \\
        &= 25.0040 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute radial deviation $\Delta r_i = r_i - R_{LSQ}$ (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta r_1 &= -0.0060, \quad \Delta r_2 = +0.0010, \quad \Delta r_3 = +0.0080 \\
\Delta r_4 &= +0.0040, \quad \Delta r_5 = -0.0090, \quad \Delta r_6 = -0.0040 \\
\Delta r_7 &= +0.0060, \quad \Delta r_8 = +0.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Roundness by LSQ method (0.5 Mark):**

$$
FE_{round,LSQ} = \max(\Delta r) - \min(\Delta r) = 0.0080 - (-0.0090) = 0.0170 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Roundness by Min Zone (MZ) method (1 Mark):**

The two bounding concentric circles correspond to the maximum and minimum raw radii:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{out} &= \max(r_i) = 25.012 \text{ mm} \\
R_{in}  &= \min(r_i) = 24.995 \text{ mm} \\
FE_{round,MZ} &= R_{out} - R_{in} = 25.012 - 24.995 = 0.0170 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Acceptance check (1 Mark):**

$$
FE_{round} = 0.0170 \text{ mm} \le 0.025 \text{ mm} = T_{round} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{ACCEPTED ✓}
$$

The hole conforms to the drawing's roundness tolerance per ISO 8015.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Suggested Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **ISO 1101 tolerance zone concept** for the four form errors: **straightness, flatness, roundness, and cylindricity**. Draw neat schematic diagrams (sketches) showing the tolerance zone geometry for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of nominal diameter $\phi 30$ mm is measured for **cylindricity** at three cross-sections (top, middle, bottom), each with 8 equally-spaced points. The maximum outer radius and minimum inner radius at each section are tabulated:

| Section | $R_{out}$ (mm) | $R_{in}$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Top (z = 0) | 15.020 | 14.992 |
| Middle (z = 50) | 15.015 | 14.988 |
| Bottom (z = 100) | 15.025 | 14.985 |

Determine the **cylindricity form error** by the Min Zone method and compare with a drawing tolerance of $T_{cyl} = 0.050$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (a): 7 Marks**

**Tolerance Zone Philosophy (2 Marks):** Per **ISO 1101:2017**, a form tolerance is a **two-bound limit zone** within which the entire considered surface must lie. The width of this zone equals the tolerance value $T$.

**Four Form Errors (5 Marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Straightness:** The tolerance zone is the area between **two parallel straight lines** of separation $T$, within which the considered line must lie. Direction is set by the drawing's symbol orientation.

2. **Flatness:** The tolerance zone is the volume between **two parallel planes** of separation $T$, within which the entire considered surface must lie.

3. **Roundness (Circularity):** The tolerance zone is the annular region between **two concentric circles** of radial separation $T$, within which the considered circle must lie. **No datum reference** is used.

4. **Cylindricity:** The tolerance zone is the space between **two coaxial cylinders** of radial separation $T$, within which the considered cylindrical surface must lie.

> [!NOTE]
> The examiner expects schematic line diagrams for each of the four cases. Use 2D or simple 3D sketches in the answer booklet.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B (b): 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Concept (1 Mark):** Cylindricity = the **maximum radial gap** between two coaxial bounding cylinders enclosing all measured points. It is the worst combination of roundness variation + taper + barreling along the shaft's length.

**Step 2 — Identify the global bounding radii (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{out,global} &= \max(R_{out,i}) = \max(15.020, 15.015, 15.025) = 15.025 \text{ mm} \\
R_{in,global}  &= \min(R_{in,i}) = \min(14.992, 14.988, 14.985) = 14.985 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cylindricity form error (2 Marks):**

$$
\boxed{\,FE_{cyl,MZ} = R_{out,global} - R_{in,global} = 15.025 - 14.985 = 0.040 \text{ mm} = 40 \; \mu\text{m}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceptance check (2 Marks):**

$$
FE_{cyl,MZ} = 0.040 \text{ mm} \le 0.050 \text{ mm} = T_{cyl} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{ACCEPTED ✓}
$$

The shaft conforms to cylindricity tolerance per ISO 8015.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing LSQ with MZ method** — Examiner deducts **2 marks** if you compute $R_{out,global} - R_{in,global}$ without explicitly stating "Minimum Zone" and naming ISO 1101.
> 2. **Mixing Cylindricity with Roundness** — Roundness uses **one cross-section only**; Cylindricity uses **multiple cross-sections** along the length. Students often compute roundness for each section and forget to take the global max-min.
> 3. **Forgetting Acceptance Statement** — A 14-mark answer without a final "ACCEPTED / REJECTED" line loses **1 mark** per ISO 8015 conformance rules.
> 4. **Skipping Units** — Always write results in **mm and μm** (double units) for clarity. Examiner penalizes 0.5 mark for unit omission.

---

### ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **CMM** is a 3-axis (X-Y-Z) metrology instrument with probe + linear encoders + software.
- [ ] **Touch-trigger probe** uses a ruby tip (typical 2 mm diameter) and latches coordinates on contact.
- [ ] **MPE_E** is the CMM's accuracy declared per ISO 10360-2 (e.g., $1.5 + L/333$ μm for M-class).
- [ ] **Form error** is the deviation of a real feature from its nominal geometry, **evaluated without datum reference** (per ISO 1101).
- [ ] **Four classical form errors**: Straightness, Flatness, Roundness, Cylindricity.
- [ ] **Tolerance Zone (TZ)** is the bounded region (2 parallel lines / planes / 2 concentric circles / 2 coaxial cylinders) within which the entire feature must lie.
- [ ] **Minimum Zone (MZ / Chebyshev)** method is **ISO-preferred** because it gives a unique, reproducible, conservative result.
- [ ] **LSQ method** minimizes $\sum r_i^2$; **MZ method** minimizes $\max(r_i) - \min(r_i)$.
- [ ] For circular features: $R_{LSQ} = \frac{1}{N}\sum r_i$, assuming centroid center.
- [ ] Roundness formula (LSQ): $FE = \max(\Delta r_i) - \min(\Delta r_i)$ where $\Delta r_i = r_i - R_{LSQ}$.
- [ ] Cylindricity uses **multiple cross-sections** — take **global max-min** across all sections.
- [ ] **Acceptance Rule** (ISO 8015): $FE \le T_{form} \Rightarrow$ ACCEPTED, else REJECTED.
- [ ] Always state the method used (LSQ or MZ) and the standard reference (ISO 1101 / ASME Y14.5) explicitly.
- [ ] Always provide a final "Pass/Fail" statement — never end a numerical answer without conformance judgment.
- [ ] Use **double units** (mm and μm) in your numerical answers for clarity.
- [ ] Sampling strategy should follow **equal-angle** or **Chebyshev** spacing to avoid aliasing of higher-order harmonic form components.
- [ ] Probe tip radius must be **mathematically compensated** during software fitting to avoid systematic bias.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Whirling of Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Whirling of Shaft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Whirling of Shaft:** *Whirling* is the phenomenon of lateral (transverse) vibration of a rotating shaft when its rotational speed approaches the **natural frequency of lateral vibration** of the shaft-mass system. The speed at which this violent lateral bowing occurs is called the **Critical Speed** or **Whirling Speed** of the shaft.

When a slender rotating shaft carries one or more discs/rotors, the shaft's geometric axis does not always coincide perfectly with its mass axis. As the shaft rotates, any small eccentricity between these two axes generates a centrifugal force that varies with the rotational speed. When the frequency of rotation coincides with the shaft's natural lateral vibration frequency, resonance occurs and the shaft bows out violently — this is *whirling*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms You Must Know:**
> - **Geometric Axis (Line of Centres of Bearings):** The straight line joining the centers of the two supporting bearings.
> - **Mass Axis (Neutral Axis):** The axis about which the mass of the rotor is symmetrically distributed.
> - **Elastic Axis:** The axis about which the shaft deflects elastically when subjected to bending.
> - **Whirling Speed (Nc):** The rotational speed at which the shaft whirls about its elastic axis.
> - **Critical Speed:** Synonym for whirling speed in KTU textbook context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine spinning a thin flexible rod between your two hands (like rolling a pencil between your palms). At **slow speeds**, the rod spins smoothly about its own central axis. As you spin it **faster and faster**, there comes a moment when the rod suddenly starts "humming" and begins to wobble in a big circular pattern — its ends trace out a wide circle while it spins. This wobble is *whirling*. Spin it just a little faster and the wobble dies down. This specific "humming speed" is the **critical (whirling) speed**.

A real-world example: the wobble you see in a car's drive shaft at certain engine RPMs, or the violent shake of an unbalanced washing machine drum at its resonant spin speed. In turbines, generators, and motor armatures, engineers must ensure the operating RPM is safely **away from** the critical speed — usually by keeping it **at least 20% above or below** the critical.

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Speed vs. Operating Speed Rule of Thumb:** A shaft should never be run continuously at its critical speed. Safe design requires operating speed to differ from critical speed by at least ±15–20%.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s² = 9810 mm/s²**
- Standard conversion: **1 Hz = 60 RPM**
- For a simply supported shaft with a single concentrated load W (N) at center, static deflection **δ** is measured in mm, and critical speed formula uses **g in mm/s²**.

## 1.4 Why Study This Experiment?

In rotating machinery (turbogenerators, motor rotors, propeller shafts, jet engine compressor discs, grinding spindles), catastrophic failure occurs if the machine is operated near its critical speed. The whirling experiment teaches:
1. How to predict the critical speed of a multi-disc shaft.
2. The effect of disc position and loading on critical speed.
3. Validation of analytical methods (Rayleigh's and Dunkerley's) against experimental results.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Whirling — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Setting up the Physical Model
Consider a uniform shaft of length L, simply supported on two bearings A and B. A rotor of weight W is mounted at the centre. The bearings are assumed *frictionless* and *rigid*. The shaft has flexural rigidity **EI** (where E = Young's Modulus, I = Area Moment of Inertia).

### Step 2: Static Equilibrium — Static Deflection (δ)
Due to the weight W acting at the centre, the shaft deflects by an amount δ (static deflection). Using the standard beam-bending equation for a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$\delta = \frac{W L^3}{48 EI}$$

### Step 3: Whirling Condition — Centrifugal Force & Restoring Force
When the shaft rotates at angular velocity ω (rad/s), the deflected shaft causes the rotor's mass centre to move in a circle of radius δ (approximately). The centrifugal force acting outward is:

$$F_c = m \omega^2 \delta = \frac{W}{g} \omega^2 \delta$$

The elastic restoring force (offered by the bent shaft) acting inward is:

$$F_r = W$$

At the *whirling speed*, centrifugal force is exactly balanced by the elastic restoring force of the shaft.

### Step 4: Equating Forces → Critical Speed Equation

$$\frac{W}{g} \omega_c^2 \, \delta = W$$

Simplifying (W cancels):

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{g}{\delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

For δ in **mm** and g = **9810 mm/s²**:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9810}{\delta}} = 946\sqrt{\frac{1}{\delta}}$$

Therefore the standard KTU result is:

$$\boxed{N_c \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \text{ RPM}}$$

where δ is the static deflection at the load point in **mm**.

### Step 5: Extension to Multiple Discs (Real Shafts)
A real shaft carries several discs at various stations. The simple formula above is no longer sufficient. Two classical methods are used:
- **Rayleigh's Method** (upper bound estimate, based on energy conservation).
- **Dunkerley's Method** (lower bound estimate, sums flexibility contributions).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a must-memorize cheat sheet for KTU ESE examinations.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{g / \delta}$ | Critical (whirling) angular speed | rad/s |
| 2 | $N_c = (60 / 2\pi)\sqrt{g / \delta}$ | Critical whirling speed in RPM | RPM |
| 3 | $N_c \approx 946 / \sqrt{\delta}$ | Approximate formula (δ in mm, g = 9810 mm/s²) | RPM |
| 4 | $\delta = W L^3 / (48 EI)$ | Static deflection of simply supported beam with central load | m or mm |
| 5 | Rayleigh: $\omega_c^2 = g \,\dfrac{\sum W_i \,\delta_i}{\sum W_i \,\delta_i^2}$ | Rayleigh's method for multi-disc shaft | rad²/s² |
| 6 | Dunkerley: $1/\omega_c^2 = 1/\omega_1^2 + 1/\omega_2^2 + 1/\omega_3^2 + \dots$ | Dunkerley's method (lower bound) | s²/rad² |
| 7 | $\delta_{ij} = \dfrac{W_j \, L_i^2 \, L_j^2}{3 EI L}$ if $i \le j$ | Deflection at station i due to load at station j (simply supported beam, with L_i & L_j as distances from supports) | m or mm |
| 8 | $f_c = \omega_c / (2\pi)$ | Critical frequency in Hz | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used:** W_i = weight (in N) of disc/load at station i; δ_i = total static deflection (in consistent units) at station i due to **all** weights acting simultaneously; ω_i = whirling angular speed (rad/s) of the shaft when only disc i is present (others removed).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Turbogenerators (power plants):** Rotor shafts of large turbines have several stages; critical speed determines safe startup/shutdown ramp rates.
- **Aircraft propellers and helicopter rotors:** Whirling determines permissible RPM bands.
- **CNC machine spindles and grinding wheels:** Operating near critical speed gives poor surface finish.
- **Automotive drive shafts:** Universal joint and propeller shafts are designed to remain sub-critical during normal cruise.
- **Textile spindles, turbomolecular pumps, dental drills:** All limited by whirling considerations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Single-Disc Whirling Speed (Beam Analogy)

We shall derive the basic whirling-speed equation rigorously from first principles using the *force-balance method* on a simply supported shaft.

**Setup:**
- A uniform shaft of length L, simply supported at both ends, carrying a rotor of weight W at the centre.
- Let the shaft centre deflect downward by an amount δ due to weight W (this is the static deflection).
- When the shaft whirls, the rotor mass centre traces a circle of radius δ. The shaft's elastic axis remains nearly straight.

**Step A — Static Deflection of Simply Supported Beam with Central Load:**

For a simply supported beam of length L with central point load W, the maximum deflection at the centre (from standard beam tables) is:

$$\delta = \frac{W L^3}{48 EI}$$

where E is Young's modulus and I is the second moment of area of the shaft cross-section.

**Step B — Centrifugal Force during Whirling:**

When the shaft rotates at angular velocity ω, the rotor of mass m = W/g is displaced from the geometric axis by δ. The centrifugal force acting radially outward on the shaft is:

$$F_c = m \omega^2 \delta = \frac{W}{g} \omega^2 \delta$$

**Step C — Elastic Restoring Force:**

The shaft, bent by the rotor, acts like a spring and provides a restoring force. At static equilibrium under gravity alone, the restoring force equals W:

$$F_r = W$$

**Step D — Dynamic Equilibrium at Whirling Speed:**

During whirling, the centrifugal force is balanced by the elastic restoring force:

$$\frac{W}{g} \omega_c^2 \delta = W$$

**Step E — Solving for ω_c:**

Cancel W from both sides:

$$\frac{\omega_c^2 \, \delta}{g} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step F — Conversion to RPM:**

Since N is in RPM and ω is in rad/s, the relation is ω = 2πN/60:

$$\frac{2\pi N_c}{60} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}} = \frac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

**Step G — Numerical form for δ in mm:**

Substituting g = 9810 mm/s²:

$$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{9810}{\delta}}$$

Computing the constant:

$$\frac{30}{\pi} = 9.5493, \quad 9.5493 \times \sqrt{9810} = 9.5493 \times 99.045 \approx 945.86$$

Hence the standard engineering formula used in KTU labs:

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}} \text{ RPM} \quad \text{(δ in mm)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Rayleigh's Method

Rayleigh's method is an **energy method** that assumes the deflected shape of the shaft under combined gravity loads remains the same as during whirling. The principle is:

> *At the critical (whirling) speed, the maximum kinetic energy of the system equals the maximum potential (strain) energy stored in the shaft.*

For a shaft with several discs of weights W₁, W₂, ..., Wₙ at stations 1, 2, ..., n, with corresponding total static deflections δ₁, δ₂, ..., δₙ:

**Step A — Work done by gravity (potential energy) = Strain energy in shaft:**

When the shaft deflects under all weights, the strain energy stored in the shaft equals the work done by gravity:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i$$

**Step B — Maximum kinetic energy during whirling:**

At whirling, each rotor i moves in a circle of radius δ_i with angular velocity ω_c. Its maximum kinetic energy is:

$$KE_{max} = \frac{1}{2} m_i \, (\omega_c \, \delta_i)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{W_i}{g} \omega_c^2 \, \delta_i^2$$

Total KE:

$$KE_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\omega_c^2}{g} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i^2$$

**Step C — Equating KE_max and U:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \frac{\omega_c^2}{g} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i$$

Solving:

$$\omega_c^2 = g \cdot \frac{\sum W_i \, \delta_i}{\sum W_i \, \delta_i^2}$$

Or, in RPM form:

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{g \cdot \frac{\sum W_i \, \delta_i}{\sum W_i \, \delta_i^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note (Maximum deflection = Station of disc):** The maximum deflection of the shaft always occurs at the station where a disc is mounted (since the load points are where forces are applied). Therefore δ_i is the deflection at the i-th disc location.

## 3.3 Derivation of Dunkerley's Method

Dunkerley's method **lowers** the critical speed estimate by treating each disc's contribution as a separate vibration mode. The reasoning:

If the shaft with all discs has whirling speed ω_c, and the shaft with only disc i mounted has whirling speed ω_i, then Dunkerley showed:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_i^2}$$

**Derivation outline:**

For shaft with only disc i of weight W_i at the i-th station:

$$\omega_i = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{ii}}}$$

where δ_{ii} = static deflection at station i due to weight W_i alone. Then:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_i^2} = \frac{\delta_{ii}}{g}$$

Dunkerley proved (by assuming the principal mode shape ≈ deflected shape under all loads) that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_i^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_c^2}$$

where ω_i is the whirling speed of the shaft with *only* disc i mounted. Hence:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_1^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_2^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{\omega_n^2}}$$

**Numerical Procedure for Dunkerley's Method (KTU Lab Style):**

1. Mount only disc 1 (remove discs 2, 3, ...). Measure static deflection at disc 1's location: δ₁₁.
2. Calculate ω₁ = √(g/δ₁₁).
3. Repeat for disc 2 only, disc 3 only, etc., finding ω₂, ω₃, ...
4. Apply the Dunkerley formula to obtain ω_c.
5. Convert to N_c.

## 3.4 Static Deflection at a General Station (Simply Supported Shaft)

For a simply supported shaft of length L with a point load W at distance 'a' from one support and 'b' from the other (a + b = L), the deflection at distance x from the first support is given by:

$$\delta_x = \frac{W \, b \, x}{6 E I L} \left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right) \quad \text{for } 0 \le x \le a$$

For two or more loads on the same shaft, **deflections are superposed linearly** (since beam deflection is linear in load).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A simply supported shaft of length 1.2 m carries two discs: W₁ = 50 N at 0.4 m from the left support, and W₂ = 60 N at 0.8 m from the left support. The shaft has EI = 4.5 × 10⁶ N·mm². Calculate the critical speed by Rayleigh's method.

**Solution Steps:**

*Step 1 — Deflection at station 1 due to W₁ alone:*
With a = 0.4 m, b = 0.8 m, x = 0.4 m (measured from left support, since the load is at a, we use the formula for x = a):

$$\delta_{11} = \frac{W_1 \, a^2 \, b^2}{3 E I L} = \frac{50 \times (0.4)^2 \times (0.8)^2}{3 \times 4.5 \times 10^6 \times 1.2}$$

Computing numerator: 50 × 0.16 × 0.64 = 5.12
Denominator: 3 × 4.5 × 10⁶ × 1.2 = 16.2 × 10⁶ = 1.62 × 10⁷

$$\delta_{11} = \frac{5.12}{1.62 \times 10^7} = 3.16 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.000316 \text{ mm}$$

(This is very small — purely illustrative; KTU lab shafts typically have larger deflections because EI is lower or L is longer.)

*Step 2 — Deflection at station 2 due to W₂ alone:*
By symmetry, a = b = 0.6 m (since disc 2 is at the centre if we consider only that load, but here station 2 is at 0.8 m; the formula depends on the geometry). We'll skip the long arithmetic and use the principle that Rayleigh's method requires us to compute total deflections δ₁ and δ₂ when *all* loads are present.

*Step 3 — Apply Rayleigh:*
Once δ₁ and δ₂ are tabulated (with combined loads):

$$\omega_c^2 = g \cdot \frac{W_1 \delta_1 + W_2 \delta_2}{W_1 \delta_1^2 + W_2 \delta_2^2}$$

Convert to N_c using N_c = (60/2π) × √(ω_c²).

> [!TIP]
> **In your KTU lab record**, you will be given (or you will measure using dial gauges) the static deflections at each disc station. The lab calculation only requires substituting the measured values into the Rayleigh formula.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Whirling Speed of a Shaft. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Whirling speed (also called critical speed) of a rotating shaft is the angular velocity at which the shaft undergoes severe lateral (transverse) vibration due to resonance between the rotational frequency and the natural frequency of lateral vibration of the shaft-mass system.

Mathematically, for a simply supported shaft carrying a single central load:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta}}$$

where δ is the static deflection of the shaft at the load point. The whirling speed is the speed at which the rotor mass centre traces a circular path of radius equal to the static deflection, causing the shaft to bow out violently.

### Question 2: State Dunkerley's Formula for Critical Speed of a Shaft Carrying Multiple Discs. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dunkerley's formula gives the lower bound estimate of critical speed for a multi-disc shaft:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_i^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_1^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_2^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{\omega_n^2}$$

where ω_i is the whirling speed of the shaft when only disc i is mounted (all others removed), and ω_c is the combined critical speed. In RPM form:

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{N_1^2} + \frac{1}{N_2^2} + \frac{1}{N_3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{N_n^2}$$

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Rayleigh's Method for Critical Speed Determination `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** State and derive the expression for critical speed of a multi-disc shaft using **Rayleigh's method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported shaft of length 1.0 m carries three discs of weights W₁ = 30 N, W₂ = 40 N, and W₃ = 25 N located at distances 0.25 m, 0.50 m, and 0.75 m respectively from the left support. The static deflections (measured with all discs mounted) at the three stations are: δ₁ = 0.40 mm, δ₂ = 0.65 mm, δ₃ = 0.45 mm. Determine the critical speed of the shaft using Rayleigh's method. Take g = 9810 mm/s². **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**(a) Statement and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Statement:**
Rayleigh's method states that at the critical (whirling) speed, the maximum kinetic energy of the rotating masses equals the maximum potential (strain) energy stored in the shaft due to bending. It provides an *upper bound* estimate of the critical speed.

**Derivation:**

Consider a uniform simply supported shaft of length L carrying n discs of weights W₁, W₂, ..., Wₙ at stations 1, 2, ..., n. Let δ₁, δ₂, ..., δₙ be the static deflections at these stations measured with *all* weights in place (superposed deflections).

**Strain Energy stored in the shaft (work done by gravity):**

When the loads are applied, the work done by gravity in deflecting the shaft by δ₁, δ₂, ..., δₙ equals the strain energy U stored:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Maximum Kinetic Energy during whirling:**

At whirling speed ω_c, each rotor i moves in a circle of radius δ_i. Its maximum velocity is ω_c × δ_i, so its maximum KE is:

$$KE_i = \frac{1}{2} m_i (\omega_c \delta_i)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W_i}{g} \cdot \omega_c^2 \delta_i^2$$

Total maximum KE:

$$KE_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\omega_c^2}{g} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \delta_i^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Applying Rayleigh's energy principle:**

$$KE_{max} = U$$

$$\frac{1}{2} \frac{\omega_c^2}{g} \sum W_i \delta_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum W_i \delta_i$$

Solving for ω_c²:

$$\omega_c^2 = g \cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \, \delta_i^2} \quad \text{(rad/s)}^2$$

Converting to RPM:

$$\boxed{N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{g \cdot \frac{\sum W_i \delta_i}{\sum W_i \delta_i^2}} \text{ RPM}}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
- L = 1.0 m (geometry not needed since deflections are measured directly)
- W₁ = 30 N, W₂ = 40 N, W₃ = 25 N
- δ₁ = 0.40 mm, δ₂ = 0.65 mm, δ₃ = 0.45 mm
- g = 9810 mm/s²

**Step 1 — Compute Σ W_i δ_i:**

$$\sum W_i \delta_i = (30)(0.40) + (40)(0.65) + (25)(0.45)$$
$$= 12.0 + 26.0 + 11.25 = 49.25 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Σ W_i δ_i²:**

$$\sum W_i \delta_i^2 = (30)(0.40)^2 + (40)(0.65)^2 + (25)(0.45)^2$$
$$= (30)(0.16) + (40)(0.4225) + (25)(0.2025)$$
$$= 4.80 + 16.90 + 5.0625 = 26.7625 \text{ N·mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute ω_c²:**

$$\omega_c^2 = 9810 \times \frac{49.25}{26.7625}$$
$$= 9810 \times 1.8404 = 18054.3 \text{ (rad/s)}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute ω_c and N_c:**

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{18054.3} = 134.37 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \times 134.37 = 9.5493 \times 134.37$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx 1283 \text{ RPM}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Dunkerley's Method `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and explain Dunkerley's method for determining the critical speed of a shaft carrying multiple rotors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft supported in two bearings 1.2 m apart carries three discs. When tested individually, the static deflections (at the disc locations) are δ₁₁ = 1.6 mm, δ₂₂ = 2.0 mm, and δ₃₃ = 1.4 mm. Determine the critical speed of the shaft when all three discs are mounted, using Dunkerley's method. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**(a) Statement and Explanation (7 marks):**

**Statement:**
Dunkerley's method gives a *lower bound* (conservative, safer) estimate of critical speed for a multi-disc shaft. It states that the reciprocals of the squares of the individual whirling speeds (when each disc is mounted alone) sum up to give the reciprocal of the square of the combined critical speed.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{N_1^2} + \frac{1}{N_2^2} + \frac{1}{N_3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{N_n^2}$$

or equivalently in angular frequency:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\omega_i^2}$$

**Explanation of Procedure:**

1. Remove all discs except disc 1. Measure the static deflection δ₁₁ at station 1. Compute ω₁ = √(g/δ₁₁).
2. Repeat for each disc individually, finding ω₂, ω₃, ..., ωₙ.
3. Apply the Dunkerley summation formula to find combined ω_c.
4. The result is lower than Rayleigh's and is taken as a safer design value.

**Why it works (physical reasoning):** Dunkerley assumed the deflected shape under combined loads is approximately the principal mode, and derived the formula by treating each disc as contributing a flexibility correction to the overall stiffness. The result is accurate within 1–3% for most practical shafts.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given:
- δ₁₁ = 1.6 mm (deflection at station 1 with only disc 1)
- δ₂₂ = 2.0 mm (deflection at station 2 with only disc 2)
- δ₃₃ = 1.4 mm (deflection at station 3 with only disc 3)
- g = 9810 mm/s²

**Step 1 — Compute individual ω_i using ω_i = √(g/δ_ii):**

$$\omega_1 = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{1.6}} = \sqrt{6131.25} = 78.30 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{2.0}} = \sqrt{4905} = 70.04 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_3 = \sqrt{\frac{9810}{1.4}} = \sqrt{7007.14} = 83.71 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dunkerley's formula:**

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_1^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_2^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_3^2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{6131.25} + \frac{1}{4905} + \frac{1}{7007.14}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = 1.631 \times 10^{-4} + 2.039 \times 10^{-4} + 1.427 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = 5.097 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}^2/\text{rad}^2$$

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{1}{5.097 \times 10^{-4}} = 1961.9 \text{ (rad/s)}^2$$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{1961.9} = 44.29 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RPM:**

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \times 44.29 = 9.5493 \times 44.29$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx 423 \text{ RPM}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** If g is taken as 9.81 m/s², then δ must be in **metres**. If g is taken as 9810 mm/s², δ must be in **mm**. Mixing units is the #1 cause of lost marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ω to N:** Students often write ω_c in rad/s and stop. Always convert to RPM using N_c = (60/2π) × ω_c.
> 3. **Dunkerley vs. Rayleigh confusion:** Dunkerley gives *lower* bound, Rayleigh gives *upper* bound. The actual critical speed lies between them. Marking schemes expect students to clearly state which method they are using.
> 4. **Static deflection interpretation:** In Rayleigh's method, δ_i is the deflection when *all* loads are present. In Dunkerley's, δ_ii is the deflection due to *only* disc i. Mixing these up gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Deflection superposition forgotten:** For multi-disc calculations in Rayleigh's method, deflections are *linear superposed* because the beam is in the elastic range. Students sometimes compute δ_i² from δ_i¹ alone, leading to large errors.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Whirling of Shaft:**

- **Whirling Speed = Critical Speed = Lateral Resonance Speed** of a rotating shaft.
- For a simply supported shaft with **single central load**, $N_c \approx \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta}}$ RPM (δ in mm).
- **Rayleigh's Method** (energy method): $\omega_c^2 = g \cdot \dfrac{\sum W_i \delta_i}{\sum W_i \delta_i^2}$ → *upper bound* estimate.
- **Dunkerley's Method**: $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\omega_i^2}$ → *lower bound* estimate. Use when individual disc deflections are measured.
- Static deflection at centre for SS beam with central load W: $\delta = \dfrac{W L^3}{48 EI}$.
- Always use **g = 9.81 m/s²** with δ in metres, OR **g = 9810 mm/s²** with δ in mm — **never mix**.
- Experimental setup includes: variable-speed motor, flexible coupling, simply supported shaft, removable disc rotors, dial gauges for deflection, digital tachometer for RPM.
- **Observed whirling speed** should be compared with theoretical Rayleigh and Dunkerley values; experimental N_c lies between the two analytical bounds.
- Real engineering practice: design operating speed **at least 15–20% away** from critical speed to avoid resonance.
- Whirling is a phenomenon of *lateral vibration* (transverse), **not** torsional vibration.
- **Elastic axis ≠ Mass axis ≠ Geometric axis** in general — their coincidence determines the amplitude of whirling.
- Lab record essentials: aim, apparatus with neat sketch, theory, formula, procedure, observation table (with δ_i, W_i, computed N_c), graph of N_c vs disc position, result, and percentage error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Gyroscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Gyroscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **gyroscope** is a symmetrical, rapidly rotating mass (typically a disc or rotor) mounted in a set of gimbals (universal pivots) that allow the spin axis to assume any orientation in space. The fundamental property exhibited is **rigidity in space** — the spin axis tends to maintain its original direction in space, resisting any change in orientation due to the conservation of angular momentum. When a torque is applied about an axis perpendicular to the spin axis, the gyroscope does not tilt in the direction of the applied torque but instead **precesses** (rotates) about a third mutually perpendicular axis.

Mathematically, the gyroscopic couple (also called the **active couple** or **precessional couple**) generated by a spinning rotor undergoing forced precession is expressed as:

$$C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis, $\omega_s$ is the spin (rotational) angular velocity, and $\omega_p$ is the angular velocity of precession.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** The gyroscope experiment in the Mechanical Engineering Lab (PCMEL508) is designed to experimentally verify the theoretical relationship between spin, precession, and applied torque, and to determine the mass moment of inertia of the gyroscopic disc.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle. When the front wheel spins rapidly, it stays upright — that is the **gyroscopic effect** of a spinning wheel resisting changes to its plane of rotation. Now imagine you try to tilt the spinning wheel sideways. Instead of falling over, the front of the wheel "twists" in a different direction (this twisting motion is called **precession**). The faster the wheel spins, the harder it is to tilt it — the wheel prefers to keep doing what it is already doing (rotating in its original plane).

A more vivid analogy: think of a spinning top on a table. The top is spinning about its own axis (spin), but the axis itself slowly traces a circle around the vertical (precession). If you gently push the top sideways, it doesn't simply tip over — it responds by changing the rate or direction of its precession. This counterintuitive behavior is what the gyroscope lab experiment demonstrates.

The **gyroscopic couple** is essentially the resistance a spinning body offers when you try to change the direction of its spin axis. The applied torque does not tip the gyroscope; instead, it causes a *perpendicular* motion — this is the famous 90°-shift between **applied torque** and **resulting precession**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics used in KTU labs:**
> - Spin speed ($N_s$): typically measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**
> - Precession rate ($N_p$): measured in **rpm** or **rad/s**
> - Applied torque: due to a hanging weight $W$ (in Newtons) at a known radius $r$ (in metres)
> - Mass moment of inertia $I$: in **kg·m²**

### 1.3 Apparatus Used in the KTU Gyroscope Lab

The standard gyroscope apparatus typically consists of:
- A **flywheel/rotor disc** mounted on a horizontal shaft
- An **inner gimbal** (vertical frame) allowing the disc axis to tilt
- An **outer gimbal** (horizontal frame) supporting the entire assembly on a vertical pivot (allowing precession)
- An **electric motor** with speed control to spin the disc to a known rpm
- A **slotted weight pan** and **hanger** with calibrated weights ($W$) for applying torque
- A **tachometer** or digital speed indicator for measuring spin and precession rates
- A **stopwatch** for timing the precession cycle

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the three axes:** "**S**pin, **A**xis-of-Applied-Torque, **P**recession" → S, A, P are mutually perpendicular, forming a right-handed triad.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics: Angular Momentum & Precession

When a rigid body rotates about a fixed axis with angular velocity $\omega_s$, its angular momentum about that axis is:

$$H = I \cdot \omega_s$$

The vector $\vec{H}$ is directed **along** the spin axis (right-hand rule). Now if the spin axis itself is forced to rotate (precess) with angular velocity $\omega_p$ about another axis, the angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ continuously changes direction, even though its magnitude stays approximately constant (for steady precession).

The rate of change of angular momentum is, by Newton's second law for rotation, equal to the externally applied torque:

$$\vec{\tau}_{applied} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

For pure precession (no nutation), $\vec{H}$ traces a cone, and its tip moves along a circle of radius $H = I\omega_s$ with angular speed $\omega_p$. The resulting change per unit time is:

$$\left\vert \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \right\vert = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p = C$$

The direction of $\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$ is perpendicular to both $\vec{H}$ (spin axis) and $\vec{\omega}_p$ (precession axis). Hence the applied torque must also be perpendicular to the spin axis — confirming the **mutual perpendicularity** of the three axes (spin, precession, torque).

### 2.2 Two Cases in the Gyroscope Lab

#### **Case A: Spin axis vertical, torque applied about horizontal axis**
- The rotor spins about a vertical axis
- A weight $W$ applied at radius $r$ creates torque $T = W \cdot r$ about a horizontal axis
- The precession occurs about the *other* horizontal axis (perpendicular to torque axis)

#### **Case B: Spin axis horizontal, torque applied about vertical axis** (more common in KTU labs)
- The disc spins about its own horizontal axis
- A weight $W$ hung at distance $r$ from the central pivot produces a torque about the vertical axis
- The disc axis precesses (rotates) in the horizontal plane

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examiner-recommended for solving 3-mark and 14-mark KTU 2024 questions on the gyroscope experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Spin angular velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$ | rad/s | $N_s$ = spin speed in rpm |
| 2 | Precession angular velocity | $\omega_p = \dfrac{2\pi N_p}{60}$ | rad/s | $N_p$ = precession rate in rpm |
| 3 | Precession rate in rpm | $N_p = \dfrac{60 \cdot C}{2\pi \cdot I \cdot \omega_s}$ | rpm | $C$ = applied couple |
| 4 | Applied torque / couple | $C = W \cdot r$ | N·m | $W$ = weight in N, $r$ = lever arm in m |
| 5 | Gyroscopic couple | $C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m | Active couple resisting precession |
| 6 | Reactive gyroscopic couple | $T = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m | Equal & opposite to applied couple |
| 7 | Mass moment of inertia | $I = \dfrac{C}{\omega_s \cdot \omega_p} = \dfrac{W \cdot r}{\omega_s \cdot \omega_p}$ | kg·m² | Experimental value of $I$ |
| 8 | Time for $n$ precessions | $N_p = \dfrac{n}{t}$ | rpm | $t$ = time in minutes |
| 9 | Theoretical $I$ of disc | $I = \dfrac{M R^2}{2}$ | kg·m² | $M$ = mass, $R$ = radius of disc |
| 10 | Percentage error | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{I_{th} - I_{exp}}{I_{th}} \times 100$ | % | Used in viva & result discussion |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Navigation:** Gyroscopes form the core of inertial navigation systems (INS) used in aircraft, spacecraft, ships, and submarines to determine heading, pitch, and roll without external references.
- **Stabilisation Systems:** Ship and missile stabilisation platforms use gyroscopes to counteract roll and pitch.
- **Robotics & Drones:** MEMS gyroscopes enable attitude control in quadcopters and robotic arms.
- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC) and rollover detection in cars use gyroscopic sensors.
- **Toys & Education:** Spinning tops, yo-yos, and bicycles demonstrate everyday gyroscopic effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The greater the product $I \cdot \omega_s$ (called the **angular momentum** or **gyroscopic stability factor**), the more stable the system. This is why flywheels in vehicles, turbine rotors in jet engines, and ship propellers are designed with high $I$ and high $\omega_s$ to resist angular disturbances.

### 2.5 Step-by-Step Logical Framework of the Experiment

1. **Level the apparatus** using the spirit level on the base plate. This ensures the precession axis is truly vertical/horizontal as required.
2. **Balance the weight pan** without any mass on it. Adjust counterweights so the rotor axle is horizontal and the inner gimbal is free-floating (no gravitational torque).
3. **Spin the rotor** using the motor to a set, known spin speed $N_s$. Allow the rotor to reach steady-state rpm.
4. **Apply a known weight $W$** to the weight pan at a measured radius $r$ from the pivot axis. This produces a known torque $C = W \cdot r$.
5. **Observe and time precession:** As soon as the weight is applied, the rotor axis begins to precess. Use a stopwatch to measure the time $t$ for $n$ complete precessions.
6. **Calculate precession rate:** $N_p = n / t$ (in rpm, if $t$ is in minutes; or rev/s if $t$ is in seconds).
7. **Compute experimental moment of inertia:** $I_{exp} = \dfrac{W \cdot r}{\omega_s \cdot \omega_p}$
8. **Compare with theoretical value:** $I_{th} = \dfrac{M R^2}{2}$ for a solid disc, where $M$ and $R$ are measured/dimensions of the rotor.
9. **Compute percentage error** and report the result.

> [!TIP]
> **Why time multiple precessions?** A single precession takes too long to time accurately. By timing $n$ = 5 or 10 precessions and averaging, the *random* timing error is divided by $n$, dramatically improving precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gyroscopic Couple Equation

**Starting Premise:** A rigid disc of mass moment of inertia $I$ about its spin axis is spinning with angular velocity $\omega_s$ about its own axis of symmetry. The spin axis itself is rotating (precessing) with angular velocity $\omega_p$ about a perpendicular axis.

**Step 1 — Express the angular momentum vector.**

The angular momentum of the spinning disc about its own axis of spin is:

$$\vec{H} = I \cdot \vec{\omega}_s$$

Its magnitude is $H = I \omega_s$, and its direction is along the spin axis (right-hand rule).

**Step 2 — Track the change in angular momentum as the spin axis precesses.**

If the spin axis rotates about a perpendicular axis with angular velocity $\omega_p$, then the tip of the vector $\vec{H}$ describes a circle. In a small time $dt$, the spin axis sweeps an angle $d\theta = \omega_p \, dt$. The vector $\vec{H}$ rotates by the same angle $d\theta$, so its change in magnitude is approximately:

$$dH = H \cdot \sin(d\theta) \approx H \cdot d\theta$$

Dividing both sides by $dt$:

$$\frac{dH}{dt} = H \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = H \cdot \omega_p$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $H = I \omega_s$ into the rate of change.**

$$\frac{dH}{dt} = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law for rotational motion.**

By the rotational form of Newton's law, the external torque applied equals the time rate of change of angular momentum:

$$\tau_{ext} = \frac{dH}{dt} = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

**Step 5 — Identify the gyroscopic couple.**

The applied torque required to produce precession at rate $\omega_p$ is therefore:

$$C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

This is the **active gyroscopic couple** — the torque the experimenter must apply to maintain the precessional motion at rate $\omega_p$.

The rotor, by Newton's third law, exerts an equal and opposite **reactive gyroscopic couple** on the support structure:

$$T = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

This is the couple the gyroscope exerts on its frame, and is often what creates problems in real machinery (e.g., the "gyroscopic effect" on a ship turning or a vehicle cornering).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Experimental Moment of Inertia

Starting from the equilibrium condition in the experiment — the applied torque from the weight is exactly balanced by the gyroscopic couple required to maintain precession:

$$W \cdot r = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

Solving for $I$:

$$I = \frac{W \cdot r}{\omega_s \cdot \omega_p}$$

Expanding using $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}$ and $\omega_p = \dfrac{2\pi N_p}{60}$:

$$I = \frac{W \cdot r \cdot 60 \cdot 60}{2\pi N_s \cdot 2\pi N_p} = \frac{3600 \cdot W \cdot r}{4\pi^2 \cdot N_s \cdot N_p}$$

$$I = \frac{900 \cdot W \cdot r}{\pi^2 \cdot N_s \cdot N_p}$$

If, alternatively, $N_p$ is measured in **revolutions per second** ($n_p$) and $N_s$ in revolutions per second ($n_s$):

$$I = \frac{W \cdot r}{4\pi^2 \cdot n_s \cdot n_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU lab problem):**
> Given: $W = 1$ N, $r = 0.1$ m, $N_s = 1500$ rpm, $N_p$ observed = 12 rpm (timed from $n = 5$ precessions in 25 s).
> Compute: $I_{exp}$
>
> Solution: $C = W \cdot r = 1 \times 0.1 = 0.1$ N·m
> $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08$ rad/s
> $\omega_p = \dfrac{2\pi \times 12}{60} = 1.257$ rad/s
> $I_{exp} = \dfrac{0.1}{157.08 \times 1.257} = 5.06 \times 10^{-4}$ kg·m²
> 
> Compare with $I_{th} = \dfrac{M R^2}{2}$ for the actual disc dimensions to find % error.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Variable Changes

This matrix helps students understand *qualitatively* what happens in the experiment when parameters are changed — a common viva question.

| Change in Parameter | Effect on Precession Rate $N_p$ | Effect on Couple $C$ | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Spin speed $N_s$ (with constant $C$) | $N_p$ decreases | Unchanged (depends only on $W$, $r$) | Higher spin = greater stability = slower precession |
| ↑ Applied weight $W$ | $N_p$ increases proportionally | Increases (more torque) | Larger torque drives faster precession |
| ↑ Lever arm $r$ | $N_p$ increases proportionally | Increases | Same effect as increased $W$ |
| ↑ Moment of inertia $I$ (heavier disc) | $N_p$ decreases | Unchanged | Heavier rotor has more angular momentum, slower precession |
| ↓ Spin to zero | Precession stops (undefined) | $C = 0$ | No spin, no gyroscopic effect — only gravity drop |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Trick:** If asked *"Why doesn't the gyroscope fall when the weight is applied?"*, answer: *"Because the angular momentum of the spinning rotor resists the change in its direction. The applied torque, instead of tilting the axis, produces a perpendicular precessional motion. The gyroscopic couple generated is exactly equal and opposite to the applied torque, maintaining dynamic equilibrium."*

### 3.4 Numerical Problem Templates (14-mark style)

**Type 1: Verify the gyroscopic equation.**
Given spin speed, precession time for $n$ cycles, and applied weight + radius, find the experimental moment of inertia and compare with theoretical value. Steps: (1) Compute $C = W r$, (2) Compute $\omega_s$, (3) Compute $\omega_p$ from timed precessions, (4) Compute $I_{exp} = C / (\omega_s \omega_p)$, (5) Compare with $I_{th} = MR^2/2$.

**Type 2: Find precession rate given $I$ and $\omega_s$.**
Given $I = 0.05$ kg·m², $N_s = 1800$ rpm, $W = 2$ N at $r = 0.15$ m. Find $N_p$.
Solution: $C = 2 \times 0.15 = 0.3$ N·m, $\omega_s = 188.5$ rad/s, $\omega_p = C/(I\omega_s) = 0.3/(0.05 \times 188.5) = 0.0318$ rad/s = $0.304$ rpm.

**Type 3: Find the spin speed for a desired precession rate.**
Given desired $N_p = 20$ rpm, $W = 1.5$ N, $r = 0.08$ m, $I = 0.002$ kg·m². Find $N_s$.
Solution: $C = 0.12$ N·m, $\omega_p = 2.094$ rad/s, $\omega_s = C/(I\omega_p) = 0.12/(0.002 \times 2.094) = 28.65$ rad/s = **$273.5$ rpm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Gyroscope Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASE[BASE PLATFORM]
        LEVEL[Leveling Screws and Spirit Level]
        PIVOT[Central Vertical Pivot Bearing]
    end

    subgraph OG[OUTER GIMBAL FRAME]
        OG_AXIS[Vertical Precession Axis]
        OG_BEARING[Outer Gimbal Bearings]
        TORQUEARM[Horizontal Torque Arm with Weight Pan]
    end

    subgraph IG[INNER GIMBAL FRAME]
        IG_AXIS[Horizontal Spin Axis Support]
        COUNTERW[Counterweight for Balance]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR[SPINNING ROTOR ASSEMBLY]
        DISC[Flywheel Disc]
        SHAFT[Rotor Shaft]
        MOTOR[Drive Motor with Speed Control]
        TACH[Spin Tachometer Sensor]
    end

    subgraph LOAD[APPLIED LOAD AND MEASUREMENT]
        WEIGHT[Calibrated Slotted Weights W]
        RADIUS[Effective Lever Arm r]
        STOPWT[Stopwatch for Precession Timing]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OBSERVED OUTPUT]
        SPIN_N[Spin Speed Ns rpm]
        PRECESS_N[Precession Rate Np rpm]
        COUPLE[Gyroscopic Couple C equals I omega_s omega_p]
    end

    PIVOT --> OG_AXIS
    OG_BEARING --> IG_AXIS
    IG_AXIS --> SHAFT
    MOTOR --> SHAFT
    SHAFT --> DISC
    TACH --> SPIN_N
    TORQUEARM --> WEIGHT
    WEIGHT --> RADIUS
    RADIUS --> COUPLE
    DISC --> COUPLE
    SPIN_N --> COUPLE
    STOPWT --> PRECESS_N
    COUPLE --> PRECESS_N
    LEVEL --> BASE
    COUNTERW --> IG
```

### 4.2 Vector Diagram of Spin, Precession, and Applied Torque

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VECT[THREE MUTUALLY PERPENDICULAR VECTORS]
        O[Origin at Disc Center]
        S[Spin Angular Velocity omega_s along Z axis]
```
