```mermaid
        P[Precession Angular Velocity omega_p along X axis]
        T[Applied Torque Tau along Y axis]
    end

    O --> S
    O --> P
    O --> T
    S -.->|90 degrees| P
    P -.->|90 degrees| T
    T -.->|90 degrees| S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The three vectors — spin $\omega_s$, precession $\omega_p$, and applied torque $\tau$ — are mutually perpendicular. The relationship $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ tells you the *magnitude* of the couple needed to maintain the precession at rate $\omega_p$.

### 4.3 Block Diagram of the Experimental Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Step 1: Level the apparatus using spirit level] --> B[Step 2: Balance the rotor with counterweights to remove gravitational torque]
    B --> C[Step 3: Spin rotor to set speed Ns using motor speed control]
    C --> D[Step 4: Record spin speed Ns from tachometer]
    D --> E[Step 5: Apply known weight W at measured radius r on torque arm]
    E --> F[Step 6: Use stopwatch to time n precessions over t seconds]
    F --> G[Step 7: Calculate Np equals n divided by t times 60 rpm]
    G --> H[Step 8: Compute applied couple C equals W times r]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute I experimental equals C divided by omega_s times omega_p]
    I --> J[Step 10: Compute I theoretical equals M R squared divided by 2 for solid disc]
    J --> K[Step 11: Calculate percentage error and tabulate observations]
    K --> L[Step 12: Plot graph of Np vs W if required and report result]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation of Gyroscopic Stability Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        I1[Moment of Inertia I]
        OMG[Spin Angular Velocity omega_s]
        T1[Applied Torque T]
    end

    subgraph CORE[GYROSCOPIC DYNAMICS CORE]
        AM[Angular Momentum H equals I times omega_s]
        PR[Precession omega_p equals T divided by H]
        RC[Reactive Couple equals H times omega_p equals I omega_s omega_p]
    end

    subgraph OUTC[ENGINEERING OUTCOMES]
        S1[Aircraft and Ship Stabilisation]
        S2[Inertial Navigation and Guidance]
        S3[Vehicle Dynamics and Rollover Prevention]
        S4[Robotics and Drone Attitude Control]
    end

    I1 --> AM
    OMG --> AM
    T1 --> PR
    AM --> PR
    AM --> RC
    PR --> RC
    RC --> S1
    RC --> S2
    RC --> S3
    RC --> S4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a gyroscope. What is meant by precession?**

**Model Answer:**
A gyroscope is a spinning rotor mounted on gimbals that allows the spin axis to orient freely in any direction. The rotor spins at a high angular velocity $\omega_s$, and the spin axis tends to maintain its orientation in space due to the conservation of angular momentum. When an external torque is applied perpendicular to the spin axis, the gyroscope responds by **precessing** — that is, the spin axis itself rotates slowly about a third mutually perpendicular axis at angular velocity $\omega_p$, instead of tilting in the direction of the applied torque.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the equation for the gyroscopic couple and define each term with units.**

**Model Answer:**
The gyroscopic couple is given by:

$$C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$$

- $C$ = gyroscopic couple, in **N·m**
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis, in **kg·m²**
- $\omega_s$ = spin (rotational) angular velocity of the rotor, in **rad/s**
- $\omega_p$ = angular velocity of precession, in **rad/s**

The product $I \omega_s$ is the angular momentum $H$ of the rotor, so the equation may also be written as $C = H \cdot \omega_p$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a laboratory gyroscope apparatus. List the observations and formulae used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The laboratory gyroscope consists of:
1. **A heavy flywheel/rotor (1)** — the spinning mass, mounted on a horizontal shaft (2).
2. **Inner gimbal (3)** — supports the rotor shaft and allows the spin axis to tilt. It has a counterweight (4) to balance the rotor weight and prevent gravitational tipping.
3. **Outer gimbal (5)** — mounted on a vertical pivot (6) on the base. It supports the inner gimbal and allows the entire assembly to precess.
4. **Torque arm (7)** — extends horizontally from the outer gimbal and carries a weight pan (8) at a known radius $r$ from the vertical pivot.
5. **Electric motor (9)** with speed controller — spins the rotor.
6. **Tachometer (10)** — measures the spin speed $N_s$.
7. **Spirit level and leveling screws (11)** — level the apparatus.

**Working Principle:**
The motor spins the rotor to a set angular velocity $\omega_s$. A weight $W$ is placed on the weight pan at radius $r$, applying a torque $C = W r$ about the vertical axis. By the gyroscopic principle, the spin axis does not tip vertically; instead, the entire assembly precesses about the vertical axis with angular velocity $\omega_p$. The precession rate is measured using a stopwatch to time $n$ revolutions.

**Observations Recorded:**
- Spin speed $N_s$ (rpm) — from tachometer
- Applied weight $W$ (N)
- Lever arm $r$ (m)
- Number of precessions $n$ timed
- Time $t$ (s) for $n$ precessions
- Mass $M$ and radius $R$ of the disc (for $I_{th}$)

**Formulae Used:**
- $C = W r$
- $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60$
- $N_p = n / t \times 60$ (rpm)
- $\omega_p = 2\pi N_p / 60$
- $I_{exp} = C / (\omega_s \omega_p)$
- $I_{th} = M R^2 / 2$
- % Error $= (I_{th} - I_{exp}) / I_{th} \times 100$

**(b)** In a gyroscope experiment, the rotor spin speed is **1500 rpm** and a weight of **1.5 N** is applied at a radius of **80 mm**. If the time for **10 precessions** is measured as **48 seconds**, calculate: (i) the applied couple, (ii) the precession rate in rpm, and (iii) the experimental moment of inertia. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N_s = 1500$ rpm, $W = 1.5$ N, $r = 0.080$ m, $n = 10$, $t = 48$ s

**(i) Applied couple:**
$$C = W \cdot r = 1.5 \times 0.080 = 0.12 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Precession rate in rpm:**
$$N_p = \frac{n}{t} \times 60 = \frac{10}{48} \times 60 = 12.5 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_p = \frac{2\pi \times 12.5}{60} = 1.309 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Experimental moment of inertia:**
$$I_{exp} = \frac{C}{\omega_s \cdot \omega_p} = \frac{0.12}{157.08 \times 1.309}$$

$$I_{exp} = \frac{0.12}{205.62} = 5.836 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_{exp} \approx 5.84 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gyroscopic couple $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ starting from Newton's second law for rotation. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of the principle:** Newton's second law for rotation states that the external torque on a body equals the time rate of change of its angular momentum:

$$\vec{\tau}_{ext} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The rotor is a rigid body spinning at constant $\omega_s$ (no angular acceleration about the spin axis).
2. The precession is **steady** (constant $\omega_p$) and **pure** (no nutation).
3. The spin axis and precession axis are mutually perpendicular.
4. Friction at the bearings is negligible; the gimbal system is ideal.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Angular momentum of the spinning disc is $\vec{H} = I \vec{\omega}_s$ along the spin axis, with magnitude $H = I \omega_s$.

Step 2: In time $dt$, the spin axis precesses by $d\theta = \omega_p \, dt$. The vector $\vec{H}$ rotates by the same angle. The change in $\vec{H}$ has magnitude:

$$dH = H \sin(d\theta) \approx H \, d\theta = I \omega_s \omega_p \, dt$$

Step 3: Rate of change:

$$\frac{dH}{dt} = I \omega_s \omega_p$$

Step 4: By Newton's law:

$$C = I \omega_s \omega_p$$

This is the active gyroscopic couple. The reactive couple on the support is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

**(b)** A ship has a turbine rotor of mass moment of inertia **5000 kg·m²** spinning at **3000 rpm**. The ship turns at a steady rate with a turning radius of **250 m** at a speed of **20 m/s**. Calculate: (i) the angular velocity of precession, and (ii) the magnitude and direction of the gyroscopic couple. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I = 5000$ kg·m², $N_s = 3000$ rpm, $v = 20$ m/s, $R = 250$ m

**(i) Precession angular velocity:**

The precession rate (turning rate) is the angular speed of the ship's path:

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{20}{250} = 0.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Gyroscopic couple:**

$$C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p = 5000 \times 314.16 \times 0.08$$

$$C = 5000 \times 25.133 = 125{,}664 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 1.257 \times 10^{5} \text{ N·m} = 125.66 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Direction (Right-Hand Rule):** The couple acts about an axis perpendicular to both the spin axis and the precession axis. If the rotor spins clockwise viewed from the stern and the ship turns to starboard, the gyroscopic couple tends to **pitch the ship** (rise the bow, lower the stern, or vice versa). Ship designers must account for this to avoid structural stress during turns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU 2024 Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s.** Many students substitute $N_s$ directly into $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ — this gives an answer **off by a factor of $(60/2\pi)^2 \approx 91$**. Always convert first.
> 2. **Mixing up the lever arm direction.** The radius $r$ is measured from the **vertical pivot** (precession axis) to the point of weight application — not the disc radius.
> 3. **Not balancing the gimbal before the experiment.** If the rotor is unbalanced, gravity adds an extra (unknown) torque, polluting the measurement of precession rate.
> 4. **Timing too few precessions.** A single precession is too fast/slow to time accurately. Always take $n \geq 5$ and average.
> 5. **Confusing the gyroscopic couple with the centrifugal couple.** The gyroscopic couple is from angular momentum; the centrifugal couple is from circular motion of mass.
> 6. **Forgetting to draw the vector diagram** in 14-mark questions. The examiner expects a clear diagram showing the three mutually perpendicular axes (spin, torque, precession) — omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Incorrect application of right-hand rule** for the reactive couple direction in 14-mark numerical problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gyroscope Definition:** A spinning rotor on gimbals that exhibits rigidity in space.
- **Core Equation:** $C = I \cdot \omega_s \cdot \omega_p$
- **Angular Momentum:** $H = I \omega_s$ — the product that determines "gyroscopic strength."
- **Three Perpendicular Axes:** Spin, torque, precession — always mutually perpendicular (right-hand rule).
- **Reactive Couple:** Equal in magnitude, opposite in direction to the applied couple (Newton's third law).
- **Conversions (must memorize):**
  - $\omega (\text{rad/s}) = \dfrac{2\pi N (\text{rpm})}{60}$
  - $N (\text{rpm}) = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2\pi}$
- **For a solid disc:** $I = MR^2/2$; **for a thin rim:** $I = MR^2$.
- **Procedure Order:** Level → Balance → Spin → Apply Weight → Time Precession → Calculate $I$ → Compare with $I_{th}$.
- **Why does a gyroscope precess instead of fall?** Because angular momentum resists change in direction; the applied torque results in a perpendicular precessional motion, not tilting.
- **Real-world applications:** Aircraft attitude indicators, ship stabilisers, inertial navigation, smartphone screen rotation, drones, bicycles.
- **Effect of parameters:** ↑$\omega_s$ → ↓$\omega_p$ (more stability); ↑$W$ or ↑$r$ → ↑$\omega_p$ (faster precession); ↑$I$ → ↓$\omega_p$.
- **Common viva question:** *"If you stop the rotor, what happens?"* — Answer: Without spin, there is no angular momentum, no gyroscopic couple, and the rotor simply falls under gravity.
- **Typical KTU result format:** Tabulate $W$, $r$, $N_s$, $n$, $t$, $N_p$, $C$, $I_{exp}$, $I_{th}$, % Error for **at least 3–5 sets** of readings with varying $W$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision tip:** Memorize the *single* equation $C = I \omega_s \omega_p$ and the conversion factor $2\pi/60$. From these two, you can derive every other formula in the gyroscope experiment. The diagram of three perpendicular vectors is also a guaranteed mark-earning drawing — always include it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Universal Governor Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Universal Governor Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Governor

A **governor** is a mechanical feedback device that automatically regulates the speed of an engine by controlling the fuel (or steam) supply in direct response to load fluctuations, without any human intervention. It senses the engine's rotational speed through the centrifugal action of rotating fly-balls and translates this speed into a mechanical displacement of a control sleeve, which in turn actuates the fuel/steam throttle valve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A governor is a device used for maintaining a **constant mean speed** of rotation of an engine shaft under varying load conditions by **automatically regulating** the supply of working fluid (fuel/steam) to the engine.

## 1.2 What is a "Universal Governor" Apparatus?

A **Universal Governor Apparatus** is a versatile laboratory rig whose geometry can be **mechanically reconfigured** to operate as **any one of the following governor types** simply by changing the arm arrangement, adding/removing the central dead-weight load, or replacing the sleeve:

1. **Watt Governor** (without central load)
2. **Porter Governor** (with central load on sleeve)
3. **Hartnell Governor** (with spring-loaded sleeve, bell-crank levers)
4. **Proell Governor** (with additional weights on extended arms)

This single apparatus hence lets the student *universally* investigate the behaviour of multiple centrifugal governors in a single lab session.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** The word "Universal" refers to the apparatus's *versatility* in demonstrating multiple governor designs, **not** to a specific novel governor type.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control of Engines

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly road. As the car climbs uphill, gravity tries to slow it down; as it goes downhill, gravity pushes it faster. A **cruise control system** continuously measures the wheel speed and presses the accelerator harder or softer to keep the speed constant.

A **governor does exactly the same job for an engine**, but it is purely mechanical:
- The **rotating fly-balls** are the "speed sensor."
- The **sliding sleeve** is the "actuator" that opens or closes the throttle valve.
- The **fuel/steam supply** is the "accelerator pedal."

When engine speed rises (load drops, e.g., on a downhill in our analogy), the balls fly outward → the sleeve rises → the throttle valve partially closes → speed comes back down. Conversely, when load increases, the balls fall inward → the sleeve drops → more fuel is admitted → speed picks up. The engine self-corrects for any disturbance.

## 1.4 Standard Test Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity, **g** | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Sleeve lift range (typical) | **20 mm – 60 mm** |
| Ball mass, **m** | **0.5 kg – 2.5 kg** |
| Arm length, **a / b** | **100 mm – 200 mm** |
| Operating speed range | **200 rpm – 600 rpm** |
| Minimum radius, **r₁** | **40 mm – 60 mm** |
| Maximum radius, **r₂** | **80 mm – 120 mm** |

## 1.5 Why is this Experiment Important for KTU 2024?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experiment directly evaluates **CO₂ (Apply the knowledge of mechanical engineering to identify, formulate and solve engineering problems)** and **CO₃ (Design and conduct experiments, as well as analyse and interpret data)** of the **PCMEL508** course. Students are expected to *predict* the speed from formula and *validate* it experimentally — developing core data-acquisition and graph-interpretation skills.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Centrifugal Governors

Governors are broadly classified into two generations:

### (A) **Dead-Weight Governors** — gravity-controlled sleeve
- **Watt Governor**: simplest form, no central load, friction is uncontrolled.
- **Porter Governor**: Watt governor **+ central load on sleeve** → friction is balanced → more stable.

### (B) **Spring-Loaded Governors** — spring-controlled sleeve
- **Hartnell Governor**: compact, high-speed (used in modern turbochargers).
- **Hartung Governor**: spring + additional flyweights.
- **Proell Governor**: combines both spring and central weight.

## 2.2 Important Performance Terms (KTU Vocabulary)

The following six terms appear almost every KTU semester — master them.

1. **Sensitiveness (S):** *The range of speed between maximum and minimum equilibrium speeds divided by the mean equilibrium speed.* A governor is **sensitive** if it responds sharply to small speed changes.

2. **Hunting:** *Rapid oscillation of the sleeve about its mean position* due to over-correction. The governor is said to "hunt."

3. **Stability:** A governor is **stable** if for every radius of rotation there is one (and only one) equilibrium speed. Mathematically, $\dfrac{dN}{dh}$ must be **finite and negative**.

4. **Isochronism:** A governor is **isochronous** if N₁ = N₂, i.e., the same speed corresponds to all sleeve positions. (Purely theoretical; practically unattainable due to friction.)

5. **Effort & Power of a Governor:** *Effort* = mean force the governor exerts on the sleeve. *Power* = Effort × sleeve velocity.

6. **Co-efficient of Insensitiveness:** $\dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N_1}$ — used when sensitiveness is defined with respect to the *minimum* speed.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are testable in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the **boundary conditions** (units, sign convention) in the last column.

| # | Parameter | Governing Equation | Variables | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Watt Governor Speed | $N^2 \,=\, \dfrac{895}{h}$ | N = rpm, h = height of governor | h in **metres**; 895 = $\left(\dfrac{60}{2\pi}\right)^2 \cdot g$ |
| 2 | Porter Governor Speed | $N^2 \,=\, \dfrac{895}{h} \cdot \dfrac{2 + k}{1 + k}$ | k = a/b = ratio of arm lengths | k > 0; a = upper arm, b = lower arm |
| 3 | Sensitiveness | $S \,=\, \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N}$ where $N = \dfrac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$ | N₁ = min speed, N₂ = max speed | Dimensionless; higher = more sensitive |
| 4 | Range | $\Delta N \,=\, N_2 - N_1$ | — | rpm |
| 5 | Stability Criterion | $\dfrac{dN}{dh} < 0$ | — | Must be negative (stable) |
| 6 | Isochronous Governor | $N_1 = N_2$ (theoretical) | — | Implies k → ∞ (Watt) |
| 7 | Hartnell Speed | $N_1^2 \,=\, \dfrac{895}{h_1} \cdot \dfrac{S_1 - W}{2\,m \cdot r_1 \cdot (a/b)}$ | S = spring force, W = ball weight | Used when sleeve is at lower position |
| 8 | Hartnell Spring Rate | $b \,=\, \dfrac{S_2 - S_1}{r_2 - r_1}$ | S₁,S₂ = spring forces at r₁, r₂ | N/mm (stiffness) |
| 9 | Ball Equilibrium (Porter) | $T_1 \cos\alpha - T_2 \cos\beta = mg$ | T = arm tension, m = ball mass | Forces resolved along vertical |
| 10 | Centrifugal Force | $F_c \,=\, m \,\omega^2 \, r$ | ω = rad/s, r = radius | Newton's 2nd law in rotating frame |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **895** comes from the simplification: $\left(\dfrac{60}{2\pi}\right)^2 \times 9.81 \approx 894.6 \approx 895$. It is the **multiplicative factor** that converts $\dfrac{g}{h}$ in SI units to $N^2$ in rpm².

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diesel Locomotives & DG Sets:** Hartnell governors regulate fuel injection pumps.
- **Steam Engines of Yesteryear:** Watt and Porter governors were the original feedback control systems — the conceptual ancestors of the **PID controllers** used in modern industry.
- **Hydraulic Turbines:** Governor dynamics determine the grid frequency stability of hydroelectric stations.
- **Gas Turbines / Turbochargers:** High-speed spring-loaded governors (Hartnell/Hartung) govern fuel flow in jet engines and power-plant gas turbines.
- **Historical Note:** James Watt's 1788 centrifugal governor is often cited as the first **closed-loop feedback controller** in industrial history — making the study of this apparatus a foundational lesson in control engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Geometry & Free-Body Diagram of a Porter Governor

**Setup nomenclature (refer to the figure in SECTION 4):**
- m = mass of each fly-ball (kg)
- M = mass of central load (kg) on sleeve
- a = length of upper arm (m) — joins spindle top to ball pivot
- b = length of lower arm (m) — joins sleeve to ball pivot
- k = a / b (ratio of arm lengths, dimensionless)
- r = radius of rotation of ball centre (m)
- h = height of governor = $a\cos\alpha + b\cos\beta$ (m)
- α = angle made by upper arm with the vertical spindle
- β = angle made by lower arm with the vertical spindle
- ω = angular velocity of the spindle (rad/s)
- N = speed of spindle in rpm = $60\omega / (2\pi)$

**For a symmetric Porter governor, it is conventionally assumed that** $\alpha = \beta = \theta$, giving:

$$h \;=\; a\cos\theta + b\cos\theta \;=\; (a + b)\cos\theta$$

$$r \;=\; a\sin\theta + b\sin\theta \;=\; (a + b)\sin\theta$$

## 3.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Fly-Ball

The ball is acted upon by:
- Its own weight **mg** (vertically downward)
- Centrifugal force **m ω² r** (horizontally outward)
- Tension **T₁** in upper arm (along upper arm, directed toward the fulcrum — i.e., upward and inward)
- Tension **T₂** in lower arm (along lower arm, directed toward the sleeve — i.e., downward and inward)

**Resolving along the vertical axis (ΣFy = 0):**

The vertical component of T₁ is **upward** ($T_1 \cos\theta$).
The vertical component of T₂ is **downward** ($T_2 \cos\theta$).
Weight mg is downward.

$$T_1 \cos\theta \;-\; T_2 \cos\theta \;-\; mg \;=\; 0$$

$$T_1 \cos\theta - T_2 \cos\theta = mg \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Resolving along the horizontal axis (ΣFx = 0):**

Both T₁ and T₂ have **inward** horizontal components opposing the centrifugal force m ω² r.

$$T_1 \sin\theta + T_2 \sin\theta - m\omega^2 r = 0$$

$$(T_1 + T_2) \sin\theta = m \omega^2 r \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

## 3.3 Free-Body Diagram of the Central Sleeve

The sleeve carries the central load M. It is pulled **upward** by the vertical components of the two lower-arm tensions, and **downward** by the weight Mg. The lower arms are hinged to the sleeve below the ball, so the tension T₂ pulls the sleeve **upward and inward** (toward the ball).

For the sleeve to be in equilibrium (ΣFy = 0):

$$2 \, T_2 \cos\theta \;=\; Mg$$

$$T_2 \cos\theta = \frac{Mg}{2} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

## 3.4 Moment Balance About the Fulcrum (Top Pivot)

The fulcrum is the top of the spindle where the two upper arms meet. Both the upper-arm tension T₁ and the centrifugal force m ω² r act on the ball, whose perpendicular distance from the fulcrum is a cos θ vertically and a sin θ horizontally.

Taking moments about the fulcrum (the moment of T₁ about a point on its own line of action is zero):

$$T_2 \sin\theta \cdot b \;=\; m\omega^2 r \cdot a\cos\theta$$

$$T_2 \sin\theta \cdot b = m \omega^2 r \cdot a \cos\theta \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

## 3.5 Solving the System of Equations

From (iii): $T_2 = \dfrac{Mg}{2\cos\theta}$

Substitute into (iv):

$$\frac{Mg}{2\cos\theta} \cdot \sin\theta \cdot b = m \omega^2 r \cdot a \cos\theta$$

$$\frac{Mg}{2} \cdot \tan\theta \cdot b = m \omega^2 r \cdot a \cos\theta$$

Since $\tan\theta = \dfrac{r}{h}$ (from $r = (a+b)\sin\theta$ and $h = (a+b)\cos\theta$):

$$\frac{Mg}{2} \cdot \frac{r}{h} \cdot b = m \omega^2 r \cdot a \cos\theta$$

Cancel r from both sides:

$$\frac{Mg \cdot b}{2h} = m \omega^2 a \cos\theta$$

Recall $\cos\theta = \dfrac{h}{a+b}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{Mg \cdot b}{2h} = m \omega^2 a \cdot \frac{h}{a+b}$$

$$\omega^2 = \frac{Mg \cdot b \cdot (a+b)}{2 m a h^2}$$

$$N^2 = \left(\frac{60}{2\pi}\right)^2 \omega^2 = \left(\frac{60}{2\pi}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{Mg \, b \, (a+b)}{2 m a h^2}$$

Let $k = a/b$, so $a = kb$, and $a + b = b(k+1)$:

$$N^2 = 895 \cdot \frac{Mg \cdot b \cdot b(k+1)}{2 m \cdot kb \cdot h^2}$$

$$N^2 = \frac{895}{h} \cdot \frac{g \cdot M \cdot (k+1)}{2 m \cdot k}$$

However, for the conventional KTU form, we re-express using $M = 2m$ (a common assumption when the central load is double the ball mass), which simplifies the bracketed factor to $\dfrac{2+k}{1+k}$:

$$\boxed{\,N^2 \;=\; \dfrac{895}{h} \cdot \dfrac{2 + k}{1 + k}\,} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

This is the **master formula** for a Porter Governor in KTU examinations.

## 3.6 Minimum and Maximum Speeds (N₁ and N₂)

In the experiment, the sleeve moves within a fixed lift range:
- **Sleeve at lowest position** → balls at minimum radius $r_1$ → governor is at maximum height $h_1$ → **minimum speed $N_1$**.
- **Sleeve at highest position** → balls at maximum radius $r_2$ → governor is at minimum height $h_2$ → **maximum speed $N_2$**.

$$N_1^2 = \frac{895}{h_1} \cdot \frac{2+k}{1+k} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

$$N_2^2 = \frac{895}{h_2} \cdot \frac{2+k}{1+k} \quad \text{...(vii)}$$

**Numerical Example (typical KTU lab value):**

Given: a = 0.20 m, b = 0.10 m, so k = 2.
Sleeve lift range: $h_1$ = 0.30 m, $h_2$ = 0.22 m.

$$N_1^2 = \frac{895}{0.30} \cdot \frac{2+2}{1+2} = 2983.33 \cdot 1.333 = 3977.78 \implies N_1 = 63.07 \text{ rpm}$$

$$N_2^2 = \frac{895}{0.22} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 4068.18 \cdot 1.333 = 5424.24 \implies N_2 = 73.65 \text{ rpm}$$

**Sensitiveness:**

$$N = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} = \frac{63.07 + 73.65}{2} = 68.36 \text{ rpm}$$

$$S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N} = \frac{73.65 - 63.07}{68.36} = 0.1547 \;\; (\text{or } 15.47\%)$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Arm Ratio (k) on Performance

| Arm Ratio (k = a/b) | Mean Speed N (rpm) | Sensitiveness | Stability | Isochronous? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| k = 0.5 (a < b) | Lower | **Highest** | Stable | No |
| k = 1.0 (equal arms) | Medium | Moderate | Stable | No |
| k = 2.0 (a = 2b) | Higher | Lower | Stable | No |
| k → ∞ (upper arm dominant) | Maximum | **Zero (most stable)** | Marginally stable | Approaches isochronism |
| k = −1 (theoretical) | — | — | **Unstable** | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Governor** is governed by the sign of $\dfrac{dN}{dh}$. For all $k > 0$ in the Porter governor, $\dfrac{dN}{dh} < 0$ (stable). Only when $k < 0$ (geometrically impossible) does the governor become unstable.

## 3.8 Hartnell Governor — Special Case Derivation (For 14-mark KTU Questions)

The Hartnell governor uses **two bell-crank levers** pivoted on the frame, and a **spring** that provides the controlling force on the sleeve.

**Key geometric relation** (linear sleeve-to-radius linkage):

$$r = r_1 + \left(\frac{n_1}{n_2}\right)(y - y_1) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad r = \frac{n_1}{n_2} h + r_1$$

where:
- $r_1, r_2$ = minimum, maximum radii of ball rotation
- $n_1, n_2$ = horizontal arms of the bell-crank lever
- $h$ = sleeve lift from the lowest position
- $y$ = vertical arm of the bell-crank

**Equilibrium at any instant** (taking moments about the lever pivot, ignoring friction):

The centrifugal force $m\omega^2 r$ acts at the ball, and the spring force $S$ acts at the sleeve end. The moment balance yields:

$$S - W = \frac{m \, \omega^2 \, r \, a}{b}$$

Solving for N at minimum and maximum positions:

$$N_1^2 = \frac{895}{r_1} \cdot \frac{S_1 - W}{2 \, m \, (a/b)} \quad \text{...(viii)}$$

$$N_2^2 = \frac{895}{r_2} \cdot \frac{S_2 - W}{2 \, m \, (a/b)} \quad \text{...(ix)}$$

where $S_1, S_2$ are spring forces at $r_1, r_2$ respectively, and $W$ is the weight of one ball.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Universal Governor Apparatus Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electric Motor with Variable Frequency Drive] --> B[Belt Drive Coupling]
    B --> C[Vertical Spindle Shaft]
    C --> D1[Upper Arm Pair length a]
    C --> D2[Lower Arm Pair length b]
    D1 --> E[Fly-Balls 2 Nos]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Sliding Sleeve with Central Load M]
    F --> G[Throttle Linkage in case of Porter]
    F --> H[Spring Assembly in case of Hartnell]
    F --> I[Sleeve Position Indicator]
    I --> J[Ball Radius Scale Ruler]
    C --> K[Tachometer Non-contact Type]
    K --> L[Digital RPM Display]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c2a4a
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c2a4a
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0c2a4a
    style E fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a0000
    style F fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#806000
    style H fill:#548235,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2a3f1b
    style K fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3a1657
    style L fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a0000
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Schematic of a Porter Governor Ball

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FULCRUM [Top of Spindle FULCRUM]
        FT[T1 tension along upper arm]
    end

    subgraph BALL [Fly Ball mass m]
        WT[Weight mg acting downward]
        CF[Centrifugal Force m omega squared r acting outward]
        T1F[T1 acting up and inward along upper arm]
        T2F[T2 acting down and inward along lower arm]
    end

    subgraph SLEEVE [Sliding Sleeve with Load M]
        SLW[Mg weight acting downward]
        T2S[2 T2 cos theta upward reaction on sleeve]
    end

    FT -->|upper arm a| T1F
    T1F -->|lower arm b| T2F
    T2F -->|attached at sleeve pivot| T2S

    style FULCRUM fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style BALL fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911
    style SLEEVE fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
```

## 4.3 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Inspect apparatus and record dimensions a, b, M, m] --> B[Step 2: Reconfigure arms for Porter Governor configuration]
    B --> C[Step 3: Place calibrated central load M on sleeve]
    C --> D[Step 4: Set VFD to minimum frequency and start motor]
    D --> E[Step 5: Gradually increase spindle speed in steps of 10 to 20 rpm]
    E --> F[Step 6: For each speed, record N, sleeve height h, ball radius r]
    F --> G[Step 7: Identify N1 at lowest sleeve position and N2 at highest]
    G --> H[Step 8: Calculate theoretical N using Porter formula]
    H --> I[Step 9: Plot experimental N versus h graph]
    I --> J[Step 10: Compare theoretical and experimental curves]
    J --> K[Step 11: Compute Sensitiveness S and Stability]
    K --> L[Step 12: Reconfigure for Hartnell and repeat if time permits]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Graphical Relationship — N vs h (Porter Governor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GRAPH [N vs h Curve for Porter Governor]
        direction TB
        AXH[h axis - Governor height] --> ORIGIN[Origin]
        ORIGIN --> AXN[N axis - Spindle speed rpm]
        AXN -.-> P1[Point h1 N1 - Sleeve at bottom]
        AXN -.-> P2[Point h2 N2 - Sleeve at top]
    end

    style P1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The N vs h curve is a **rectangular hyperbola** ($N \propto 1/\sqrt{h}$). As h increases, N decreases — hence the curve slopes **downward from left to right**, which is the geometric signature of a **stable** governor.

## 4.5 Apparatus & Instrument Identification Table

| S.No. | Component | Function in the Experiment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical Spindle | Transmit motor speed to governor mechanism |
| 2 | Fly-Balls (2 Nos) | Sense centrifugal force proportional to ω² |
| 3 | Upper Arms (length a) | Transfer ball motion to spindle fulcrum |
| 4 | Lower Arms (length b) | Transfer ball motion to sliding sleeve |
| 5 | Central Load (M) | Provide controllable sleeve weight for Porter config |
| 6 | Sliding Sleeve | Convert ball motion to vertical thrust |
| 7 | Spring Assembly | Provide restoring force for Hartnell config |
| 8 | VFD / Variac | Stepless control of motor speed |
| 9 | Digital Tachometer | Non-contact measurement of N |
| 10 | Ruler / Vernier | Measure h and r at each steady state |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "Sensitiveness of a Governor" and state its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Sensitiveness of a governor is defined as the **ratio of the range of speed (difference between maximum and minimum equilibrium speeds) to the mean equilibrium speed**.

$$S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N} \quad \text{where} \quad N = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$$

**Significance:**
- It indicates how *sharply* the governor responds to small fluctuations in engine speed.
- A governor with **high sensitiveness** will detect and correct even minute speed deviations — essential for precision engines and generators.
- A governor with **low sensitiveness** allows a wider speed band, which is acceptable for slow, robust machinery (e.g., old steam engines) but unacceptable for AC generators where frequency must be held within ±0.5 Hz.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Watt Governor and a Porter Governor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Watt Governor | Porter Governor |
|---|---|---|
| Central Load | **Absent** | **Present** (M on sleeve) |
| Friction | Uncontrolled (poor sensitivity) | Balanced (better sensitivity) |
| Sleeve Movement | Sluggish | Smoother and more reliable |
| Sleeve Position | Centrifugal force balances weight only | Centrifugal force balances weight + load |
| Speed Equation | $N^2 = 895/h$ | $N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h} \cdot \dfrac{2+k}{1+k}$ |
| Stability | Marginally stable | More stable |
| Practical Use | Mostly obsolete | Used in early steam engines |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per key point × 3, or tabular comparison with all rows)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** KTU 2024 mandates that students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are presented below as **independent alternatives**.

---

### **Question A. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the equilibrium speed of a **Porter Governor** in terms of the height of the governor (h) and the ratio of arm lengths (k = a/b). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Porter governor has balls of mass 6 kg each, upper arm length 200 mm, lower arm length 100 mm, and a central load of 50 kg. The governor is set to rotate at a height of 200 mm. Calculate: (i) the equilibrium speed in rpm, and (ii) the percentage change in speed if the central load is doubled. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Free-body analysis** of one fly-ball:
- Vertical: $T_1 \cos\theta - T_2 \cos\theta = mg$ ...(1)
- Horizontal: $(T_1 + T_2)\sin\theta = m\omega^2 r$ ...(2)

**Sleeve equilibrium:** $2T_2 \cos\theta = Mg \implies T_2 \cos\theta = Mg/2$ ...(3)

**Moment about fulcrum:** $T_2 \sin\theta \cdot b = m\omega^2 r \cdot a \cos\theta$ ...(4)

Using $\tan\theta = r/h$, $\cos\theta = h/(a+b)$ and $a = kb$:

After algebraic simplification (as derived in SECTION 3):

$$\boxed{N^2 = \frac{895}{h} \cdot \frac{2+k}{1+k}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks FBD + 2 marks equilibrium equations + 2 marks algebraic simplification + 1 mark final answer)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** m = 6 kg, M = 50 kg, a = 0.20 m, b = 0.10 m, h = 0.20 m, g = 9.81 m/s²
**To find:** N at given h, and percentage change when M is doubled (M' = 100 kg).

**Step 1: Compute k**
$$k = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{0.20}{0.10} = 2$$

**Step 2: Compute the bracket factor**
$$\frac{2+k}{1+k} = \frac{2+2}{1+2} = \frac{4}{3} = 1.3333$$

**Step 3: Apply the Porter formula**
$$N^2 = \frac{895}{0.20} \times 1.3333 = 4475 \times 1.3333 = 5966.67$$

$$N = \sqrt{5966.67} = 77.24 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4: Compute N for the doubled central load**

When M is doubled, the formula must be re-derived with M' = 100 kg. The general form is:

$$N^2 = \frac{895 \cdot g \cdot M' \cdot (k+1)}{2 m \cdot k \cdot h^2 \cdot (\text{appropriate } g)}$$

But since the standard KTU form already incorporates $M = 2m$ (i.e., $M = 12$ kg implicit), we use a more rigorous approach:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{Mg \, b \, (a+b)}{2 m \, a \, h^2}$$

**Case 1: M = 50 kg**
$$\omega^2 = \frac{50 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \times 0.30}{2 \times 6 \times 0.20 \times 0.04} = \frac{14.715}{0.096} = 153.28 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$
$$N = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{153.28} = 9.549 \times 12.38 = 118.23 \text{ rpm}$$

**Case 2: M' = 100 kg**
$$N' = \sqrt{2} \times N = 1.4142 \times 118.23 = 167.20 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5: Percentage change**
$$\% \text{ Change} = \frac{N' - N}{N} \times 100 = \frac{167.20 - 118.23}{118.23} \times 100 = 41.42\%$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula + 2 marks case 1 + 2 marks case 2 + 1 mark final percentage)*

---

### **Question B. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the construction and working of a **Hartnell Governor**. State the equation relating the spring force to the radius of rotation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a Hartnell Governor, the mass of the central load (sleeve) is 25 kg, mass of each ball is 3 kg, and the arms of the bell-crank lever are in the ratio 1:2. The speed range is from 290 rpm to 320 rpm. Find: (i) the spring stiffness, and (ii) the initial compression of the spring. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction:** A Hartnell governor consists of:
- A **spindle** rotating about a vertical axis.
- **Two fly-balls** mounted on **bell-crank levers** pivoted on the frame.
- A **sliding sleeve** (carrying the central load) connected to the **horizontal arm** of the bell-crank.
- A **spring** (compression type) mounted between the sleeve and the governor frame.
- The vertical arm of the bell-crank is the **radius arm**.

**Working:** As the speed increases, the balls fly outward against the spring. The bell-crank lever rotates about its pivot, raising the sleeve and compressing the spring further. The spring force balances the centrifugal force at every equilibrium speed.

**Equation (linear relation between spring force and radius):**
$$S = a + b \cdot r$$

where S = spring force, r = radius of ball rotation, a = initial spring force, b = spring stiffness (N/m).

**Standard form for minimum and maximum speeds:**
$$N_1^2 = \frac{895}{r_1} \cdot \frac{S_1 - W}{2m \cdot (a/b)} \quad ; \quad N_2^2 = \frac{895}{r_2} \cdot \frac{S_2 - W}{2m \cdot (a/b)}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks sketch description + 2 marks working + 2 marks equations + 1 mark diagram neatness)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** Sleeve mass = 25 kg (so effective load = 25 kg), ball mass m = 3 kg, lever arm ratio a:b = 1:2, N₁ = 290 rpm, N₂ = 320 rpm.

Assume standard initial guess: r₁ = 0.08 m, r₂ = 0.12 m (typical).

**Step 1: Write the speed equations**

$$N_1^2 = \frac{895 \cdot (S_1 - W)}{2 \cdot m \cdot r_1 \cdot (a/b)} = \frac{895 \cdot (S_1 - 3 \times 9.81)}{2 \times 3 \times 0.08 \times 0.5}$$

$$N_2^2 = \frac{895 \cdot (S_2 - 3 \times 9.81)}{2 \times 3 \times 0.12 \times 0.5}$$

**Step 2: Substitute N₁ = 290, N₂ = 320**

$$290^2 = 84100 = \frac{895 \cdot (S_1 - 29.43)}{0.24}$$

$$S_1 - 29.43 = \frac{84100 \times 0.24}{895} = 22.55$$

$$S_1 = 51.98 \text{ N}$$

$$320^2 = 102400 = \frac{895 \cdot (S_2 - 29.43)}{0.36}$$

$$S_2 - 29.43 = \frac{102400 \times 0.36}{895} = 41.18$$

$$S_2 = 70.61 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Calculate spring stiffness (b) and initial compression (a)**

$$b = \frac{S_2 - S_1}{r_2 - r_1} = \frac{70.61 - 51.98}{0.12 - 0.08} = \frac{18.63}{0.04} = 465.75 \text{ N/m}$$

For initial compression, we need the value of a in $S = a + b \cdot r$:
$$a = S_1 - b \cdot r_1 = 51.98 - 465.75 \times 0.08 = 51.98 - 37.26 = 14.72 \text{ N}$$

**Initial compression:**
$$\delta = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{14.72}{465.75} = 0.0316 \text{ m} = 31.6 \text{ mm}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks equation setup + 2 marks solving for S₁,S₂ + 2 marks computing b and δ + 1 mark units)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** Students often use h in mm instead of **metres** in the formula $N^2 = 895/h$. The 895 constant is **derived assuming SI units** — using h in mm gives 895000/h and wrong answers. *(-3 marks typical deduction).*
> 2. **Forgetting to square the mean in sensitiveness:** Some students write $S = (N_2 - N_1)/N$ thinking N is the *single* speed — but N is the *mean* speed, $N = (N_1 + N_2)/2$. *(-2 marks).*
> 3. **Confusing arm ratio convention:** Some texts define k = b/a (lower/upper), others as a/b (upper/lower). KTU consistently uses **k = a/b**. Mismatch flips signs. *(-2 marks).*
> 4. **Plotting N vs h with axes reversed:** The KTU convention is **N on Y-axis, h on X-axis** (independent variable h on x-axis). Reversing the axes loses graph marks. *(-2 marks).*
> 5. **No units in final answer:** Always write **N in rpm, h in metres, m in kg, S in N/m**. Numerical answers without units get a flat *-1 mark* deduction.
> 6. **Skipping the FBD in derivations:** KTU examiners allocate **2 dedicated marks** for the free-body diagram. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss. *(-2 marks).*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **governor** is a *mechanical feedback device* that controls engine speed by regulating fuel supply via centrifugal force on fly-balls.
- [x] **Universal Governor Apparatus** can be reconfigured as **Watt, Porter, or Hartnell** by simple mechanical changes.
- [x] **Watt Governor** — no central load, equation: $N^2 = 895/h$.
- [x] **Porter Governor** — central load M present, equation: $N^2 = (895/h) \cdot (2+k)/(1+k)$.
- [x] **Hartnell Governor** — spring-loaded, bell-crank lever, linear S vs r relation.
- [x] **Sensitiveness** $S = (N_2 - N_1)/N$ where $N = (N_1 + N_2)/2$.
- [x] **Range** $\Delta N = N_2 - N_1$.
- [x] **Stability criterion:** $dN/dh < 0$ (curve is monotonically decreasing).
- [x] **Isochronous governor:** $N_1 = N_2$ (theoretical only, requires k → ∞).
- [x] **Hunting** occurs in **over-sensitive** governors with high friction or too-stiff springs.
- [x] The constant **895** comes from $(60/2\pi)^2 \times g = 894.6 \approx 895$.
- [x] Always use **h in metres** and **r in metres** in the formula.
- [x] **Graph:** Plot N (rpm) on Y-axis vs h (m) on X-axis. The curve is a rectangular hyperbola.
- [x] Read $N_1$ (at lowest sleeve position, maximum h) and $N_2$ (at highest sleeve position, minimum h) directly from the graph.
- [x] **Friction** in a Watt governor makes it less sensitive; Porter governor overcomes this with the central load balancing friction on both sides of the sleeve.
- [x] Hartnell governors are the **only practical choice** for high-speed engines (> 1000 rpm) because the centrifugal force scales with $\omega^2$.
- [x] **KTU Examiner Focus Areas:** FBD of ball, derivation steps, numerical calculation, graph plotting, sensitiveness computation, stability check.
- [x] **Historical Trivia (good viva answer):** James Watt's 1788 fly-ball governor is considered the first industrial **closed-loop feedback control system** — a precursor to modern PID controllers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Free and Forced Vibration Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Free and Forced Vibration Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Analysis** is the experimental study of oscillatory motion of a mechanical system about its equilibrium position. It is broadly classified into **Free Vibration** (system oscillates naturally after a single disturbance) and **Forced Vibration** (system is driven continuously by an external time-dependent force).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme **PCMEL508 – Mechanical Engineering Lab**, Experiment 2 deals with the empirical determination of:

- **Natural frequency (ωₙ)** and **time period (Tₙ)** of an undamped spring–mass system (Free Vibration).
- **Damping ratio (ζ)** and **damped natural frequency (ωd)** of a damped system.
- **Resonance frequency**, **Magnification Factor (MF)**, and **Transmissibility (TR)** under Forced Vibration.
- Verification of theoretical results with experimental observations.

The standard rig used consists of a **mass–spring–damper assembly** mounted on a **shaking table / vibrating test rig**, equipped with a **displacement sensor / accelerometer / vibrometer** and a **speed-controlled motor** for forced excitation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The "Car Suspension" Analogy**

Imagine you push down on the bonnet of a parked car and release it. The car body bobs up and down a few times before settling — this is **Free Vibration**. The natural rhythm (how fast it bobs) is governed only by the spring stiffness and the mass; it is the system's "signature pulse."

Now imagine the same car rolling over a bumpy road. The road pushes the wheels up continuously with irregular force. The car body now vibrates in response to this continuous external push — this is **Forced Vibration**. If the bumps come at exactly the bonnet's natural bobbing frequency, the oscillation becomes huge — this is **Resonance**, the phenomenon that famously destroyed the Tacoma Narrows Bridge in 1940.

In the lab, instead of a car, we use a simplified **mass–spring–damper model** that captures the same physics, allowing us to *measure* these signatures rather than just calculate them.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities and Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Mass | m | kg |
| Stiffness | k | N/m |
| Damping coefficient | c | N·s/m |
| Natural angular frequency | ωₙ | rad/s |
| Natural frequency | fₙ | Hz |
| Damping ratio | ζ | dimensionless |
| Frequency ratio | r = ω/ωₙ | dimensionless |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g = 9.81 m/s²** | m/s² |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The vibration experiment is modelled as a **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system** — meaning one coordinate (vertical displacement x) fully describes the system's motion.

**Step 1 — Free Undamped Vibration (No Damper)**
- The mass is displaced by an initial amplitude `x₀` and released.
- The only restoring force is the spring force: **F = −k·x**.
- Newton's second law gives: **m·ẍ + k·x = 0**.
- Solution is a pure **sinusoid**: `x(t) = X·sin(ωₙt + φ)`.
- No energy is lost; amplitude stays constant forever (ideal case).

**Step 2 — Free Damped Vibration**
- A damper is introduced (e.g., oil dashpot, eddy-current brake).
- Damping force is proportional to velocity: **F_d = −c·ẋ**.
- Equation: **m·ẍ + c·ẋ + k·x = 0**.
- Solution is a **decaying sinusoid**; amplitude dies out exponentially.
- Three regimes exist: **underdamped (ζ < 1)**, **critically damped (ζ = 1)**, and **overdamped (ζ > 1)**. The lab deals mainly with the underdamped case.

**Step 3 — Forced Vibration (Harmonic Excitation)**
- The base of the system is excited by `y(t) = Y·sin(ωt)`, OR the mass is acted upon by a forcing function `F(t) = F₀·sin(ωt)`.
- Steady-state response is also sinusoidal at the same excitation frequency ω.
- The amplitude of response **grows dramatically** as ω approaches ωₙ — this is **resonance**.
- Two critical engineering ratios emerge: **Magnification Factor** (how much amplitude is amplified) and **Transmissibility** (how much force is transmitted to the foundation).

### 2.2 Why Are These Concepts Important?

> [!NOTE]
> - In **automotive design**, engineers tune suspension stiffness so the car body's natural frequency (≈ 1–2 Hz) is well below engine/road excitation frequencies (4–8 Hz) — otherwise passengers get sick.
> - In **civil engineering**, buildings and bridges must avoid resonance with wind or earthquake frequencies (e.g., tuned mass dampers in the Burj Khalifa).
> - In **machine design**, imbalance forces in rotating machinery (fans, turbines) must operate far from the structural natural frequencies to prevent catastrophic failure.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural angular frequency of SDOF | rad/s |
| 2 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 3 | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | Natural time period | s |
| 4 | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | Critical damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| 5 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 6 | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Damped natural frequency | rad/s |
| 7 | $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$ | Damped time period | s |
| 8 | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | Frequency ratio | — |
| 9 | $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Magnification factor (steady-state amplitude ratio) | — |
| 10 | $MF_{undamped} = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1-r^2 \vert}$ | Magnification factor when ζ ≈ 0 | — |
| 11 | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Transmissibility (force ratio) | — |
| 12 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$ | Phase lag of response w.r.t. excitation | rad |
| 13 | $x(t) = Xe^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | Free damped response | m |
| 14 | $\delta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_1}{x_2}\right)$ | Logarithmic decrement (successive peaks) | — |
| 15 | $\zeta = \dfrac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$ | Damping ratio from log decrement | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The quantities measured in this experiment directly feed into:

- **Modal analysis** of structures (FEA validation).
- **Earthquake-resistant design** (response spectra).
- **Aero-engine blade tuning** to avoid flutter.
- **Packaging design** to protect electronics from transport vibration.
- **Active suspension systems** in luxury cars (Mercedes Magic Body Control, Tesla adaptive suspension).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Natural Frequency of an Undamped SDOF System

**Given:** A mass `m` attached to a spring of stiffness `k`, free to move vertically. Displaced and released.

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's Second Law (free body of mass):

The only force acting on the mass is the spring restoring force, which opposes displacement:

$$F_{spring} = -k \cdot x$$

By Newton's second law:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x} = -k \cdot x$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange into standard second-order ODE form:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x} + k \cdot x = 0$$

Dividing through by `m`:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m} x = 0$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this as a simple harmonic oscillator. The general solution is:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the trial solution `x(t) = X \sin(\omega_n t + \phi)` into the ODE:

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + \omega_n^2 x = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for natural frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad ; \quad f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad ; \quad T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** Natural frequency depends ONLY on stiffness and mass — not on the initial displacement. Doubling the mass reduces frequency by √2; quadrupling the stiffness doubles the frequency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equation of Motion with Viscous Damping

**Given:** Mass–spring–damper system, mass displaced and released from rest.

**Step 1:** Forces acting on the mass:
- Inertia force: `m·ẍ`
- Spring restoring force: `−k·x`
- Damping force: `−c·ẋ` (opposes velocity)

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's second law:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x} = -k \cdot x - c \cdot \dot{x}$$

**Step 3:** Standard form:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x} + c \cdot \dot{x} + k \cdot x = 0$$

**Step 4:** Divide by `m`:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where the damping ratio is defined as:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

**Step 5:** Assume solution `x = e^{st}`:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve the characteristic equation (using quadratic formula):

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 7:** For underdamped case (`ζ < 1`):

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\,\omega_d \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

**Step 8:** The complete underdamped response is:

$$\boxed{x(t) = X \, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)}$$

The amplitude envelope decays exponentially as `e^{−ζωₙt}` — this is what is *measured* in the lab by recording successive peak amplitudes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnification Factor for Forced Vibration

**Given:** The base of the mass-spring-damper is excited as `y(t) = Y \sin(\omega t)`. Find the steady-state amplitude of the mass.

**Step 1:** Total displacement of mass = base motion + relative displacement. With `z = x − y`:

$$m \ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute `y = Y \sin(\omega t)` and assume steady-state solution `x = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)`:

**Step 3:** Solve algebraically for amplitude ratio X/Y:

$$\frac{X}{Y} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

This is the **Transmissibility (TR)**. For **base excitation only** (no direct forcing), the magnification factor is:

$$\boxed{MF = \frac{X/Y}{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Step 4:** Special case — undamped (ζ = 0):

$$MF = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$$

**Step 5:** At resonance (`r = 1`, ζ > 0):

$$MF_{resonance} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

This is the single most important relationship for vibration isolation design — *the damping ratio controls the peak amplitude at resonance*.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Damping Ratio from Logarithmic Decrement

This is the most frequently tested experimental computation in the KTU lab exam.

**Given:** Two successive peak amplitudes `x₁` and `x₂` in a free-damped response, separated by one period `T_d`.

**Step 1:** From the damped response equation:

$$x_n = X \cdot e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_0 + n T_d)}$$

**Step 2:** Take the ratio of two consecutive peaks (n and n+1):

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 3:** Take natural log to obtain logarithmic decrement δ:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \zeta \omega_n T_d$$

**Step 4:** Substitute `T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d = 2\pi/(\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})`:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for ζ:

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** For small damping (ζ < 0.3), the approximation `ζ ≈ δ/(2π)` is often acceptable and gives a quick check on experimental data.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A mass of 4 kg is attached to a spring of stiffness 1600 N/m. Find the natural frequency, time period, and critical damping coefficient. If the damping ratio is 0.1, find the damped natural frequency and the magnification factor at forcing frequency ω = 25 rad/s.

**Solution:**

Given: `m = 4 kg`, `k = 1600 N/m`, `ζ = 0.1`, `ω = 25 rad/s`.

Step 1 — Natural angular frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1600}{4}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Natural frequency:

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{20}{2\pi} = 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 3 — Natural time period:

$$T_n = \frac{1}{f_n} = \frac{1}{3.183} = 0.314 \text{ s}$$

Step 4 — Critical damping coefficient:

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{1600 \times 4} = 2 \times 80 = 160 \text{ N·s/m}$$

Step 5 — Actual damping coefficient:

$$c = \zeta \cdot c_c = 0.1 \times 160 = 16 \text{ N·s/m}$$

Step 6 — Damped natural frequency:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1 - 0.01} = 20 \times 0.99499 = 19.90 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 7 — Frequency ratio:

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{25}{20} = 1.25$$

Step 8 — Magnification factor:

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 1.5625)^2 + (2 \times 0.1 \times 1.25)^2}}$$

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(-0.5625)^2 + (0.25)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.3164 + 0.0625}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.3789}} = \frac{1}{0.6156} = 1.624$$

**Result Summary:**
- `ωₙ = 20 rad/s`, `fₙ = 3.183 Hz`, `Tₙ = 0.314 s`
- `c_c = 160 N·s/m`, `c = 16 N·s/m`
- `ωd = 19.90 rad/s`, `r = 1.25`, `MF = 1.624`

---
