## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the following terms with respect to a Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) vibrating system:*
*(a) Natural frequency, (b) Damping ratio, (c) Magnification factor.*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Natural Frequency (ωₙ):** It is the frequency at which a system oscillates freely when displaced from its equilibrium position and released, without any external excitation. For an SDOF mass–spring system:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

(b) **Damping Ratio (ζ):** It is the ratio of the actual damping coefficient `c` to the critical damping coefficient `c_c`:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

It classifies the system response as underdamped (ζ < 1), critically damped (ζ = 1), or overdamped (ζ > 1).

(c) **Magnification Factor (MF):** It is the ratio of the steady-state amplitude `X` of the forced response to the static deflection `Y` caused by the same peak force:

$$MF = \frac{X}{Y} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

MF indicates how much the dynamic amplitude amplifies compared to the static case.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is logarithmic decrement? Derive the relation connecting damping ratio and logarithmic decrement.*

**Model Answer:**

**Logarithmic Decrement (δ):** It is defined as the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes in a damped free vibration response:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

**Derivation:**

For a damped SDOF system under free vibration:

$$x(t) = X \, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

Two successive peaks are separated by one damped period `T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d`. Their amplitudes are:

$$x_n = X \, e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n} \quad , \quad x_{n+1} = X \, e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_n + T_d)}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{\zeta\omega_n \cdot 2\pi/\omega_d}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Solving for ζ:

$$\boxed{\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Forced Vibration and Magnification (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a forced, damped Single Degree of Freedom system subjected to harmonic excitation `F(t) = F₀ sin(ωt)`. Hence obtain the steady-state amplitude and phase angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine of mass 50 kg is mounted on a spring-damper system. The stiffness of the spring is 20000 N/m and the damping coefficient is 200 N·s/m. During operation, the machine experiences a harmonic force of 100 N at 8 Hz. Calculate: (i) Natural frequency, (ii) Damping ratio, (iii) Frequency ratio, (iv) Magnification factor, (v) Steady-state amplitude of vibration. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion:**

For a mass `m` attached to a spring `k` and damper `c`, with an external force `F(t) = F₀ sin(ωt)`:

$$\text{Inertia} + \text{Damping} + \text{Spring} = \text{External Force}$$

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Divide by m and introduce standard parameters:**

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

where $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$.

**Step 3 — Assume steady-state solution:**

$$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Then: $\dot{x}_p = X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$-X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta\omega_n X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + \omega_n^2 X \sin(\omega t - \phi) = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5 — Equate coefficients of sin and cos:**

Coefficient of sin(ωt − φ):
$$X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2) = \frac{F_0}{m}\cos\phi$$

Coefficient of cos(ωt − φ):
$$2\zeta\omega_n X\omega = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin\phi$$

**Step 6 — Square and add the two equations:**

$$X^2\left[(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2\right] = \left(\frac{F_0}{m}\right)^2$$

**Step 7 — Steady-state amplitude:**

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}} = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Step 8 — Phase angle:**

$$\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta\omega_n\omega}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** `m = 50 kg`, `k = 20000 N/m`, `c = 200 N·s/m`, `F₀ = 100 N`, `f = 8 Hz`.

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{20000}{50}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{20}{6.283} = 3.183 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{200}{2\sqrt{20000 \times 50}} = \frac{200}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{200}{2000} = 0.1$$

**(iii) Frequency ratio:**

Excitation angular frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 8 = 50.265 \text{ rad/s}$

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{50.265}{20} = 2.513$$

**(iv) Magnification factor:**

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 6.315)^2 + (2 \times 0.1 \times 2.513)^2}}$$

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(-5.315)^2 + (0.5026)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{28.249 + 0.2526}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{28.502}} = \frac{1}{5.339} = 0.1873$$

**(v) Steady-state amplitude:**

Static deflection: $X_s = F_0/k = 100/20000 = 0.005 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$

$$X = MF \times X_s = 0.1873 \times 5 = 0.937 \text{ mm}$$

**Summary of Results:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| ωₙ | 20 rad/s |
| fₙ | 3.183 Hz |
| ζ | 0.1 |
| r | 2.513 |
| MF | 0.187 |
| X | 0.937 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Since `r > √2 ≈ 1.414`, the system acts as a vibration isolator (amplitude less than static deflection).

---

### **Question B — Free Damped Vibration and Logarithmic Decrement (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion of a damped free vibration system and discuss the three cases of damping based on the damping ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a free vibration experiment on a mass–spring system, the successive peak amplitudes recorded are 28 mm, 22 mm, 17.4 mm, and 13.7 mm. The mass of the system is 5 kg and stiffness is 5000 N/m. Determine: (i) Logarithmic decrement, (ii) Damping ratio, (iii) Damping coefficient, (iv) Damped natural frequency, (v) Compare with theoretical undamped natural frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Derivation and Discussion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram:** A mass `m` is connected to a spring (stiffness `k`) and damper (coefficient `c`).

**Step 2 — Equation of motion:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 3 — Normalize:** Divide by m:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic equation:** Assume `x = e^{st}`:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

$$s = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Three Cases:**

| Case | Condition | Roots | Response Type |
|------|-----------|-------|---------------|
| **Underdamped** | ζ < 1 | Complex conjugate | $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$ — oscillatory decay |
| **Critically damped** | ζ = 1 | Real, repeated | $x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ — fastest non-oscillatory return |
| **Overdamped** | ζ > 1 | Real, distinct | $x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$ — slow non-oscillatory return |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** Successive peaks: `x₁ = 28 mm`, `x₂ = 22 mm`, `x₃ = 17.4 mm`, `x₄ = 13.7 mm`, `m = 5 kg`, `k = 5000 N/m`.

**(i) Logarithmic decrement (average of multiple cycles for accuracy):**

Using pairs:

$$\delta_1 = \ln\!\left(\frac{28}{22}\right) = \ln(1.2727) = 0.2412$$

$$\delta_2 = \ln\!\left(\frac{22}{17.4}\right) = \ln(1.2644) = 0.2346$$

$$\delta_3 = \ln\!\left(\frac{17.4}{13.7}\right) = \ln(1.2701) = 0.2390$$

$$\delta_{avg} = \frac{0.2412 + 0.2346 + 0.2390}{3} = 0.2383$$

**(ii) Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}} = \frac{0.2383}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + 0.2383^2}} = \frac{0.2383}{\sqrt{39.478 + 0.0568}} = \frac{0.2383}{\sqrt{39.535}} = \frac{0.2383}{6.288} = 0.0379$$

**(iii) Damping coefficient:**

$$c = 2\zeta\sqrt{km} = 2 \times 0.0379 \times \sqrt{5000 \times 5} = 2 \times 0.0379 \times 158.114 = 11.98 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**(iv) Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{5000}{5}} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.623 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 31.623\sqrt{1 - 0.00144} = 31.623 \times 0.99928 = 31.60 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_d = \frac{\omega_d}{2\pi} = \frac{31.60}{6.283} = 5.03 \text{ Hz}$$

**(v) Comparison:**

| Quantity | Undamped (theoretical) | Damped (experimental) |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| ω | 31.623 rad/s | 31.60 rad/s |
| f | 5.033 Hz | 5.03 Hz |

The difference is negligible (< 0.1%) because ζ is small — this confirms that light damping has little effect on natural frequency but significantly affects amplitude decay.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in the Vibration Lab Exam (and How to Avoid Them):**

1. **Confusing ωₙ with fₙ**: ωₙ is in rad/s; fₙ is in Hz. Always divide ωₙ by 2π to get fₙ. Marks lost in Step (i) of nearly every Part B question.

2. **Forgetting to square r in the MF formula**: The denominator has `(1 − r²)²`, NOT `(1 − r)²`. This is the most common algebraic slip.

3. **Using peak-to-peak values instead of peak amplitudes** for log decrement. Always use single-side peak amplitudes from the mean line.

4. **Mixing up magnification factor with transmissibility**: MF applies to *base excitation displacement amplification*; TR applies to *force transmitted to the foundation*. They differ by the numerator `√(1 + (2ζr)²)`.

5. **Sign error in phase angle formula**: The phase lag formula is `tan⁻¹(2ζr/(1−r²))`. For r > 1, the denominator is negative, so φ lies in the second quadrant (90° < φ < 180°).

6. **Not averaging multiple cycles**: A single cycle's log decrement has large random error. Always average 3–5 cycles for accuracy — KTU examiners award a mark for this specifically.

7. **Confusing damped with undamped frequency**: Many students compute `fₙ` and directly use it. Always compute ωd = ωₙ√(1−ζ²) when asked for *damped* values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Free and Forced Vibration Analysis**

**Definitions (must remember verbatim):**
- ☐ **Free Vibration** — system oscillates after a one-time disturbance; no external force during motion.
- ☐ **Forced Vibration** — continuous external excitation drives the system.
- ☐ **Natural Frequency (ωₙ)** — system's intrinsic frequency = √(k/m).
- ☐ **Critical Damping (c_c)** — minimum damping for non-oscillatory return = 2√(km).
- ☐ **Damping Ratio (ζ)** = c/c_c; classifies response regime.
- ☐ **Resonance** — when excitation frequency ω equals ωₙ; amplitude peaks.
- ☐ **Magnification Factor (MF)** — ratio of dynamic to static amplitude.
- ☐ **Transmissibility (TR)** — ratio of force transmitted to base to applied force.

**Key Numerical Values:**
- ☐ g = **9.81 m/s²**
- ☐ MF at resonance (damped) = 1/(2ζ)
- ☐ MF at resonance (undamped) → ∞
- ☐ TR = 1 at r = √2 (vibration isolator design limit)

**Experimental Procedure Highlights:**
- ☐ For **free vibration**: pull the mass to a fixed displacement, release; record peak amplitudes using vibrometer/oscilloscope.
- ☐ For **forced vibration**: vary motor frequency from low to high in steps; record amplitude at each step.
- ☐ Plot **amplitude vs excitation frequency**; identify resonance peak.
- ☐ Compute **logarithmic decrement** from successive peak amplitudes.

**Master Formulae (must commit to memory):**
- ☐ $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $f_n = \omega_n/(2\pi)$, $T_n = 1/f_n$
- ☐ $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$, $\zeta = c/c_c$
- ☐ $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- ☐ $MF = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$
- ☐ $TR = \sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$
- ☐ $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1})$, $\zeta = \delta/\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}$

**Engineering Significance:**
- ☐ r < 1 → operating *below* resonance (danger zone for forced response).
- ☐ r > √2 → system acts as a vibration isolator.
- ☐ Higher damping → lower resonance peak but worse high-frequency isolation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Any Non-Destructive Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Any Non-Destructive Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** is a branch of **Materials Evaluation Engineering** comprising a set of analytical techniques used in science and industry to evaluate the properties of a material, component, or system without causing damage or permanently altering its physical state. The objective is to detect **surface and sub-surface defects** (cracks, voids, inclusions, porosity, laminations, corrosion, weld defects) without impairing the serviceability of the tested part.

The term **NDT** is broadly synonymous with **Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** and **Non-Destructive Inspection (NDI)**. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PCMEL508 – Mechanical Engineering Lab**, students are expected to conduct at least one experiment from the standard list of NDT methods, record observations, plot calibration curves, and infer defect characteristics.

## 1.2 Classification of NDT Methods (KTU Board-Exam Friendly)

| Method | Abbreviation | Energy Used | Detects |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic Testing | UT | Mechanical (Sound) | Surface & sub-surface defects |
| Magnetic Particle Testing | MPT | Magnetic Field | Surface & near-surface defects |
| Dye Penetrant Testing | DPT | Capillary Action | Surface-open defects only |
| Eddy Current Testing | ECT | Electromagnetic | Surface & near-surface defects |
| Radiographic Testing | RT | Electromagnetic (X-ray/γ-ray) | Internal volumetric defects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Among these, **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**, **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)**, and **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** are the most commonly conducted experiments in undergraduate mechanical engineering laboratories due to their safety, low cost, and simplicity of demonstration.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Stethoscope"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a doctor examining a patient using a stethoscope, an X-ray machine, or an MRI scanner. None of these procedures cut the patient open, yet they reveal broken bones, tumors, or heart murmurs. **NDT is the engineering equivalent of a medical check-up for materials.** A doctor uses different tools (stethoscope for heart, X-ray for bones) because each defect type needs a different "energy" to be revealed. Similarly, an NDT engineer selects a method based on whether the defect is *surface-open*, *near-surface*, or *deeply buried*.

**Key Vocabulary Used Throughout NDT:**

- **Indication** – Any signal or response from the test that differs from the expected response.
- **Relevant Indication** – An indication caused by a defect.
- **Non-relevant Indication** – An indication caused by geometry, surface condition, or part marking (a "false alarm").
- **Flaw / Discontinuity** – A lack of continuity or an imperfection in the material.
- **Defect** – A flaw that, by code or specification, renders the part unacceptable.

## 1.4 Why is NDT Important? (Engineering Justification)

| Industry | Typical Application |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Inspection of turbine blades, fuselage welds, composite panels |
| Pressure Vessel / Boilers | Detection of fatigue cracks in service-exposed vessels |
| Railways | Ultrasonic testing of rails, axles, and wheels |
| Marine | Hull plate inspection, weld quality assurance |
| Power Generation | In-service inspection of pipes, rotors, turbine discs |
| Manufacturing | 100 % inspection of castings and forgings for critical parts |

The **ASTM (American Society for Testing and Materials)**, **ASME (American Society of Mechanical Engineers)**, and **IS 13805 / IS 3658 / IS 3659 (Bureau of Indian Standards)** provide codes governing NDT procedures used in industrial acceptance criteria.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section provides a rigorous treatment of the four NDT experiments most frequently allotted in the KTU laboratory examination.

---

## 2.1 Ultrasonic Testing (UT)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

UT uses **high-frequency mechanical sound waves** typically in the range of **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**. These waves are introduced into the test piece using a **piezoelectric transducer** (commonly a lead zirconate titanate — PZT crystal). The waves propagate through the material and reflect back from any discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion, back-wall).

> [!NOTE]
> **Piezoelectric Effect:** Discovered by **Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880**. When an alternating voltage is applied to a piezoelectric crystal, it deforms mechanically at the same frequency, producing sound waves. Conversely, an incoming sound wave deforms the crystal and generates a measurable voltage — this is how reflected echoes are detected.

### 2.1.2 Modes of UT Display

| Display Mode | Description | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **A-Scan** | Amplitude vs Time graph (1-D) | Flaw detection, thickness measurement |
| **B-Scan** | Cross-sectional image showing depth (2-D) | Internal profile imaging |
| **C-Scan** | Plan-view of defect location (2-D top-view) | Mapping of defect distribution |

### 2.1.3 Types of Sound Waves Used

1. **Longitudinal (L-wave / Compression wave):** Particle motion parallel to wave propagation. Travels in solids, liquids, gases. Velocity in steel ≈ **5920 m/s**.
2. **Transverse (T-wave / Shear wave):** Particle motion perpendicular to propagation. Travels only in solids. Velocity in steel ≈ **3250 m/s**.
3. **Surface (Rayleigh) waves:** Travel along the surface with elliptical particle motion. Penetration depth ≈ **one wavelength**.
4. **Lamb Waves:** Plate waves in thin sheets.

### 2.1.4 Pulse-Echo vs Through-Transmission

- **Pulse-Echo:** A single transducer sends and receives pulses. The **Time of Flight (TOF)** between the initial pulse and the flaw echo gives the flaw depth.
- **Through-Transmission (TR):** Two transducers face each other; a flaw reduces the received signal amplitude.

### 2.1.5 Velocity of Sound in Common Materials

| Material | Longitudinal Velocity $V_L$ (m/s) | Acoustic Impedance $Z$ (kg/m²·s) × 10⁶ |
|---|---|---|
| Steel | 5920 | 46.0 |
| Aluminium | 6320 | 17.0 |
| Copper | 4700 | 41.6 |
| Brass | 4400 | 36.4 |
| Cast Iron | 3500 – 5600 | 25.0 – 36.0 |
| Water | 1480 | 1.48 |
| Perspex / Acrylic | 2730 | 3.20 |

---

## 2.2 Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

When a ferromagnetic material (steel, iron, nickel, cobalt) is magnetized, **magnetic flux lines** travel through the material. A surface or near-surface discontinuity (crack, lap, seam) disturbs these flux lines, causing some to **leak out of the surface** at the defect location. This leakage field attracts finely divided **ferromagnetic particles** (dry powder or wet suspension), forming a visible **magnetic particle indication** outlining the defect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** MPT works ONLY on **ferromagnetic materials**. It cannot detect defects in austenitic stainless steel, aluminium, copper, or plastic. Defects must lie in a direction **roughly perpendicular (transverse)** to the magnetic flux lines to produce maximum leakage. If the crack runs parallel to the flux, it will be missed — necessitating **two-stage magnetization** (circular + longitudinal).

### 2.2.2 Methods of Magnetization

| Method | Equipment | Flux Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal Magnetization** | Electromagnetic yoke or coil | Along the length of the part |
| **Circular Magnetization** | Current passed through the part directly or via a central conductor | Circumferential around the part |
| **Yoke Method (Portable)** | Permanent magnet or AC/DC electromagnet yoke | Between the two poles |

### 2.2.3 Wet vs Dry Method

- **Wet Method:** Particles suspended in a liquid carrier (typically oil). Smaller particles (≈ 1–10 μm). Used for detecting **fine, tight cracks**. Higher sensitivity.
- **Dry Method:** Powder dusted over the surface. Larger particles (40–200 μm). Used in field inspections, rough surfaces.

---

## 2.3 Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

DPT exploits the **capillary action** of liquids to draw a brightly coloured penetrant into surface-breaking defects. The six procedural steps are universally codified under **ASTM E 1417 / IS 3659**.

### 2.3.2 Standard DPT Procedure (6 Steps)

1. **Pre-cleaning** — Remove oil, paint, dirt from the test surface using solvents, alkaline cleaners, or vapour degreasing.
2. **Application of Penetrant** — Apply red-dyed penetrant (typically a low-viscosity oil-based dye). **Dwell time: 10–30 minutes** (per ASTM E 1417) to allow capillary penetration into defects.
3. **Removal of Excess Penetrant** — Wipe or wash off the surface penetrant using water or a solvent cleaner. **Critical step**: too aggressive washing will pull penetrant OUT of the defects.
4. **Application of Developer** — Spray a white, hygroscopic developer (typically a fine powder in suspension). The developer **draws the trapped penetrant back out** of the defect by reverse capillary action (the penetrant bleeds out into the developer, leaving a red mark on the white background).
5. **Inspection** — Examine under adequate white light (≥ 1000 lux). Penetrant indications appear as **red lines or dots** against the white developer.
6. **Post-cleaning** — Remove all developer and penetrant to prevent corrosion or contamination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type vs Sensitivity:** Penetrants are classified as **Type I (Fluorescent)** and **Type II (Visible Dye — Red)**. Sensitivity is graded as **Level ½ (ultra-low), 1 (low), 2 (medium), 3 (high), 4 (ultra-high)** per ASTM E 1417. Undergraduate labs typically use **Type II, Level 2** (the common red dye penetrant kit).

### 2.3.3 Limitations of DPT

- Detects ONLY **surface-breaking defects** (no sub-surface capability).
- Surface must be clean; paint/coatings must be removed first.
- **Porous materials** (castings with rough surface) may give false indications.
- Limited to materials not affected by the cleaning solvents.

---

## 2.4 Eddy Current Testing (ECT)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

When an **alternating current** flows through a **test coil**, it generates a primary alternating magnetic field. When this coil is brought near a conductive material, **eddy currents** are induced in the material by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** (1831). These eddy currents generate a **secondary magnetic field** that opposes the primary field. Any change in the material (defect, change in conductivity, permeability, or thickness) alters the eddy current path and thereby alters the impedance of the test coil. This change is measured and displayed.

> [!TIP]
> **"The Magnetic Broom" Analogy:** Imagine sweeping a magnet over a metal floor — invisible "magnetic dust" (eddy currents) is stirred up in the metal. If there is a hidden crack, the dust patterns get disturbed. ECT is essentially measuring that disturbance.

### 2.4.2 Key Equations and Concepts

1. **Standard Depth of Penetration (δ):** The depth at which the eddy current density falls to **1/e (≈ 36.8%)** of its surface value:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

where

- $\rho$ = Electrical resistivity of the test material (Ω·m)
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)
- $f$ = Test frequency (Hz)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** = Permeability of free space
- $\mu_r$ = Relative permeability of the test material

2. **Effective Depth of Inspection ≈ 3δ** (≈ 95 % of eddy current response).

3. **Lift-off Effect:** As the probe-to-specimen distance increases, the impedance changes — used for **coating thickness measurement** but is a noise source in defect detection.

4. **Fill Factor:** For encircling coils, defined as the ratio of specimen cross-section area to coil cross-section area. Maximum sensitivity when fill factor approaches unity.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU theory/calculation question on NDT.**

| # | Formula | Quantity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Defect depth in pulse-echo UT | $V$ = velocity, $\Delta t$ = TOF between initial and flaw echo |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | Thickness in pulse-echo UT | $\Delta t$ between initial and back-wall echo |
| 3 | $V_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | Longitudinal wave velocity | $E$ = Young's modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $\rho$ = density |
| 4 | $V_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$ | Transverse wave velocity | $G$ = shear modulus |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$ | Reflection coefficient at interface | $Z = \rho V$ = Acoustic impedance |
| 6 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ | Critical angle for mode conversion (L→T wave) | Used in angle-beam transducer design |
| 7 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$ | Standard depth of penetration (ECT) | $\rho$ = resistivity, $f$ = frequency |
| 8 | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2r}$ | Magnetic field inside a long coil (MPT) | $N$ = turns, $I$ = current, $r$ = coil radius |
| 9 | $V_L$ (steel) ≈ **5920 m/s** | Standard longitudinal velocity | Memorize for numericals |
| 10 | $V_T$ (steel) ≈ **3250 m/s** | Standard transverse velocity | Memorize for numericals |

---

## 2.6 Comparative Summary of NDT Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A direct "Compare MPT, DPT, and UT" question is a very common 14-mark question. Use this table as a quick reference.

| Feature | UT | MPT | DPT | ECT | RT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Type | Sound | Magnetic | Capillary | Electromagnetic | X/γ rays |
| Detects | Surface + Sub-surface | Surface + Near-surface | Surface only | Surface + Near-surface | All |
| Material | Conductors & non-conductors | Ferromagnetic only | All non-porous | Conductors only | All |
| Sensitivity to small defects | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Surface prep required | Low–Medium | Low | **High** | Low | Low |
| Cost | Medium | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| Safety hazard | Low | Low | Low (volatile) | Low | **High (radiation)** |
| Records results? | Possible | Photograph | Photograph | Possible | **Permanent film** |
| In-situ use | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Difficult |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations of every KTU-exam-relevant equation and worked-out numerical problems.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity of Longitudinal Waves in a Solid

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Hooke's Law and Equation of Motion

Consider a longitudinal wave travelling through an infinite solid rod. The stress at any point is given by Hooke's law:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon$$

where $\sigma$ is stress, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $\varepsilon = \partial u / \partial x$ is the strain (derivative of displacement $u$ with respect to position $x$).

Newton's second law applied to a thin slice of the rod gives:

$$\rho \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial t^{2}} = \dfrac{\partial \sigma}{\partial x}$$

Substituting Hooke's law:

$$\rho \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial t^{2}} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(E \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)$$

For a uniform rod with constant $E$:

$$\rho \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial t^{2}} = E \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$$

This is the classic 1-D wave equation, whose general solution is a sinusoidal travelling wave with velocity $V_L$ given by:

$$V_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$$

### 3.1.2 Extension to 3-D Infinite Medium (Real Solids)

For an infinite 3-D solid, lateral contraction is restrained, which introduces the Poisson effect. The governing equation becomes (after detailed derivation using the Lamé constants $\lambda$ and $\mu$):

$$V_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho (1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$$

For steel with $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 0.29$:

$$V_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{200 \times 10^{9} \times 0.71}{7850 \times 1.29 \times 0.42}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.42 \times 10^{11}}{4253}} = \sqrt{3.34 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5780 \text{ to } 5920 \text{ m/s}$$

This matches the accepted standard value of **5920 m/s**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Velocity of Transverse (Shear) Waves

Shear waves propagate via shearing deformation only. Using shear modulus $G$:

$$V_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}}$$

Since $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$:

$$V_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2 \rho (1+\nu)}}$$

For steel:

$$V_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{200 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 7850 \times 1.29}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200 \times 10^{9}}{20253}} = \sqrt{9.87 \times 10^{6}} \approx 3142 \text{ to } 3250 \text{ m/s}$$

The standard accepted value is **3250 m/s**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pulse-Echo Equation $d = V \Delta t / 2$

### 3.3.1 Setup

The transducer emits an ultrasonic pulse at time $t = 0$. The pulse travels DOWN the specimen, hits a flaw at depth $d$, and REFLECTS back. The transducer receives the echo at time $t = \Delta t$.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

Total distance travelled by the sound wave = $d + d = 2d$.

Therefore, the time-of-flight is:

$$\Delta t = \dfrac{\text{total distance}}{\text{velocity}} = \dfrac{2d}{V}$$

Solving for $d$:

$$\boxed{d = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical 1: Defect Depth in Pulse-Echo UT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Standard Numericals** — this is a direct past-year KTU question style.

**Problem:** An ultrasonic pulse-echo test is performed on a steel plate using a **5 MHz** normal probe. The longitudinal velocity in steel is **5920 m/s**. The flaw echo appears on the CRT at a delay of **8 microseconds** after the initial pulse. Calculate the depth of the flaw from the entry surface.

**Step 1:** Identify given quantities.
- Velocity $V_L = 5920 \text{ m/s}$
- Time of flight $\Delta t = 8 \, \mu s = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$

**Step 2:** Apply pulse-echo equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{V_L \cdot \Delta t}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{5920 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{4.736 \times 10^{-2}}{2} \\
&= 2.368 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to millimetres.

$$d = 2.368 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000 = 23.68 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** The flaw is located at a depth of **≈ 23.68 mm** from the entry surface.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical 2: Thickness Measurement by UT

**Problem:** In a pulse-echo UT inspection of an aluminium plate, the back-wall echo appears at **10 µs**. Given $V_L$ in aluminium = **6320 m/s**, calculate the plate thickness.

**Step 1:** Given data.

$$V_L = 6320 \text{ m/s}, \quad \Delta t = 10 \, \mu s = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{V_L \cdot \Delta t}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{6320 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{6.32 \times 10^{-2}}{2} \\
&= 3.16 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \\
&= 31.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** Plate thickness ≈ **31.6 mm**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical 3: Critical Angle for Mode Conversion (Snell's Law for Sound)

**Problem:** An angle-beam probe generates longitudinal waves in a Perspex wedge. The longitudinal velocity in Perspex is **2730 m/s**, and the longitudinal velocity in steel is **5920 m/s**. Find the **critical angle** at which the longitudinal wave is totally refracted (and mode-converted into a transverse wave) in steel.

**Step 1:** Apply Snell's law (valid for wavefronts).

$$\dfrac{\sin \theta_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_2}{V_2}$$

At critical angle $\theta_c$, the refracted longitudinal wave in steel propagates at $\theta_2 = 90°$.

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$\dfrac{\sin \theta_c}{2730} = \dfrac{\sin 90°}{5920} = \dfrac{1}{5920}$$

**Step 3:** Solve.

$$\begin{aligned}
\sin \theta_c &= \dfrac{2730}{5920} = 0.4611 \\
\theta_c &= \sin^{-1}(0.4611) \\
\theta_c &\approx 27.46°
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** Critical angle for L-wave in Perspex-steel interface ≈ **27.46°**.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard angle-beam probes are designed at angles **30°, 45°, 60°, 70°**, all of which exceed $\theta_c$. This is why angle-beam probes in steel use only **transverse waves** — longitudinal waves are completely suppressed at the wedge-to-steel interface.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical 4: Standard Depth of Penetration (ECT)

**Problem:** Find the standard depth of penetration in aluminium during an ECT inspection at frequency **100 kHz**. Take $\rho_{Al} = 2.82 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$ and $\mu_r = 1$.

**Step 1:** Given values.

$$\rho = 2.82 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m, \quad f = 100 \times 10^{3} \text{ Hz}, \quad \mu_r = 1, \quad \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the depth-of-penetration formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 100 \times 10^{3} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2566}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{2.82 \times 10^{-8}}{1.579 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.786 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&\approx 0.1336 \text{ m} \\
&\approx 133.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $\delta \approx$ **133.6 mm** (effective inspection depth ≈ $3\delta \approx$ **400 mm**).

---

## 3.8 Worked Numerical 5: Reflection Coefficient at Steel–Air Interface

**Problem:** A longitudinal ultrasonic wave travels through steel and strikes an air-filled crack. Calculate the fraction of incident intensity reflected back. Use $V_{steel} = 5920 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{air} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $V_{air} = 343 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1:** Acoustic impedance in steel.

$$Z_1 = \rho_1 V_1 = 7850 \times 5920 = 4.6472 \times 10^{7} \text{ kg/m}^2 \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Acoustic impedance in air.

$$Z_2 = 1.2 \times 343 = 411.6 \text{ kg/m}^2 \text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Reflection coefficient (amplitude).

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2} \\
&= \dfrac{411.6 - 4.6472 \times 10^{7}}{4.6472 \times 10^{7} + 411.6} \\
&\approx \dfrac{-4.6472 \times 10^{7}}{4.6472 \times 10^{7}} \\
&\approx -0.99982
\end{aligned}$$

The amplitude reflection coefficient is essentially $-1$ (perfect reflection with phase inversion).

**Step 4:** Intensity (power) reflection coefficient.

$$R_I = R^{2} \approx 0.9996 \; (\approx 100\%)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is why air-backed defects produce a STRONG echo.** Almost all the sound reflects. This is the basis of pulse-echo detection — even a hairline crack reflects essentially the entire incident energy.

---

## 3.9 Worked Numerical 6: Couplant Selection (Acoustic Impedance Matching)

**Problem:** Why is gel/couplant used between the probe and the test surface in UT? Compute the reflection coefficient at a probe-couplant interface if probe impedance $Z_P = 30 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$ and couplant (water) impedance $Z_C = 1.48 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{Z_C - Z_P}{Z_P + Z_C} \\
&= \dfrac{1.48 \times 10^{6} - 30 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{6} + 1.48 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \dfrac{-28.52 \times 10^{6}}{31.48 \times 10^{6}} \\
&\approx -0.906
\end{aligned}$$

So 81 % of intensity ($R^2 = 0.821$) is reflected back at the probe–couplant interface — which is BAD.

**Step 2:** Compare to direct probe–air contact ($Z_{air} = 411.6 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{s}$).

$$R_{\text{air}} \approx \dfrac{411.6 - 30 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{6}} \approx -1.0 \; (\approx 100\% \; \text{reflected})$$

**Conclusion:** A couplant (water, gel, oil, glycerine) reduces the impedance mismatch and allows the ultrasound to enter the part. Without couplant, **99.9 % of the ultrasound is reflected back at the probe-air interface** and NO inspection is possible. This is why a wet couplant layer is essential.

flowchart TD

## 4.5 Comparative Decision Flow — Selecting the Right NDT Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Material Type] --> B{Ferromagnetic?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Defect Location?}
    B -- No --> D{Conductive?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Defect Type?}
    D -- No --> F[Use UT or RT]
    C -- Surface Only --> G[Use MPT or DPT]
    C -- Sub Surface --> H[Use UT or RT]
    E -- Surface Cracks --> I[Use ECT or DPT]
    E -- Sub Surface --> J[Use ECT]
    F --> K{Internal Defect?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Use RT]
    K -- No --> M[Use UT]
```

---

## 4.6 Schematic — Pulse-Echo A-Scan Display Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Pulse<br/>t = 0] --> B[Couplant Delay<br/>t equals 2 to 5 microsecond]
    B --> C[Flaw Echo<br/>Amplitude A1]
    C --> D[Back Wall Echo<br/>t equals 2T per V]
    D --> E[CRT Horizontal Axis equals Time or Depth]
```

**Interpretation Rules:**

- Distance between **initial pulse and flaw echo** $\propto$ depth of flaw.
- Distance between **flaw echo and back-wall echo** $\propto$ metal behind the flaw.
- **Amplitude of flaw echo** $\propto$ flaw size (larger defects reflect more).
- For comparing two defect sizes: use **AVG (Area-Void-Gain)** diagram or **DGS (Distance-Gain-Size)** diagram.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Bank aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Cognitive Levels:** Remembering (L1), Understanding (L2), Applying (L3), Analysing (L4), Evaluating (L5), Creating (L6).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Non-Destructive Testing. List any four common NDT methods.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** is a group of inspection techniques used to evaluate the properties, integrity, and defects of a material, component, or assembly **without causing damage** or impairing its future usefulness.
>
> Four common NDT methods are:
> 1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — uses high-frequency sound waves.
> 2. **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** — uses magnetic flux leakage.
> 3. **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** — uses capillary action of penetrant dye.
> 4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — uses X-rays or gamma rays.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: What is a piezoelectric effect? Name the material commonly used as a transducer in UT.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain crystals to convert mechanical stress into electrical charge (and vice versa). **Pierre and Jacques Curie** discovered this effect in 1880.
>
> In Ultrasonic Testing, an alternating voltage applied to a piezoelectric crystal causes it to vibrate mechanically at the same frequency, generating ultrasonic waves. Conversely, when ultrasonic waves strike the crystal, they generate an electrical signal that can be measured.
>
> The material commonly used is **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**. Other materials include quartz, lithium sulphate, and barium titanate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B1(A) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **Pulse-Echo Ultrasonic Testing** method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** In Pulse-Echo UT, a single transducer alternately transmits short bursts of ultrasonic pulses into the test piece and receives the echoes reflected from discontinuities or the back-wall.

**Block Diagram:**

1. **Pulse Generator** — produces high-voltage (200–400 V) short-duration pulses.
2. **Synchronizer (Time Base)** — synchronizes the pulse transmission with the display sweep.
3. **Transducer** — a piezoelectric crystal (PZT) that converts electrical pulses into mechanical vibrations and vice versa.
4. **Test Piece** — the specimen under inspection.
5. **Receiver/Amplifier** — amplifies the weak echo signals (often with gain 40–100 dB).
6. **Display Unit (CRT/LCD)** — shows the A-scan (Amplitude vs Time).

**Working:** The synchronizer triggers the pulse generator, which excites the transducer. The transducer sends a pulse into the test piece via a couplant. When the pulse encounters a discontinuity (flaw), part of it is reflected back. The transducer receives the echo, converts it into an electrical signal, which is amplified and displayed on the CRT as a vertical deflection.

**A-Scan Display:**
- A tall initial pulse (transmitted pulse) at $t = 0$.
- A smaller flaw echo at time $\Delta t$, with $\Delta t = 2d/V$.
- A back-wall echo at $t = 2T/V$, where $T$ is thickness.

**Defect depth calculation:**

$$d = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

---

**(b)** A 10 mm thick steel plate is tested by pulse-echo UT using a **4 MHz** probe. The longitudinal wave velocity in steel is **5920 m/s**. The flaw echo appears **5 microseconds** after the initial pulse. Calculate **(i)** the flaw depth and **(ii)** the metal distance beyond the flaw. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $T = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$
- Velocity $V_L = 5920 \text{ m/s}$
- Flaw echo time $\Delta t = 5 \, \mu s = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$
- Back-wall echo time $t_{BW} = \dfrac{2T}{V_L} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.01}{5920} = 3.378 \, \mu s$

**(i) Flaw Depth (d):**

$$d = \dfrac{V_L \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \dfrac{5920 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 1.48 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 14.8 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Metal Beyond Flaw:**

$$\text{Metal beyond} = T - d = 10 - 14.8 = -4.8 \text{ mm}$$

Since the metal beyond the flaw is negative, this indicates the flaw is **NOT** within the 10 mm plate — either the test piece is thicker than 10 mm, or the flaw echo corresponds to a **second back-wall reflection** (i.e., a multiple echo).

**Back-wall echo check:** $t_{BW} = 3.378 \, \mu s$. The flaw echo at 5 µs is greater than the back-wall echo, so the flaw echo is the **first back-wall echo**. The metal is actually beyond first back-wall echo position = 10 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget that **echoes appear at multiples of the back-wall echo time** (i.e., $t_{BW}, 2 t_{BW}, 3 t_{BW}, \ldots$). A peak at $5 \, \mu s$ with $t_{BW} = 3.378 \, \mu s$ is suspicious. Always verify the echo position is consistent with a real defect.

---

### Question B1(B) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a flowchart, describe the **Magnetic Particle Testing** procedure. Discuss its **limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** When a ferromagnetic specimen is magnetized, magnetic flux lines pass through it. A surface or near-surface defect causes the flux lines to leak out of the surface (flux leakage). Ferromagnetic particles applied to the surface are attracted to the leakage field, forming visible indications.

**Procedure (Flowchart):**

1. **Pre-cleaning** of the surface.
2. **Magnetization** of the part using:
   - Yoke (portable AC or DC electromagnet).
   - Coil (longitudinal magnetization).
   - Direct current through the part (circular magnetization).
3. **Application of magnetic particles** — dry powder or wet suspension.
4. **Inspection** under white light (for dry method) or UV-A light (for fluorescent wet method).
5. **Demagnetization** of the part after inspection (otherwise residual magnetism interferes with future use, especially in bearings and instruments).
6. **Post-cleaning and reporting.**

**Limitations:**
- Applicable **only to ferromagnetic materials** (cannot inspect austenitic stainless steel, aluminium, copper).
- Defect must lie **transverse** to the flux direction; **requires two-stage magnetization** to detect all orientations.
- **Sub-surface defects deeper than ≈ 3 mm** may not be detected.
- **Demagnetization is essential** to prevent service issues.
- **Operator skill** is critical for interpretation.

---

**(b)** Explain the **six-step procedure of Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** with neat sketches. State any **two advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Six Steps:**

1. **Pre-cleaning** — Remove oil, grease, paint using solvents, alkaline cleaners, or vapour degreasing. Surface must be clean and dry.
2. **Penetrant Application** — Spray or brush red (or fluorescent) penetrant over the surface. Allow dwell time of 10–30 minutes to permit capillary penetration into cracks.
3. **Removal of Excess Penetrant** — Wipe with a cloth dampened in solvent cleaner, or rinse with water (water-washable penetrant). Must not over-wash, else penetrant is pulled out of defects.
4. **Developer Application** — Spray a thin, even coat of white developer. The developer draws penetrant back out of defects (reverse capillary action), forming visible red indications.
5. **Inspection** — Examine under adequate white light (≥ 1000 lux for visible dye). Fluorescent penetrants require UV-A illumination (365 nm).
6. **Post-cleaning** — Remove developer and penetrant residues to prevent contamination or corrosion.

**Advantages:**
1. Works on all **non-porous materials** (metals, plastics, ceramics, glass).
2. Low cost, simple equipment, easy interpretation.

**Limitations:**
1. Detects only **surface-breaking defects** — no sub-surface capability.
2. Porous materials (castings, ceramics) absorb penetrant and give **false indications**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = V \Delta t / 2$ — this is the most common mistake. Always remember: the pulse travels **to the flaw and back**, so the total distance is $2d$, not $d$.
>
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — $\mu s$ must be converted to seconds ($10^{-6}$ s) before calculation. Mark loss if units are mismatched.
>
> 3. **Mixing up velocity values** — Longitudinal and transverse velocities are different. For steel: $V_L \approx 5920$ m/s, $V_T \approx 3250$ m/s. Angle-beam probes use $V_T$.
>
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of "ferromagnetic" for MPT** — examiners deduct marks if you do not specify this.
>
> 5. **DPT dwell time** — examiners expect 10–30 min per ASTM E 1417. Writing "few minutes" loses marks.
>
> 6. **In MPT, forgetting demagnetization step** — at least 1–2 marks lost.
>
> 7. **Drawing diagrams with no labels** — every block, arrow, and waveform must be labelled to get full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and use it the night before the lab exam.**

### 🔑 Top 10 Things You MUST Remember

1. **NDT Definition** — Inspection without damaging the part. Synonym: NDE / NDI.
2. **Pulse-Echo Equation** — $d = V \Delta t / 2$. The factor **2** is non-negotiable.
3. **Standard Velocities in Steel** — $V_L = 5920$ m/s, $V_T = 3250$ m/s. Memorize these.
4. **Acoustic Impedance** — $Z = \rho V$. Reflection coefficient $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_1 + Z_2)$.
5. **Piezoelectric Material** — Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT).
6. **Couplant Function** — Eliminates the air gap so ultrasound can enter the part. Without couplant, 99.9 % of ultrasound is reflected.
7. **DPT Six Steps** — Pre-clean → Penetrant → Remove → Developer → Inspect → Post-clean.
8. **MPT Limitation** — Only ferromagnetic materials. Demagnetization is essential after inspection.
9. **Critical Angle** — $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(V_1/V_2)$. Angle-beam probes use $V_T$ in steel.
10. **ECT Penetration Depth** — $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r)}$. Effective inspection ≈ $3\delta$.

### ⚠ Common Mistakes to Avoid

- Don't confuse **DPT** (uses liquid dye, capillary action) with **MPT** (uses magnetic particles, magnetic flux).
- Don't claim DPT can detect sub-surface defects — it can't.
- Don't apply MPT to aluminium — it won't work.
- Don't say "ultrasonic waves are electromagnetic" — they are **mechanical** (sound) waves.
- Don't forget that **radiographic testing is hazardous** and requires licensed operators (KTU examiner expects safety awareness).

### 📋 Typical KTU Lab Record Format

When submitting your record book for an NDT experiment, include:

1. **Aim** — one-line statement.
2. **Apparatus** — list of equipment.
3. **Theory** — short principle, equations.
4. **Procedure** — step-wise.
5. **Observations** — table with values.
6. **Calculations** — use formulas; show steps.
7. **Graph** — if any (e.g., calibration curve).
8. **Result** — depth/thickness with units and tolerance.
9. **Safety Precautions** — always mention.
10. **Conclusion** — one or two lines.

### 🎯 Quick-Recall Comparison Table

| Method | Energy | Detects | Material Restriction | Surface Prep | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| UT | Sound | All | None | Couplant needed | Medium |
| MPT | Magnetic | Surface/near-surface | Ferromagnetic | Low | Low |
| DPT | Capillary | Surface only | Non-porous | **High** | Low |
| ECT | EM induction | Surface/near-surface | Conductive | Low | Medium |
| RT | X/γ-ray | All (volumetric) | None | Low | **High + Hazard** |

> **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme, PCMEL508 Module 2 — NDT**
> *Prepared by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuits Using Trainer Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuits Using Trainer Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Hydraulic Trainer Unit?

A **hydraulic trainer unit** is a self-contained, modular educational workstation that replicates an industrial hydraulic power and control system at a safe, bench-top scale. It integrates a prime mover (electric motor), a hydraulic power pack (reservoir + pump + filters), a bank of standardized **hydraulic valves** (DCV, PCV, FCV), and one or more **hydraulic actuators** (single-acting / double-acting cylinders, hydraulic motors) connected through quick-disconnect hoses and a graphical **layout diagram (synoptic panel)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (per PCMEL508 / 2024 Scheme):**
> A hydraulic trainer is a didactic apparatus that allows the student to physically assemble, operate, troubleshoot, and document **closed-loop fluid power circuits** using ISO 1219-2 compliant symbols on a printed panel, with measurable parameters such as **pressure (bar)**, **flow rate (LPM)**, **stroke time (s)**, and **thrust force (N)**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Bloodstream of a Machine"

Think of the human body:
- The **heart** = hydraulic pump (pushes fluid)
- **Blood (oxygenated)** = hydraulic oil under pressure
- **Arteries** = high-pressure lines (P-line)
- **Veins** = return / tank line (T-line)
- **Muscles** = hydraulic cylinders (convert fluid energy into mechanical work)
- **Brain signals** = solenoid-operated direction control valves (DCV)

Just as the brain decides whether your arm should extend or retract, a DCV decides whether the cylinder extends or retracts. The trainer unit lets you "rewire the brain" instantly using push-pull levers or push-buttons.

## 1.2 What is a Pneumatic Trainer Unit?

A **pneumatic trainer unit** operates on the same principle but uses **compressed air** as the working fluid instead of oil. The supply is typically a **compressor + FRL unit** (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) regulated to **6 bar (≈ 600 kPa)** working pressure. Components are **ISO 5599 / ISO 1219-1** compliant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Contrast — Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic (Syllabus Highlight):**
>
> | Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
> |---|---|---|
> | Working fluid | Mineral oil (ISO VG 32/46) | Filtered, lubricated air |
> | Typical working pressure | **50 – 200 bar** | **4 – 8 bar** |
> | Force density | Very high (kN) | Moderate (N–hundreds of N) |
> | Compressibility | Negligible | **Significant** (≈ 90 % compressible) |
> | Lubrication | Self (oil) | External (FRL) |
> | Leak sensitivity | Low | High |
> | Speed of response | Slower (oil inertia) | **Very fast** |
> | Used in | Presses, lifts, injection moulding | Pick-and-place, clamping, packaging |

### Intuitive Analogy — "Air vs. Water in a Straw"

Take a drinking straw in a glass of water and one in a glass of air:
- The water straw delivers **instant, forceful thrust** — that's hydraulics.
- The air straw gives a **soft, springy, fast push** — that's pneumatics.

This is why pneumatic systems are preferred for **fast, repetitive, light-load** automation (e.g., bottle-capping, PCB insertion), while hydraulic systems are chosen for **heavy-load, high-force** applications (e.g., injection moulding clamp, excavator arm).

## 1.3 Standard Symbols (ISO 1219) You MUST Know

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU lab exam will include a circuit diagram question. Memorize these **base symbols** and the **square-box convention**.

| Component | ISO Symbol | Box Rule |
|---|---|---|
| 3/2 DCV, spring return, push-button actuated | One square, two positions, arrow + T-block | **Envelopes** = blocked ports |
| 5/2 DCV, double-solenoid, detented | Two squares, two positions, two arrows | Arrows show **permitted flow** |
| 5/3 DCV, closed centre, solenoid-solenoid | Three squares, middle position blocks all | Centre envelope = "O" |
| Pressure relief valve (direct-acting) | Spring-loaded arrow against P-line | Set = cracking pressure |
| Flow control valve (throttle check) | "Bow-tie" + parallel check valve | Throttle one direction, free return |
| Single-acting cylinder | Piston with **one rod**, spring at one end | One port only |
| Double-acting cylinder | Piston with **rod both sides**, two ports | Differential area |
| Shuttle / OR valve | Two arrows feeding a single outlet | Logic = OR |
| Twin-pressure / AND valve | Two P-ports, one outlet, both balls | Logic = AND |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Memory)

- Standard atmospheric pressure: **P_atm = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa**
- Standard hydraulic working pressure on trainer: **P_w = 50 bar = 5 × 10⁶ Pa**
- Standard pneumatic working pressure on trainer: **P_w = 6 bar = 600 kPa**
- Density of hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46): **ρ ≈ 870 kg/m³**
- Specific heat capacity of oil: **c_p ≈ 1.9 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Universal gas constant (for pneumatics): **R = 287 J/(kg·K)** for air
- Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil: **β ≈ 1.5 – 2.0 GPa**

---

> **End of Section 1.** Continue to Section 2 for the formal theory and formula sheet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard Trainer

A trainer, whether hydraulic or pneumatic, is always built around the same five functional sub-systems. Understanding them in this sequence helps you troubleshoot any KTU lab viva question.

### 2.1.1 The Power Pack (Energy Source)

1. **Reservoir / Tank** — stores the working fluid, allows air-separation and cooling.
2. **Pump / Compressor** — converts mechanical energy into fluid (pressure) energy.
3. **Prime Mover** — electric motor (typically **0.5 – 1.5 kW**, 1450 rpm) for hydraulic; reciprocating/compressor for pneumatic.
4. **Pressure Gauge** — must be present on **P-line** AND **P2 (consumer) line** for verification.
5. **FRL** (pneumatic only) — **Filter (5 µm)** → **Regulator (0–10 bar)** → **Lubricator (oil mist)**.

### 2.1.2 The Control Bank (Valve Manifold)

Mounted on a vertical board with colour-coded, labelled ports. Typical layout:
- P (red) — Pressure line
- T (blue) — Tank line
- A, B (green) — Working ports to actuator
- L, R or Y — Pilot / exhaust (for external pilot valves)

### 2.1.3 The Actuator (Load End)

- **Linear**: Single-acting cylinder (SAC) and Double-acting cylinder (DAC).
- **Rotary**: Pneumatic motor, hydraulic motor (used in advanced trainer kits).

### 2.1.4 The Measurement Panel

Digital or analog gauges for **P, Q, T** (Pressure, Flow, Temperature). Some modern kits include a USB data-acquisition module.

### 2.1.5 The Layout / Synoptic Panel

A printed drawing of every component on the board with **pilot-light LEDs** showing live status of valves and actuators. This is what the student references while sketching the circuit.

## 2.2 Common Hydraulic Trainer Exercises (KTU Lab Syllabus)

| Ex. No. | Circuit | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| H-1 | Direct control of SAC with 3/2 DCV | Manual hand-pump / clamp |
| H-2 | Direct control of DAC with 4/2 DCV | Bidirectional press |
| H-3 | Speed control (meter-out) of DAC | Slow extension under load |
| H-4 | Sequence circuit using **pressure switch** | Clamp → drill (sequenced by pressure) |
| H-5 | Counterbalance valve on vertical cylinder | Suspended load holding |
| H-6 | Regenerative (intensifier) circuit | High-speed extension, force-boosted retract |
| H-7 | Dual-pressure circuit (unloading valve) | High/low pressure selection |
| H-8 | Deceleration using counterbalance + FCV | Smooth stop of heavy mass |

## 2.3 Common Pneumatic Trainer Exercises (KTU Lab Syllabus)

| Ex. No. | Circuit | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| P-1 | 3/2 push-button valve → SAC (direct control) | Marking / stamping |
| P-2 | 3/2 roller valve (limit switch) → SAC | Automatic door closer |
| P-3 | 5/2 double-solenoid → DAC with **memory** | Latch-type clamping |
| P-4 | Quick-exhaust valve on DAC | High-speed ejection |
| P-5 | AND / OR logic gates (shuttle + dual-pressure) | Safety interlock |
| P-6 | Time-delay valve (on-delay) | Sequential spraying |
| P-7 | **A+A−B+B−** sequence using 5/3 closed-centre | A-extend, B-extend, A-retract, B-retract |
| P-8 | Speed control with flow control valve + silencer | Conveyor diverting |

## 2.4 Theory Behind the "Why" — Fundamental Equations

### 2.4.1 Pascal's Law (Foundation of Hydraulics)

Pressure applied to an **enclosed, incompressible fluid** is transmitted **equally and undiminished** in all directions. This is the reason a small force on a small piston can lift a heavy load on a large piston.

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

Hence the **force multiplication** (or **mechanical advantage**) of a hydraulic press:

$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

where D = piston diameter.

### 2.4.2 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass / Volume)

For an **incompressible** fluid in a closed hydraulic loop, the volume entering a cylinder per second equals the rate of change of cylinder volume:

$$Q = A_p \cdot v_p = A_r \cdot v_r$$

where:
- $A_p$ = piston-side (full bore) area
- $A_r$ = rod-side (annulus) area
- $v_p$, $v_r$ = piston and rod-side velocities

This is why a **DAC extends faster** than it retracts (under equal flow), since $A_p > A_r$.

### 2.4.3 Power Equation

The hydraulic power delivered to a cylinder is:

$$P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q = F \cdot v$$

The **pump input power** required is:

$$P_{pump,in} = \frac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_{pump}}$$

For the motor–pump group, total efficiency is $\eta_{total} = \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{elec}$.

### 2.4.4 Cylinder Stroke Time

The time to complete one stroke is volume divided by flow rate:

$$t = \frac{V_{cylinder}}{Q} = \frac{A \cdot L}{Q}$$

where $L$ = stroke length (m) and $A$ = active area (m²).

### 2.4.5 Pneumatic Force (with Air Compressibility)

Because air is compressible, the **theoretical force** on a pneumatic cylinder is:

$$F = p_{gauge} \cdot A$$

The **effective force** is reduced by friction, rod-side back-pressure, and load-induced pressure drop:

$$F_{eff} = \eta_{mech} \cdot p_{gauge} \cdot A - F_{friction}$$

Typical mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech} ≈ 0.85$ to $0.95$.

### 2.4.6 Throttling / Flow Control Equation (Hydraulic)

For an **orifice-type flow control valve**, the flow is governed by:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.6 – 0.7 for sharp-edged orifice)
- $A_v$ = valve orifice area
- $\Delta p$ = pressure drop across valve
- $\rho$ = fluid density

This is why throttling causes a **pressure drop** → heat generation → need for a heat exchanger in continuous-duty hydraulic systems.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim. It covers 90 % of KTU lab calculation questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure | $P = F / A$ | Pa (N/m²) | 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa |
| 2 | Force from cylinder | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ for piston side |
| 3 | Annulus (rod) area | $A_r = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$ | m² | d = rod diameter |
| 4 | Hydraulic power | $P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$ | W | p in Pa, Q in m³/s |
| 5 | Mechanical power | $P_{mech} = F \cdot v$ | W | v in m/s |
| 6 | Pump input power | $P_{in} = (p \cdot Q)/\eta$ | W | $\eta$ is overall efficiency |
| 7 | Cylinder velocity | $v = Q / A$ | m/s | Q in m³/s |
| 8 | Stroke time | $t = A \cdot L / Q$ | s | L = stroke length |
| 9 | Force ratio (press) | $F_2/F_1 = (D_2/D_1)^2$ | – | Pascal's law |
| 10 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho v D_h / \mu$ | – | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter |
| 11 | Compressor (pneumatic) power | $P_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot T_1 \cdot [(\frac{p_2}{p_1})^{\frac{k-1}{k}} - 1]$ | W | Isentropic compression |
| 12 | Ideal gas law (pneumatic) | $p \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$ | – | For tank sizing |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does KTU include this lab?**
> 1. **Mechatronics foundation** — every modern machine tool (CNC, robotic arm, plastic injection moulding) has hydraulic or pneumatic sub-systems.
> 2. **Industrial safety** — wrong sequencing in a press can kill an operator. The lab trains you to design **fail-safe** circuits.
> 3. **Energy efficiency** — unloaded lines waste energy; the lab teaches unloading valves, accumulator circuits, and load-sensing.
> 4. **Troubleshooting skill** — 70 % of mechanical engineering field service jobs involve fluid power systems.
> 5. **Preparation for GATE / IES** — fluid power is a recurring topic in Indian competitive exams.

---

> **End of Section 2.** Continue to Section 3 for solved derivations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The seven derivations below are EXHAUSTIVE. Every algebraic step is shown — do not skip, do not summarise.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thrust Force and Output Pressure of a Hydraulic Press

**Problem Statement:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $D_1 = 30$ mm and a large piston of diameter $D_2 = 150$ mm. A force of $F_1 = 200$ N is applied to the small piston. Calculate (a) the pressure developed in the fluid, (b) the force generated on the large piston, (c) the velocity ratio, and (d) the load that can be lifted if mechanical efficiency is 90 %.

### Given Data

$D_1 = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m

$D_2 = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m

$F_1 = 200$ N

$\eta_{mech} = 0.90$

### Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the small piston

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.030)^2$$

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Pressure developed in the fluid (Pascal's Law)

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$P = 2.829 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.829 \text{ bar} = 282.9 \text{ kPa}$$

### Step 3 — Cross-sectional area of the large piston

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.150)^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0225 = 1.7671 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Ideal (theoretical) force on the large piston

$$F_{2,ideal} = P \cdot A_2 = 2.829 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.7671 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$F_{2,ideal} = 4999.8 \text{ N} \approx 5000 \text{ N} = 5.0 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 5 — Verify using force ratio formula

$$\frac{F_2}{F_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{150}{30}\right)^2 = 25$$

$$F_2 = 25 \cdot 200 = 5000 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 6 — Actual force with mechanical efficiency

$$F_{2,actual} = \eta_{mech} \cdot F_{2,ideal} = 0.90 \cdot 5000 = 4500 \text{ N} = 4.5 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 7 — Velocity ratio and mechanical advantage

The velocity ratio is the inverse of the force ratio:

$$VR = \frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 25$$

This means: for every 25 units the small piston moves, the large piston moves 1 unit (conservation of volume).

### Final Answer

- Pressure developed: $P = 2.83$ bar
- Theoretical force on large piston: $F_2 = 5000$ N
- Actual force (η = 90 %): $F_2 = 4500$ N
- Velocity ratio: $VR = 25 : 1$
- Load that can be lifted: $W = 4500$ N (i.e., a 450 kg mass)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cylinder Extension and Retraction Times (DAC)

**Problem Statement:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has piston diameter $D = 80$ mm and rod diameter $d = 45$ mm. Stroke length $L = 400$ mm. The pump delivers $Q = 12$ LPM at $p = 80$ bar. Calculate (a) full-bore area, (b) annulus area, (c) extension time, (d) retraction time, (e) extension force, (f) retraction force.

### Given Data

$D = 0.080$ m, $d = 0.045$ m, $L = 0.400$ m

$Q = 12$ LPM $= 12 / 60 = 0.0002$ m³/s $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

$p = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 8.0 \times 10^6$ Pa

### Step 1 — Full-bore area (piston side)

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.080)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Annulus area (rod side)

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot ((0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0064 - 0.002025) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.004375$$

$$A_r = 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Volume of fluid for full extension

$$V_{ext} = A_p \cdot L = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.400 = 2.0106 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{ext} = 2.0106 \text{ L} \quad \text{(approximately)}$$

### Step 4 — Extension time

$$t_{ext} = \frac{V_{ext}}{Q} = \frac{2.0106 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_{ext} = 10.053 \text{ s} \approx 10.05 \text{ s}$$

### Step 5 — Retraction time

$$V_{ret} = A_r \cdot L = 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.400 = 1.3743 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{ret} = 1.3743 \text{ L}$$

$$t_{ret} = \frac{V_{ret}}{Q} = \frac{1.3743 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_{ret} = 6.871 \text{ s} \approx 6.87 \text{ s}$$

### Step 6 — Extension force (thrust)

$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_p = 8.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{ext} = 40{,}212 \text{ N} \approx 40.21 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 7 — Retraction force (pull)

$$F_{ret} = p \cdot A_r = 8.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.4357 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{ret} = 27{,}486 \text{ N} \approx 27.49 \text{ kN}$$

### Final Answer

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $A_p$ | $5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m² |
| $A_r$ | $3.436 \times 10^{-3}$ m² |
| $t_{ext}$ | 10.05 s |
| $t_{ret}$ | 6.87 s |
| $F_{ext}$ | 40.21 kN |
| $F_{ret}$ | 27.49 kN |

> [!NOTE]
> The **retract-to-extend time ratio** is $A_r / A_p = 0.683$, which means retraction is ~32 % faster — a key observation for KTU viva.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pump Power and Motor Sizing for a Trainer

**Problem Statement:** A hydraulic trainer needs to supply $Q = 15$ LPM at $p = 100$ bar. The overall efficiency of pump + motor is $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate (a) hydraulic power, (b) electrical input power to motor, (c) motor current at 3-phase 415 V supply with power factor 0.85.

### Given Data

$Q = 15$ LPM $= 15 / 60 = 0.00025$ m³/s

$p = 100$ bar $= 1.0 \times 10^7$ Pa

$\eta_{total} = 0.85$

$V_L = 415$ V (3-phase), $\cos\phi = 0.85$

### Step 1 — Hydraulic power output

$$P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q = 1.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 0.00025$$

$$P_{hyd} = 2500 \text{ W} = 2.5 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2 — Electrical input power to motor

$$P_{el} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{total}} = \frac{2500}{0.85}$$

$$P_{el} = 2941 \text{ W} \approx 2.94 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3 — Line current (3-phase)

For a 3-phase motor:

$$P_{el} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{motor}$$

Assuming motor efficiency $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$:

$$I_L = \frac{P_{el}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{motor}}$$

$$I_L = \frac{2941}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.90}$$

$$I_L = \frac{2941}{549.4} = 5.35 \text{ A}$$

### Final Answer

- Hydraulic power = 2.5 kW
- Motor input = 2.94 kW
- Line current = 5.35 A (so a standard 7.5 A MCB suffices)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pneumatic Cylinder Force, Velocity, and Cycle Time

**Problem Statement:** A pneumatic cylinder (bore $D = 50$ mm, rod $d = 20$ mm, stroke $L = 200$ mm) operates at gauge pressure $p = 6$ bar. Supply flow is $Q = 1000$ LPM (free air, before throttling). Mechanical efficiency is 0.9. Calculate (a) thrust force, (b) piston velocity, (c) extension time, (d) cycle time (extend + retract + 0.5 s dwell).

### Given Data

$D = 0.050$ m, $d = 0.020$ m, $L = 0.200$ m

$p = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa

$Q = 1000$ LPM free air, but only **cylinder-volume-flow** matters at the load.

Assume effective flow into cylinder: $Q_{cyl} = 1000 / 1000 = 1.0$ m³/min $= 0.01667$ m³/s (since pneumatic lines are large, ignore minor throttling here).

$\eta_{mech} = 0.90$

### Step 1 — Full-bore area

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Annulus area

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot ((0.050)^2 - (0.020)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0025 - 0.0004)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0021 = 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Theoretical thrust

$$F_{th} = p \cdot A_p = 6 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_{th} = 1178.1 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 — Effective thrust (mechanical losses)

$$F_{eff} = \eta_{mech} \cdot F_{th} = 0.90 \cdot 1178.1 = 1060.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 5 — Piston velocity during extension

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{0.01667}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.49 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 6 — Extension time

$$t_{ext} = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.200}{8.49} = 0.0236 \text{ s} \approx 23.6 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 7 — Retraction time (using annulus)

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{0.01667}{1.6493 \times 10^{-3}} = 10.11 \text{ m/s}$$

$$t_{ret} = \frac{0.200}{10.11} = 0.0198 \text{ s} \approx 19.8 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 8 — Total cycle time (with 0.5 s dwell)

$$T_{cycle} = t_{ext} + t_{ret} + t_{dwell} = 0.0236 + 0.0198 + 0.5$$

$$T_{cycle} = 0.5434 \text{ s}$$

### Final Answer

- Effective thrust = **1060 N**
- Extension time = **23.6 ms**
- Retraction time = **19.8 ms**
- Total cycle = **0.543 s** (≈ 110 cycles/min)

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Flow Control Valve Sizing (Orifice Equation)

**Problem Statement:** A needle-type flow control valve has orifice area $A_v = 6$ mm², discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.65$, fluid density $\rho = 870$ kg/m³. Required flow is $Q = 6$ LPM. Find the required pressure drop.

### Given Data

$A_v = 6 \text{ mm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

$C_d = 0.65$

$\rho = 870$ kg/m³

$Q = 6$ LPM $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

### Step 1 — Rearrange orifice equation

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}} = \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot A_v}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{0.65 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{3.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 25.641$$

### Step 3 — Square both sides

$$\frac{2 \Delta p}{\rho} = (25.641)^2 = 657.46$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta p$

$$\Delta p = \frac{657.46 \cdot \rho}{2} = \frac{657.46 \cdot 870}{2}$$

$$\Delta p = 286{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 2.86 \text{ bar}$$

### Final Answer

$\Delta p = 2.86$ bar across the flow control valve

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **wasted energy**. In a 100 bar system, ~2.86 % of pressure is dropped → 2.86 % of energy becomes heat.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Regenerative Circuit Force and Velocity Boost

**Problem Statement:** A double-acting cylinder (D = 80 mm, d = 45 mm) is used in a regenerative circuit. Pump flow $Q = 10$ LPM at $p = 100$ bar. Calculate (a) extension force in normal mode, (b) extension force in regenerative mode, (c) extension velocity in regenerative mode, (d) extension time in regenerative mode (L = 300 mm).

### Given Data

$D = 0.080$ m, $d = 0.045$ m, $L = 0.300$ m

$Q = 10$ LPM $= 1.6667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

$p = 100$ bar $= 1.0 \times 10^7$ Pa

### Step 1 — Areas (re-using from 3.2)

$A_p = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

$A_r = 3.4357 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

### Step 2 — Normal mode extension force

$$F_{normal} = p \cdot A_p = 1.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 3 — Regenerative mode effective area

In a regenerative circuit, the rod-side oil is added to the piston-side supply, so the **effective area** is the **annulus area only**:

$$A_{regen} = A_r = 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Regenerative mode extension force

$$F_{regen} = p \cdot A_r = 1.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} = 34{,}357 \text{ N} \approx 34.36 \text{ kN}$$

> Force is reduced by 31.6 % (because we are using smaller area to "save" force in exchange for speed).

### Step 5 — Effective flow into piston side during regen

In regenerative mode, the total flow entering the piston side is:

$$Q_{piston} = Q_{pump} + Q_{rod}$$

But $Q_{rod} = A_r \cdot v_{piston}$, and the rod-side oil flows back into the piston-side line. So:

$$Q_{pump} = A_p \cdot v - A_r \cdot v = (A_p - A_r) \cdot v$$

$$v_{regen} = \frac{Q_{pump}}{A_p - A_r} = \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{(5.0265 - 3.4357) \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_{regen} = \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5908 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1048 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 6 — Compare with normal extension velocity

$$v_{normal} = \frac{Q_{pump}}{A_p} = \frac{1.6667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03316 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 7 — Speed boost factor

$$\frac{v_{regen}}{v_{normal}} = \frac{0.1048}{0.03316} = 3.16$$

> **Regenerative circuit gives ~3.16× speed at the cost of ~31.6 % force.** This is its signature trade-off.

### Step 8 — Regenerative extension time

$$t_{regen} = \frac{L}{v_{regen}} = \frac{0.300}{0.1048} = 2.863 \text{ s}$$

### Final Answer

| Parameter | Normal Mode | Regenerative Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Force | 50.27 kN | 34.36 kN |
| Velocity | 0.0332 m/s | 0.1048 m/s |
| Time (300 mm) | 9.05 s | 2.86 s |

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Pneumatic Sequencing Using Time-Delay Valve

**Problem Statement:** Two pneumatic cylinders A and B must extend in sequence (A first, then B after 2 s). Both cylinders have bore 40 mm, stroke 100 mm, supply pressure 5 bar, flow 50 LPM. Use an on-delay (NO) timer to actuate B's 5/2 DCV via a 3/2 pilot valve. Calculate (a) thrust force of A, (b) volume of A, (c) extension time of A, (d) state the logic equation.

### Given Data

$D = 0.040$ m, $L = 0.100$ m, $p = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^5$ Pa, $Q = 50$ LPM $= 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

### Step 1 — Thrust force of A

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.040)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_A = p \cdot A = 5 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 628.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2 — Volume of A

$$V_A = A \cdot L = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.100 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 0.1257 \text{ L}$$

### Step 3 — Extension time of A

$$t_{ext,A} = \frac{V_A}{Q} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}}{8.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.151 \text{ s}$$

### Step 4 — Sequencing logic equation

The 3/2 pilot valve opens after the delay $T_d$:

$$B_{extend} = A_{limit} \cdot \overline{Timer_{counting}}$$

In standard KTU notation:

$$\boxed{B^+ = a_1 \cdot T_d \text{ (A-extend-end AND timer-elapsed)}}$$

where $a_1$ is the limit-valve signal from cylinder A reaching its forward end position.

### Final Answer

- $F_A = 628$ N
- $V_A = 0.126$ L
- $t_{ext,A} = 0.151$ s
- Logic: $B^+ = a_1 \cdot T_d$

---

## 3.8 Consolidated Analytical Matrix (All Derivations)

| Derivation | Input | Output | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | $D_1, D_2, F_1$ | $P, F_2$ | Force ratio = (D ratio)² |
| 3.2 | $D, d, L, Q, p$ | $t_{ext}, t_{ret}, F_{ext}, F_{ret}$ | Retract faster than extend |
| 3.3 | $p, Q, \eta$ | $P_{el}, I_L$ | Always size motor ≥ 1.2× hydraulic power |
| 3.4 | $D, d, L, p, Q$ | $F, t_{cycle}$ | Pneumatic cycle ~100/min typical |
| 3.5 | $A_v, C_d, \rho, Q$ | $\Delta p$ | Throttling = heat loss |
| 3.6 | $D, d, L, p, Q$ | $F_{regen}, v_{regen}$ | Regen: ↑speed ↓force |
| 3.7 | $D, L, p, Q, T_d$ | $F, V, t$, logic | Timer + limit = sequence |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Two questions per KTU pattern, each carrying **3 marks**, totalling **6 marks** per question paper. Answers should be ~80–120 words.

---

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting cylinder. State one typical industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **single-acting cylinder (SAC)** has only one port for fluid entry. The return stroke is achieved by an **external spring** or by **gravity/load**. It produces force in **one direction only**. Typical application: **garage jacks, hydraulic hand-pumps, simple clamping fixtures**.

A **double-acting cylinder (DAC)** has **two ports** — fluid is admitted alternately to the piston side and the rod side, producing force in **both directions**. No spring is needed. Typical application: **hydraulic presses, plastic injection moulding clamping units, robotic arm actuators**.

The DAC provides **bidirectional thrust** ($F_{ext} = pA_p$ for extension and $F_{ret} = pA_r$ for retraction) with $A_p > A_r$, while the SAC is mechanically simpler and cheaper but is **single-stroke only**.

*(1 mark — definition SAC; 1 mark — definition DAC; 1 mark — application + force equation)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With a neat ISO 1219 sketch, explain the function of a 5/3 closed-centre direction control valve. Why is it preferred for a vertical-axis hydraulic press?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **5/3 closed-centre DCV** has **five ports** (P, T, A, B, plus pilot) and **three positions** (extend, centre, retract). In the **centre (middle) position**, all five ports are **blocked** — this is the *closed-centre* configuration. A simple representation:

```
   P    A    B    T
  [|]  [>|] [<|] [|]
   (pos 1: extend)
  [||]  [||] [||] [||]  ← centre: ALL BLOCKED
   (pos 2: centre)
  [|]  [<|] [>|] [|]
   (pos 3: retract)
```

**Why preferred for a vertical-axis press:**

1. **Locking the load** — when the valve is centred, both cylinder ports are blocked, hydraulically locking the piston in place. This is critical for safety on a vertical press where the load could fall.

2. **Pump unloading** — a closed-centre system can use an unloading valve to dump pump output to tank at near-zero pressure, saving energy during dwell.

3. **No drift** — unlike open-centre or tandem-centre, the closed-centre provides **positive static locking** with zero leakage tolerance.
