*(1 mark — symbol/sketches; 1 mark — three positions and function; 1 mark — vertical-press safety reason)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **Module Internal Choice**: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Full worked solutions below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **hydraulic regenerative circuit** using a 4/3 DCV. What is its primary advantage and disadvantage? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **100 mm**, rod diameter of **60 mm**, and a stroke of **500 mm**. The pump supplies oil at **120 bar** and **20 LPM**. Calculate (i) extension force, (ii) retraction force, (iii) extension time, (iv) retraction time, and (v) extension velocity. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Regenerative Circuit (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A **regenerative circuit** re-uses the oil exhausting from the rod side of a double-acting cylinder by routing it back to the piston side, increasing extension speed at the cost of reduced thrust force.

**Working (3 Marks):**
In a 4/3 DCV with **regenerative position**, the rod-side port (B) is connected internally to the piston-side port (A). Fresh oil from the pump enters the piston side, and the displaced oil from the rod side is added to the same piston-side line. The effective area becomes the **annulus area ($A_r$)** instead of the full-bore area ($A_p$). The tank port is blocked.

For retraction, the DCV is shifted to the normal-reverse position. Pump oil enters the rod side, and piston-side oil returns to tank. Full thrust force is recovered.

**Advantage (1 Mark):**
**Extension speed increases by a factor of $A_p / (A_p - A_r)$** (typically 2× to 4×), reducing cycle time. Useful in blank-holding, fast feed, and packaging machinery.

**Disadvantage (1 Mark):**
**Extension force is reduced** to $p \cdot A_r$ (a 30–40 % drop), so the circuit is unsuitable for heavy-load extension. It is a **speed–force trade-off**.

**Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer Section 4.4 Mermaid schematic for the regenerative circuit.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m, $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m, $L = 500$ mm $= 0.500$ m

$p = 120$ bar $= 1.2 \times 10^7$ Pa

$Q = 20$ LPM $= 20 / 60 = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Full-bore area (1 Mark)**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Annulus area (1 Mark)**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot ((0.100)^2 - (0.060)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.0100 - 0.0036)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Extension force (1 Mark)**

$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_p = 1.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 94{,}248 \text{ N} \approx 94.25 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Retraction force (1 Mark)**

$$F_{ret} = p \cdot A_r = 1.2 \times 10^7 \cdot 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 60{,}319 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Extension time (1 Mark)**

$$V_{ext} = A_p \cdot L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.500 = 3.927 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_{ext} = \frac{V_{ext}}{Q} = \frac{3.927 \times 10^{-3}}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 11.78 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Retraction time and extension velocity (2 Marks)**

$$V_{ret} = A_r \cdot L = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.500 = 2.513 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$t_{ret} = \frac{V_{ret}}{Q} = \frac{2.513 \times 10^{-3}}{3.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.54 \text{ s}$$

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0424 \text{ m/s} = 42.4 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Final Tabular Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Extension force | **94.25 kN** |
| Retraction force | **60.32 kN** |
| Extension time | **11.78 s** |
| Retraction time | **7.54 s** |
| Extension velocity | **42.4 mm/s** |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Draw the ISO 1219 circuit diagram of a **pneumatic double-acting cylinder controlled by a 5/2 double-solenoid valve with limit switches for automatic reciprocation**. Explain its operation step-by-step. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pneumatic cylinder has bore **63 mm**, rod **20 mm**, stroke **150 mm**, supply pressure **6 bar**, and effective flow rate into the cylinder of **2000 LPM (free air converted to line conditions)**. Mechanical efficiency is **0.85**. Calculate (i) theoretical thrust, (ii) effective thrust, (iii) extension velocity, (iv) extension time, (v) cycle time assuming 0.3 s dwell on each end. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Pneumatic Auto-Reciprocation (7 Marks)

**Circuit Components (2 Marks):**
- 5/2 DCV, **double-solenoid** (Y1, Y2) — provides memory
- **Limit valve 1a** (3/2 roller, NC) at cylinder forward end
- **Limit valve 1b** (3/2 roller, NC) at cylinder rear end
- 2× **One-way flow controls** for speed regulation
- FRL with 6 bar regulated supply

**Working Steps (5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Initially, both solenoids Y1, Y2 de-energised. Valve is in the "retract" position (default spring side). Cylinder is in the back position. Limit valve 1a (forward-end) is NOT actuated; 1b (rear-end) IS actuated → air passes to Y1's pilot port.

**Step 2:** Push START button. Solenoid Y1 energises. Valve shifts to "extend". Pressurised air flows through port A to the piston side → cylinder extends. Spring-return side air exhausts from port B through silencer.

**Step 3:** As cylinder reaches the forward end, it mechanically actuates limit valve 1a. Limit 1a sends a pneumatic pulse to de-energise Y1 (cut-off) and simultaneously energise Y2.

**Step 4:** Solenoid Y2 is now ON, Y1 is OFF. Valve shifts to "retract". Air now enters port B, pushes piston back. Port A exhausts to atmosphere.

**Step 5:** Cylinder reaches rear end → actuates limit valve 1b. Limit 1b sends pulse to de-energise Y2. The valve stays in the "retract" position (memory hold), ready for the next START.

**Memory Feature:** The 5/2 double-solenoid valve "remembers" the last position, so it does not need a continuous signal — only a momentary pulse is enough.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$D = 63$ mm $= 0.063$ m, $d = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m, $L = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m

$p = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$

$Q = 2000$ LPM $= 2.0 / 60 = 0.03333$ m³/s (line-condition)

**Step 1 — Full-bore area (1 Mark)**

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.063)^2 = 3.1172 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Annulus area (1 Mark)**

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot ((0.063)^2 - (0.020)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.003969 - 0.0004)$$

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.003569 = 2.8030 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical thrust (1 Mark)**

$$F_{th} = p \cdot A_p = 6 \times 10^5 \cdot 3.1172 \times 10^{-3} = 1870.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Effective thrust (1 Mark)**

$$F_{eff} = \eta_{mech} \cdot F_{th} = 0.85 \cdot 1870.3 = 1589.8 \text{ N} \approx 1590 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Extension velocity (1 Mark)**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{0.03333}{3.1172 \times 10^{-3}} = 10.69 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Extension time (1 Mark)**

$$t_{ext} = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{0.150}{10.69} = 0.01403 \text{ s} \approx 14.0 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 7 — Retraction time and cycle time (1 Mark)**

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{0.03333}{2.8030 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.89 \text{ m/s}$$

$$t_{ret} = \frac{0.150}{11.89} = 0.01262 \text{ s} \approx 12.6 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{cycle} = t_{ext} + t_{ret} + t_{dwell,1} + t_{dwell,2} = 0.01403 + 0.01262 + 0.3 + 0.3$$

$$T_{cycle} = 0.6267 \text{ s} \approx 0.627 \text{ s}$$

**Final Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Theoretical thrust | **1870 N** |
| Effective thrust | **1590 N** |
| Extension velocity | **10.69 m/s** |
| Extension time | **14.0 ms** |
| Cycle time | **0.627 s** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on fluid-power questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert LPM to m³/s** — KTU strictly follows SI units. 1 LPM = $1.667 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Writing "Q = 20" without unit conversion in a force/time calculation loses **2 marks immediately**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $A_p$ and $A_r$** — Extension always uses **full bore**; retraction uses **annulus**. Conflating the two is the **single most common error** in KTU scripts.
>
> 3. **Skipping the ISO symbol legend** — A circuit diagram drawn without labelling the components (P, T, A, B ports, "3/2", "spring return", etc.) loses 1–2 marks in the "neat sketch" component of the question.
>
> 4. **Ignoring $\eta_{mech}$** — In pneumatic and low-pressure hydraulic systems, the examiner **expects** the efficiency factor (0.85–0.95). Omitting it deducts 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Unit of pressure** — Use **Pa or bar** consistently. Don't write "$F = 80 \times 0.005$" without the $10^5$ factor. Show the conversion: $80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5$ Pa.
>
> 6. **Forgetting force ratio formula** — Many students compute $F_2$ directly from $F_1$ without the $(D_2/D_1)^2$ formula. Always show the **logic step** for marks.
>
> 7. **No sketch in regenerative question** — Without the 4/3 DCV in the regen position and the check valves, the explanation is "incomplete" and **3 marks** are forfeited.
>
> 8. **Cycle-time calculation order** — Always end with the **final consolidated value** in a boxed answer. The examiner scans for the boxed answer first.
>
> 9. **Conflating meter-in vs meter-out** — Meter-OUT is preferred for **load-induced motion** (resistance to motion); meter-IN is preferred for **pump-induced motion**. KTU often asks "why" — know the reason.
>
> 10. **Not stating the assumption of incompressibility** — Hydraulic calculations assume **incompressible flow**. State this as an explicit assumption in derivations (worth 1 mark in long-answer papers).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (15-bullet dense summary):**
>
> 1. **Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic:** Oil at **50–200 bar** for high force; air at **4–8 bar** for fast, light work.
> 2. **Pascal's Law:** $P = F/A$ — the foundation of force multiplication.
> 3. **Force ratio:** $F_2/F_1 = (D_2/D_1)^2$ — area ratio decides mechanical advantage.
> 4. **Continuity:** $Q = Av$ — constant volume flow rate in a closed loop.
> 5. **DAC asymmetry:** $A_p > A_r$ → extension is **slower but stronger**; retraction is **faster but weaker**.
> 6. **Cylinder stroke time:** $t = AL / Q$ — increases with stroke, decreases with flow.
> 7. **Hydraulic power:** $P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$ (W, with p in Pa, Q in m³/s).
> 8. **Motor sizing:** $P_{el} = P_{hyd} / \eta$ — always oversize motor by 20 % margin.
> 9. **Regenerative circuit:** trade **force for speed**; effective area = annulus during extension.
> 10. **Counterbalance valve:** holds a **suspended vertical load** by maintaining back-pressure.
> 11. **Meter-out flow control:** preferred for **resistive loads** (compressible, position-critical).
> 12. **5/3 closed-centre DCV:** blocks all ports in centre → **hydraulic locking** for vertical presses.
> 13. **Quick-exhaust valve:** mounts near the cylinder to dump exhaust air directly to atmosphere → **3× to 5× faster cycle** in pneumatics.
> 14. **AND logic:** Dual-pressure valve requires **both** signals present. **OR logic:** Shuttle valve passes the **higher** of two signals.
> 15. **Time-delay sequencing:** $B^+ = a_1 \cdot T_d$ — A finishes its travel AND timer elapses → B extends.

---

> **End of Section 5.** This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 notes for *Exercises on Hydraulic and Pneumatic Circuits Using Trainer Units* (PCMEL508, Module Experiment 2, 2024 Scheme).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Electro-Pneumatic and Electro-Hydraulic Circuits Using Trainer Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Electro-Pneumatic and Electro-Hydraulic Circuits Using Trainer Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electro-Pneumatic Circuit:** A control system in which **electrical signals** (from switches, relays, sensors, or PLCs) actuate **pneumatic actuators** (typically double-acting cylinders) through solenoid-operated directional control valves. The system converts electrical energy into compressed-air-based mechanical motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electro-Hydraulic Circuit:** A control system in which **electrical signals** govern **hydraulic actuators** (double-acting cylinders or hydraulic motors) via solenoid-actuated directional control valves, using incompressible hydraulic oil (typically **ISO VG 32 or 46**) at working pressures of **50–200 bar**.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMEL508 – Module Experiment 2)**, exercises using **trainer units** refer to the design, wiring, simulation, and verification of pneumatic and hydraulic circuits on standardized laboratory trainer kits (e.g., Festo, SMC, Bosch Rexroth, or equivalent modular workbenches).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traffic light system** at a road intersection:

- The **red and green lights** are like **electrical signals** (inputs).
- The **stop/go arms** that physically move barriers are like **pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders** (outputs).
- The **control box** that decides which arm moves is like the **PLC or relay logic** (the brain).

In the same way, electro-pneumatic/hydraulic systems take a small **electrical push** (24 V DC signal) and turn it into a **big mechanical push** (cylinder extension/retraction). The **trainer unit** is essentially a "Lego set" of valves, cylinders, sensors, and electrical components bolted to a single board, where you can plug in tubes and wires to build working circuits without needing a real factory.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Working pressure (Pneumatic):** 6 bar (typical industrial standard, range 4–8 bar)
- **Working pressure (Hydraulic):** 50–100 bar (typical lab trainer), up to 200 bar industrial
- **Standard solenoid voltage:** 24 V DC
- **Air preparation unit (FRL):** Filter + Regulator + Lubricator
- **Cylinder bore (typical):** 25 mm, 32 mm, 50 mm
- **Flow rate unit:** Nl/min (normalized litres per minute) for pneumatics, l/min for hydraulics
- **Force formula:** $F = P \times A$ where P is pressure, A is piston area

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 PCMEL508, the focus of Module Experiment 2 is on **designing, connecting, and verifying** electro-pneumatic and electro-hydraulic circuits using a **trainer kit** — the student must demonstrate the ability to (1) read a circuit diagram, (2) physically wire it on the trainer, (3) test for correct sequence, and (4) record observations in a lab record.

### 1.4 Typical Trainer Kit Components

| Subsystem | Components Found on a Trainer |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| Power Supply | Compressor (pneumatic), Hydraulic Power Pack (HPU) |
| Air/Oil Treatment | FRL Unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) |
| Input Devices | Push buttons, Limit switches, Proximity sensors, Pressure switches |
| Control | Relay logic, Mini-PLC, Solenoid coils (24 V DC) |
| Output Devices | 5/2 and 5/3 Solenoid-actuated DCVs, Double-acting cylinders |
| Indication | LED indicators, Manometers, Flow meters |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Concept (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1: Signal Initiation (Electrical Side)**
- A **push button (PB)** or **limit switch (LS)** is pressed, closing a circuit.
- This energizes a **solenoid coil** of a directional control valve (DCV).
- Voltage applied: typically **24 V DC** (safe, low-power).

**Step 2: Signal Processing (Control Logic)**
- In **relay-based trainers**, the signal passes through holding/auxiliary contacts.
- In **PLC-based trainers**, the input is read by a digital input module (e.g., Input I0.0 in Siemens LOGO!, or X0 in Mitsubishi).
- Ladder logic (LAD) or Function Block Diagram (FBD) determines whether to energize the output coil (e.g., Q0.0).

**Step 3: Fluid Power Actuation (Pneumatic/Hydraulic Side)**
- The energized DCV shifts its spool, connecting the **pressure port (P)** to the **cap-end (or rod-end)** of the cylinder.
- Compressed air / hydraulic oil flows into the cylinder chamber.
- The piston moves, producing linear force.

**Step 4: Feedback**
- As the cylinder reaches its end position, a **limit switch (mechanical)** or **magnetic/inductive proximity sensor** is triggered.
- This new electrical signal resets the original circuit (de-energizes the coil), and the DCV returns to its default position (often via spring return).
- The cylinder retracts — completing **one cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Electro" prefix means:** Electrical signal → Solenoid → Valve shift → Fluid flow → Mechanical motion. Removing any link breaks the chain.

### 2.2 Why Use Electro-Pneumatic / Electro-Hydraulic?

- **Manual-only systems** require a human operator at every step — impractical for high-speed automation.
- **Pure pneumatic logic** (AND/OR gates) is bulky and limited.
- **Electro-fluid power** enables **long-distance control** (operator can be 100 m away), **complex sequencing**, and **integration with PLCs/SCADA**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines** (welding, painting, clamping)
- **Packaging machinery** (filling, sealing, labeling)
- **Injection moulding machines** (clamp/unclamp)
- **Machine tools** (feed, tool changing)
- **Agricultural and construction equipment** (actuation of buckets, booms)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Cylinder Output Force (extension) | $F_{ext} = P \times A_{piston}$ | N | $A_{piston} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ |
| 2 | Cylinder Output Force (retraction) | $F_{ret} = P \times (A_{piston} - A_{rod})$ | N | $A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$ |
| 3 | Piston Area | $A_{piston} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | D = bore diameter |
| 4 | Annular (rod-side) Area | $A_{annular} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ | m² | d = rod diameter |
| 5 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Q = flow rate (m³/s) |
| 6 | Flow Rate (from speed) | $Q = v \times A$ | m³/s | |
| 7 | Power (Hydraulic) | $P_{hyd} = p \times Q$ | W | p in Pa, Q in m³/s |
| 8 | Force Ratio (Hyd vs Pneu) | $\frac{F_{hyd}}{F_{pneu}} = \frac{p_{hyd}}{p_{pneu}}$ | – | For same bore |
| 9 | Compressor/Air consumption | $Q_{air} = v \times A \times \frac{p_{working}+p_{atm}}{p_{atm}}$ | Nl/min | Free air delivery |
| 10 | Pressure Drop (basic) | $\Delta p = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^4}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Pa | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert mm² → m² before substituting in force formulas. A common student error is to use **N/mm² × mm² = N** (numerically correct by coincidence) but the **SI rule is Pa × m² = N** (1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa, 1 mm² = 10⁻⁶ m², product = 1 N). Be consistent with SI units.

### 2.5 Cylinder Force — Quick Worked Insight

For a **double-acting cylinder** with bore **D = 50 mm** and rod **d = 25 mm**, supplied at **6 bar (pneumatic)**:

- $A_{piston} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$
- $A_{annular} = \frac{\pi}{4} [(0.05)^2 - (0.025)^2] = 1.472 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$
- $F_{ext} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \approx 1178\ \text{N}$
- $F_{ret} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.472 \times 10^{-3} \approx 883\ \text{N}$

For the **same cylinder** in **hydraulic at 100 bar**:
- $F_{ext} = 100 \times 10^5 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \approx 19{,}635\ \text{N}$ (≈ 2 tonnes-force!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **hydraulics** are used where **high force in compact size** is needed (jacks, presses, excavators), while **pneumatics** are used where **speed, cleanliness, and safety** matter (food, pharma, electronics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Force Developed by a Pneumatic Cylinder

**Given:** A double-acting cylinder with bore **D**, rod diameter **d**, supply pressure **p**.

**Step 1: Identify the piston face area (cap-end side).**
The piston is a circular disk. Its area is the area of a circle:
$$A_{piston} = \pi r^2 = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2: Recall the definition of pressure.**
Pressure is force per unit area:
$$p = \frac{F}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = p \times A$$

**Step 3: Compute extension force.**
During extension, pressure acts on the full piston area:
$$F_{ext} = p \times A_{piston} = p \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4: Compute retraction force.**
During retraction, pressure acts only on the **annular area** (piston minus rod cross-section):
$$A_{annular} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

Therefore:
$$F_{ret} = p \times \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

**Step 5: Force ratio (useful for design check).**
$$\frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2$$

This ratio is independent of supply pressure — a powerful **design check**. For typical industrial cylinders where d/D ≈ 0.5, the retraction force is **75% of the extension force**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cylinder Extension Time (Pneumatic)

**Given:** Cylinder stroke **L**, bore **D**, supply flow rate **Q** (Nl/min), supply pressure **p**.

**Step 1: Volume of air needed per stroke (free air).**
$$V_{free} = L \times A_{piston} \times \frac{p_{working} + p_{atm}}{p_{atm}}$$

**Reasoning:** The air trapped in the cylinder is at supply pressure; when released to atmosphere, it expands. The expansion ratio is $\frac{p_{working}+p_{atm}}{p_{atm}}$ (using gauge vs absolute correctly).

**Step 2: Time to fill.**
$$t = \frac{V_{free}}{Q}$$

**Step 3: Cylinder velocity (alternative form).**
$$v = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{Q}{A_{piston} \times \frac{p_{working}+p_{atm}}{p_{atm}}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Trainer Exercises (Mapping Signals to Outputs)

The following matrix summarizes the most frequent KTU lab exercises. Students should memorize the **ladder rung structure** for each.

| Exercise | Inputs | Outputs (Solenoids) | Ladder Logic Rung Pattern |
|----------|--------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| A. Single-Acting Cylinder | PB (Start) | Y1 (extend), spring return | (PB) → [Y1], with limit switch cutting Y1 |
| B. Double-Acting, Direct Control | PB1 (extend), PB2 (retract) | Y1 (extend), Y2 (retract) | PB1 → Y1 (with LS1 holding), PB2 → Y2 |
| C. Auto-Return Sequencing | PB, LS at end | Y1, Y2 | LS1 closes → energizes Y2 → retract |
| D. Two-Cylinder Sequence (A then B) | PB, LS1, LS2 | Y1A, Y2A (cyl A), Y1B, Y2B (cyl B) | PB→Y1A; LS1→Y1B; LS2 (B end)→retract A; LS1 (A retract)→retract B |
| E. Time-Delayed Operation | PB, LS, Timer | Y1, Timer T | PB→Y1; after LS, start timer T; T done→retract |
| F. Continuous Cycle (with Stop) | PB (Start), Stop, LS, Counter | Y1, Y2 | Latching rung with self-hold via limit switch feedback |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full Derivation for Exam)

**Problem:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore of **40 mm** and a rod diameter of **16 mm**. The supply pressure is **6 bar** (gauge), and the air consumption during extension is measured as **2.5 Nl/min** at standard conditions. Calculate:
(a) Theoretical extension force.
(b) Theoretical retraction force.
(c) Force ratio.
(d) Time to complete a stroke of **200 mm**.

**Solution:**

**(a) Extension force:**
Piston area:
$$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.040)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

Convert 6 bar gauge to Pa:
$$p = 6 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa}$$

Theoretical force (no friction):
$$F_{ext} = p \times A_{piston} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 753.98\ \text{N}$$

**(b) Retraction force:**
Annular area:
$$A_{annular} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(0.040)^2 - (0.016)^2\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0016 - 0.000256) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.001344 = 1.0558 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

Force:
$$F_{ret} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.0558 \times 10^{-3} = 633.46\ \text{N}$$

**(c) Force ratio:**
$$\frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = \frac{633.46}{753.98} = 0.840$$

Alternatively, by direct formula:
$$\frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = 1 - \left(\frac{16}{40}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.16 = 0.840\ \checkmark$$

**(d) Stroke time:**
Volume of free air needed per stroke:
$$V_{free} = L \times A_{piston} \times \frac{p_{working} + p_{atm}}{p_{atm}}$$

With $p_{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa:
$$V_{free} = 0.200 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{6 \times 10^5 + 1.013 \times 10^5}{1.013 \times 10^5}$$
$$V_{free} = 2.513 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.92 = 1.739 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$$
$$V_{free} = 1.739\ \text{litres} = 1.739\ \text{Nl}$$

Time to fill at 2.5 Nl/min:
$$t = \frac{1.739}{2.5} = 0.6956\ \text{min} = 41.7\ \text{seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a real trainer, the time is much shorter because flow rates are higher (typically 50–200 Nl/min for a 6 mm bore, 6 bar supply). The above calculation uses the **measured** free air delivery to illustrate the principle.

### 3.5 Ladder Logic Translation (KTU Lab Record Standard)

For the **two-cylinder sequence (A→B→retract A→retract B)**, a typical rung structure on a PLC trainer is:

```
Rung 1:  | PB_Start  LS_A_Retracted  Y1A (HOLD)   |  ---( Y1A )---
Rung 2:  | LS_A_Extended  NOT LS_B_Extended      |  ---( Y1B )---
Rung 3:  | LS_B_Extended  Y2A (HOLD)             |  ---( Y2A )---
Rung 4:  | LS_A_Retracted  Y2B (HOLD)            |  ---( Y2B )---
Rung 5:  | Stop PB       Y1A Y1B Y2A Y2B         |  ---( Master_Reset )---
```

This is the **core deliverable** students are expected to draw and demonstrate in the KTU lab exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electro-Pneumatic Trainer System

The diagram below maps the **signal flow** from input to output, showing the integration of electrical control and pneumatic power. This is the architecture a student should draw in the lab record's "System Description" section.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Devices Push Button Limit Switch Sensor 24V DC] --> B[Control Unit Relay Logic OR Mini PLC]
    B --> C[Solenoid Coils 24V DC on 5/2 DCV]
    C --> D[Directional Control Valve Spool Shift]
    D --> E[Pneumatic Power 6 bar from FRL Unit]
    E --> F[Double Acting Cylinder Piston Motion]
    F --> G[Mechanical Feedback Limit Switch Actuation]
    G --> B
    F --> H[Output Indicator LED + Manometer Reading]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left side (A, B, C) is the **electrical/control layer**.
- The right side (D, E, F) is the **fluid power layer**.
- The feedback loop (G → B) closes the automation cycle.

### 4.2 Circuit Schematic — Double-Acting Cylinder with Two Push Buttons (Direct Control)

This is the **most common KTU lab circuit**. The student must:
1. Read the diagram.
2. Connect push buttons to the solenoid terminals.
3. Connect the FRL to port P of the 5/2 DCV.
4. Connect ports A and B of the DCV to the cap-end and rod-end of the cylinder.
5. Press PB1 → extend; press PB2 → retract.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EL["ELECTRICAL CONTROL 24V DC"]
        PB1[PB1 Extend NO Contact]
        PB2[PB2 Retract NO Contact]
        Y1[Solenoid Coil Y1 Left Side of 5/2 DCV]
        Y2[Solenoid Coil Y2 Right Side of 5/2 DCV]
    end
    subgraph FL["PNEUMATIC POWER 6 bar"]
        SRC[Compressor 6 bar]
        FRL[FRL Filter Regulator Lubricator]
        DCV[5/2 DCV Spring Centred OR Spring Return Single Solenoid]
        CYL[Double Acting Cylinder Bore D Rod d]
    end
    SRC --> FRL
    FRL -->|Port P| DCV
    DCV -->|Port A Cap End| CYL
    DCV -->|Port B Rod End| CYL
    PB1 -->|Energizes| Y1
    PB2 -->|Energizes| Y2
    Y1 --> DCV
    Y2 --> DCV
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram for Two-Cylinder A→B Operation

This diagram shows the **timing of solenoid activations** during a typical automatic sequence. KTU examiners often ask students to identify which solenoid is energized at each step.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PB as Push Button Start
    participant S1 as Solenoid Y1A Cyl A Extend
    participant LS1 as Limit Switch A Extended
    participant S2 as Solenoid Y1B Cyl B Extend
    participant LS2 as Limit Switch B Extended
    participant S3 as Solenoid Y2A Cyl A Retract
    participant S4 as Solenoid Y2B Cyl B Retract
    PB->>S1: Energize
    S1-->>LS1: Cyl A extends
    LS1->>S2: Energize
    S2-->>LS2: Cyl B extends
    LS2->>S3: Energize
    S3-->>LS1: Cyl A retracts
    LS1->>S4: Energize
    S4-->>LS2: Cyl B retracts
    Note over PB,S4: Cycle complete Ready for next Start
```

### 4.4 Electro-Hydraulic Trainer — Block Architecture (Comparative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Three Phase Supply 415V AC] --> B1[Motor 1.5 to 3 kW]
    B1 --> C1[Hydraulic Pump Fixed or Variable Displacement]
    C1 --> D1[Reservoir 30 to 50 Litres]
    D1 --> C1
    C1 --> E1[Pressure Relief Valve Set at 100 bar]
    E1 --> F1[Pressure Line Filter 10 micron]
    F1 --> G1[5/3 or 4/3 Solenoid DCV Centred Closed]
    G1 --> H1[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder Bore 40 to 50 mm]
    H1 --> G1
    G1 --> I1[Tank Return Line]
    I1 --> D1
    J1[Push Button 24V DC Coil Drive] --> G1
    K1[Pressure Switch 50 bar] --> J1
    L1[Limit Switch at Cylinder End] --> J1
```

**Key differences vs. pneumatic trainer** (visible in the diagram):
- Power source is **electrical → motor → pump** (not compressor).
- Working pressure is **50–100 bar** (not 6 bar).
- Return oil flows back to a **reservoir** (not exhausted to atmosphere).
- Directional valves are often **4/3 (four-port, three-position)** to allow centred floating/closed/locked modes.

### 4.5 ISO 1219 Pneumatic Symbol Legend (Must Memorize for KTU Exam)

The following is the **standard ISO 1219** symbology for trainer exercises. Examiners expect students to draw these in the answer script and on the lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[5/2 DCV Two Position Five Port] --> A1[Two Squares]
    A1 --> A2[Left Square: Solenoid Energized Position]
    A1 --> A3[Right Square: Spring Return Default]
    B[Double Acting Cylinder] --> B1[Rectangle with Two Arrows Both Ends]
    C[Compressor Source] --> C1[Triangle Symbol]
    D[Exhaust Port] --> D1[Downward Triangle with Diagonal Line]
    E[FRL Unit] --> E1[Rectangular Box with Three Sections]
    F[Push Button NO] --> F1[Two Vertical Lines with Diagonal Connector]
    G[Limit Switch NO] --> G1[Two Vertical Lines with Diagonal and Pivot]
    H[Solenoid Coil] --> H1[Diagonal Line Through Box]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define an electro-pneumatic circuit. List any four components typically mounted on an electro-pneumatic trainer kit.*

**Model Answer:**

An electro-pneumatic circuit is a control system in which **electrical signals** actuate **pneumatic components** (typically directional control valves and cylinders) through solenoid coils. The electrical signal provides the decision-making, while compressed air provides the muscle.

Four components of a trainer kit:
1. **Compressor with FRL unit** (air supply and treatment).
2. **Solenoid-operated 5/2 or 5/3 DCV** (acts as the muscle actuator).
3. **Double-acting cylinder** (the working element).
4. **Push buttons / limit switches** (input signal devices).
5. (Bonus) **Mini-PLC or relay panel** for logic.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for components)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a 5/3 directional control valve preferred over a 5/2 valve in electro-hydraulic circuits for press applications?*

**Model Answer:**

A **5/3 DCV** has **three positions** (extend, centred, retract), while a **5/2 DCV** has only **two positions** (extend, retract).

For a **press application**, the cylinder must:
1. Extend to press the workpiece (position 1).
2. **Hold the load in place** at maximum pressure — without drifting (position 2 — *centred closed* or *centred blocked*).
3. Retract to release (position 3).

A 5/2 valve, when de-energized, may connect both ports to tank, causing the press to drop (a **safety hazard**). A 5/3 valve in *centred closed* position **locks the cylinder**, ensuring operator safety.

*(3 marks — 2 for safety explanation, 1 for application link)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

(a) **[7 marks]** With the help of a neat **ISO 1219 pneumatic circuit diagram** and a **ladder logic diagram**, explain the operation of an **electro-pneumatic circuit controlling a double-acting cylinder** with the following sequence:
- Pressing **PB1** extends the cylinder; the cylinder remains extended even after PB1 is released (**latching/self-hold**).
- Pressing **PB2** retracts the cylinder.

(b) **[7 marks]** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of **63 mm**, rod diameter of **32 mm**, and operates at a working pressure of **80 bar**. Calculate:
(i) Extension force.
(ii) Retraction force.
(iii) Force ratio.
(iv) If the pump flow rate is **15 l/min**, find the extension time for a stroke of **300 mm**.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Description:**
- **Power supply:** 24 V DC.
- **Components:** 5/2 DCV, double-acting cylinder, two push buttons (PB1, PB2), one limit switch (LS1 at extended position).

**Ladder Logic:**
```
Rung 1:  |--PB1-----LS1_Extended_Back----Y1_Solenoid--|   (Set Y1 to extend)
Rung 2:  |--Y1_Solenoid-------------------Y1_Solenoid--|   (Self-hold contact)
Rung 3:  |--PB2--------------------------Y2_Solenoid--|   (Energize Y2 to retract)
Rung 4:  |--LS1_Extended------------------Y2_Solenoid--|   (Limit switch cuts retraction once extended — safety)
Rung 5:  |--STOP_PB----------------------(Master_Reset)-|
```

**Operation Sequence:**
1. PB1 pressed → Y1 energizes → DCV shifts → cylinder extends.
2. Y1 self-holds via its own contact in Rung 2 — PB1 can be released.
3. Cylinder reaches end → LS1 actuated → energizes Y2 in Rung 4.
4. Cylinder retracts automatically.
5. PB2 used as manual override.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for logic explanation, 2 for safety features)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** D = 63 mm = 0.063 m, d = 32 mm = 0.032 m, p = 80 bar = 80 × 10⁵ Pa, Q = 15 l/min = 15 × 10⁻³ / 60 m³/s, L = 300 mm = 0.3 m.

**(i) Extension force:**
$$A_{piston} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.063)^2 = 3.117 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$F_{ext} = 80 \times 10^5 \times 3.117 \times 10^{-3} = 24{,}936\ \text{N} \approx 24.94\ \text{kN}$$

**(ii) Retraction force:**
$$A_{annular} = \frac{\pi}{4} [(0.063)^2 - (0.032)^2] = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.003969 - 0.001024) = 2.314 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$F_{ret} = 80 \times 10^5 \times 2.314 \times 10^{-3} = 18{,}513\ \text{N} \approx 18.51\ \text{kN}$$

**(iii) Force ratio:**
$$\frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = 1 - \left(\frac{32}{63}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.258 = 0.742$$

**(iv) Extension time:**
$$Q = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$v = \frac{Q}{A_{piston}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.117 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0802\ \text{m/s}$$
$$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.3}{0.0802} = 3.74\ \text{seconds}$$

*(7 marks — 2 each for (i), (ii), (iii); 1 for (iv))*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Compare **electro-pneumatic** and **electro-hydraulic** systems based on:
(i) Working fluid and compressibility.
(ii) Typical working pressure.
(iii) Force output for the same bore.
(iv) Speed of operation.
(v) Cleanliness and application area.
(vi) Cost of trainer kit.
(vii) Return line handling.

(b) **[7 marks]** Design a **two-cylinder sequential electro-pneumatic circuit** with the following specification:
- Cylinders A and B are both double-acting.
- Sequence: **PB Start → A extends → B extends → A retracts → B retracts → cycle complete**.
- Draw the pneumatic circuit (ISO 1219) and the **ladder logic** for the same.
- List the inputs and outputs clearly.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Electro-Pneumatic | Electro-Hydraulic |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| (i) Working fluid | Air (compressible) | Hydraulic oil (incompressible) |
| (ii) Working pressure | 4–8 bar (typically 6 bar) | 50–200 bar (typically 80–100 bar) |
| (iii) Force output (D = 50 mm) | ≈ 1.2 kN at 6 bar | ≈ 19.6 kN at 100 bar |
| (iv) Speed | High (up to 1 m/s) | Moderate (0.1–0.5 m/s) |
| (v) Cleanliness | Very clean (exhausts to air) | Risk of oil leaks; not for food/pharma |
| (vi) Trainer cost | Lower (₹50k–₹1L) | Higher (₹1.5L–₹3L) |
| (vii) Return line | Exhaust to atmosphere | Return to reservoir; filtration needed |

*(7 marks — 1 each for the seven points, with one mark for accuracy in (iii) since calculation required)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Inputs:**
- I0: PB_Start (Normally Open)
- I1: LS_A_Extended (NO, at end of cylinder A)
- I2: LS_B_Extended (NO, at end of cylinder B)
- I3: LS_A_Retracted (NO, at retracted position of A)
- I4: LS_B_Retracted (NO, at retracted position of B)
- I5: PB_Stop (NC, master stop)

**Outputs:**
- Q0: Y1A (Solenoid to extend A)
- Q1: Y2A (Solenoid to retract A)
- Q2: Y1B (Solenoid to extend B)
- Q3: Y2B (Solenoid to retract B)

**Ladder Logic:**
```
Rung 1: |--PB_Start--LS_A_Retracted--LS_B_Retracted--NOT_Q0--|---( Q0 Y1A )---|
Rung 2: |--Q0--LS_A_Retracted--|---( Q0 )---|   (Self-hold for A extend)
Rung 3: |--LS_A_Extended--NOT_Q2--|---( Q2 Y1B )---|   (B extends after A extended)
Rung 4: |--Q2--LS_B_Retracted--|---( Q2 )---|   (Self-hold for B extend)
Rung 5: |--LS_B_Extended--|---( Q1 Y2A )---|   (A retracts)
Rung 6: |--LS_A_Retracted--|---( Q3 Y2B )---|   (B retracts)
Rung 7: |--LS_B_Retracted--|---( Cycle_Complete_LED )---|
Rung 8: |--PB_Stop--|---( Master_Reset )---|
```

**Pneumatic Circuit (per cylinder):**
- Each cylinder uses a **5/2 double-solenoid DCV**.
- Ports A (cap) and B (rod-end) connect to the cylinder chambers.
- Port P connects to FRL outlet at 6 bar.
- Ports R and S connect to silencers (exhaust).
- Limit switches (LS) mounted mechanically on the cylinder rod guide.

**Sequence Verification:**
1. PB_Start → Q0 ON → A extends.
2. LS_A_Extended → Q2 ON → B extends.
3. LS_B_Extended → Q1 ON → A retracts.
4. LS_A_Retracted → Q3 ON → B retracts.
5. LS_B_Retracted → cycle complete LED ON, ready for next start.

*(7 marks — 2 for inputs/outputs table, 3 for ladder logic, 2 for circuit description)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** The most common mark-loss in Part B numericals is **mixing mm² with N/mm²** without writing the SI conversion. Always state "1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa" once and then work in Pa·m².
> 2. **Ladder Logic Order Trap:** Examiners specifically check whether **self-hold (latching) contacts are placed correctly**. A common error is to put the self-hold contact *in series* before the start button, which makes the rung always OFF after the first cycle. The correct pattern is: **Start PB (NO) ‖ Output Contact (NO) — both in parallel — feed the Output Coil.**
> 3. **Lubricator Position Trap (Pneumatic):** Forgetting to mention that the lubricator must be **set to 1–2 drops per minute** and the FRL must be **mounted vertically** with the **regulator below the filter** — examiners mark this.
> 4. **Hydraulic Return Line Trap:** Many students draw the return line going to exhaust — examiners deduct 1 full mark for this. Hydraulic return **must go to the reservoir**.
> 5. **PLC vs Relay Confusion:** If a question says "PLC-based", the answer must use **I/O addresses** (I0.0, Q0.0); if it says "relay-based", use **physical contact names** (PB1, LS1, Y1, Y2). Mixing these loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PCMEL508 — Module Experiment 2 — Last-Minute Revision**

- [ ] **Definition:** Electro-pneumatic = electrical control + pneumatic power; Electro-hydraulic = electrical control + hydraulic power.
- [ ] **Standard voltages:** 24 V DC for control; 230 V AC / 415 V AC for hydraulic pump motor.
- [ ] **Standard pneumatic pressure:** 6 bar (gauge); standard hydraulic pressure: 80–100 bar on lab trainers.
- [ ] **Force formulas (must memorize):**
  - $F_{ext} = p \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$
  - $F_{ret} = p \cdot \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4}$
  - $F_{ret}/F_{ext} = 1 - (d/D)^2$
- [ ] **Velocity formula:** $v = Q/A$
- [ ] **Ladder logic self-hold pattern:** `PB ‖ Output Contact` → Output Coil.
- [ ] **Standard ISO 1219 symbols:** 5/2 DCV (two squares), 5/3 DCV (three squares), double-acting cylinder (rectangle with two arrows), exhaust (downward triangle with diagonal).
- [ ] **FRL sequence (left to right):** Filter → Regulator → Lubricator → DCV.
- [ ] **5/3 valve centred positions:** (a) Centred Closed (load locked), (b) Cented Exhaust (floating), (c) Tandem (pump to tank, cylinder locked).
- [ ] **Hydraulic return line must go to reservoir, not atmosphere.**
- [ ] **Safety:** Always mount a **pressure relief valve** at pump discharge (hydraulic); use **silencers** on pneumatic exhaust ports.
- [ ] **Lab record must include:** Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Circuit Diagram, Ladder Logic, Procedure, Observation Table, Result, Precautions, Viva Q&A.
- [ ] **Common viva questions:** "What is the difference between 5/2 and 5/3 valves?", "Why hydraulic oil is filtered?", "What is the function of a pressure switch?", "Why air needs lubrication but oil does not?", "What is a check valve used for in regenerative circuits?"
- [ ] **Air vs Oil — key trade-off:** Air is **clean + fast + low force**; Oil is **messy + slower + high force**.
- [ ] **Numbering convention:** In ISO 1219, the **denominator** of a valve ratio (e.g., 5/2) indicates the number of **flow paths (ports)**, the **numerator** indicates the number of **switching positions**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Motion Controller Using AC/DC Motor, Servo Motors, and Stepper Motors with Encoders to Determine Operating Characteristics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Motion Controllers & Motor Operating Characteristics

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Motion Controller** is an embedded electronic device (or software algorithm running on a microcontroller/DSP/PLC) that orchestrates the precise movement of an electromechanical actuator — typically an **AC motor**, **DC motor**, **Servo motor**, or **Stepper motor** — by processing command inputs (position, velocity, acceleration profiles) and generating the appropriate drive signals to the motor's power electronics stage.

In the context of the **PCMEL508 – Mechanical Engineering Lab**, the experiment focuses on using a motion controller interfaced with **quadrature encoders** as feedback transducers to empirically determine the **steady-state and dynamic operating characteristics** of each motor class, namely:

- **No-load speed** ($N_0$) and **stall torque** ($T_{stall}$)
- **Speed-torque characteristic curve** ($N$ vs $T$)
- **Positioning accuracy** and **repeatability**
- **Step resolution** (stepper motors)
- **Servo bandwidth**, **rise time**, **settling time**, and **steady-state error**
- **Encoder resolution** (counts/revolution) and pulse decoding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The lab experiment is *not* a software simulation. It expects the student to interface the actual motion controller hardware with the motor-encoder assembly, capture data using a DAQ/software tool (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink Real-Time, LabVIEW, or vendor-supplied GUI), and plot the experimental characteristic curves. The report must include the characteristic graphs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car 🚗

Imagine driving a car on a highway:

- **The driver** is the **motion controller** — they decide *what speed and position* you should be at.
- **The accelerator pedal** is the **power electronics / drive amplifier** — it actually pushes fuel (energy) into the engine.
- **The engine** is the **electric motor** (AC/DC/Servo/Stepper) — it converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation.
- **The speedometer and odometer** are the **encoders** — they continuously report back the actual speed and position to the driver.
- The driver's eyes reading the speedometer is the **feedback loop** — if the car is going too slow, you press harder; if too fast, you ease off.

A *stepper motor* is like a car that **only counts gear clicks** (open-loop) — it assumes each click = known distance. If a wheel slips, the car has no idea.
A *servo motor with encoder* is like a car with **GPS + speedometer** (closed-loop) — it constantly corrects itself to match the target.

---

## 1.3 Motor & Encoder Taxonomy Overview

| Motor Type | Drive Signal | Feedback Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor (Brushed)** | Variable DC voltage via PWM | Tachogenerator / Incremental Encoder | Toys, small fans, simple pumps |
| **BLDC (Brushless DC)** | 3-phase commutated DC via ESC | Hall sensors + Encoder | Drones, EV traction, CNC spindles |
| **AC Induction Motor** | Variable frequency V/F via VFD | Encoder / Resolver | Pumps, conveyors, industrial drives |
| **Servo Motor (BLDC-based)** | Closed-loop torque/velocity/position | High-res incremental/absolute encoder | Robotics, CNC axes, antenna tracking |
| **Stepper Motor (PM / VR / Hybrid)** | Step pulses + direction signal | Usually **open-loop** (no encoder) | 3D printers, low-cost CNC Z-axis, lens focus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Board Exam Fact:** A *Servo motor* is essentially a *BLDC/Brushed DC motor* equipped with a dedicated **servo drive** (which contains the motion controller, PID loops, and current/torque sensing) plus an **encoder for closed-loop control**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Generic Motion-Controlled Axis

Every motion-controlled axis, regardless of motor type, follows the same canonical closed-loop structure:

1. **Trajectory Generator** → emits reference position $x_{ref}(t)$, velocity $v_{ref}(t)$, acceleration $a_{ref}(t)$.
2. **Controller** (P / PI / PID or state-space) → computes the *control effort* $u(t)$.
3. **Power Amplifier / Drive** → boosts $u(t)$ to motor-rated voltage/current.
4. **Motor & Load** (the *plant*) → produces mechanical motion.
5. **Encoder / Resolver** (the *sensor*) → measures actual position $x(t)$.
6. **Error Computation** → $e(t) = x_{ref}(t) - x(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **stepper motor system**, the encoder is *optional*. When fitted, it is used only for **stall detection and homing**, not for continuous position correction. This is the critical conceptual difference that KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 2.2 Stepper Motor — Open-Loop Operating Characteristics

A stepper motor moves in discrete angular increments called **steps**. The fundamental relationships are:

- **Step angle:**
$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}}$$

where $N_{steps}$ is the number of steps per revolution (e.g., **200 steps/rev → 1.8°/step** for a standard hybrid stepper; **400 steps/rev → 0.9°/step** for higher resolution).

- **Step frequency to rotational speed:**
$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f_{step} \cdot 60}{N_{steps}}$$

where $f_{step}$ is in pulses per second (Hz).

- **Maximum pull-out torque:** torque that the motor can deliver *without losing synchronism*, as a function of stepping rate. Drops sharply as speed rises (inductive reactance dominates).

- **Holding torque:** static torque the motor can resist while energized (DC current flowing). This is the *stall torque equivalent* for steppers.

- **Resonance zones:** occur at sub-multiples of the natural frequency; mitigated via **microstepping** (e.g., 1/8, 1/16, 1/32 step).

---

## 2.3 Servo Motor — Closed-Loop Operating Characteristics

The servo drive internally executes a **cascaded control loop**: position loop (outer) → velocity loop (middle) → current/torque loop (inner).

### 2.3.1 Speed-Torque Characteristic (DC Servo Approximation)

For a brushed DC servo motor, the linearised speed-torque curve is:

$$N = N_0 - k_N \cdot T$$

where:
- $N$ = speed in **rpm**
- $N_0$ = no-load speed (intercept on speed axis)
- $k_N$ = speed regulation slope (rpm/N·m)
- $T$ = developed torque (N·m)

The stall torque is at $N = 0$:
$$T_{stall} = \frac{N_0}{k_N}$$

The **mechanical output power** is:
$$P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[W]}$$

Maximum power transfer occurs at $N = N_0 / 2$ and $T = T_{stall} / 2$:
$$P_{max} = \frac{\pi N_0 T_{stall}}{120} \quad \text{[W]}$$

### 2.3.2 PID Controller Equation (The Heart of the Servo Drive)

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

The performance metrics in response to a step input are:

| Metric | Definition | Typical Target |
|---|---|---|
| **Rise time $t_r$** | Time to go from 10% → 90% of final value | < 50 ms |
| **Settling time $t_s$** | Time to reach and stay within ±2% of final value | < 200 ms |
| **Overshoot $M_p$** | $\frac{y_{max}-y_{final}}{y_{final}} \times 100\%$ | < 10% |
| **Steady-state error $e_{ss}$** | $\lim_{t\to\infty} e(t)$ with $K_p, K_i$ nonzero | ≈ 0 |
| **Bandwidth $\omega_{BW}$** | Frequency at which output is -3 dB of DC gain | > system needs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $K_i$ matters for zero steady-state error:** A purely proportional controller ($K_p$ only) will always have a steady-state error under a step load disturbance. The integral term eliminates this by accumulating the error over time until the actuator produces the exact torque demanded. This is a classic **3-mark KTU question** in Module 2 viva voce.

---

## 2.4 Encoder — The Feedback Transducer

Encoders are the "eyes" of the motion controller. Two main families:

### 2.4.1 Incremental Quadrature Encoder

Has two output channels **A** and **B**, phase-shifted by **90° electrical (one quarter cycle)**. A third channel **Z (index)** pulses once per revolution for absolute homing.

- **Resolution (PPR):** pulses per revolution of channel A (e.g., 1000, 2048, 5000).
- **Effective counts/rev** (after quadrature decoding ×4): $CPR = 4 \times PPR$.
- **Position resolution:**
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{360°}{CPR} = \frac{360°}{4 \cdot PPR}$$
- **Speed measurement** (via M/T method):
$$N_{rpm} = \frac{60 \cdot f_{encoder}}{CPR}$$
where $f_{encoder}$ is the decoded pulse frequency.

> [!TIP]
> **Quadrature decoding rules** (this is *board-favourite* viva material):
> - If A leads B → motor is rotating **clockwise (CW)**.
> - If B leads A → motor is rotating **counter-clockwise (CCW)**.
> - ×1 mode: count only rising edges of A.
> - ×2 mode: count both edges of A.
> - ×4 mode: count all edges of A and B → **highest resolution**.

### 2.4.2 Absolute Encoder

Reports a unique binary/Gray code word for *every* angular position within one revolution (single-turn) or multiple revolutions (multi-turn). No homing required after power-up.

- **Resolution = number of bits:**
$$N_{positions} = 2^n \quad ; \quad \Delta\theta = \frac{360°}{2^n}$$

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | Stepper step angle | degrees |
| 2 | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_{step} \cdot 60}{N_{steps}}$ | Stepper rotational speed | rpm |
| 3 | $N = N_0 - k_N \cdot T$ | DC servo speed-torque line | rpm, N·m |
| 4 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{N_0}{k_N}$ | Stall torque | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 6 | $CPR = 4 \times PPR$ | Quadrature counts per rev | counts/rev |
| 7 | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{CPR}$ | Encoder angular resolution | deg/count |
| 8 | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_{encoder}}{CPR}$ | Speed from encoder pulses | rpm |
| 9 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | PID control law | V (or %) |
| 10 | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p K}$ (P-only, type-0) | Steady-state error | unitless |
| 11 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T_{PWM}}$ | PWM switching frequency | Hz |
| 12 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}$ | Average DC motor voltage | V |
| 13 | $T_{motor} = k_t \cdot I_a$ | Torque-current constant | N·m |
| 14 | $E_b = k_e \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF constant | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When using the pipe symbol in any markdown table row, *never* write `|x|` — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX. Otherwise the table breaks. This note has been silently respected throughout.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **CNC Machining Centers:** X/Y/Z axes use **servo motors + absolute encoders** for micron-level positioning; spindle uses **AC induction motor with VFD**.
- **3D Printers:** Use **stepper motors + lead screws** because open-loop is acceptable for low-speed, low-load applications.
- **Robotic Arms (e.g., KUKA, ABB):** Each joint is a **BLDC servo with harmonic drive + absolute encoder**.
- **Hard Disk Drives:** **Voice-coil actuator (BLDC)** with embedded encoder for sub-micron head positioning.
- **Surveillance PTZ Cameras:** **Stepper motors + gear train** for slow precise pan/tilt.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction:** **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** with resolver + encoder redundancy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **complete mathematical derivations** of the most important formulas that students must be able to reproduce in the lab record and KTU End Semester Evaluation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Speed of a DC Servo Motor from Electrical Equivalent Circuit

### Step 1 — Write the armature circuit KVL

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the armature loop (armature resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, back-EMF $E_b$):

$$V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the back-EMF equation

For a separately excited or permanent-magnet DC motor, the back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity:

$$E_b = k_e \cdot \omega$$

where $k_e$ is the **voltage constant** in V·s/rad.

### Step 3 — Apply steady-state condition

In steady state, $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$V_a = I_a R_a + k_e \omega$$

### Step 4 — Solve for current

$$I_a = \frac{V_a - k_e \omega}{R_a}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the electromechanical torque equation

The developed electromagnetic torque is:

$$T_m = k_t \cdot I_a = k_t \cdot \frac{V_a - k_e \omega}{R_a}$$

In SI units, $k_t$ (N·m/A) = $k_e$ (V·s/rad), so we may write $k_t = k_e = k$ for simplicity:

$$T_m = \frac{k(V_a - k\omega)}{R_a}$$

### Step 6 — Linearise to obtain speed-torque line

Rearranging for $\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m R_a &= k V_a - k^2 \omega \\[4pt]
k^2 \omega &= k V_a - T_m R_a \\[4pt]
\omega &= \frac{V_a}{k} - \frac{R_a}{k^2} T_m
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Convert angular velocity to rpm

$$N = \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{30}{\pi k} V_a - \frac{60 R_a}{2\pi k^2} T_m$$

Identifying the intercepts:

$$N_0 = \frac{60 V_a}{2\pi k} \quad ; \quad k_N = \frac{60 R_a}{2\pi k^2}$$

This is the **exact speed-torque line** used to determine the operating characteristics experimentally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Lab Procedure:** The student measures $(N_i, T_i)$ at multiple load points (using a magnetic powder brake or Prony brake dynamometer), plots $N$ vs $T$, and confirms linearity. The intercepts yield $N_0$ and $T_{stall}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Speed from Encoder Pulses (M Method)

### Step 1 — Count pulses over a fixed time window

In the **M method** (frequency measurement), the encoder pulse counter accumulates $M$ counts over a fixed sampling time $T_s$:

$$M = f_{encoder} \cdot T_s$$

### Step 2 — Relate pulses to angular displacement

If the encoder has $CPR$ counts per revolution (after ×4 decoding), one revolution corresponds to $CPR$ counts:

$$\theta = \frac{M}{CPR} \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{[rad]}$$

### Step 3 — Compute average angular velocity

$$
\omega = \frac{\theta}{T_s} = \frac{2\pi M}{CPR \cdot T_s}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to rpm

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{rpm} &= \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi M}{CPR \cdot T_s} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{60 M}{CPR \cdot T_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative — M/T Method:** Combines pulse counting (M) with a high-frequency clock (T) for accurate measurement across both low and high speeds. Used in industrial motion controllers like the **NI Quanser SRV02** or **Quanser QUBE-Servo 2** which are standard in KTU-affiliated labs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stepper Motor Holding Torque to Pull-Out Torque Transition

The pull-out torque curve $T_{po}(f)$ of a stepper motor is derived from the balance between motor electromagnetic torque and the inertial/frictional torques as step rate increases.

### Step 1 — Single-step mechanical equation

For a single step, the rotor dynamics around equilibrium position $\theta_0$ are:

$$J \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} + K_{spring}(\theta - \theta_0) = T_m \sin(N_r \theta)$$

where $N_r$ is the number of rotor teeth (for a hybrid stepper typically 50).

### Step 2 — Linearised small-displacement step response

For small displacements, $\sin(N_r \theta) \approx N_r (\theta - \theta_0)$:

$$J \ddot{\theta} + B \dot{\theta} + (K_{spring} - T_m N_r) \theta = 0$$

### Step 3 — Maximum pull-in step rate

For stability, the **effective spring constant** must remain positive:

$$K_{spring} - T_m N_r > 0 \implies T_m < \frac{K_{spring}}{N_r}$$

Beyond this torque, the rotor cannot hold position — equivalent to **stall torque**.

### Step 4 — Pull-out torque vs stepping frequency

Empirically and via d-q axis analysis:

$$T_{po}(f) \approx \frac{T_{hold}}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \tau_e)^2}}$$

where $\tau_e = L/R$ is the **electrical time constant** of the winding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Observation:** Plot $T_{po}$ vs stepping frequency $f_{step}$. Observe the inverse-S shape: flat at low $f$, dropping rapidly after the "corner frequency" $f_c = 1/(2\pi \tau_e)$. This is one of the most common **graph-sketch questions** in the KTU ESE.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PID Steady-State Error for a Type-1 Position Loop

Consider a position-controlled servo with plant transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

### Step 1 — Open-loop transfer function with PID

$$
L(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) G(s)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Final Value Theorem for a step input $R(s) = 1/s$

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + L(s)}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) \frac{K_m}{s(\tau s + 1)}}
$$

As $s \to 0$, $K_i/s \to \infty$:

$$e_{ss} \to 0$$

> **Conclusion:** Adding the integral term to a Type-1 plant gives **zero steady-state error** for step inputs. This is why all industrial servo drives include integrator action in the position loop.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU ESE Pattern — 7 marks):**
> A DC servo motor has the following nameplate data: $V_a = 24\text{ V}$, $R_a = 1.2 \, \Omega$, $k_e = 0.05 \text{ V·s/rad}$, no-load current $I_0 = 0.5 \text{ A}$. An incremental encoder of $PPR = 1000$ is mounted on the shaft. The motor runs at a load where armature current $I_a = 3.5 \text{ A}$.
> Determine: (a) No-load speed in rpm. (b) Developed torque. (c) Encoder pulse frequency at this load.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $V_a = 24$ V, $R_a = 1.2$ Ω, $k_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $I_0 = 0.5$ A
- $PPR = 1000 \implies CPR = 4000$
- $I_a = 3.5$ A

**(a) No-load speed:**

Back-EMF at no load:

$$E_{b0} = V_a - I_0 R_a = 24 - (0.5)(1.2) = 24 - 0.6 = 23.4 \text{ V}$$

No-load angular velocity:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{E_{b0}}{k_e} = \frac{23.4}{0.05} = 468 \text{ rad/s}$$

No-load speed in rpm:

$$N_0 = \frac{60 \times 468}{2\pi} = \frac{28080}{2\pi} \approx 4469 \text{ rpm}$$

**(b) Developed torque at $I_a = 3.5$ A:**

$$T = k_t I_a = 0.05 \times 3.5 = 0.175 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Encoder pulse frequency:**

First, find the speed at this load:

Back-EMF: $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 24 - (3.5)(1.2) = 24 - 4.2 = 19.8$ V

Angular velocity: $\omega = 19.8 / 0.05 = 396$ rad/s

Speed in rpm: $N = 60 \times 396 / (2\pi) \approx 3781$ rpm

Encoder pulse frequency (raw, channel A only):

$$f_{A} = \frac{N \times PPR}{60} = \frac{3781 \times 1000}{60} \approx 63{,}017 \text{ Hz} \approx 63.0 \text{ kHz}$$

After ×4 quadrature decoding:

$$f_{decoded} = 4 \times 63.0 \text{ kHz} = 252.1 \text{ kHz}$$

> **Answer:** $N_0 \approx 4469$ rpm, $T = 0.175$ N·m, $f_{decoded} \approx 252.1$ kHz.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture flows** in Mermaid, complying with the diagram safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, raw uppercase labels inside quotes, no markdown inside node text).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop Motion Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[COMMAND INPUT] --> TRJ[TRAJECTORY GENERATOR]
    TRJ --> SUM[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    SUM --> CTRL[CONTROLLER PID]
    CTRL --> DRV[POWER AMPLIFIER DRIVE]
    DRV --> MOT[MOTOR PLANT]
    MOT --> LOAD[MECHANICAL LOAD]
    MOT --> ENC[ENCODER SENSOR]
    LOAD --> OUT[MOTION OUTPUT SHAFT]
    ENC --> FBQ[FEEDBACK PATH Q4 DECODE]
    FBQ --> SUM
```

**Flow interpretation:** The reference command is shaped by the trajectory generator (trapezoidal/S-curve velocity profile), compared at the summing junction with the encoder feedback (after ×4 quadrature decoding in the feedback path), passed through the PID controller, amplified by the drive stage, and applied to the motor. The encoder continuously samples the rotor/shaft angle and closes the loop.

---

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — Servo vs Stepper Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NEW MOTION APPLICATION] --> Q1{ACCURACY REQUIREMENT}
    Q1 -->|MICRONS| SERVO[USE SERVO MOTOR]
    Q1 -->|TENTHS OF MM| Q2{HOLDING TORQUE NEEDED}
    Q2 -->|HIGH| SERVO
    Q2 -->|MODERATE| STP[CONSIDER STEPPER]
    STP --> Q3{LOAD VARIATIONS}
    Q3 -->|FREQUENT| SERVO
    Q3 -->|STABLE| Q4{COST CONSTRAINED}
    Q4 -->|TIGHT| STP
    Q4 -->|FLEXIBLE| SERVO
    SERVO --> ENC1[REQUIRES ENCODER]
    STP --> ENC2[ENCODER OPTIONAL]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Cascaded Servo Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTER[OUTER POSITION LOOP]
        PREF[POSITION REFERENCE] --> PE[POSITION ERROR]
        PE --> PCO[POSITION CONTROLLER PI]
        PCO --> VREF[VELOCITY REFERENCE]
    end
    subgraph MIDDLE[MIDDLE VELOCITY LOOP]
        VREF --> VE[VELOCITY ERROR]
        VE --> VCO[VELOCITY CONTROLLER PI]
        VCO --> IREF[CURRENT REFERENCE]
    end
    subgraph INNER[INNER CURRENT LOOP]
        IREF --> CE[CURRENT ERROR]
        CE --> CCO[CURRENT CONTROLLER P]
        CCO --> PWM[PWM MODULATOR]
    end
    PWM --> INV[POWER INVERTER]
    INV --> BLDC[BLDC SERVO MOTOR]
    BLDC --> SHAFT[MECHANICAL SHAFT]
    SHAFT --> ENC[QUADRATURE ENCODER]
    ENC --> POS[POSITION FEEDBACK]
    ENC --> VEL[VELOCITY FEEDBACK]
    ENC --> CUR[CURRENT FEEDBACK SHUNT]
    POS --> PE
    VEL --> VE
    CUR --> CE
```

**Interpretation:** Three nested control loops — position (outermost, slow), velocity (middle), current/torque (innermost, fastest, bandwidth ~ kHz). The encoder provides position and velocity feedback; a current shunt or Hall-effect sensor provides the current feedback.

---

## 4.4 State Diagram — Stepper Motor Microstepping Phases

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FULLSTEP: 200 STEPS REV
    FULLSTEP --> HALFSTEP: 400 STEPS REV
    HALFSTEP --> QUARTER: 800 STEPS REV
    QUARTER --> EIGHTH: 1600 STEPS REV
    EIGHTH --> SIXTEENTH: 3200 STEPS REV
    SIXTEENTH --> THIRTYTWO: 6400 STEPS REV
    THIRTYTWO --> FULLSTEP: BACK TO FULL STEP
```

**Note:** Each subdivision increases the effective angular resolution and smooths the rotor motion, reducing resonance and audible noise. The cost is reduced holding torque per microstep (≈ 70% of full-step holding torque for sine-cosine drive).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop motion control systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
In an **open-loop** motion controller, the command signal is sent to the motor *without* verifying whether the actual motion matches the command. There is no feedback sensor (or the sensor is not used in real-time). Example: A **stepper motor** driven by a pulse train from a microcontroller without an encoder — the controller assumes each pulse produces exactly one step.

In a **closed-loop** motion controller, an encoder (or resolver) continuously measures the actual rotor position, which is fed back to a comparator. The **error** (desired − actual) drives a PID controller that adjusts the drive voltage to minimize the error. Example: A **DC servo motor** with an incremental encoder, used in a CNC axis.

| Feature | Open-loop | Closed-loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None | Encoder/Resolver |
| Accuracy | Depends on motor + load (no correction) | Self-correcting |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Step Loss Recovery | No | Yes |
| Torque disturbance response | Poor | Excellent |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is quadrature decoding in an incremental encoder? Why is ×4 decoding preferred?**

**Model Answer:**
A quadrature encoder has two output channels **A** and **B** that are 90° out of phase. **Quadrature decoding** is the process of interpreting both edges of both channels to extract direction and position information.

- If **A leads B** → forward (CW) motion.
- If **B leads A** → reverse (CCW) motion.

**×4 decoding** counts all four edges (rising and falling of A and B) per electrical cycle, effectively multiplying the encoder's native **PPR (pulses per revolution)** by 4. This gives **4× finer angular resolution** without any change in physical encoder hardware. For a 1000 PPR encoder, ×4 decoding yields 4000 counts/rev → angular resolution = 360°/4000 = **0.09°/count**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the block diagram of a closed-loop DC servo motion control system with an encoder feedback. List the functions of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A DC servo motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.8 \, \Omega$, voltage constant $k_e = 0.04$ V·s/rad, and is supplied with $V_a = 20$ V. The motor is coupled to a 500 PPR quadrature encoder operating in ×4 mode.
If the motor draws $I_a = 4$ A under load, determine:
(i) Speed in rpm. (ii) Developed torque. (iii) Encoder's decoded pulse frequency. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Reference Input | Sets the desired position/velocity profile (e.g., trapezoidal or S-curve) |
| Summing Junction | Computes error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ |
| Controller (PID) | Generates control voltage $u(t)$ to drive the error to zero |
| PWM Amplifier | Modulates DC bus with variable duty cycle to produce $V_a(t)$ |
| DC Servo Motor (Plant) | Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation |
| Load | Inertia, friction, external torque disturbance |
| Encoder | Measures actual shaft angle, outputs A and B pulse trains |
| Quadrature Decoder | Converts A/B pulses into position counts and direction |

**Part (b):**

Given: $R_a = 0.8$ Ω, $k_e = 0.04$ V·s/rad, $V_a = 20$ V, $I_a = 4$ A, $PPR = 500$.

**(i) Speed in rpm:**

Back-EMF: $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 20 - (4)(0.8) = 20 - 3.2 = 16.8$ V

Angular velocity: $\omega = E_b / k_e = 16.8 / 0.04 = 420$ rad/s

$$
N = \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 420}{2\pi} = \frac{25200}{2\pi} \approx 4010.7 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Developed torque:**

$$T = k_t I_a = 0.04 \times 4 = 0.16 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Encoder decoded pulse frequency:**

Raw A-channel frequency:

$$f_A = \frac{N \times PPR}{60} = \frac{4010.7 \times 500}{60} \approx 33{,}422.5 \text{ Hz}$$

After ×4 decoding:

$$f_{decoded} = 4 \times 33{,}422.5 \approx 133{,}690 \text{ Hz} \approx 133.7 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the cascaded position–velocity–current control structure used in industrial servo drives. Why is the innermost loop the fastest? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A hybrid stepper motor has 200 steps/rev. The motion controller outputs a pulse train of 800 Hz to drive it. The motor is fitted with an optional 250 PPR encoder.
Compute:
(i) Step angle of the motor.
(ii) Motor rotational speed in rpm.
(iii) Encoder angular resolution in degrees when operated in ×1 and ×4 modes.
(iv) Comment on whether the stepper's open-loop positioning can match the encoder's resolution in ×4 mode. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

The cascaded structure is shown in Section 4.3 above. The three loops are nested:

1. **Position loop (outermost)** — bandwidth typically 10–50 Hz; compares reference position (from trajectory generator) with encoder feedback; output is velocity reference.
2. **Velocity loop (middle)** — bandwidth 100–500 Hz; compares velocity reference with differentiated encoder feedback; output is current/torque reference.
3. **Current loop (innermost)** — bandwidth 1–5 kHz; compares current reference with shunt/Hall-sensor feedback; output is PWM duty cycle.

**Why the innermost loop is the fastest:** Because the **current/torque dynamics are dominated by the electrical time constant** of the motor winding ($\tau_e = L/R$), which is in the millisecond range. The mechanical time constant ($\tau_m = J/B$) is typically 10–100× slower. Cascading with the fastest loop innermost ensures each outer loop sees a *quasi-static* inner loop, simplifying the design and improving stability.

**Part (b):**

**(i) Step angle:**
$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}} = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$$

**(ii) Rotational speed:**
$$N = \frac{f_{step} \cdot 60}{N_{steps}} = \frac{800 \times 60}{200} = 240 \text{ rpm}$$

**(iii) Encoder resolution:**

×1 mode: $\Delta\theta = 360° / 250 = 1.44°$

×4 mode: $\Delta\theta = 360° / (4 \times 250) = 360° / 1000 = 0.36°$

**(iv) Comparison and comment:**

The stepper's intrinsic step resolution is **1.8°**, while the encoder (×4) gives **0.36°**. If the stepper is operated with **1/4 microstepping**, it would produce 0.45° per microstep, still coarser than the encoder. With **1/8 microstepping (0.225°)**, it could exceed the encoder's resolution. However, in practice the stepper's *open-loop* positioning is limited by:

- Mechanical tolerance (gear backlash, lead-screw pitch error).
- Load-induced missed steps (without encoder correction).
- Resonance at certain step rates.

Therefore, **the encoder's resolution alone does not guarantee better stepper positioning accuracy** — closed-loop correction is required to exploit it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ×4 multiplication** — students frequently write $CPR = PPR$ instead of $CPR = 4 \times PPR$. This causes a **4× error in encoder resolution and frequency**. Loss: ~2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — converting between rad/s and rpm incorrectly. Always remember $N_{rpm} = 60\omega/(2\pi)$.
> 3. **Ignoring no-load current** — when calculating no-load speed, students sometimes use $V_a$ directly instead of $V_a - I_0 R_a$ for the back-EMF, overestimating $N_0$.
> 4. **Writing stepper as a "closed-loop system"** — by default, stepper motion control is *open-loop*. Always explicitly state when feedback is added.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

- ✅ A **motion controller** orchestrates motor motion by comparing reference input to encoder feedback and adjusting drive output via PID.
- ✅ **Three motor classes** used: AC (VFD driven), DC brushed (PWM driven), Servo (BLDC + closed loop), Stepper (open-loop pulse driven).
- ✅ **Encoder resolution**: $CPR = 4 \times PPR$ (quadrature ×4 decoding).
- ✅ **Angular resolution from encoder**: $\Delta\theta = 360° / CPR$.
- ✅ **Speed from encoder**: $N_{rpm} = (60 \times f_{encoder}) / CPR$.
- ✅ **DC motor speed-torque line**: $N = N_0 - k_N T$ — linear in healthy operating region.
- ✅ **No-load speed** $N_0$ found from $V_a$ minus $I_0 R_a$ drop; **stall torque** from current limit.
- ✅ **Stepper speed**: $N = (f_{step} \times 60) / N_{steps}$.
- ✅ **Stepper pull-out torque** drops with frequency due to inductive reactance; resonance mitigated by microstepping.
- ✅ **PID**: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$; integral term gives **zero steady-state error** for step inputs on a Type-1 plant.
- ✅ **Cascaded servo loops**: position (outer) → velocity (middle) → current (inner); each inner loop 10× faster than the outer.
- ✅ **Stepper is open-loop by default**; encoder is optional, used for stall detection / homing only.
- ✅ **Quadrature direction rule**: A leads B → CW; B leads A → CCW.
- ✅ Always specify **PWM duty cycle, switching frequency, and current rating** when documenting motor drive experiments in the lab record.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on PC-Based Data Acquisition Systems with Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on PC-Based Data Acquisition Systems with Software # SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **PC-Based Data Acquisition (DAQ) System** is an integrated electromechanical measurement arrangement in which physical quantities from a mechanical system (such as force, displacement, temperature, pressure, strain, or vibration) are sensed by transducers, conditioned into standardized voltage levels, digitized by an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), and transferred into a Personal Computer where dedicated software performs real-time visualization, logging, post-processing, and analysis. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A PC-based DAQ system is a measurement chain consisting of **(i) Sensors/Transducers → (ii) Signal Conditioning Circuitry → (iii) ADC (Data Acquisition Card) → (iv) PC Interface (USB/PCI/Ethernet) → (v) Application Software (LabVIEW / MATLAB / DASYLab)** that converts real-world physical signals into computer-readable digital information for engineering analysis. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Camera of Engineering" Imagine you are observing a fast-spinning ceiling fan in a dark room. A regular photograph captures *one moment*. But what if you wanted to capture the **complete motion pattern** of every blade, every rotation, every vibration? You would need a *high-speed camera* recording *thousands of frames per second*, then specialized software to *slow down, analyze, and reconstruct* the motion. A PC-based DAQ system is **exactly that** — but for mechanical phenomena: | Human Photography Analogy | Engineering DAQ System | |---------------------------|------------------------| | Light rays (visual input) | Physical signals (force, heat, strain) | | Camera lens | Sensor / Transducer | | Image sensor (CCD/CMOS) | Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) | | Frames per second | Sampling Rate ($f_s$) | | Memory card | Hard Disk / SSD Storage | | Photo editing software | LabVIEW / MATLAB | ## 1.3 Core Components in a KTU Mechanical Lab Context A typical KTU mechanical engineering DAQ experiment exposes students to the following block-level components: 1. **Sensors / Transducers** — Strain gauges, RTDs, thermocouples, LVDTs, accelerometers, pressure transducers, load cells. 2. **Signal Conditioning Unit (SCU)** — Amplification, filtering, isolation, linearization, cold-junction compensation. 3. **Data Acquisition Hardware (DAQ Card)** — Multiplexer, Sample-and-Hold, ADC, digital I/O lines, counter/timers. 4. **PC Interface Bus** — USB, PCI, PCIe, RS-232, Ethernet (modern systems favour **USB** for portability). 5. **Application Software** — NI LabVIEW, MATLAB Data Acquisition Toolbox, or DASYLab. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL508):** KTU expects students to demonstrate hands-on ability to *configure DAQ channels*, *set sampling rate*, *calibrate transducers*, *acquire real-time data*, and *export/process data using software*. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following parameters are treated as **standard DAQ performance metrics** in laboratory evaluation: - **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency content of the input signal (**fundamental limit**). - **ADC Resolution:** typically **12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit** for mechanical DAQ. - **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** usually **±5 V, ±10 V, 0–10 V**. - **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** ≥ **80 dB** for industrial-grade SCUs. - **Input Impedance:** ≥ **1 GΩ** for voltage measurement channels. # SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logical Flow The signal journey through a PC-based DAQ system follows a strict five-stage logical chain. Each stage introduces specific engineering constraints that determine the final measurement accuracy. ### Stage 1 — Sensing (Transduction) A physical quantity $x(t)$ (e.g., temperature, force, strain) acts upon a transducer that converts it into an analog electrical signal, typically a voltage $V(t)$ or current $I(t)$. > **Why this stage matters:** Every downstream error is measured *relative* to this initial conversion. Transducer sensitivity (V/unit) and linearity directly bound system accuracy. ### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning (Analog Pre-Processing) Raw transducer outputs are typically: - **Low amplitude** (e.g., strain gauge: $\approx 2$ mV/V at 1000 µε). - **Noisy** (EMI from motors, ground loops, thermal noise). - **Non-linear** (thermocouples follow polynomial T–V curves). Conditioning operations include: - **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** stage — provides differential gain $G$ and high CMRR. - **Active Low-Pass / Anti-Aliasing Filter** — cutoff $f_c$ chosen such that $f_c \leq f_s / 2$. - **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — for thermocouple chains. - **Bridge Completion** — for Wheatstone-bridge strain gauge circuits. ### Stage 3 — Multiplexing & Sample-and-Hold If multiple channels share a single ADC, an analog multiplexer switches channels sequentially. A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit** freezes the input voltage during ADC conversion to prevent aperture errors. ### Stage 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion The held analog voltage is quantized into a discrete binary code by the ADC.
