The resolution is governed by the number of bits $n$: $$\text{LSB size} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$$ ### Stage 5 — PC Interface & Software Processing Digital codes are streamed into PC memory through USB/PCI bus. Software (LabVIEW Virtual Instruments, MATLAB scripts) handles: - **Real-time plotting** (waveform charts). - **Data logging** (to .csv, .tdms, .mat files). - **Digital filtering** (moving average, Butterworth, FIR). - **FFT / spectral analysis** (frequency-domain study). - **Statistical post-processing** (mean, RMS, standard deviation). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem | Hz | | 2 | $\Delta V = V_{FSR}/2^n$ | ADC Least Significant Bit (LSB) Size | Volts | | 3 | $e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Quantization Error (worst case) | Volts | | 4 | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{FSR}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ | ADC Digital Output Code | counts | | 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Engineering Strain | dimensionless | | 6 | $G_f = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\Delta L / L}$ | Gauge Factor (typical ≈ **2.0** for metallic) | dimensionless | | 7 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$ | Full-bridge Output Voltage | Volts | | 8 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon (1 + \nu)$ | Quarter-bridge with Poisson Compensation | Volts | | 9 | $R_t = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ | RTD Resistance Equation (linear approx.) | Ohms | | 10 | $V_{th} = S \cdot \Delta T + \text{poly. terms}$ | Thermocouple Seebeck Voltage (e.g., Type K) | Volts | | 11 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | | 12 | $t_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{2^n}{f_{clk}}$ | ADC Conversion Time | seconds | **Note on Gauge Factor:** For metallic foil gauges, $G_f \approx \mathbf{2.0}$. For semiconductor gauges, $G_f$ can range from **50 to 200**, making them highly sensitive but more temperature-dependent. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility PC-based DAQ systems are indispensable across: - **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Continuous strain monitoring of bridges, aircraft wings, turbine blades. - **NVH Analysis (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Automotive chassis and powertrain validation. - **Thermal Mapping:** Engine cylinder head temperature profiling using thermocouple grids. - **Process Control:** Closed-loop pressure and flow regulation in hydraulic test rigs. - **Biomechanics:** Gait analysis using accelerometers and load cells. In all these domains, the **software layer** is what differentiates a modern DAQ from a 1970s chart recorder — providing real-time feedback, automated calibration, and reproducible analytical workflows. # SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem ### Statement A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be **uniquely and perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation Let $x(t)$ be a bandlimited signal with Fourier Transform $X(f)$ such that $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_{max}$. The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is obtained by multiplying $x(t)$ with an impulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$: $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \, \delta(t - n T_s)$$ Taking the Fourier Transform, convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency: $$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$ This means the spectrum of $x_s(t)$ consists of **replicas** of $X(f)$ shifted by integer multiples of $f_s$. For **non-overlapping** (alias-free) replicas, the shifted spectra must not overlap: $$f_s - f_{max} \geq f_{max}$$ Solving: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ The minimum theoretical sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aliasing Consequence:** If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, the replicas overlap. A high-frequency component at $f = f_s - f_{max}$ will *appear* as a low-frequency component at $f_{max} - (f_s - f_{max})$. This is why the **anti-aliasing filter** (cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$) is mandatory before the ADC. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Resolution and Quantization Error ### Given - Full-Scale Range: $V_{FSR} = 10 \text{ V}$ - ADC Resolution: $n = 12 \text{ bits}$ ### Step 1: Compute the LSB Size The total number of discrete quantization levels is $2^n$. The smallest distinguishable voltage step (LSB) is: $$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 2: Compute Worst-Case Quantization Error Quantization error is the deviation between the true analog input and its nearest digital representation. It is bounded within half an LSB on either side: $$e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^{n+1}} = \pm \frac{10}{8192} \approx \pm 1.221 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 3: Theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio For an ideal ADC with only quantization noise, the SNR (assuming a full-scale sinusoidal input) is: $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}$$ ### Step 4: Convert Digital Code to Engineering Units If the ADC returns code $D = 2048$ at $V_{FSR} = 10$ V, $n = 12$: $$V_{in} = D \cdot \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = 2048 \cdot \frac{10}{4096} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$ This four-step ladder (LSB → Error → SNR → Reconstruction) is the **standard examiner expectation** in any KTU DAQ numerical problem. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quarter-Bridge Strain Gauge Output ### Wheatstone Bridge Output (Generalized) A quarter-bridge strain gauge configuration has one active arm (resistance $R + \Delta R$) and three fixed arms (resistance $R$). Excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the bridge, and differential output voltage $V_o$ is measured: $$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right)$$ Assuming $\Delta R \ll R$ (small strain approximation, $\varepsilon < 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$): $$V_o \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ ### Substituting Gauge Factor Definition By definition: $$G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} \implies \frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$ Therefore: $$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$ ### Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Given:** $V_{ex} = 5 \text{ V}$, $G_f = 2.0$, $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ **Compute:** $$V_o = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.0 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 2.5 \times 2.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \text{ mV}$$ This tiny millivolt-level signal is exactly why an **instrumentation amplifier with high gain** (often $G = 100$ to $1000$) is mandatory in the signal conditioning stage before the ADC. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Sampling Rate for Typical Mechanical Signals | Mechanical Phenomenon | Frequency Range | Recommended $f_s$ | Rationale | |-----------------------|-----------------|--------------------|-----------| | Quasi-static strain (bridge deflection) | 0 – 1 Hz | 10 Hz | Margin above $2f_{max}$ | | Slow temperature drift | 0 – 0.1 Hz | 1 Hz | Long time constant sensor | | Machine vibration (rotating shaft) | 10 – 1000 Hz | 5 kHz | Captures harmonics | | Acoustic emission (crack detection) | 20 kHz – 1 MHz | 5 MHz | High-frequency transient | | Engine combustion pressure | 0 – 5 kHz | 25 kHz | Crankshaft-resolved | > [!NOTE] > **Examiners' Test Pattern:** Students are often asked, *"Why must sampling rate be at least twice the maximum signal frequency?"* The canonical answer must invoke **aliasing prevention** and reference the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**. # SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete PC-Based DAQ Architecture The following Mermaid flowchart captures every functional block a student must be able to draw from memory in a KTU lab exam. ```mermaid flowchart LR phys[Physical Quantity
Force, Strain, Temp, Vibration] trd[Sensor or Transducer
Strain Gauge, RTD, LVDT, Accelerometer] scu[Signal Conditioning Unit
Amplification, Filtering, CJC] mux[Analog Multiplexer
Channel Selection] shc[Sample and Hold Circuit
Freezes Analog Voltage] adc[Analog to Digital Converter
12 bit or 16 bit ADC] buss[PC Interface Bus
USB, PCI, or Ethernet] pcmem[PC Memory Buffer
RAM Storage] sft[Application Software
LabVIEW or MATLAB] disp[Real Time Display
Waveform Charts] log[Data Logging
CSV or TDMS File] pst[Post Processing
FFT, Filtering, Statistics] phys --> trd trd --> scu scu --> mux mux --> shc shc --> adc adc --> buss buss --> pcmem pcmem --> sft sft --> disp sft --> log sft --> pst ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Strain Gauge DAQ Subsystem (Quarter Bridge) For strain measurement exercises, students must understand the dedicated conditioning chain. ```mermaid flowchart TD spec[Specimen Under Load
Cantilever Beam] sg[Strain Gauge Bonded
on Beam Surface] wb[Wheatstone Bridge
Quarter Bridge Circuit] ina[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain G equals 100 to 500] aaf[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter
Cutoff fc equals fs divided by 2] adcc[ADC inside DAQ Card
16 bit Resolution] usbb[USB Interface to PC] labv[LabVIEW Virtual Instrument
Front Panel and Block Diagram] out[Output Display
Strain versus Time Graph] spec --> sg sg --> wb wb --> ina ina --> aaf aaf --> adcc adcc --> usbb usbb --> labv labv --> out ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Software Acquisition Workflow This diagram illustrates the temporal sequence of events during a typical DAQ run controlled by LabVIEW or MATLAB. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant Software as LabVIEW or MATLAB participant Driver as DAQ Device Driver participant Hardware as DAQ Hardware Card participant Sensor as Sensor on Test Rig User->>Software: Open VI or Script User->>Software: Configure Channel, Range, Sampling Rate Software->>Driver: Initialize Device Driver->>Hardware: Send Configuration Commands Hardware-->>Driver: Acknowledgment User->>Software: Click Start Acquisition Software->>Driver: Begin Continuous Sampling Driver->>Hardware: Trigger ADC Conversion Hardware->>Sensor: Read Analog Signal Sensor-->>Hardware: Return Voltage Hardware->>Hardware: ADC Digitization Hardware-->>Driver: Stream Digital Codes Driver-->>Software: Buffer Fill Event Software->>Software: Plot Waveform in Real Time Software->>Software: Save to File User->>Software: Click Stop Software->>Driver: Terminate Acquisition Software->>Software: Post Process and Export Data ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Sampling & Aliasing Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR ana[Continuous Analog Signal
High Frequency Component] sa[Sample and Hold
at fs Samples per Second] dig[Discrete Digital Samples
Quantized Codes] fft[FFT Spectral Analysis
in Software] ana --> sa sa --> dig dig --> fft ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a PC-based Data Acquisition System. List its four main functional blocks.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A PC-based Data Acquisition (DAQ) system is an integrated measurement chain that converts physical quantities (such as force, temperature, strain, or vibration) into digital data readable by a personal computer for storage, display, and analysis. **The four main functional blocks are:** 1. **Sensor / Transducer** — Converts physical quantity into an electrical signal. 2. **Signal Conditioning Unit (SCU)** — Amplifies, filters, and linearizes the raw sensor output. 3. **Data Acquisition Hardware (DAQ Card)** — Performs multiplexing, sample-and-hold, and analog-to-digital conversion. 4. **PC with Application Software** — Provides visualization, logging, and post-processing (e.g., LabVIEW, MATLAB). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to mention the **Signal Conditioning Unit** as a *separate* block. Listing only three blocks (sensor, ADC, PC) is a **2-mark deduction**. Always treat SCU as a distinct block. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that a bandlimited continuous-time signal containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples, provided the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which a high-frequency signal component (above $f_s/2$) gets *misrepresented* as a lower-frequency component in the digitized output, because the spectral replicas overlap during sampling. **Practical Example:** A shaft rotating at 1500 RPM with a vibration at 250 Hz, sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz (below Nyquist for a 250 Hz signal), will appear as a false 150 Hz component — a *ghost frequency* that does not physically exist. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. --- ### **QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** #### (a) Explain with a block diagram the architecture of a PC-based Data Acquisition System used for strain measurement on a cantilever beam. Discuss the role of the instrumentation amplifier and anti-aliasing filter in detail. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Block Diagram (2 Marks):** ``` Specimen → Strain Gauge → Wheatstone Bridge → Instrumentation Amplifier → Anti-Aliasing Filter → ADC → USB → PC (LabVIEW) → Display & Logging ``` **Functional Explanation (5 Marks):** 1. **Specimen & Strain Gauge:** A metallic foil strain gauge (Gauge Factor $G_f \approx 2.0$) is bonded on the cantilever beam using cyanoacrylate adhesive. When the beam deflects under load, the gauge experiences strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$. 2. **Wheatstone Bridge:** The gauge forms one active arm of a quarter-bridge circuit. With excitation $V_{ex} = 5 \text{ V}$, the differential output voltage is $V_o \approx (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, which is typically in the **millivolt range** (e.g., 2.5 mV at $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon$). 3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA):** Required because the bridge output is a low-amplitude differential signal sitting on a high common-mode voltage. The INA provides: - **High differential gain** ($G = 100$ to $500$), boosting 2.5 mV to the 0.25 – 1.25 V ADC input range. - **High CMRR** (≥ 80 dB), rejecting common-mode noise from the excitation supply. - **High input impedance** (≥ 1 GΩ), preventing bridge loading errors. 4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s / 2$ removes any frequency components above half the sampling rate. Without this filter, high-frequency noise could fold back into the measurement bandwidth, producing erroneous strain readings. 5. **ADC & PC:** The conditioned voltage is digitized at a sampling rate (e.g., 1 kHz for a quasi-static strain test) and streamed to PC software for real-time plotting and logging. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Marks are frequently lost when students confuse **amplifier gain with bridge sensitivity**. Always state that *the INA provides gain*, while *the bridge provides sensitivity to strain*. Do not interchange these terms. --- #### (b) A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAQ system with a full-scale range of ±10 V. Compute (i) the LSB size, (ii) the worst-case quantization error, (iii) the theoretical SNR, and (iv) the digital output code for an input of +3.5 V. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Given:** $n = 12$, $V_{FSR} = \pm 10 \text{ V}$ (i.e., span = 20 V), $V_{in} = +3.5 \text{ V}$ **(i) LSB Size (2 Marks):** The total range is $V_{FSR} = 20 \text{ V}$. Therefore: $$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{20}{4096} \approx 4.883 \text{ mV}$$ **(ii) Worst-Case Quantization Error (2 Marks):** $$e_q = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2} = \pm \frac{20}{8192} \approx \pm 2.441 \text{ mV}$$ **(iii) Theoretical SNR (1 Mark):** $$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \, n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}$$ **(iv) Digital Output Code for +3.5 V (2 Marks):** For a bipolar range where 0 V corresponds to mid-scale ($2^{n-1} = 2048$): $$D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{FSR}} \cdot 2^n + 2048 = \frac{3.5}{20} \cdot 4096 + 2048$$ $$D = 716.8 + 2048 = 2764.8 \approx 2765 \text{ counts}$$ **Final Answer:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | LSB size | 4.883 mV | | Quantization error | ± 2.441 mV | | SNR | 74.0 dB | | Digital code | 2765 counts | --- ### **QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** #### (a) Describe the working principle of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) connected to a PC-based DAQ system. Derive the relationship between resistance and temperature and explain how the DAQ software displays the temperature in real time. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution:** **Working Principle (2 Marks):** An **RTD** (Resistance Temperature Detector) exploits the predictable change of electrical resistance of a pure metal (commonly platinum, nickel, or copper) with temperature. Platinum is most widely used due to its excellent linearity, stability, and chemical inertness. The standard industrial RTD is the **PT100**, with nominal resistance $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $T_0 = 0 \, ^\circ\text{C}$. **Derivation of R–T Relationship (3 Marks):** The Callendar–Van Dusen equation gives the exact nonlinear resistance–temperature curve for platinum: $$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 + C (T - 100) T^3 \right]$$ where $T$ is in °C, and for $T \geq 0$ °C the cubic term vanishes ($C = 0$): $$R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + A T + B T^2 \right)$$ Standard IEC 60751 coefficients for platinum: $$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}, \quad B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, ^\circ\text{C}^{-2}$$ For **small temperature ranges** (linear approximation), this reduces to: $$R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$$ where $\alpha \approx \mathbf{3.85 \times 10^{-3}} \, ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ is the average temperature coefficient of platinum between 0 and 100 °C. **DAQ System Integration (2 Marks):** The RTD is connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** or **4-wire Kelvin configuration** to eliminate lead-resistance errors. The bridge output is conditioned, digitized, and sent to the PC. In LabVIEW or MATLAB, a calibration equation or lookup table converts the measured voltage back into temperature. The software displays a **real-time temperature vs. time chart** on the front panel, with options to log data to a file. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often quote only the **linear approximation** $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ without mentioning the **Callendar–Van Dusen polynomial**. For full marks, you must state that *linear approximation is valid only for limited ranges* and that the *polynomial form* is used for precision work. --- #### (b) Explain the phenomenon of aliasing in signal acquisition. How is it prevented in a PC-based DAQ system? A vibration signal contains components up to 800 Hz. **(7 Marks)** **Calculate (i) the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing, (ii) the new apparent frequency if the actual signal is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz, and (iii) the cutoff frequency required for the anti-aliasing filter.** #### Model Solution: **Aliasing Explanation (2 Marks):** Aliasing is the **distortion artifact** that occurs when a signal is sampled at a rate below the Nyquist rate ($2 f_{max}$). High-frequency components *fold back* into the lower frequency band and appear as false (ghost) frequencies in the digitized spectrum, indistinguishable from genuine low-frequency components. **Prevention Methods (2 Marks):** 1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** A low-pass analog filter placed *before* the ADC, with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s / 2$, removes all frequency components above the Nyquist frequency. 2. **Oversampling:** Sampling at a rate significantly higher than $2 f_{max}$ (e.g., $5 \times$ to $10 \times$) pushes the alias fold-back region far outside the band of interest. **Numerical Solutions (3 Marks):** **Given:** $f_{max} = 800 \text{ Hz}$, $f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz}$ **(i) Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist Rate):** $$f_{s,\min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 800 = 1600 \text{ Hz}$$ **(ii) Apparent Frequency at $f_s = 1000$ Hz:** Since $f_s = 1000 < 1600$, aliasing will occur. The apparent (aliased) frequency is given by: $$f_{apparent} = \vert f_{actual} - f_s \vert = \vert 800 - 1000 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}$$ So a true 800 Hz vibration appears as a false 200 Hz component — a potentially dangerous misdiagnosis in machinery health monitoring. **(iii) Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff:** For safe acquisition with the chosen $f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz}$: $$f_c = \frac{f_s}{2} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Nyquist Rate | 1600 Hz | | Aliased frequency (at 1000 Hz sampling) | 200 Hz | | Anti-aliasing cutoff | 500 Hz | --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Notes — Read Twice Before Exam** - [x] A PC-based DAQ system has **five functional blocks**: Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC (with MUX & S/H) → PC Interface → Software. - [x] **Nyquist–Shannon Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — this is non-negotiable. - [x] **Aliasing** is the ghost-frequency phenomenon when sampling is too slow; it is **prevented** by anti-aliasing filters and oversampling. - [x] **ADC LSB size:** $\Delta V = V_{FSR}/2^n$. **Quantization error:** $\pm \Delta V / 2$. - [x] **Theoretical SNR** of an ideal ADC: $6.02n + 1.76$ dB. - [x] **Strain Gauge Gauge Factor:** $G_f = (\Delta R / R) / (\Delta L / L)$. For metallic foil, $G_f \approx \mathbf{2.0}$. - [x] **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$. - [x] **RTD (PT100):** $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at 0 °C; platinum coefficient $\alpha \approx \mathbf{3.85 \times 10^{-3}} \, ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$. - [x] **Thermocouples** obey the Seebeck effect; output voltage follows polynomial calibration (Type K, Type J, etc.). - [x] **Signal conditioning** always includes INA (high CMRR, high gain) and **anti-aliasing low-pass filter**. - [x] **Sampling rate selection** depends on the highest frequency of interest: vibration → kHz range; quasi-static strain → 10–100 Hz; acoustic emission → MHz range. - [x] **LabVIEW** uses *Virtual Instruments (VIs)* with *Front Panel* (UI) and *Block Diagram* (code). - [x] **MATLAB Data Acquisition Toolbox** supports session-based acquisition with explicit channel and rate configuration. - [x] **Data files** are commonly saved in **.csv**, **.tdms** (NI native), or **.mat** (MATLAB) formats. - [x] **Wheatstone Bridge** configurations: Quarter (1 active gauge), Half (2 gauges), Full (4 gauges). Full bridge offers **4× higher sensitivity** and **built-in temperature compensation**. - [x] **CMRR** should be ≥ 80 dB for industrial-grade instrumentation amplifiers. - [x] **4-wire (Kelvin) connection** for RTDs eliminates lead-resistance errors. > **Final Exam Tip:** Always quote the *sampling theorem equation* explicitly, always state the *units* of every calculated quantity, and always include a *labeled block diagram* wherever the question allows — KTU examiners allocate dedicated marks (typically **2 marks**) for a correct block diagram.

topics.title: Study of PLC Programming and Controlling Variable Speed Drive Through PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of PLC Programming and Controlling Variable Speed Drive Through PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — STUDY OF PLC PROGRAMMING AND CONTROLLING VARIABLE SPEED DRIVE THROUGH PLC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** — A PLC is a ruggedized, solid-state industrial digital computer specifically designed for the real-time control of manufacturing processes and automation machinery. It operates using a continuously cycling scan cycle (Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan) governed by a central processing unit (CPU), and uses **Ladder Logic (LAD)** as its primary programming language under the **IEC 61131-3** standard.

**Variable Speed Drive (VSD) / Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** — A power-electronics-based motor controller that varies the frequency and voltage supplied to a three-phase AC induction motor to achieve continuous, stepless control of shaft speed, torque, and direction. The governing relation is the synchronous speed equation:

$$n_s = \frac{120 \cdot f}{p}$$

where $f$ is the supply frequency in **Hz**, $p$ is the number of poles, and $n_s$ is the synchronous speed in **RPM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC ≠ VSD**. The PLC is the **decision-making brain** (logic controller), while the VSD is the **muscle** (power actuator). The PLC does NOT directly switch motor current; it sends low-power command signals to the VSD, which then modulates the high-power mains to the motor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **car's automatic gearbox** controlled by a **smart driver**:
- The **smart driver** = PLC — reads dashboard (sensors), decides what to do based on a set of rules.
- The **automatic gearbox** = VSD — actually changes the rotational speed delivered to the wheels.
- The **steering wheel / pedals** = Push-buttons and selector switches wired to PLC inputs.
- The **engine RPM gauge** = Encoder / speed feedback sensor on the motor shaft.

When you press the accelerator (input), the smart driver (PLC) decides how much throttle (analog 4–20 mA signal) to send to the gearbox (VSD), which then delivers the appropriate speed to the wheels (motor). The PLC never touches the engine directly — it only commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment evaluates **CO2** — *Apply PLC programming for controlling industrial drives and automation systems*. Students are expected to demonstrate hands-on wiring, ladder logic creation, and VSD parameterization.

---

### 1.3 Why this Experiment Matters in Industry

| Industry Sector | Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Conveyor Systems** | Speed matching between upstream/downstream belts |
| **Pumps & HVAC** | Energy saving via flow-pressure modulation |
| **CNC Machines** | Spindle speed control based on tool/cutter load |
| **Textile / Paper Mills** | Maintaining tension by varying roll speed |
| **Packaging Lines** | Synchronized start-stop with adjustable acceleration ramp |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PLC Architecture — Five Functional Blocks

Every PLC, irrespective of manufacturer (Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix, Mitsubishi FX, Delta DVP, Schneider M221), contains **five core building blocks**:

1. **CPU Module** — Hosts the microprocessor, executes the user program, manages memory.
2. **Input Module (I)** — Interfaces with field devices (PB, selector, sensor). Accepts **24 V DC** digital or analog (0–10 V / 4–20 mA) signals.
3. **Output Module (O)** — Delivers decision results to actuators (relay coil, contactor, VSD command). Types: Relay, Transistor (sinking/sourcing), Triac.
4. **Power Supply (PSU)** — Converts 230 V AC mains to **24 V DC** for internal logic and sensors.
5. **Memory** — RAM (volatile, runtime), ROM/Flash (non-volatile, program retention), EEPROM (retain data on power loss).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Address Convention (IEC 61131-3):**
> - `I0.0`, `I0.1` … → Digital **Inputs** (Boolean, 1 bit)
> - `Q0.0`, `Q0.1` … → Digital **Outputs** (Boolean, 1 bit)
> - `IW64`, `IW66` → Analog **Inputs** (16-bit Word)
> - `QW80`, `QW82` → Analog **Outputs** (16-bit Word)
> - `M0.0`, `MW10` → Internal **Memory/Flags** (Merker)

## 2.2 PLC Scan Cycle (CRITICAL — Asked every year)

The CPU executes a **cyclic 3-stage scan** continuously:

$$\text{Scan Time } T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{overhead}$$

| Stage | Operation |
|---|---|
| **Stage 1 — Input Scan** | CPU reads ALL physical input terminals and copies their ON/OFF states into the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory. |
| **Stage 2 — Program Scan** | CPU executes the user program (ladder logic) **top-to-bottom, left-to-right** using the values from PII. Results are written to the **Process Image Output (PIO)**. |
| **Stage 3 — Output Scan** | CPU flushes PIO values to the physical output terminals — relays energize, VSD receives new command. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During the Program Scan, the CPU is **BLIND** to real input changes. Only at the start of the next Input Scan are fresh values captured. This causes a **response lag = 1 scan cycle**. For high-speed VSD ramps, set the PLC to **interrupt-driven mode**.

## 2.3 Variable Speed Drive (VSD) — Operating Principle

A modern VSD uses a **three-stage power conversion** topology:

$$\text{AC Mains} \xrightarrow{\text{Rectifier}} \text{DC Bus} \xrightarrow{\text{Inverter (IGBT)}} \text{Variable f, V AC}$$

| Stage | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Rectifier | 6-pulse diode bridge / active front end | Converts fixed-frequency AC to rippled DC |
| 2. DC Link | Capacitor bank (smooths DC) + Brake chopper | Stores energy; provides stiff DC bus |
| 3. Inverter | 6 IGBTs with PWM switching | Chops DC into synthesized sine via **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** |

The inverter uses **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** to recreate a sinusoidal voltage whose effective frequency and amplitude vary together — keeping the **V/f ratio constant** to maintain rated motor flux.

## 2.4 Two Methods of PLC → VSD Communication

### Method A — Digital (Multi-Speed) Control
The PLC sends discrete ON/OFF signals to VSD digital terminals (DI1, DI2, DI3, DI4). Each combination selects a **preset frequency** (P04, P05, P06, P07 etc. in the VSD parameter table).

| DI3 | DI2 | DI1 | Preset Freq. (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Reference from Pot/Analog |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **10 Hz** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **20 Hz** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **30 Hz** |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | **40 Hz** |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **50 Hz** (max) |

> **Disadvantage:** Only fixed steps — no smooth variation.

### Method B — Analog Control (Continuous)
PLC outputs an **analog signal** (0–10 V or 4–20 mA) from its DAC or analog-output module. VSD is wired in "External analog reference" mode. Frequency varies **continuously** from 0 Hz to max setpoint.

$$f_{motor} = f_{max} \times \frac{V_{PLC}}{10\text{ V}} \quad \text{or} \quad f_{motor} = f_{max} \times \frac{I_{PLC} - 4}{16\text{ mA}}$$

> **Advantage:** Stepless, smooth control — ideal for conveyors, pumps.

### Method C — Fieldbus Control (Advanced)
Industrial protocols: **Modbus RTU (RS-485)**, **PROFIBUS**, **PROFINET**, **EtherNet/IP**, **CANopen**. PLC writes frequency, start/stop, ramp, and reads motor current, voltage, fault codes — all as **registers**.

$$\text{Register 0x2001 (Holding Register)} \rightarrow \text{Frequency command (Hz × 10)}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronous speed | $n_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{p}$ | RPM | $f$ = supply freq., $p$ = poles |
| Slip | $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s} \times 100\%$ | % | $n_r$ = rotor actual speed |
| Rotor speed | $n_r = n_s (1 - s)$ | RPM | Typical $s$ = 3–5 % |
| V/f ratio | $K = \dfrac{V_{LL}}{f}$ | V/Hz | Held constant for constant flux |
| PLC scan time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out}$ | ms | Typically 5–20 ms |
| Analog scaling (0–10 V) | $f_{cmd} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{10} \times f_{max}$ | Hz | Linear interpolation |
| Analog scaling (4–20 mA) | $f_{cmd} = \dfrac{I_{in} - 4}{16} \times f_{max}$ | Hz | Linear interpolation |
| Acceleration ramp | $t_{acc} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{a_{rate}}$ | s | Set in VSD parameter |
| Motor HP ↔ kW | $1 \text{ HP} = 0.746 \text{ kW}$ | kW | Standard conversion |
| Encoder PPR | $n_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_{pulses}}{PPR} \times 60$ | RPM | For closed-loop feedback |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

The PLC+VSD combination forms the **backbone of Industry 4.0**:
- **Energy savings:** 25–60 % in pump/fan applications (Affinity Laws: $P \propto f^3$).
- **Soft starting:** Eliminates inrush current (up to 6× FLC on DOL start).
- **Predictive maintenance:** VSD telemetry (motor current signature) fed back to PLC/SCADA detects bearing wear.
- **Process optimization:** Speed vs pressure curves in HVAC tuned in real time.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why V/f must be kept Constant

The air-gap flux $\Phi$ in an induction motor is governed by Faraday's law applied to the stator:

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi \cdot k_w$$

For a given motor construction ($N$, $k_w$ are constants), and assuming $E \approx V$ (neglecting stator impedance drop):

$$\Phi \propto \frac{V}{f}$$

**Step-by-step logical derivation:**

**Step 1 — State the proportionality.**
Since $N$ and $k_w$ are fixed at manufacturing, flux $\Phi$ depends only on the ratio $V/f$.

$$\Phi = k \cdot \frac{V}{f} \quad \text{where } k = \frac{1}{4.44 \cdot N \cdot k_w}$$

**Step 2 — Define rated operating point.**
At nameplate, $V = V_{rated}$ and $f = f_{rated} = 50\text{ Hz}$. Rated flux is $\Phi_{rated}$.

$$\Phi_{rated} = k \cdot \frac{V_{rated}}{50}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constant-flux constraint.**
To maintain $\Phi = \Phi_{rated}$ at any frequency $f$:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \frac{V_{rated}}{50} = K_{constant}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V at any f.**

$$V(f) = f \cdot \frac{V_{rated}}{50}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**
A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz, 4-pole motor. Find the stator voltage required to maintain rated flux at 25 Hz.

$$\begin{aligned}
V(25\text{ Hz}) &= 25 \times \frac{415}{50} \\
&= 25 \times 8.30 \\
&= 207.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below ~3 Hz, the stator IR drop becomes significant and constant $V/f$ fails. VSDs inject a **voltage boost** (typically 5–10 %) at low frequency to compensate — set as parameter **P03 (Torque Boost)** in most drives.

## 3.2 Derivation — Analog Signal to Frequency Mapping

**Problem:** A PLC analog output of 6.5 V is fed to a VSD with $f_{max}$ = 50 Hz (set in parameter P02). Find the resulting motor speed for a 4-pole motor at 5 % slip.

**Step 1 — Scale the voltage to fraction of full scale.**

$$\text{Fraction} = \frac{6.5}{10} = 0.65$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by max frequency.**

$$f_{cmd} = 0.65 \times 50 = 32.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute synchronous speed.**

$$n_s = \frac{120 \times 32.5}{4} = 975 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Apply slip.**

$$n_r = n_s (1 - s) = 975 \times (1 - 0.05) = 926.25 \text{ RPM}$$

## 3.3 Ladder Logic Program — Worked Analytical Solution

**Objective:** Wire a PLC program that controls a conveyor motor via a VSD. Requirements:
- **PB1 (I0.0):** START → Motor runs forward at 30 Hz.
- **PB2 (I0.1):** STOP → Motor decelerates to 0 Hz (ramp = 5 s).
- **PB3 (I0.2):** SPEED+ → Increase speed by 5 Hz per press.
- **PB4 (I0.3):** SPEED− → Decrease speed by 5 Hz per press.
- **PB5 (I0.4):** EMERGENCY STOP (NC contact) → Coast to stop.
- **Output Q0.0:** VSD Forward Run command (digital DI1).
- **Output QW0:** Analog frequency command (0–10 V scaled to 0–50 Hz).

### Rung 1 — Emergency Stop & Start Latch

```
   PB1    E-STOP    Motor_Run
   I0.0   I0.4(NC)  M0.0
  ─┤ ├────┤/├──────( )──┐
                          │
   PB2                   │
   I0.1                  │
  ─┤/├──────────────────┘
```
**Logic:** `Motor_Run = (PB1 AND NOT E_STOP) OR Motor_Run` — classic seal-in latch.

### Rung 2 — VSD Forward Run Command

```
   Motor_Run
   M0.0
  ─┤ ├──────────────────( Q0.0 )──
```
**Action:** When `Motor_Run = 1`, PLC output Q0.0 closes the VSD's DI1 terminal → VSD enters RUN mode.

### Rung 3 — Speed Increment (UP counter logic)

```
   SPEED+    Edge_Bit
   I0.2      M0.2
  ─┤ ├───────( P )──┐
                      │
   M0.2               │
  ─┤ ├───────────────┤ COUNT_UP
                      │
   M0.2  CW_limit    │
  ─┤ ├────┤/├────────┘
```
**Logic:** Every press of PB3 increments a counter `CW_count`. On reaching value **6** (= 30 Hz at 5 Hz/step), bit `M0.4` activates to cap further counting.

### Rung 4 — Speed Scaling to Analog Output

The PLC integer register `CW_count` (0 to 10) must be scaled to 0–27648 (Siemens standard for 0–10 V).

$$\text{Analog Value} = \text{Count} \times \frac{27648}{10} = \text{Count} \times 2764.8$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If Count} &= 6 \text{ (i.e., } 30 \text{ Hz)}: \\
\text{AQW0} &= 6 \times 2764.8 = 16588.8 \approx 16589
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Digital vs Analog vs Fieldbus

| Criterion | Digital (Multi-Speed) | Analog (0–10 V) | Fieldbus (Modbus) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wiring cost** | Low (5 wires max) | Low (2 wires) | High (twisted pair, shielded) |
| **Resolution** | 2ⁿ steps (n = # DIs) | **Infinite** (smooth) | Infinite + diagnostic |
| **Cable noise immunity** | High (binary) | Low (susceptible to EMI) | Very High (CRC checked) |
| **Program complexity in PLC** | Simple ladder | Simple scaling block | Function block / MSG instruction |
| **KTU exam recommendation** | YES — easy to draw | YES — requires scaling math | Optional / advanced |
