# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a PLC. List the four main parts of a PLC.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, industrial-grade digital computer that uses a programmable memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control industrial machines and processes.

The **four main parts** of a PLC are:

1. **CPU Module** — the brain executing the control program.
2. **Input Module** — receives signals from sensors, push-buttons, switches.
3. **Output Module** — sends control signals to actuators, contactors, drives.
4. **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** — converts mains AC to regulated **24 V DC** for internal circuitry.

> *(A 5th part, Memory, may also be listed.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**State and explain the function of a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) in motor control.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)**, also called Variable Speed Drive (VSD), is an electronic power converter that adjusts the **frequency and voltage** supplied to a 3-phase AC induction motor to vary its speed and torque continuously.

**Main functions:**
- **Speed Control:** $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$ — varying $f$ changes speed.
- **Soft Starting:** Limits inrush current to ~1.5× FLC (vs 6× in DOL).
- **Energy Saving:** Particularly in pumps and fans (Affinity Law: $P \propto f^3$).
- **Protection:** Built-in overload, over-voltage, under-voltage, phase-loss, short-circuit.
- **Process Control:** Accepts external analog/digital/fieldbus reference.

It uses **AC → DC → Variable AC (PWM)** conversion with constant V/f ratio for constant flux.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PLC**. List any **five applications** of PLCs in industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a ladder diagram how a **PLC controls the speed of a 3-phase induction motor through a VFD** using **analog reference (0–10 V)**. Show the scaling equation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PLC Architecture — Block Diagram**

```
 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
 │              PLC Module                  │
 │  ┌─────────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────────────┐  │
 │  │   CPU   │ │ I/P  │ │ Memory RAM   │  │
 │  │  Module │ │ Mod  │ │  + ROM/Flash │  │
 │  └────┬────┘ └──┬───┘ └──────────────┘  │
 │       │         │                         │
 │  ┌────┴────┐ ┌──┴───┐                    │
 │  │   O/P   │ │ PSU  │                    │
 │  │  Module │ │ 24V  │                    │
 │  └─────────┘ └──────┘                    │
 └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Functions:**
- **CPU:** Heart of PLC; executes program scan cyclically.
- **Input Module:** Interfaces with field sensors (PBs, limit switches, proximity, encoders). Provides optical isolation.
- **Output Module:** Drives actuators — relay (5 A), transistor (0.5 A), triac. Provides isolation.
- **Power Supply:** Converts 230 V AC → 24 V DC, ±5 V DC, ±12 V DC.
- **Memory:** Program storage (Flash), data retention (EEPROM/RAM with battery).

**Five Industrial Applications:**
1. **Bottle-filling plant** — counting, level sensing, conveyor control.
2. **Elevator control** — floor logic, door interlock, overload sensing.
3. **Traffic light controller** — sequential switching with timer.
4. **Automatic washing machine** — sequence of wash, rinse, spin cycles.
5. **Conveyor sorting system** — barcode reading + diverter actuation.
6. **Mixing tank in process industry** — agitator control based on level + temperature.

---

**(b) PLC + VFD Analog Speed Control**

**Wiring setup:**
- PLC analog output `QW0` (0–10 V) → VFD terminal `AI` (analog input).
- PLC digital output `Q0.0` → VSD terminal `DI1` (Run command).
- VSD parameter `P01 = 1` (External analog reference).
- VSD parameter `P02 = 50 Hz` (Max frequency).

**Scaling Equation (Siemens S7-1200 standard):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{f_{desired}}{f_{max}} \times 10\text{ V} = \frac{f_{desired}}{50} \times 10$$

Equivalently in PLC integer (0–27648 for 0–10 V):

$$QW0 = \frac{f_{desired}}{50} \times 27648$$

**Numerical example:** For 35 Hz → $QW0 = \dfrac{35}{50} \times 27648 = 19354$.

**Ladder Diagram:**

```
   START    ESTOP   MOTOR_RUN
   I0.0    I0.4(NC)  M0.0
  ─┤ ├─────┤/├──────( )─┐
                          │
   STOP                   │
   I0.1                  │
  ─┤/├──────────────────┘

   MOTOR_RUN
   M0.0
  ─┤ ├────────────( Q0.0 )──    VFD RUN command

   MOTOR_RUN
   M0.0
  ─┤ ├─────[ MOVE_WORD ]──────►  QW0   Analog to VFD
         EN                     Source: MW10 (Freq Desired)
         MW10                   
       27648
```

**Procedure:**
1. Press START → `MOTOR_RUN` latches → `Q0.0` energizes → VSD RUN terminal closed → motor accelerates as per P04 ramp.
2. Operator enters speed value (e.g., via HMI) into memory word `MW10`.
3. PLC continuously executes `MOVE` block → `QW0` outputs scaled analog voltage to VSD.
4. VSD compares to `P02` (50 Hz) and synthesizes appropriate frequency.
5. Press STOP → `MOTOR_RUN` resets → `Q0.0` opens → VSD decelerates as per P05.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **basic principle of operation of a VFD**. With necessary equations, show how motor speed is controlled by varying supply frequency while maintaining **constant V/f ratio**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 3-phase, 415 V, **50 Hz** squirrel-cage induction motor is driven by a VFD. The PLC analog output is **5.5 V** and the VFD is set to a maximum frequency of **50 Hz**. Calculate: **(i)** command frequency, **(ii)** synchronous speed, **(iii)** rotor speed at 4 % slip. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) VFD Principle of Operation**

A VFD operates on the principle of **frequency conversion** using power electronics. It has three sections:

| Section | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Rectifier | Diode bridge | AC → DC (uncontrolled) |
| 2. DC Link | Capacitor bank + optional brake chopper | Smooths DC, stores energy |
| 3. Inverter | 6 IGBT switches with SPWM | DC → Variable-magnitude, variable-frequency AC |

The frequency $f$ supplied to the motor controls its synchronous speed:

$$n_s = \frac{120 f}{p}$$

If the motor is operated below rated frequency without reducing voltage, the air-gap flux would increase, saturating the iron core and overheating the motor. To keep flux constant, voltage must be reduced in proportion to frequency:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \text{Constant} = K$$

Therefore at any operating frequency $f$:

$$V(f) = K \cdot f = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} \cdot f$$

This is achieved in the VFD by coordinating the **PWM modulation index** with the desired output frequency in the DSP-based controller. The result is a constant-flux, variable-speed operation with **constant torque capability** below rated speed.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation**

**Given:** Poles $p = 4$, $V_{rated} = 415\text{ V}$, $f_{rated} = 50\text{ Hz}$, $V_{PLC} = 5.5\text{ V}$, $f_{max} = 50\text{ Hz}$, slip $s = 4 \% = 0.04$.

**(i) Command Frequency:**

$$f_{cmd} = \frac{V_{PLC}}{10\text{ V}} \times f_{max}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{cmd} &= \frac{5.5}{10} \times 50 \\
&= 0.55 \times 50 \\
&= 27.5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Synchronous Speed:**

$$n_s = \frac{120 \times f}{p}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
n_s &= \frac{120 \times 27.5}{4} \\
&= \frac{3300}{4} \\
&= 825 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Rotor Speed at 4 % Slip:**

$$n_r = n_s (1 - s)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
n_r &= 825 \times (1 - 0.04) \\
&= 825 \times 0.96 \\
&= 792 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU Valuation (Exam-Wise):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention V/f constant ratio** — Examiners deduct **2 marks** if flux-maintenance principle is missing in VFD theory answers.
> 2. **Confusing PLC outputs with motor power** — Writing "PLC drives the motor directly" loses **1 mark**; PLC only commands the VFD.
> 3. **Scan cycle confusion** — Many students write "PLC reads inputs continuously". This is **WRONG** — inputs are sampled at scan start only. **Lose 1 mark** if not specified.
> 4. **Wrong units in numericals** — Always write **Hz** with frequency, **RPM** with speed, **V/Hz** with V/f ratio. Examiners penalise **½ mark** per unit slip.
> 5. **Ladder logic without contact labelling** — Always label every contact/coil with its address (I0.0, Q0.0, M0.0). Unlabelled ladder → **1 mark deduction**.
> 6. **No seal-in (latching) explanation** — For motor START/STOP, just drawing a ladder without explaining the latch logic → **2 marks lost**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **PLC Definition:** Ruggedized industrial computer using ladder logic (IEC 61131-3).
- **Five PLC Parts:** CPU, I/P, O/P, PSU, Memory.
- **Scan Cycle (in order):** Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan. Cyclic, ~5–20 ms typical.
- **PLC Memory Addressing:** `I` = Input, `Q` = Output, `M` = Memory/Flag, `IW/QW` = Analog Word.
- **VSD/VFD Definition:** Power electronics device controlling motor speed by varying frequency and voltage.
- **VSD Topology:** Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter (AC→DC→Variable AC).
- **Governing Equation:** $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{p}$.
- **V/f Constant Ratio:** $V/f = V_{rated}/f_{rated}$ — to maintain flux and avoid saturation.
- **Control Methods:** Digital (preset speeds), Analog (0–10 V / 4–20 mA), Fieldbus (Modbus RTU/PROFINET).
- **PLC does NOT power the motor** — it commands the VSD only.
- **Scaling for Siemens 0–10 V:** $QW0 = \dfrac{f}{50} \times 27648$.
- **Scaling for 4–20 mA:** Zero = 4 mA, Span = 16 mA → $f = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times f_{max}$.
- **Energy Savings:** Pumps/fans obey $P \propto f^3$ — 50 % speed reduction ⇒ 87.5 % energy saved.
- **Common VSD Parameters to memorize:** P01 (source), P02 (max freq), P04 (accel), P05 (decel).
- **Safety:** Always wire E-STOP (NC) **directly** to VSD enable + PLC input for redundancy.
- **Slip formula:** $s = \dfrac{n_s - n_r}{n_s}$; rotor speed $n_r = n_s(1-s)$.
- **Power conversion:** 1 HP = **0.746 kW**; 1 kW = **1.341 HP**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for VSD Wiring — "READ":**
> **R**ectifier → **E**DC bus → **A**ct as energy buffer → **D**rive via Inverter (PWM).
> **Mnemonic for PLC Scan — "IPO":**
> **I**nput → **P**rogram → **O**utput. Repeat. Forever.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Robotic Trainer Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Robotic Trainer Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Trainer Unit (RTU):** A scaled-down, educationally-oriented robotic manipulator system (typically a **5-DOF** or **6-DOF** articulated serial arm) equipped with stepper/servo motors, an embedded controller, an end-effector (gripper/suction cup), and a teach pendant/PC interface. It is used in undergraduate laboratories to experimentally verify the principles of **kinematics, coordinate transformations, trajectory planning, and pick-and-place automation** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Mechanical Engineering Lab syllabus.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are teaching a friend to reach for a cup on a table — but your friend is **blindfolded**. You would say, *"Move your shoulder up 30°, then bend your elbow 45°, then rotate your wrist 90°."* A robotic trainer unit works exactly like this, except the *"friend"* is a mechanical arm and the instructions are **joint angles** (called **configuration space variables**). The lab exercises train students to translate *"I want the gripper HERE"* (Cartesian task space) into *"Each joint must rotate by THIS MUCH"* (joint space).

### Anatomy of a Typical Trainer Arm

| Component | Function | Standard Notation |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Base / Pedestal** | Anchor; provides 1st rotational joint | Joint 1 (often $\theta_1$) |
| **Shoulder / Link 1** | Vertical reach | Link length $L_1$ |
| **Elbow / Link 2** | Horizontal extension | Link length $L_2$ |
| **Wrist assembly** | Orientation of gripper | Joints 4 & 5 ($\theta_4, \theta_5$) |
| **End-effector (Gripper)** | Grasping/holding payload | Tool point $P$ |
| **Controller Box** | Sends PWM/step pulses to motors | MCU/DSP based |
| **Teach Pendant / PC Software** | Human–machine interface | HMI |

### Physical Constants / Standard Specs

- **Standard joint speed:** $15\text{ RPM}$ to $60\text{ RPM}$ for educational units.
- **Typical payload:** $\mathbf{0.5\,kg}$ to $\mathbf{2\,kg}$.
- **Reach envelope radius:** $\mathbf{300\,mm}$ to $\mathbf{500\,mm}$.
- **Positional repeatability:** $\pm\mathbf{0.1\,mm}$ to $\pm\mathbf{0.5\,mm}$.
- **Working voltage:** $\mathbf{24\,V\,DC}$ for safety in lab environments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Robot Kinematics Pipeline

The exercises on a trainer arm revolve around **two** fundamental mappings:

1. **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Joint angles $\;\longrightarrow\;$ End-effector pose.
2. **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** Desired end-effector pose $\;\longrightarrow\;$ Required joint angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both?** A human thinks *"I want the cup here"* (Cartesian goal). The robot motor controllers only understand *"rotate joint by X degrees"* (joint command). IK is the *translator* between human intent and machine action.

### 2.2 Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) Convention

Every link of the manipulator is described by **four parameters** placed in a transformation matrix:

| DH Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|--------------|--------|---------|-------|
| Link length | $a_i$ | Distance along $X_{i-1}$ between $Z_{i-1}$ and $Z_i$ | mm |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Angle about $X_{i-1}$ from $Z_{i-1}$ to $Z_i$ | deg / rad |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Distance along $Z_{i-1}$ between $X_{i-1}$ and $X_i$ | mm |
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Angle about $Z_{i-1}$ from $X_{i-1}$ to $X_i$ | deg / rad |

For a **revolute joint**, $\theta_i$ is the **variable**; for a **prismatic joint**, $d_i$ is the variable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise these four matrices — they form the entire backbone of trainer-arm analysis.**

**A. Single-link homogeneous transformation matrix:**
$$^{i-1}\!A_i \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**B. Forward kinematics of the entire chain:**
$$T_{\text{base}}^{\text{tool}} \;=\; ^{0}\!A_1 \,\cdot\, ^{1}\!A_2 \,\cdot\, ^{2}\!A_3 \,\cdot\, \dots \,\cdot\, ^{n-1}\!A_n$$

**C. Planar 2-link (RR) inverse kinematics (most common lab exercise):**
$$\theta_2 \;=\; \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2+y^2-L_1^2-L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$$
$$\theta_1 \;=\; \arctan2(y,x)\;-\;\arctan2\!\left(L_2\sin\theta_2,\;L_1+L_2\cos\theta_2\right)$$

**D. Workspace radius bounds:**
$$R_{\max} \;=\; L_1 + L_2$$
$$R_{\min} \;=\; \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$$

**E. Tool-position vector (from FK):**
$$P_x = f_x(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$$
$$P_y = f_y(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$$
$$P_z = f_z(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n)$$

**F. Path-velocity projection (Jacobian form):**
$$\dot{P} \;=\; J(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}$$

**G. Stepper pulse count to angle conversion (used in lab programming):**
$$N_{\text{pulses}} \;=\; \dfrac{\theta_{\text{required}}}{\text{Step angle}} \times \text{Gear ratio inverse}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial pick-and-place lines:** Concept directly translates to SCARA and articulated arms in FMCG, pharma, electronics assembly.
- **Surgical robotics (Da Vinci):** Same FK/IK math, scaled with redundancy resolution.
- **Autonomous warehouse robots (Kiva/Amazon):** Mobile base + manipulator uses identical transformation chains.
- **Space Canadarm:** FK verified on ground trainer arms before launch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Forward Kinematics of a Planar 2-Link RR Arm

> This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU university viva and lab records. Master it step-by-step.

**Given:** Two revolute joints in the vertical plane, link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ (shoulder) and $\theta_2$ (elbow, measured relative to link 1).

**Step 1 — Establish DH parameter table for the planar 2R arm:**

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|-----|-------|-----------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $L_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $L_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\theta_2$ |

Because the arm operates in a plane, $\alpha_i = 0$, $d_i = 0$, and $a_i$ equals the link length.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the general transformation matrix with $\alpha = 0$:**

$$\cos(0) = 1,\qquad \sin(0) = 0$$

Substituting into $^{i-1}\!A_i$ gives the simplified 2D rotation-plus-translation matrix:

$$\begin{aligned} ^{i-1}\!A_i \;&=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & 0 & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Write $^{0}\!A_1$ for the shoulder joint (using $\theta_1$ and $a_1 = L_1$):**

$$\begin{aligned} ^{0}\!A_1 \;&=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

This matrix tells us that **after rotating the shoulder by $\theta_1$**, the elbow position relative to the base is $(L_1\cos\theta_1,\;L_1\sin\theta_1)$.

**Step 4 — Write $^{1}\!A_2$ for the elbow joint (using $\theta_2$ and $a_2 = L_2$):**

$$\begin{aligned} ^{1}\!A_2 \;&=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & 0 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

This matrix is expressed in the **shoulder frame** (frame 1), not the world frame.

**Step 5 — Multiply the two matrices to get the tool position relative to base:**

$$T \;=\; ^{0}\!A_1 \,\cdot\, ^{1}\!A_2$$

Computing the **upper-left 2×2 rotation block** first:

$$\begin{aligned} R \;=\; &\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] =\; &\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & -(\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 + \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2) \\ \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 + \cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

Applying the identities:
- $\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1\sin\theta_2 = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$
- $\cos\theta_1\sin\theta_2 + \sin\theta_1\cos\theta_2 = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$

$$\begin{aligned} R \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the position column (1st column of the rotation × shoulder offset, plus elbow offset rotated into world frame):**

$$\begin{aligned} P_x \;=\; &L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\[4pt] P_y \;=\; &L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the full homogeneous transformation:**

$$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{base}}^{\text{tool}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & 0 & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation of the result:**
> - The tool's orientation equals the sum of the two joint angles $\theta_1 + \theta_2$.
> - The tool's position is the **vector sum** of the two link vectors.
> - These two scalar equations ($P_x, P_y$) are the inputs to the inverse-kinematics problem.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Forward Kinematics

**Given:** $L_1 = 250\,\text{mm}$, $L_2 = 200\,\text{mm}$, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into $P_x$:**
$$\begin{aligned} P_x \;=\; &L_1\cos(30°) + L_2\cos(30°+45°) \\[4pt] =\; &250 \times 0.8660 + 200 \times \cos(75°) \\[4pt] =\; &250 \times 0.8660 + 200 \times 0.2588 \\[4pt] =\; &216.51 + 51.76 \\[4pt] =\; &\mathbf{268.27\,mm} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into $P_y$:**
$$\begin{aligned} P_y \;=\; &L_1\sin(30°) + L_2\sin(75°) \\[4pt] =\; &250 \times 0.5000 + 200 \times 0.9659 \\[4pt] =\; &125.00 + 193.19 \\[4pt] =\; &\mathbf{318.19\,mm} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Tool orientation:**
$$\phi \;=\; \theta_1 + \theta_2 \;=\; 30° + 45° \;=\; \mathbf{75°}$$

**Result:** The gripper tip is located at $(268.27,\;318.19)\,\text{mm}$ with orientation $\phi = 75°$ measured from the base X-axis.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Inverse Kinematics

**Given:** Target point $P = (300, 200)\,\text{mm}$, with $L_1 = 250\,\text{mm}$, $L_2 = 200\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the squared distance from base to target:**
$$\begin{aligned} r^2 \;=\; x^2 + y^2 \;=\; 300^2 + 200^2 \;=\; 90000 + 40000 \;=\; 130000 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Verify reachability. Workspace requires $R_{\min} \leq \sqrt{r^2} \leq R_{\max}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} R_{\max} \;=\; L_1 + L_2 \;=\; 250 + 200 \;=\; 450\,\text{mm} \\[4pt] R_{\min} \;=\; \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \;=\; \vert 250 - 200 \vert \;=\; 50\,\text{mm} \\[4pt] \sqrt{130000} \;=\; 360.55\,\text{mm} \\[4pt] 50 \leq 360.55 \leq 450 \quad &\text{(reachable)} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for elbow angle $\theta_2$ using law of cosines:**
$$\begin{aligned} \cos\theta_2 \;=\; \dfrac{x^2+y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \;=\; \dfrac{130000 - 62500 - 40000}{2 \times 250 \times 200} \\[6pt] =\; \dfrac{27500}{100000} \;=\; 0.275 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \theta_2 \;=\; \arccos(0.275) \;=\; \mathbf{74.04°} \end{aligned}$$

(Elbow-up solution; elbow-down would use $\theta_2 = -74.04°$.)

**Step 4 — Solve for shoulder angle $\theta_1$:**
$$\begin{aligned} \theta_1 \;=\; &\arctan2(y,x) - \arctan2\!\left(L_2\sin\theta_2,\;L_1+L_2\cos\theta_2\right) \\[6pt] =\; &\arctan2(200,300) - \arctan2\!\left(200 \times \sin(74.04°),\;250 + 200 \times \cos(74.04°)\right) \\[6pt] =\; &33.69° - \arctan2(192.36,\;304.96) \\[6pt] =\; &33.69° - 32.24° \\[6pt] =\; &\mathbf{1.45°} \end{aligned}$$

**Result:** To place the gripper at $(300,\,200)\,\text{mm}$, command the joints to:
$$\theta_1 \approx 1.45°,\qquad \theta_2 \approx 74.04°$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Trainer Workcell

> The diagram below maps the **signal and command flow** from the user's intent to the physical motion of the gripper.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["USER INTENT: Cartesian target XYZ phi"] --> B["TEACH PENDANT / PC GUI"]
    B --> C["INVERSE KINEMATICS MODULE"]
    C --> D["TRAJECTORY PLANNER: cubic / linear interpolation"]
    D --> E["JOINT CONTROLLER: PID per motor"]
    E --> F["MOTOR DRIVER: PWM or step pulses"]
    F --> G["ACTUATORS: servo or stepper motors at joints 1 to N"]
    G --> H["LINKAGE CHAIN: FK matrices A1 A2 to An"]
    H --> I["END EFFECTOR: gripper or suction cup"]
    I --> J["FEEDBACK: encoder counts to position loop"]
    J --> E
```

### 4.2 DH-Frame Assignment on a 5-DOF Trainer Arm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F0["FRAME 0: BASE\norigin at pedestal centre\nZ0 points up"]
    F1["FRAME 1: SHOULDER\nZ1 along joint-1 axis\nX1 along link-1 direction"]
    F2["FRAME 2: ELBOW\nZ2 along joint-2 axis\nX2 along link-2 direction"]
    F3["FRAME 3: FOREARM TIP\nZ3 along joint-3 axis\nX3 along link-3 direction"]
    F4["FRAME 4: WRIST PITCH\nZ4 along joint-4 axis"]
    F5["FRAME 5: TOOL FRAME\norigin at gripper tip\nZ5 normal to gripper jaws"]
    F0 -->|"rotate theta1, translate L1"| F1
    F1 -->|"rotate theta2"| F2
    F2 -->|"rotate theta3, translate L2"| F3
    F3 -->|"rotate theta4"| F4
    F4 -->|"rotate theta5"| F5
```

### 4.3 Lab Exercise Workflow (Pick-and-Place Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: HOME the arm - all joints to zero sensors"]
    S2["STEP 2: MOVE to PICK position via IK"]
    S3["STEP 3: CLOSE gripper - digital output ON"]
    S4["STEP 4: LIFT - increase theta2 slightly to clear obstacle"]
    S5["STEP 5: MOVE to PLACE position via IK"]
    S6["STEP 6: OPEN gripper - digital output OFF"]
    S7["STEP 7: RETURN to HOME"]
    S8["STEP 8: LOG cycle time and repeat for accuracy study"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

### 4.4 Joint Variable vs Task Variable Mapping (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JOINT_SPACE
        Q1["theta1: base rotation"]
        Q2["theta2: shoulder pitch"]
        Q3["theta3: elbow pitch"]
        Q4["theta4: wrist pitch"]
        Q5["theta5: wrist roll"]
    end
    subgraph TASK_SPACE
        X["Px mm"]
        Y["Py mm"]
        Z["Pz mm"]
        PHI["orientation phi deg"]
    end
    Q1 --> X
    Q1 --> Y
    Q2 --> X
    Q2 --> Y
    Q2 --> Z
    Q3 --> X
    Q3 --> Y
    Q3 --> Z
    Q4 --> PHI
    Q5 --> PHI
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a robotic trainer unit. List any four exercises commonly performed on it.*

**Model Answer:**

A robotic trainer unit is a compact, table-top articulated serial manipulator equipped with servo/stepper motors, an embedded controller and an end-effector, used in undergraduate labs to experimentally study kinematics, dynamics and automation concepts.

**Four common exercises:**
1. Verification of **forward kinematics** — moving each joint and recording tool position.
2. Verification of **inverse kinematics** — commanding a Cartesian goal and observing joint rotations.
3. **Pick-and-place cycle** programming with gripper actuation.
4. **Workspace mapping** by moving the tool to boundary points and plotting the envelope.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for list with brief relevance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What are the four Denavit–Hartenberg parameters? State the units of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------|------|
| Link length | $a_i$ | Common normal distance between successive joint axes | mm |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Angle between successive joint axes about the common normal | deg or rad |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Distance along previous axis to the common normal | mm |
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Rotation about previous axis to the common normal | deg or rad |

*(3 marks: 2 for naming, 1 for unit accuracy)*

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Forward Kinematics Heavy

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the forward kinematics of a **planar 2R manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint variables $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Use the DH convention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same arm, given $L_1 = 300\,\text{mm}$, $L_2 = 250\,\text{mm}$, $\theta_1 = 40°$, $\theta_2 = 60°$, compute the **end-effector position** $(P_x, P_y)$ and its **orientation** $\phi$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Construct the DH table — already shown in Section 3.1.

**Step 2:** Write the individual $^{0}\!A_1$ and $^{1}\!A_2$ matrices.

**Step 3:** Multiply to obtain $T_{\text{base}}^{\text{tool}}$.

**Step 4:** Extract position and orientation columns.

Result (already derived in Section 3.1, Step 7):
$$P_x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$P_y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

*(Award 1 mark each for DH table, $^{0}\!A_1$, $^{1}\!A_2$, matrix product, trig simplification, $P_x$ & $P_y$ extraction, orientation. Total 7.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $P_x$:**
$$\begin{aligned} P_x \;=\; &L_1\cos(40°) + L_2\cos(40°+60°) \\[4pt] =\; &300 \times 0.7660 + 250 \times \cos(100°) \\[4pt] =\; &300 \times 0.7660 + 250 \times (-0.1736) \\[4pt] =\; &229.81 - 43.41 \\[4pt] =\; &\mathbf{186.40\,mm} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_y$:**
$$\begin{aligned} P_y \;=\; &L_1\sin(40°) + L_2\sin(100°) \\[4pt] =\; &300 \times 0.6428 + 250 \times 0.9848 \\[4pt] =\; &192.84 + 246.21 \\[4pt] =\; &\mathbf{439.05\,mm} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Orientation:**
$$\phi \;=\; \theta_1 + \theta_2 \;=\; 40° + 60° \;=\; \mathbf{100°}$$

*(1 mark each for substituting values, evaluating trig, final $P_x$, $P_y$, orientation, unit consistency, and statement. Total 7.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Inverse Kinematics & Application Heavy

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to compute the **inverse kinematics** of a planar 2R manipulator. Derive the expressions for $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ in terms of $(x, y, L_1, L_2)$. Discuss the **multiple solutions** (elbow-up vs elbow-down) and **workspace reachability**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pick-and-place trainer arm has $L_1 = 200\,\text{mm}$, $L_2 = 150\,\text{mm}$. Compute the joint angles required to position the gripper tip at $(250, 200)\,\text{mm}$. Verify reachability before solving. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Set up the FK equations as the starting point:**
$$P_x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$P_y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the unknown $\theta_2$ using algebraic manipulation:**
Square both equations and add:
$$\begin{aligned} P_x^2 + P_y^2 \;=\; L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $\cos\theta_2$:
$$\begin{aligned} \cos\theta_2 \;=\; \dfrac{x^2+y^2-L_1^2-L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \end{aligned}$$
$$\theta_2 = \pm\arccos(\cdot)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_1$** by using the geometric sum-of-angles trick:
$$\theta_1 = \arctan2(y,x) - \arctan2(L_2\sin\theta_2,\;L_1+L_2\cos\theta_2)$$

**Step 4 — Discuss multiple solutions:**
- **Elbow-up** vs **elbow-down** give **two** valid configurations for the same target.
- For a planar 2R arm, there are at most **two solutions**.
- Choose based on obstacle avoidance and joint-limit constraints.

**Step 5 — Reachability condition:**
The point $(x, y)$ is reachable **iff**
$$\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \;\leq\; \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \;\leq\; L_1 + L_2$$
If $\sqrt{x^2+y^2} > L_1 + L_2$, the point lies **outside** the reachable workspace. If $\sqrt{x^2+y^2} < \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$, the point lies inside the **inner dead zone**.

*(Marks distribution: 1 each for setup, squaring-and-adding, $\theta_2$ expression, $\theta_1$ expression, multiple-solution discussion, reachability condition, neat conclusion. Total 7.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Reachability check:**
$$\begin{aligned} r^2 \;=\; 250^2 + 200^2 \;=\; 62500 + 40000 \;=\; 102500 \\[4pt] R_{\max} \;=\; L_1 + L_2 \;=\; 350\,\text{mm} \\[4pt] R_{\min} \;=\; \vert 200 - 150 \vert \;=\; 50\,\text{mm} \\[4pt] \sqrt{102500} \;=\; 320.16\,\text{mm} \\[4pt] 50 \leq 320.16 \leq 350 \quad &\text{(reachable)} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\cos\theta_2$:**
$$\begin{aligned} \cos\theta_2 \;=\; \dfrac{102500 - 40000 - 22500}{2 \times 200 \times 150} \;=\; \dfrac{40000}{60000} \;=\; 0.6667 \\[4pt] \theta_2 \;=\; \arccos(0.6667) \;=\; \mathbf{48.19°} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\theta_1$:**
$$\begin{aligned} \theta_1 \;=\; &\arctan2(200,250) - \arctan2\!\left(150 \times \sin(48.19°),\;200 + 150 \times \cos(48.19°)\right) \\[6pt] =\; &38.66° - \arctan2(111.80,\;300.10) \\[6pt] =\; &38.66° - 20.42° \\[6pt] =\; &\mathbf{18.24°} \end{aligned}$$

**Final answer (elbow-up):**
$$\boxed{\theta_1 \approx 18.24°,\quad \theta_2 \approx 48.19°}$$

**Verification by forward substitution:**
$$\begin{aligned} P_x \;=\; &200\cos(18.24°) + 150\cos(66.43°) \\[4pt] \approx\; &200 \times 0.9496 + 150 \times 0.3997 \\[4pt] \approx\; &189.92 + 59.96 \;\approx\; 249.88 \;\approx\; 250\,\text{mm}\;\checkmark \\[6pt] P_y \;=\; &200\sin(18.24°) + 150\sin(66.43°) \\[4pt] \approx\; &200 \times 0.3130 + 150 \times 0.9166 \\[4pt] \approx\; &62.60 + 137.49 \;\approx\; 200.09 \;\approx\; 200\,\text{mm}\;\checkmark \end{aligned}$$

*(Marks distribution: 1 reachability, 1 setup, 1 $\cos\theta_2$ formula, 1 numerical value of $\theta_2$, 1 $\theta_1$ formula, 1 numerical value of $\theta_1$, 1 verification step. Total 7.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 1–2 marks each in the lab record and viva:**
>
> 1. **DH table axis-confusion:** Many students write $\alpha_i = 90°$ for planar arms. Since planar arms lie in a single vertical plane, $\alpha_i = 0$ for every link. Writing the wrong twist will silently propagate errors into all subsequent matrices.
> 2. **Wrong $\theta$ convention:** Elbow angle $\theta_2$ must be measured **relative to link 1**, not from the global X-axis. This is a frequent viva trap question.
> 3. **Reachability skipped:** Always check $\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$ against $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ and $L_1+L_2$ **before** applying IK. If you skip this and $\vert\cos\theta_2\vert > 1$, you will lose a full step's marks.
> 4. **Radian–degree mix-up in numerical evaluation:** Keep your calculator in **degree mode** for KTU numerical problems unless the question explicitly states otherwise.
> 5. **Joint-limit neglect:** A theoretically reachable point may still be physically unreachable if it requires $\theta_i$ outside the trainer arm's mechanical stops (typically $\pm 90°$ to $\pm 135°$ per joint).
> 6. **Forgetting the "elbow-up vs elbow-down" choice:** Always state **which configuration** you are using and justify (e.g., "elbow-up is chosen to avoid collision with the table surface").

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Robotic trainer unit** = a tabletop, multi-DOF articulated arm used for lab-scale kinematics and automation experiments.
- **Two primary mappings:** Forward Kinematics (joints $\to$ Cartesian) and Inverse Kinematics (Cartesian $\to$ joints).
- **DH parameters** are $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$ — four numbers that completely describe one link's geometry relative to its predecessor.
- **General A-matrix** is a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation containing rotation + translation.
- **Planar 2R forward kinematics** yields $P_x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$ and $P_y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.
- **Tool orientation** for planar 2R is $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$.
- **Inverse kinematics** uses the law of cosines: $\cos\theta_2 = (x^2+y^2-L_1^2-L_2^2) / (2 L_1 L_2)$.
- **Two IK solutions** exist for planar 2R — elbow-up and elbow-down — choose based on obstacle avoidance.
- **Reachability** requires $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \le \sqrt{x^2+y^2} \le L_1 + L_2$.
- **Joint angle convention** — always measure $\theta_2$ relative to link 1's local frame.
- **Stepper pulse count** = (required angle / step angle) × gear-ratio factor (used in lab programming).
- **Verification step** — after IK, always plug joint angles back into FK to confirm the target point is hit (within rounding tolerance).
- **Coordinate frames** on a trainer arm follow a strict Z-axis = joint-rotation-axis convention.
- **Pick-and-place cycle** standard sequence: HOME $\to$ PICK $\to$ GRIP $\to$ LIFT $\to$ MOVE $\to$ PLACE $\to$ RELEASE $\to$ HOME.
- **Workspace envelope** for planar 2R is an annulus bounded by $R_{\min}$ and $R_{\max}$.
- **KTU expected lab skills:** record DH table, plot FK outputs in Excel/Python, demonstrate pick-and-place, and answer viva on singularities, joint limits, and reachability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on 3-D Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL508 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on 3-D Printing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3-D Printing (Additive Manufacturing / AM)** is defined by ASTM F2792 as the *process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies.*

In the context of the KTU Mechanical Engineering Laboratory (PCMEL508), the term **Exercises on 3-D Printing** refers to a set of hands-on laboratory exercises involving the operation of an **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** desktop printer to fabricate a designed part from a CAD model. The student is expected to perform the complete digital workflow: **CAD → STL → G-code → Physical Part**.

The core technical operations include:

| Operation | Description |
|---|---|
| CAD Modelling | Creating 3D geometry using software (SolidWorks / Fusion 360 / Creo) |
| STL Export | Converting geometry into triangulated mesh (.stl file) |
| Slicing | Converting STL into layer-wise G-code using slicer (Cura / Slic3r) |
| Printing | Layer-by-layer deposition of thermoplastic filament |
| Post-Processing | Support removal, surface finishing, dimensional inspection |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Cake Baker's Layer Cake**
> Imagine baking a cake, but instead of baking the whole cake at once in an oven (traditional manufacturing), you extrude the batter one thin horizontal layer at a time. After each layer is laid down, it solidifies instantly, and the next layer is deposited precisely on top. After 200 such layers, you have a complete cake. **3-D printing works exactly this way** — a thermoplastic filament (like PLA/ABS) is melted and extruded through a heated nozzle to form a single thin layer; it cools and solidifies; the build platform descends by one **layer thickness** (typically **0.1 mm – 0.4 mm**); and the next layer is deposited on top. After several hundred to several thousand layers, a fully formed solid part is produced.

## 1.3 Why 3-D Printing is Taught in the Mechanical Engineering Lab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment bridges the gap between **CAD design (digital domain)** and **physical fabrication (manufacturing domain)**. It exposes the student to an **Industry 4.0 / Smart Manufacturing** technology that is rapidly replacing conventional prototyping in automotive, aerospace, biomedical, and tooling industries.

Key characteristics that make 3-D printing unique compared to conventional manufacturing:

- **Additive, not subtractive** → minimal material wastage.
- **No dedicated tooling required** → ideal for rapid prototyping.
- **Geometric complexity is "free"** → internal lattices, undercuts, and organic shapes are easily produced.
- **Lead time is short** → from CAD to part in **a few hours**.

## 1.4 Standard Terminology Students MUST Memorise

The following vocabulary appears frequently in KTU viva-voce and ESE questions:

- **Filament** — Thermoplastic feedstock (usually **1.75 mm diameter** PLA / ABS / PETG) supplied as a spool.
- **Nozzle Diameter** — Standard **0.4 mm** orifice through which melted plastic is extruded.
- **Build Volume** — Maximum printable envelope, typically **(220 × 220 × 250) mm** for desktop FDM printers.
- **Infill** — Internal percentage fill of a part (commonly **20 % – 30 %** for prototypes).
- **Layer Height** — Vertical resolution per pass, typically **0.1 mm** (fine) to **0.3 mm** (draft).
- **Print Speed** — Head travel speed, typically **40 – 80 mm/s**.
- **Nozzle Temperature** — **190 °C – 240 °C** depending on material.
- **Bed Temperature** — **50 °C – 110 °C** for first-layer adhesion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FDM Process — Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

The Fused Deposition Modeling process operates on a tightly sequenced chain of physical and digital events. Each step must be understood because KTU exam questions often test the student's understanding of *why* each step exists.

### Step 1 — CAD Model Creation
The student uses a CAD package to create a solid 3D model. The model must be a **water-tight solid** (no open edges, no intersecting surfaces). Boolean operations are used to add/subtract features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this step?** The printer has no intelligence — it can only follow geometry. A single missing face in the CAD model will cause the slicer to reject the STL file.

### Step 2 — STL Export
The CAD solid is exported as an **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** file. The smooth surfaces of the CAD model are approximated by a mesh of small triangular facets.

### Step 3 — Slicing & G-code Generation
A slicer software (e.g., Ultimaker Cura) mathematically slices the STL into horizontal layers of the chosen **layer height (h)**. For each layer, it generates:
- **Perimeter paths** (outer & inner walls)
- **Infill paths** (internal pattern)
- **Travel moves** (non-printing head movement)
- **Temperature & speed commands**

The output is a **G-code** file — a text file containing M (machine) and G (geometry) codes.

### Step 4 — Physical Printing
The printer interprets the G-code and:
1. Heats the nozzle to the set **extrusion temperature**.
2. Heats the bed to the set **bed temperature**.
3. Feeds filament via the **extruder gear**.
4. Moves the print head in **X-Y** while extruding plastic.
5. Lowers the **Z-axis (build platform)** by **h** after each layer.

### Step 5 — Post-Processing
After printing:
- Part is removed from the bed.
- Support structures (if any) are manually detached.
- Surface may be sanded or chemically smoothed.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the complete set of formulas required to solve KTU numerical questions on 3-D printing exercises.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Layers | $N = \dfrac{H}{h}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Filament Volume Required (solid part) | $V_f = \dfrac{V_{part}}{\eta}$ | mm³ |
| 3 | Filament Length Required | $L_f = \dfrac{4 \, V_f}{\pi \, d^2}$ | mm |
| 4 | Filament Mass Required | $m_f = \rho \, V_f$ | g |
| 5 | Infill Volume | $V_{infill} = V_{part} \times \dfrac{I}{100}$ | mm³ |
| 6 | Shell Volume | $V_{shell} = V_{outer} - V_{inner\,shell}$ | mm³ |
| 7 | Effective Print Time (rough estimate) | $T_p \approx \dfrac{V_f}{F_e}$ | seconds |
| 8 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $F_e = v \times d \times h$ | mm³/s |
| 9 | Material Cost | $C = m_f \times p_{kg}$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Dimensional Accuracy Error | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\vert D_{actual} - D_{design} \vert}{D_{design}} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Surface Roughness (rule of thumb) | $R_a \approx k_1 \, h$ | µm |

Where the symbols mean:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $H$ | Part height (build direction) | varies |
| $h$ | Layer height | **0.1 – 0.3 mm** |
| $d$ | Filament diameter | **1.75 mm** |
| $\eta$ | Material packing / efficiency factor | **0.95 – 1.00** |
| $\rho$ | Filament density (PLA) | **1.24 g/cm³** |
| $I$ | Infill percentage | **0 – 100 %** |
| $v$ | Print head speed | **40 – 80 mm/s** |
| $p_{kg}$ | Filament price per kg | varies |
| $k_1$ | Empirical roughness coefficient | **~ 200** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The skills practiced in this experiment are directly relevant to:

- **Rapid prototyping** in automotive and consumer-product R&D.
- **Biomedical implants** (customised prosthetics, dental crowns, surgical guides).
- **Aerospace** (lightweight lattice-structured brackets using EOS / SLM metal printers).
- **Tooling** (jigs, fixtures, conformal cooling inserts in injection moulds).
- **Architecture** (scale models with intricate geometry).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A frequent viva question is *"Why is FDM called an additive process?"* — Answer: Because the material is **added** layer by layer to form the part, in contrast to subtractive processes (milling, turning) where material is **removed** from a larger block.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Layers and Total Print Time

**Problem Setup:** A cube of side **50 mm** is to be printed on an FDM printer with layer height **h = 0.2 mm**, print speed **v = 50 mm/s**, nozzle diameter **d = 0.4 mm**. Estimate the number of layers and an approximate print time.

### Step A — Compute Number of Layers
The total height of the cube in the build direction is H = 50 mm. Each layer adds height h.

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{h}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{50 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
N = 250 \text{ layers}
$$

**Explanation:** Since each pass of the nozzle deposits one slice of thickness 0.2 mm, a 50 mm tall cube requires 250 such horizontal slices stacked vertically.

### Step B — Compute Part Volume
The cube is 50 mm × 50 mm × 50 mm.

$$
V_{part} = L \times B \times H
$$

$$
V_{part} = 50 \times 50 \times 50
$$

$$
V_{part} = 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step C — Compute Volumetric Flow Rate
The extrusion flow rate equals the cross-sectional area of a printed line times the head speed.

$$
F_e = v \times d \times h
$$

$$
F_e = 50 \, \tfrac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}} \times 0.4 \, \text{mm} \times 0.2 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
F_e = 4.0 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Explanation:** In 1 second, the head travels 50 mm, depositing a bead of width 0.4 mm and height 0.2 mm. The bead cross-section is 0.08 mm², so volume per second is 0.08 × 50 = 4 mm³.

### Step D — Compute Theoretical Print Time
Assuming the entire cube is solid (100 % infill) and $\eta = 1$:

$$
T_p = \dfrac{V_{part}}{F_e}
$$

$$
T_p = \dfrac{125{,}000}{4.0}
$$

$$
T_p = 31{,}250 \text{ s}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
T_p = \dfrac{31{,}250}{3600} \approx 8.68 \text{ hours}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Filament Mass and Cost Estimation

**Problem Setup:** For the same cube above, compute the filament length, mass, and cost. Take $\eta = 0.97$, density of PLA $\rho = 1.24$ g/cm³, and filament price $p_{kg} = \text{₹ 1500/kg}$.

### Step A — Effective Filament Volume Required

$$
V_f = \dfrac{V_{part}}{\eta}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{125{,}000}{0.97} \approx 128{,}866 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Convert to cm³:

$$
V_f = 128{,}866 \times 10^{-3} = 128.87 \text{ cm}^3
$$

### Step B — Filament Mass

$$
m_f = \rho \times V_f
$$

$$
m_f = 1.24 \times 128.87
$$

$$
m_f \approx 159.8 \text{ g} \approx 0.160 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step C — Filament Length
Filament diameter $d_f = 1.75$ mm. Cross-sectional area:

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi \, d_f^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.75)^2}{4} \approx 2.405 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Length:

$$
L_f = \dfrac{V_f}{A_f}
$$

$$
L_f = \dfrac{128{,}866}{2.405} \approx 53{,}583 \text{ mm} = 53.58 \text{ m}
$$

### Step D — Material Cost

$$
C = m_f \times p_{kg}
$$

$$
C = 0.160 \times 1500 = \text{₹ 240}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dimensional Accuracy of a 3-D Printed Part

**Problem Setup:** A nominal **20.0 mm** hole was designed in CAD. The printed part was measured with Vernier calipers as **20.35 mm**. Calculate the dimensional error and comment.

### Step A — Absolute Deviation

$$
\Delta D = \vert D_{actual} - D_{design} \vert
$$

$$
\Delta D = \vert 20.35 - 20.00 \vert = 0.35 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step B — Percentage Error

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta D}{D_{design}} \times 100
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{0.35}{20.00} \times 100 = 1.75 \,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The error of **1.75 %** is typical for FDM printers without calibration. The hole printed **larger** than designed — a common artefact because the slicer over-extrudes slightly to ensure wall adhesion. Designers compensate by **shrinking hole diameters** in CAD by **0.1 – 0.3 mm** before printing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Print Parameters on Output Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is a high-yield KTU revision tool.** It is commonly tested as a 3-mark question: *"Explain how layer height affects print quality."*

| Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer Height (h) | Faster print, rougher surface, weaker Z-strength | Slower print, smoother surface, stronger Z-bond | Quality vs. Speed |
| Print Speed (v) | Faster print, possible under-extrusion & ringing | Slower print, sharper detail | Speed vs. Accuracy |
| Infill (%) | Stronger part, more material & time | Lighter part, less material, weaker | Weight vs. Strength |
| Nozzle Temp | Better layer adhesion, stringing risk | Poor adhesion, layer delamination | Adhesion vs. Stringing |
| Bed Temp | Better first-layer adhesion, less warping | Warping on large parts | Adhesion vs. EAS |
| Shell Count (walls) | Stronger part, more material | Weaker walls | Strength vs. Material |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End 3-D Printing Workflow

The complete digital-to-physical workflow in a KTU FDM laboratory exercise is represented below. Each block represents a critical stage the student must perform.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CAD_Model_3D] --> B[STL_Export_Triangulation]
    B --> C[Slicer_Software_Cura]
    C --> D[Slicing_Layer_Generation]
    D --> E[G_Code_Generation]
    E --> F[FDM_Printer_Hardware]
    F --> G[Heated_Bed_and_Nozzle]
    G --> H[Layer_wise_Deposition]
    H --> I[Physical_3D_Part]
    I --> J[Support_Removal]
    J --> K[Dimensional_Inspection]
    K --> L[Report_with_Accuracy]

    classDef digital fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef process fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef output fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef quality fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;

    class A,B,C,D,E digital;
    class F,G,H,J process;
    class I output;
    class K,L quality;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The blue blocks (left) represent the *digital* workflow. The yellow blocks (middle) represent the *physical* fabrication steps. The green block represents the final *output part*. The pink block represents the *quality assurance* steps that the student must perform and document in the lab record.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — FDM Printer Hardware Architecture

The internal hardware of the FDM printer is shown below. Each component plays a role in producing the part.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph mechanical[Mechanical_System]
        A1[Stepper_Motor_X_Axis] --> A2[Print_Head_Carriage]
        A3[Stepper_Motor_Y_Axis] --> A4[Build_Platform_Bed]
        A5[Stepper_Motor_Z_Axis] --> A6[Vertical_Lead_Screw]
    end

    subgraph extrusion[Extrusion_System]
        B1[Filament_Spool] --> B2[Extruder_Drive_Gear]
        B2 --> B3[Bowden_Tube_or_Direct_Drive]
        B3 --> B4[Heated_Nozzle_d_0_4_mm]
    end

    subgraph thermal[Thermal_System]
        C1[Nozzle_Heater_Cartridge] --> C4
        C2[Bed_Heater_Aluminium_Plate] --> C5[Heated_Build_Plate]
    end

    subgraph control[Control_System]
        D1[Microcontroller_Board] --> D2[G_Code_Interpreter]
        D2 --> A1
        D2 --> A3
        D2 --> A5
        D2 --> B2
        D2 --> C1
        D2 --> C2
    end

    A2 --> B4
    A4 --> C5

    classDef mech fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef extr fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef therm fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef ctrl fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6 mech;
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 extr;
    class C1,C2,C4,C5 therm;
    class D1,D2 ctrl;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functionality Explained:**
> - The **Control System** (purple) is the brain — it parses G-code line by line and issues motion and thermal commands.
> - The **Mechanical System** (light blue) provides the X-Y-Z motion using three independent stepper motors.
> - The **Extrusion System** (orange) feeds filament into the heated nozzle at the rate commanded by the slicer.
> - The **Thermal System** (red) maintains the nozzle and bed at the temperatures set by the user profile.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*List any three commonly used slicer software packages for FDM 3-D printing and state the function of a slicer.*

**Model Answer:**
A slicer is software that converts an STL (triangulated 3-D mesh) file into layer-by-layer **G-code instructions** that an FDM printer can execute. Three common slicer packages are:

1. **Ultimaker Cura** — Open-source, most widely used in academic labs.
2. **PrusaSlicer** — Developed by Prusa Research, supports multi-material printing.
3. **Slic3r** — Open-source, predecessor to PrusaSlicer, advanced infill controls.

**Core function:** The slicer mathematically slices the CAD geometry into horizontal layers of user-defined **layer height (h)** and generates per-layer toolpaths for **perimeters, infill, travel moves, and support structures**, embedded within a complete G-code file.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Explain the meaning of the terms (i) Infill, (ii) Support Structure, and (iii) Build Orientation in FDM printing.*

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Infill:** The internal percentage of material filled inside a printed part. A 20 % infill means 20 % of the interior volume is filled with a pattern (e.g., grid, honeycomb, gyroid), while the rest is air. Higher infill → stronger, heavier part.

- **(ii) Support Structure:** Extra material automatically generated by the slicer beneath **overhangs** (features angled more than ~45° from vertical). Supports prevent the molten plastic from sagging into empty space and are removed manually after printing.

- **(iii) Build Orientation:** The angular position of the part on the print bed. It affects **strength**, **surface finish**, **support requirements**, and **print time**. Parts should be oriented so that critical load-bearing directions align with the layer-bond (Z) direction.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*(a)* Describe, with a neat block diagram, the complete workflow of 3-D printing from CAD model to finished part. (7 Marks)
*(b)* A hollow cuboidal box of outer dimensions **80 mm × 60 mm × 40 mm** and uniform wall thickness of **2 mm** is to be printed using PLA filament on an FDM machine. Given: layer height **h = 0.2 mm**, print speed **v = 60 mm/s**, nozzle diameter **d = 0.4 mm**, density of PLA **ρ = 1.24 g/cm³**, filament diameter **d_f = 1.75 mm**, filament cost **₹ 1800/kg**, packing efficiency **η = 0.95**. Compute:
   (i) Number of layers,
   (ii) Filament mass required,
   (iii) Material cost.

(7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The block diagram of the workflow is:

```
CAD_Model_3D  →  STL_Export  →  Slicer_Software  →
Slicing_to_Layers  →  G_Code_File  →  FDM_Printer  →
Layer_wise_Deposition  →  Finished_Part  →  Post_Processing
```

**Description of Each Stage:**

1. **CAD Modelling:** The part is designed in 3D CAD software (SolidWorks / Fusion 360). The model must be a watertight solid.

2. **STL Export:** The CAD solid is tessellated into a triangular mesh and saved as an **.stl** file.

3. **Slicing:** The slicer (Cura) imports the STL and divides the geometry into horizontal layers of chosen thickness *h*.

4. **G-code Generation:** For each layer, the slicer generates perimeters, infill, travel moves, and temperature commands — all encoded as G-code.

5. **FDM Printing:** The printer interprets the G-code line-by-line. The nozzle melts PLA filament at ~210 °C and extrudes it onto a heated bed.

6. **Post-Processing:** The printed part is removed, supports (if any) are detached, and surfaces are finished if required.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Number of Layers:**
Build direction height H = 40 mm (the smallest cube dimension oriented vertically for best stability).

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{40}{0.2} = 200 \text{ layers}
$$

**(ii) Filament Mass:**
Outer volume:

$$
V_{out} = 80 \times 60 \times 40 = 192{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Inner cavity volume (since wall thickness is 2 mm on each side, the inner box is 76 × 56 × 36):

$$
V_{in} = 76 \times 56 \times 36 = 153{,}216 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Wall (shell) volume:

$$
V_{shell} = V_{out} - V_{in} = 192{,}000 - 153{,}216 = 38{,}784 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Effective filament volume with packing factor:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{V_{shell}}{\eta} = \dfrac{38{,}784}{0.95} \approx 40{,}825 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Convert to cm³:

$$
V_f = 40.825 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Filament mass:

$$
m_f = \rho \times V_f = 1.24 \times 40.825 \approx 50.62 \text{ g} \approx 0.0506 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iii) Material Cost:**

$$
C = m_f \times p_{kg} = 0.0506 \times 1800 \approx \text{₹ 91.10}
$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*(a)* With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3-D printer. List any four common print defects and their causes. (7 Marks)
*(b)* A bracket is designed in CAD with a critical hole diameter of **15.00 mm**. After printing, the hole is measured as **15.42 mm** using a Vernier caliper. Calculate the dimensional error in percentage. If the printer's layer height is **0.15 mm**, estimate the total number of layers for a part of height **45 mm**. Comment on whether the dimensional error is acceptable for a prototype. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle of FDM:**
In FDM, a thermoplastic filament (PLA / ABS) is fed by an extruder gear into a **heated nozzle** (typically **190 °C – 240 °C**). The nozzle melts the filament and extrudes it as a thin molten bead onto a **heated build platform**. The print head moves in **X-Y** following the per-layer toolpath generated by the slicer. After each layer is completed, the build platform descends by one **layer height (h)** along the Z-axis, and the next layer is deposited. The process repeats until the part is complete.

A neat sketch should show:
- Filament spool feeding through extruder gear.
- Bowden tube guiding filament to the hot end.
- Heated nozzle above the build platform.
- Stepper motors for X, Y, and Z motion.
- Layered deposition visible in the printed part.

**Four Common Print Defects:**

| # | Defect | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Warping** | Uneven cooling of first layer; bed temperature too low |
| 2 | **Stringing** | Oozing of molten plastic during travel moves; retraction setting too low |
| 3 | **Layer Delamination** | Poor inter-layer adhesion; nozzle temperature too low |
| 4 | **Under-extrusion / Gaps** | Clogged nozzle; filament diameter inconsistent; print speed too high |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Dimensional Error:**
Absolute deviation:

$$
\Delta D = \vert 15.42 - 15.00 \vert = 0.42 \text{ mm}
$$

Percentage error:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{0.42}{15.00} \times 100 = 2.80 \,\%
$$

**(ii) Number of Layers:**

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{45}{0.15} = 300 \text{ layers}
$$

**(iii) Comment:**
An error of **2.80 %** is **borderline acceptable** for a non-critical prototype. For functional parts requiring mating with standard fasteners (e.g., M8 bolt in a 8 mm hole), such error would be **unacceptable**, and the designer should compensate by reducing hole diameters in the CAD model by **0.2 – 0.4 mm** before slicing. For purely visual prototypes, the error is tolerable and can be improved by **recalibrating the extruder E-steps**, reducing print speed, and ensuring stable bed temperature.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in 3-D Printing Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Volume Calculations:** Students mix mm³ and cm³ when computing filament mass. Always convert mm³ to cm³ (divide by 1000) **before** multiplying by density in g/cm³.
> 2. **Forgetting the Packing Factor η:** Real prints do not achieve 100 % packing. KTU questions typically give η between **0.90 – 0.98**. Skipping η causes loss of **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing Build Direction vs. Part Height:** The "height" used to compute number of layers is the dimension **along the Z-axis after the part is oriented on the bed**, not the largest physical dimension of the part.
> 4. **Wrong Cost Formula:** Material cost = mass (in kg) × price (per kg). Some students mistakenly use volume — full mark loss.
> 5. **Dimensional Error Sign Convention:** Always use **absolute value** $\vert \cdot \vert$ in the numerator. Negative percentages have no physical meaning in error analysis.
> 6. **Omitting Units in Final Answer:** KTU valuation strictly deducts marks for missing units (mm³, mm/s, °C, g, ₹).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & ESE:**

- ⭐ 3-D Printing = **Additive Manufacturing**; defined by **ASTM F2792**.
- ⭐ The lab typically uses **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** technology.
- ⭐ Complete workflow: **CAD → STL → G-code → Printed Part → Inspection**.
- ⭐ Common filament: **PLA**, diameter **1.75 mm**, density **1.24 g/cm³**.
- ⭐ Typical desktop nozzle diameter: **0.4 mm**.
- ⭐ Typical layer height range: **0.1 – 0.3 mm**.
- ⭐ Typical print speed range: **40 – 80 mm/s**.
- ⭐ Typical bed temperature: **50 – 110 °C**; nozzle temperature: **190 – 240 °C**.
- ⭐ Formula — Number of layers: $N = H/h$.
- ⭐ Formula — Filament length: $L_f = 4 V_f / (\pi d_f^2)$.
- ⭐ Formula — Filament mass: $m_f = \rho V_f$.
- ⭐ Formula — Print time (approx): $T_p = V_f / F_e$, where $F_e = v \cdot d \cdot h$.
- ⭐ Formula — Dimensional error: $\varepsilon = (\Delta D / D_{design}) \times 100$.
- ⭐ Build orientation affects **strength, support needs, and print time**.
- ⭐ Higher infill → stronger but heavier and slower.
- ⭐ Lower layer height → smoother finish but longer print.
- ⭐ Common defects: **warping, stringing, delamination, under-extrusion**.
- ⭐ Holes usually print **larger** than designed; compensate in CAD.
- ⭐ Dimensional accuracy of a stock desktop FDM: typically **± 0.2 mm** or **± 1 – 2 %**.
- ⭐ 3-D printing produces **minimal wastage** compared to CNC machining.
- ⭐ The experiment teaches **digital manufacturing workflow**, an **Industry 4.0** competency.
